title: RedZeD: Computing persistent homology by Reduction to Zero Differentials
summary: We introduce a new algorithm for computing persistent homology of Vietoris--Rips filtrations, which in many cases offers a considerable speedup over the existing implementation of the persistence pairing algorithm. The key innovation, called active enumeration, is made possible by a new theoretical framework of Reduction to Zero Differentials (hence RedZeD) in which to view persistent homology.

AI.tldr: Introduces active enumeration for Vietoris–Rips PH and a Reduction to Zero Differentials (RedZeD) framework, yielding significant speedups over standard persistence‑pairing implementations.
AI.motivation: To address computational bottlenecks in persistent homology, especially for Vietoris–Rips filtrations, by reframing the pairing step through a new theoretical framework.
AI.method: Develop an active enumeration algorithm within the RedZeD framework, reframing PH computation as Reduction to Zero Differentials to enable efficient persistence pairing.
AI.result: Demonstrates considerable speedups in many cases compared to existing implementations.
AI.conclusion: RedZeD and the active enumeration approach offer a faster method for computing persistent homology of Vietoris–Rips filtrations, with strong empirical potential and avenues for further study.

title: What Should Agents Say? Action-state Communication for Efficient Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Multi-agent systems (MAS) built on large language models are typically organized around roles, pipelines, and turn schedules, while the content that agents pass to one another is often left as unconstrained natural language. However, this free-form communication can rapidly inflate token usage, consume the shared context window, and ultimately affect both system performance and inference cost. We analyze five common inter-agent communication strategies across two MAS topologies, finding that no fixed strategy is universally optimal. Instead, effective inter-agent messages consistently preserve action-centered information needed by downstream agents. Building on this, we propose the PACT (Protocolized Action-state Communication and Transmission), which treats inter-agent communication as a public state-update problem and projects each raw agent output into a compact action-state record before it enters shared history. Across different MAS topologies, PACT consistently improves the performance-cost trade-off, achieving comparable or stronger task performance with substantially fewer tokens. The gains extend to production coding harnesses: PACT lifts OpenHands' resolve rate at -10% tokens-per-resolved, and is resolve-neutral on SWE-agent while halving input tokens. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/iNLP-Lab/PACT.

AI.tldr: Inter-agent communication in LLM-based MAS is not universally optimizable by fixed message styles. A protocolization approach—PACT—reduces token usage while preserving action-centered information, improving the cost-performance trade-off across MAS topologies and even in production code harnesses.
AI.motivation: Reduce token growth and context window strain in multi-agent systems without sacrificing essential downstream action-state information.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate five common inter-agent communication strategies across two MAS topologies; identify information essential for downstream agents; design PACT to project each raw agent output into compact action-state records before adding to shared history; evaluate across MAS topologies and production code harnesses.
AI.result: PACT consistently improves the performance-to-cost trade-off, achieving comparable or better task performance with substantially fewer tokens; reduces tokens-per-resolved in production harnesses (≈10% reduction) and is resolve-neutral on SWE-agent while halving input tokens.
AI.conclusion: Treating inter-agent messaging as a public state-update problem with compact action-state representations yields robust improvements across MAS topologies and production deployments; code is publicly available.

title: NIV: Neural Axis Variations for Variable Font Generation
summary: Variable fonts enable continuous variation of glyph geometry along semantic design axes such as weight, width, slant, and optical size. However, constructing a variable font from a static font remains a labor-intensive process requiring expert typographic design and manual specification of glyph variation data. We introduce NIV (Neural Axis Variations), a method that automatically converts a static font into a fully functional variable font. Given glyph outlines and a set of desired design axes, NIV predicts per-point displacements. The model operates directly on vector glyph geometry and employs a novel Property Embedding mechanism that captures interactions between multiple axes, enabling consistent multi-axis variation within a unified framework. We train NIV on a newly constructed dataset derived from variable Google Fonts, comprising over one million variation tuples. The resulting model generalizes across unseen code points, unseen font styles, high-complexity CJK glyphs, and even out-of-distribution handwriting inputs. The generated outputs are standard variable font files supporting continuous interpolation via existing rendering engines. To facilitate research, we release the dataset, the complete training and inference implementation, and trained models at https://github.com/ndvbd/NIV. Beyond typography, our approach demonstrates how structured geometric objects with continuous parametric variation can be synthesized using neural deformations.

AI.tldr: Converts static fonts into functional variable fonts automatically using neural axis variations (NIV).
AI.motivation: Reduce manual, expert-driven effort required to create variable fonts; enable multi-axis continuous variation across diverse glyph sets.
AI.method: A neural model deforms vector glyph geometry point-wise conditioned on design axes, using a novel Property Embedding to model axis interactions; trained on a large dataset of existing variable-font variations; outputs standard variable font files.
AI.result: Generalizes to unseen code points, unseen font styles, high-complexity CJK glyphs, and handwriting; provides continuous interpolation via standard renderers; dataset and code released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a general approach for synthesizing structured geometric objects with continuous parametric variation through neural deformations; potential applicability beyond typography; facilitates research.

title: Efficient Computation of Distance Functions for Navigation Vector Fields in Lie Groups
summary: Vector-field-based methods are widely used for robot control and are often applied to the path-tracking problem. Some vector field approaches require repeatedly computing the distance between the robot configuration and the curve, as well as the corresponding closest point. Recently, vector fields have been extended to Lie Groups. In this case, this computation can be expensive, especially when performed at high control frequencies on embedded platforms. This paper proposes a method for efficiently computing the distance between a point and a curve represented as what is called a G-polynomial curve, which is a curve representation that generalizes polynomial curves to matrix Lie groups. The proposed approach exploits the structure of these curves to reduce the problem to a small number of polynomial root-finding computations. Simulation results show that the method significantly reduces computation time while maintaining accuracy compared to existing optimization-based approaches. Practical formulas are also provided for the case of the group SE(3), and the method is validated experimentally on a robotic manipulator. The methodology is implemented in a computational package, available online.

AI.tldr: Efficient distance-to-curve computation for G-polynomial curves on matrix Lie groups, enabling real-time vector-field control; reduces to few polynomial root-finding steps; explicit SE(3) formulas; validated in simulation and on a robotic manipulator; open-source software available.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational burden of distance and closest-point computations in vector-field-based path tracking on Lie groups, especially at high control frequencies on embedded platforms.
AI.method: Introduce G-polynomial curve representations on matrix Lie groups. Leverage their structure to transform the point-to-curve distance problem into a small number of polynomial root-finding tasks. Derive practical SE(3) formulas and provide an algorithm. Implement the approach in a software package.
AI.result: Significant reduction in computation time with accuracy comparable to optimization-based approaches; validated through simulations and an experimental robotic manipulator. Software package released online.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a real-time, structure-exploiting solution for distance computations to curves on Lie groups, with concrete SE(3) formulas and an open-source implementation, broadening the applicability of vector-field-based control on embedded platforms.

title: GITCO: Gated Inference-Time Context Optimization in TSFMs
comment: ICML 2026 Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data
summary: Patch-based Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) suffer from context poisoning: structurally anomalous patches capture disproportionate attention and silently degrade zero-shot forecast quality. We propose improving TSFM accuracy at inference time by optimizing the input context rather than modifying model weights. We present GITCO (Gated Inference-Time Context Optimization), a lightweight three-component framework: Gate, Router, and Critic that selectively identifies and suppresses harmful patches without any parameter updates. Evaluated on TimesFM 2.5 across 53 GIFT-Eval datasets under K-fold cross-validation, GITCO achieves an average +1.95% MASE reduction on TimesFM 2.5 while capturing 89.9% of the improvement upper bound. We introduce context sensitivity profiles as a new characterizable property of TSFMs: the mapping from time series meta-features to expected accuracy improvement under inference-time context intervention, shaped jointly by model architecture and the statistical structure of the data.

AI.tldr: GITCO enables inference-time context optimization for patch-based Time Series Foundation Models to suppress harmful patches, achieving notable accuracy gains without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Patch-based TSFMs are prone to context poisoning where structurally anomalous patches dominate attention and degrade zero-shot forecasts; a lightweight, parameter-free remedy at inference is desirable.
AI.method: A three-component framework—Gate, Router, and Critic—that identifies and suppresses harmful patches at inference time with no parameter updates; evaluated on TimesFM 2.5 across 53 GIFT-Eval datasets under K-fold cross-validation.
AI.result: Average 1.95 percentage point reduction in MASE on TimesFM 2.5; 89.9% of the improvement upper bound captured; introduces context sensitivity profiles—mapping from time-series meta-features and model architecture to expected accuracy gains under inference-time intervention.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time context optimization is a viable path to improve TSFM accuracy without altering model weights; context sensitivity profiles offer a new lens to understand model-data interactions and potential gains across architectures and datasets.

title: Do Models Share Safety Representations? Cross-Model Steering for Safe Visual Generation
summary: Recent progress in generative modeling has made safety control a central challenge, yet existing approaches remain largely model-specific, requiring retraining or tailored interventions for each new architecture. In this work, we ask whether safety can be represented as a portable latent direction, learned once and reused across heterogeneous generators. We introduce the first framework for cross-model safety steering, in which a safety direction is estimated in a source LLM from paired safe-unsafe prompts, transported to a target generator through a lightweight alignment fitted on benign data alone, and applied at inference time. Crucially, our pipeline never accesses unsafe data on the target side, isolating whether safety can be transferred through shared representation geometry. Beyond a single global direction, we also identify a multi-vector extension that captures category-specific safety behaviors, enabling more selective control. We evaluate our approach in text-to-image and text-to-video generation across diverse source-target model pairs. Across models, transferred safety directions achieve ASR reduction and CLIP-Score/FID trade-offs comparable to directions learned natively on the target model using unsafe data, while requiring no target-side unsafe data. This indicates that safety improvements do not come at the expense of generation quality. Our results point to a modular view of safety: safety-relevant behavior is not purely model-local, but can be controlled through latent directions that persist across models. This suggests a new path toward lightweight, reusable safety mechanisms that do not require target-side unsafe data.

AI.tldr: A framework for cross-model safety steering using portable latent directions learned from a source model and transferred to diverse target generators without target unsafe data; includes a multi-vector extension for category-specific safety and maintains generation quality.
AI.motivation: Safety controls in generative models are typically tied to a specific architecture and require retraining or bespoke interventions. The work proposes a reusable, lightweight safety mechanism by learning a latent safety direction that transfers across heterogeneous models.
AI.method: Estimate a safety direction in a source LLM from paired safe-unsafe prompts; transport to a target generator via a lightweight alignment trained only on benign data; apply at inference time. Also extend to multiple vectors to capture category-specific safety behaviors.
AI.result: Transferred safety directions reduce unsafe outputs (ASR) and achieve CLIP-Score and FID trade-offs similar to directions learned natively on the target with unsafe data, without accessing target Unsafe data; results hold across text-to-image and text-to-video model pairs.
AI.conclusion: Safety can be modularized as latent directions that persist across models, enabling reusable, target-free safety mechanisms that do not compromise generation quality.

title: Uncertainty Aware Functional Behavior Prediction and Material Fatigue Assessment for Circular Factory
comment: 27 pages, submitted to the Journal of Manufacturing Systems' special issue about circular factories, the manuscript is under review
summary: Returned products in circular factories re-enter production with heterogeneous degradation states, usage histories, and remaining capability. Reuse cannot be decided from the current inspection alone, because future function fulfillment and component integrity may evolve differently under the next service scenario. Existing PHM approaches support degradation prediction, but often target fixed operating conditions or isolated component benchmarks, while material-fatigue assessment is rarely linked to system-level functional prognosis. This paper addresses this gap for an angle grinder by combining uncertainty-aware functional prediction with component-level fatigue assessment in an instance-specific reliability workflow. The proposed framework combines the current tool state with recent force--torque usage windows. A convolutional encoder extracts loading patterns from spindle forces and shaft torque, and an LSTM backbone predicts nine functional variables as Gaussian mean and variance estimates. In parallel, the same loading history is translated into output-shaft fatigue information through finite-element-supported stress reconstruction, S--N/Miner damage evaluation with Haibach extension, and Paris-law crack-growth analysis. A streaming replay algorithm consolidates both branches into functional, material, and system reliability trajectories. Held-out tests show mean \(2\%\)-tolerance accuracy of 0.9652 across nine outputs. Thermal variables are predicted near-perfectly, while drive motor current and load speed remain the most demanding dynamic outputs, with \(R^2\) values of 0.9750 and 0.9924. Torque history is especially important for these variables, and the conventional LSTM outperforms GRU and xLSTM in the short-history setting. Reliability calibration is most informative for drive motor current, where predicted and observed exceedance probabilities ...

AI.tldr: An uncertainty-aware, instance-specific reliability workflow for recycled angle grinders that fuses functional prognosis with fatigue-based material degradation using loading histories, FE stress reconstruction, and Paris/Miner analysis.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap where existing PHM predicts degradation under fixed conditions and isolated components, lacking system-level functional prognosis for reused products with heterogeneous degradation and uncertain future service.
AI.method: A CNN encoder extracts loading patterns from spindle forces and shaft torque, feeding an LSTM that outputs nine functional variables as Gaussian means/variances. Simultaneously, the same history informs fatigue via FE stress reconstruction, S–N/Miner damage with Haibach extension, and Paris-law crack growth. A streaming replay combines functional and material trajectories into system reliability.
AI.result: Hold-out tests yield ~2% mean tolerance across nine outputs (mean 0.9652). Thermal variables are predicted near-perfectly; drive motor current and load speed are hardest with R^2 = 0.9750 and 0.9924. Torque history is crucial; LSTM outperforms GRU/xLSTM in short-history settings. Reliability calibration is most informative for drive motor current with observed/predicted exceedance probabilities.
AI.conclusion: Integrating uncertainty-aware functional prediction with component-level fatigue in an instance-specific framework improves reliability prognosis for recycled tools, highlighting the importance of torque history and physics-informed fatigue modeling for circular economy reuse.

title: SentinelBench: A Benchmark for Long-Running Monitoring Agents
summary:
AI agents are increasingly asked to carry out work that spans minutes, hours, or longer. Yet the default model of agent behavior is continuous action: issuing tool calls, refreshing pages, searching for alternatives, or otherwise trying to force progress. This is the wrong approach for many long-running tasks, which are better served by a strategy of sustained attention. Instead, agents should monitor an environment, notice when an external event makes progress possible, then respond promptly without wasting resources while waiting. To measure progress on this class of tasks, we introduce SentinelBench, an open-source benchmark for time-evolving monitoring tasks.
  SentinelBench contains 100 tasks across 10 synthetic web environments, including email, calendars, finance, professional networking, and entertainment. Each environment exposes a live web interface and replays a scripted sequence of events, requiring agents to navigate and reason about web pages whose state shifts underfoot. SentinelBench measures task completion, reaction time, and resource use, exposing the tradeoff between responsiveness and cost. We report results across three models and two browser-agent harnesses, establishing performance baselines for future comparison and demonstrating how agent design choices can dramatically impact key metrics. Together, these results show that SentinelBench distinguishes meaningful differences in agent behavior.

AI.tldr: SentinelBench is a benchmark for long-running, time-evolving tasks emphasizing sustained attention over continuous action. It introduces 100 tasks across 10 live web environments, benchmarks task completion, reaction time, and resource use, and demonstrates how agent design choices affect responsiveness vs. cost to differentiate agent behavior.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap where traditional agents rely on continuous action in long-running tasks. Provide a benchmark to evaluate agents’ ability to monitor environments, detect progress events, and react promptly while conserving resources.
AI.method: 100 tasks across 10 synthetic web environments with live interfaces (email, calendars, finance, networking, entertainment). Each environment replays scripted events requiring navigation and reasoning over shifting web states. Evaluations use two browser-agent harnesses and three models, measuring task completion, reaction time, and resource use to quantify the tradeoff between responsiveness and cost.
AI.result: SentinelBench yields measurable distinctions among agent behaviors; establishes baselines across models and harnesses; demonstrates how design decisions impact key metrics and provides a framework for future comparisons on time-evolving monitoring tasks.
AI.conclusion: Long-running tasks benefit from sustained-attention strategies over continuous action. SentinelBench captures essential metrics (completion, reaction time, resource use) and enables meaningful comparisons of agent designs, supporting development toward more efficient, responsive agents for evolving environments.

title: LightVesselNet: An Ultra-Lightweight Sub-100K Parameter Network for Retinal Blood Vessel Segmentation
summary: Retinal blood vessel segmentation plays a vital role in the early detection of diabetic retinopathy and glaucoma. While recent deep learning models have achieved great segmentation accuracy, they typically require heavy computational resources, making real-world deployment on edge devices difficult. In this paper, we propose LightVesselNet, an efficient neural network designed for retinal vessel segmentation in a resource-constrained environment. Despite containing only 75K parameters, LightVesselNet performs competitively with much larger models. The network employs a compact encoder decoder architecture enhanced with channel and spatial attention mechanisms, a multi-scale feature aggregation module at the bottleneck, and a subpixel upsampling strategy in the decoder. A dedicated edge residual connection preserves fine vessel detail throughout decoding. Extensive experiments on five publicly available datasets: DRIVE, STARE, CHASEDB1, FIVES, and HRF, yield sensitivity scores of 0.8189, 0.8499, 0.8640, 0.8634, 0.8096, and Dice coefficients of 0.8070, 0.8072, 0.8181, 0.8649, and 0.7686, respectively. LightVesselNet shows improved efficiency (Performance vs Parameter or GFlops) compared to State-of-the-Art models. Cross-dataset evaluation confirms the model's generalisation capability. Overall, LightVesselNet is a strong candidate for deployment in low-resource clinical settings and mobile screening tools.

AI.tldr: LightVesselNet is a 75K-parameter, attention-enhanced encoder-decoder for retinal vessel segmentation, optimized for edge devices; claims competitive accuracy with larger models and improved efficiency across five public datasets, with cross-dataset generalization.
AI.motivation: Enable accurate retinal vessel segmentation in resource-constrained environments (edge devices, mobile screening) by reducing model size and compute.
AI.method: Compact encoder-decoder architecture with channel and spatial attention, multi-scale feature aggregation at the bottleneck, subpixel upsampling in the decoder, and an edge residual connection to preserve fine vessel structures; ~75k parameters.
AI.result: Reported metrics include sensitivity and Dice on five public datasets (DRIVE, STARE, CHASE-DB1, FIVES, HRF); sensitivity values listed as 0.8189, 0.8499, 0.8640, 0.8634, 0.8096 (note: the abstract text contains a mismatch with an extra value), and Dice values 0.8070, 0.8072, 0.8181, 0.8649, 0.7686; claim improved efficiency (parameter/GFlops) over state-of-the-art and good cross-dataset generalization.
AI.conclusion: LightVesselNet is a promising low-resource solution for retinal vessel segmentation; the abstract would benefit from ablation studies, consistent reporting of metrics (dataset-wise), and details on training/augmentation to assess reproducibility and robustness.

title: State commitment learning: training language models to distinguish computation from memory
summary: Reasoning language models do not distinguish tokens used for computation from tokens that constitute persistent state: once generated, all hidden thoughts remain in context and influence future predictions. As a result, downstream reasoning may depend on failed attempts, dead ends, and private scratch work that should not be safely relied on later. We recast this phenomenon as a new training objective, state commitment learning: training models to explicitly distinguish information that should be committed as persistent state from temporary computation that can be discarded. We define a counterfactual criterion, persistent-state sufficiency, which makes it trainable and measurable whether an answer remains usable after hidden thoughts are erased. We then propose Counterfactual Erasure RL (CERL), which evaluates, under the same prefix, both a path that keeps hidden thoughts and a path that erases them, and gives reward only when the erasure path remains correct. We also introduce the Erasure Dependence Protocol and show across mathematics, long-chain logic, scientific QA, and multi-turn tool-use evaluation that CERL substantially reduces answer dependence on hidden thoughts without sacrificing accuracy, consistently outperforming correctness-only RL and long-answer SFT baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces state commitment learning and Counterfactual Erasure RL (CERL) to separate persistent state from temporary computation in reasoning LMs; uses persistent-state sufficiency as a trainable criterion and evaluates with an Erasure Protocol to reward erasure-consistent answers; demonstrates reduced dependence on hidden thoughts across domains with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning models retain hidden thoughts in context, causing downstream answers to rely on failed attempts, dead ends, or private scratch work. A training objective is needed to ensure only persistent information is retained.
AI.method: Define state commitment learning and persistent-state sufficiency; propose Counterfactual Erasure RL (CERL) that compares two prefix-consistent paths—one preserving hidden thoughts and one erasing them—and rewards the erasure path only if it remains correct; introduce the Erasure Dependence Protocol and evaluate across mathematics, long-chain logic, scientific QA, and multi-turn tool-use.
AI.result: CERL substantially reduces dependence of answers on hidden thoughts without sacrificing accuracy and outperforms correctness-only RL and long-answer SFT baselines across multiple domains.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a trainable, measurable criterion to erase hidden thoughts, yielding safer, more robust reasoning that generalizes across tasks.

title: An interpretable and trustworthy AI framework for large-scale longitudinal structure-pain association studies using data from the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI)
summary: Purpose: To develop an interpretable and trustworthy AI framework that combines deep learning based MRI Osteoarthritis Knee Score (MOAKS) prediction with interpretable statistical modeling to study structure-pain relationships at scale using data from the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI). Materials and Methods: We first developed a deep learning framework to predict MOAKS features directly from knee MRIs and incorporated conformal prediction to provide prediction uncertainty quantification. This uncertainty-aware strategy enables explicit filtering of model outputs, retaining only high-confidence MOAKS predictions at the knee level. Second, we applied a longitudinal latent class mixed model (LCMM) to examine associations between key structural abnormalities and four complementary knee pain measurements. Results: Among the three MRI-defined abnormalities (i.e., bone marrow lesions (BML), cartilage loss (CART), and meniscal extrusion (ME)), our framework substantially improved the Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) and some other metrics. For example, MCC increased from 0.69 to 0.91 for BML, from 0.45 to 0.80 for CART, and from 0.59 to 0.89 for ME. Using these high-confidence predictions, we expanded the sample size to 2,175 knees for the LCMM analysis. Two distinct pain trajectories were identified (rapid and stable pain progression). The estimated odds ratios (95% CI) for the rapid progression group were 1.62 (1.12-2.35) for BML, 1.83 (1.24-2.70) for CART loss, and 2.50 (1.75-3.57) for ME. Conclusion: These results highlight the importance of these structural abnormalities as risk factors for pain and functional progression in osteoarthritis.

AI.tldr: An uncertainty-aware, interpretable AI framework predicts MOAKS MRI features from knee MRIs using conformal prediction for confidence filtering, then uses a longitudinal latent class mixed model (LCMM) to link structural MRI abnormalities to pain trajectories, expanding analysis sample and revealing rapid vs. stable pain progression with structural risk factors.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, trustworthy analysis of structure-pain relationships in knee osteoarthritis by aligning deep learning predictions with interpretable statistical modeling and leveraging uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: 1) Train a deep learning model to predict MOAKS features from knee MRI scans and apply conformal prediction to quantify prediction uncertainty, filtering to high-confidence knee-level MOAKS outputs. 2) Use a longitudinal latent class mixed model (LCMM) to assess associations between key MRI abnormalities (BML, CART loss, ME) and four knee pain measurements, identifying pain trajectories and estimating effect sizes.
AI.result: Prediction metrics improved substantially for MRI-defined abnormalities after filtering high-confidence MOAKS outputs (e.g., MCC: BML 0.69→0.91, CART 0.45→0.80, ME 0.59→0.89). High-confidence outputs expanded LCMM sample to 2,175 knees, revealing two pain trajectories (rapid vs stable) and significant associations: rapid progression odds ratios 1.62 (BML), 1.83 (CART loss), 2.50 (ME) with 95% CIs (1.12–2.35; 1.24–2.70; 1.75–3.57).
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware, interpretable integration of DL MOAKS prediction with LCMM analysis identifies BML, CART loss, and ME as risk factors for faster pain progression and functional decline in OA, enabling scalable, trustworthy structure–pain studies.

title: Auditing Demonstration Curation Metrics: Action-Only Scorers Fail on the Structural Defects That Degrade Imitation Policies
summary: Imitation-learning policies inherit the quality of the demonstrations they are trained on, and a growing set of curation metrics promise to score and filter low-quality demonstrations automatically. These metrics are each validated on different data with different protocols, so it is unclear which of them actually identify the demonstrations that harm a policy. We build a controlled testbed in which demonstration defects are injected with known type, and audit seven curation metrics along two axes: how well each separates defective from clean demonstrations, and whether training a behavior-cloning policy on each metric's curated subset improves task success. We study two defect regimes. Subtle perturbations (correlated action noise, tremor, truncation) are detectable by multivariate outlier scoring and, once removed, recover the full downstream gap. Structural errors, where the demonstration executes a wrong action at a key moment, are invisible to every action-only metric we test, and two of them are inverted: they score defective demonstrations as higher quality and, used for curation, tend to leave the policy at or below the uncurated baseline rather than above it. Only metrics that examine the state trajectory detect structural errors, and even the best of them recovers just a third of the downstream gap. High detection accuracy does not guarantee downstream improvement. We release the testbed and all curation implementations.

AI.tldr: Seven imitation-learning data-curation metrics are evaluated in a defect-injected testbed; action-only metrics miss structural defects, while state-trajectory metrics catch some but not all; curated training sometimes fails to improve downstream task performance; dataset and implementations released.
AI.motivation: to determine which curation metrics actually identify demonstrations that harm policy performance and whether curated subsets improve imitation learning outcomes.
AI.method: Create a controlled testbed by injecting known defect types into demonstrations. Audit seven curation metrics along two axes: (i) separation of defective vs clean demonstrations, and (ii) whether training a behavior-cloning policy on the metric-curated subset improves task success. Explore two defect regimes: subtle perturbations (correlated action noise, tremor, truncation) detectable by multivariate outlier scoring, and structural errors where the demonstrated action at key moments is wrong. Compare metrics' ability to separate defects and their impact on downstream policy performance by training BC policies on curated subsets. Release testbed and implementations.
AI.result: Subtle perturbations are detectable by multivariate outlier scoring, and removing them recovers the downstream performance gap. Structural errors are invisible to action-only metrics; two such metrics are paradoxically inverted, scoring defective demonstrations as higher quality and, when used for curation, leaving the policy at or below the uncurated baseline. Only metrics that examine the full state trajectory detect structural errors, and even the best such metric recovers at most one-third of the downstream gap. High detection accuracy does not guarantee downstream improvement.
AI.conclusion: State-trajectory (and more generally state-aware) metrics are required to detect and mitigate harmful demonstrations, as action-only metrics can miss structural defects and may even mislead policy learning. However, even the best trajectory-based metrics only partially recover the performance gap, underscoring that curation alone cannot reliably restore policy performance. The authors release the testbed and curation implementations for further study.

title: Synthetic Contrastive Reasoning for Multi-Table Q&A
summary: Multi-table question answering requires models to retrieve relevant evidence, link schemas, and perform compositional reasoning across relational tables. Existing multi-table Q&A resources typically provide questions and final answers but lack reasoning supervision that explains how answers are derived. To address this gap, we construct a synthetic contrastive reasoning-trace dataset for MMQA by generating validated positive traces and plausible negative traces with heterogeneous LLMs. We then use the resulting preference pairs to fine-tune open-weight LLMs with Contrastive Preference Optimization (CPO). Across Qwen3-14B, Mistral-8B, and Llama-3.1-8B, CPO achieves absolute average improvements over Q&A supervised fine-tuning ranging from 9.7%-16.3%, with gains up to 21 percentage points on MMQA. Ablations show that heterogeneous positive and negative trace generators strengthen the contrastive signal, and automated as well as human evaluations indicate that the generated pairs are largely faithful, coherent, and meaningfully contrastive.

AI.tldr: Proposes Contrastive Preference Optimization (CPO) for multi-table QA by generating synthetic, heterogeneous reasoning traces (positive and plausible negative) to supervise model training. It fine-tunes open-weight LLMs to outperform standard QA fine-tuning on MMQA across several models, with strong ablations and evaluations supporting trace faithfulness and contrastiveness.
AI.motivation: Multi-table QA often lacks reasoning supervision that explains how answers are derived. Providing explicit tracing of reasoning can improve model transparency and performance in relational, cross-table questions.
AI.method: Create a synthetic contrastive reasoning-trace dataset for MMQA by generating validated positive traces and plausible negative traces with heterogeneous LLMs. Use the resulting preference pairs to train open-weight LLMs via Contrastive Preference Optimization (CPO). Evaluate across Qwen3-14B, Mistral-8B, and Llama-3.1-8B.
AI.result: CPO yields absolute average improvements over QA-supervised fine-tuning ranging from 9.7% to 16.3% across models, with up to 21 percentage points gains on MMQA. Ablations show heterogeneous generators strengthen the contrastive signal; automated and human evaluations indicate generated traces are faithful, coherent, and meaningfully contrastive.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive supervision via synthetic, heterogeneous reasoning traces effectively improves multi-table QA performance for open-weight LLMs, highlighting the value of trace-based signals in enhancing reasoning capabilities.

title: Biomazon: A Multimodal Dataset for 3D Forest Structure and Biomass Modeling in the Amazon Basin
summary: Accurate, spatially explicit characterization of tropical forest structure is essential for carbon accounting and ecosystem monitoring, yet most ML pipelines predict canopy-top height proxies (e.g., RH95/RH98) or AGBD as separate scalar targets, rather than learning the forest vertical structure as an ordered profile. The community lacks a ML-ready multimodal benchmark for predicting the entire GEDI RH profile jointly with AGBD, or for evaluating methods that enforce physically consistent ordering across RH percentiles. We address this with Biomazon, a 20 m multimodal benchmark dataset over the Amazon Basin that pairs GEDI RH and AGBD targets with multi-sensor predictors (Sentinel-1/2, ALOS-2 PALSAR-2, Copernicus DEM, Dynamic World LULC, and AlphaEarth embeddings) under standardized spatial splits and evaluation protocols. Using a shared encoder-decoder with task-specific heads as a baseline framework, we conduct a comprehensive ablation study of (i) backbone/model scale, (ii) modality contributions, and (iii) the use of auxiliary embeddings under standalone and fusion settings, and we report both single-target and joint-target results to quantify tradeoffs under a unified training protocol. Finally, we contextualize baseline performance through regionally aligned comparisons against existing gridded products, including GEDI L4D RH10-RH98 and AGBD, at matching temporal scale. Biomazon, together with the accompanying protocols and baseline results, establishes a reference benchmark for future work on structurally consistent RH-profile prediction and structure-biomass modeling in tropical forests.

AI.tldr: Biomazon is a 20 m multimodal benchmark for tropical forest vertical structure that jointly predicts GEDI RH percentile profiles and AGBD, using a shared encoder-decoder with task-specific heads and a diverse set of multi-sensor predictors; it enables ablations and standardized evaluation to study modality, scale, and ordering constraints, and benchmarks against existing gridded products.
AI.motivation: There is a critical need to move beyond single-proxy canopy-top height or AGBD targets to learn the full vertical structure of tropical forests as an ordered RH percentile profile. Current ML benchmarks lack joint RH-profile and AGBD targets and standardized, multimodal evaluation protocols.”
AI.method: A 20 m multimodal dataset (Biomazon) over the Amazon Basin pairing GEDI RH and AGBD targets with multi-sensor inputs (Sentinel-1/2, ALOS-2 PALSAR-2, Copernicus DEM, Dynamic World LULC, AlphaEarth embeddings). A shared encoder-decoder backbone with task-specific heads is used; extensive ablations analyze backbone scale, modality contributions, and auxiliary embeddings under standalone and fused settings, reporting both single-target and joint-target performance within standardized splits and evaluation protocols; results are contextualized against GEDI L4D RH10-RH98/AGBD products at matching times.
AI.result: Baseline framework and comprehensive ablation study are established, detailing how backbone size, sensor modalities, and auxiliary embeddings affect single-target vs joint-target performance. The paper provides a protocol and reference results for evaluating structurally consistent RH-profile prediction and structure-to-biomass modeling, and demonstrates how Biomazon compares to existing gridded products. Specific numeric gains are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Biomazon is positioned as a reference benchmark for future work on predicting ordered RH profiles and structure-biomass relationships in tropical forests, enabling structurally consistent predictions and fair comparisons through standardized evaluation and multimodal integration.

title: Three-Dimensional Retinal Microvasculature Restoration in OCT Angiography
summary: Optical coherence tomographic angiography (OCTA) is a powerful technique for imaging retinal microvasculature. However, acquiring reliable quantification of retinal blood flow and areas of retinal nonperfusion is challenging because of imaging artifacts. Existing methods primarily focus on noise suppression, projection artifact removal, or signal enhancement to improve the image quality of OCTA in cross-sectional or two-dimensional (2D) en face projections, while neglecting the intrinsic three-dimensional vascular architecture. In this study, we propose a deep learning-based algorithm for restoring capillary anatomical vasculature from a single OCTA volume. The network consists of an EfficientNet-B5 encoder and a decoder incorporating concurrent spatial and channel squeeze-and-excitation modules, connected via skip connections to preserve spatial resolution. Three adjacent B-frames are used as input to predict the restored middle B-frame. We evaluated the performance of the model using the peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) and structural similarity index measure (SSIM) against ground truth generated from averaging multiple scans. The results show that the proposed model significantly (both p < 0.001) improved image quality compared with the original single OCTA volume, with a PSNR of 26.16 +/- 1.26 vs. 22.23 +/- 0.78 and an SSIM of 0.91 +/- 0.02 vs. 0.72 +/- 0.03. The proposed model also significantly (p < 0.001) improved microvascular fidelity, measured by the Dice coefficient overlap between the model output and ground truth, in both 2D and 3D by at least 3.8% and 51.2%, respectively, across several different vascular slabs.

AI.tldr: A deep learning method restoring capillary anatomy from a single OCTA volume using a tri-frame input and EfficientNet-B5 encoder with squeeze-and-excitation decoder; yields notable improvements in PSNR, SSIM, and Dice fidelity versus ground-truth and single-volume baselines.
AI.motivation: OCTA artifacts hinder reliable three-dimensional quantification of retinal microvasculature; existing approaches emphasize 2D image quality and miss 3D vascular architecture; a 3D restoration from a single volume could improve metrics for perfusion and nonperfusion.
AI.method: A supervised deep learning model with an EfficientNet-B5 encoder and a decoder incorporating concurrent spatial and channel squeeze-and-excitation modules, connected via skip connections. Input consists of three adjacent B-frames to predict the middle B-frame (temporal context). Ground-truth is generated by averaging multiple scans. Evaluation uses PSNR and SSIM against ground truth and Dice coefficient for vascular fidelity in 2D and 3D across vascular slabs.
AI.result: Quantitatively significant improvements over single-volume input: PSNR 26.16±1.26 vs 22.23±0.78; SSIM 0.91±0.02 vs 0.72±0.03 (both p<0.001). Microvascular fidelity (Dice) improved by at least 3.8% in 2D and 51.2% in 3D across several slabs.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates feasibility of restoring anatomically faithful 3D capillary networks from a single OCTA volume, yielding higher image quality and vascular fidelity, with potential to enhance quantitative perfusion assessments; further validation and generalization studies are warranted.

title: Deep Learning-assisted AMD Staging based on OCT and OCT Angiography
summary: To develop and evaluate deep learning models for automated grading of age-related macular degeneration (AMD) severity using optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCTA) data. Two hundred seventy-one participants aged >= 50 years with varying AMD severities. Central macular 6 x 6 mm OCT/OCTA volumes were acquired using a swept-source OCTA system (SOLIX; Visionix/Optovue Inc., CA). AMD severity was graded into four stages (No AMD, Early AMD, Intermediate AMD, and Advanced AMD) according to the AREDS simplified severity scale. Three deep learning models were developed using different input modalities: (1) biomarker maps derived from segmented pathological features, including retinal fluid, drusen, geographic atrophy (GA), and macular neovascularization (MNV); (2) two-dimensional (2D) en face OCT and OCTA projections; and (3) three-dimensional (3D) OCT/OCTA volumes. EfficientNet-based architectures were trained using normalized inputs, data augmentation, and five-fold cross-validation. A total of 2,030 OCT/OCTA volumes from 351 eyes of 271 participants were analyzed. All models demonstrated strong AMD staging performance with substantial agreement with the reference standard (QWK >= 0.83). The biomarker-based model achieved the highest overall performance (QWK = 0.85 +/- 0.03, mean +/- standard deviation) and the best detection of early AMD (F1-score = 0.59 +/- 0.14). The 3D model achieved performance comparable to the 2D OCT/OCTA model (QWK = 0.83 +/- 0.04 vs. 0.83 +/- 0.09), while the 2D OCT/OCTA model showed the highest precision (0.79 +/- 0.06) and most accurately identified eyes without AMD. Deep learning models using OCT/OCTA data can accurately and automatically grade AMD severity. Among the evaluated approaches, the biomarker-based model provided the most balanced performance and showed particular value for early AMD detection.

AI.tldr: Three EfficientNet-based DL models (biomarker maps, 2D en face projections, 3D OCT/OCTA volumes) were evaluated for AMD severity grading using OCT/OCTA. All achieved QWK ≥ 0.83; biomarker-based model had the best overall performance and early AMD detection, with 2D and 3D approaches showing comparable performance and higher precision for 2D.
AI.motivation: To automate and compare deep learning approaches for staging age-related macular degeneration (AMD) severity using multimodal OCT/OCTA imaging, and to identify which input modality yields the most balanced and early-detection performance.
AI.method: Participants: 271 individuals aged ≥50 with varying AMD. Imaging: central 6x6 mm OCT/OCTA volumes via swept-source OCTA (SOLIX). Ground truth: AMD staged into four categories per AREDS simplified scale. Models (three input modalities): (1) biomarker maps from segmented features (retinal fluid, drusen, geographic atrophy, MNV); (2) 2D en face OCT/OCTA projections; (3) 3D OCT/OCTA volumes. Architecture: EfficientNet-based networks, with normalized inputs, data augmentation, and five-fold cross-validation. Dataset size: 2,030 volumes from 351 eyes of 271 participants. Evaluation: Quadratic kappa (QWK) and other metrics including F1 for early AMD; comparisons across modalities.
AI.result: All models achieved strong AMD staging with QWK ≥ 0.83. Biomarker-based model yielded the highest QWK of 0.85 ± 0.03 and the best early AMD detection (F1 = 0.59 ± 0.14). The 3D model performed comparably to the 2D model (QWK 0.83 ± 0.04 vs 0.83 ± 0.09); the 2D model achieved the highest precision (P = 0.79 ± 0.06) and was most accurate at identifying eyes without AMD.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning with OCT/OCTA data can accurately automate AMD severity grading. Among modalities, biomarker-based input provided the most balanced performance and showed particular value for early AMD detection; 2D and 3D approaches yielded comparable staging performance, with higher precision in 2D projections.

title: Accelerating and Scaling MPC-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Humanoid Locomotion and Manipulation
summary: In humanoid motion control, model predictive control (MPC) offers physically grounded prediction and constraint handling, while reinforcement learning (RL) enables robust whole-body skills through large-scale simulation. However, using MPC inside RL often requires time-consuming problem construction or excessive training overhead, making such frameworks difficult to justify in practice. This work studies efficient training-time MPC guidance for humanoid locomotion and manipulation, termed MPC-RL. We introduce a centroidal-dynamics MPC reward formulation that leverages guidance from MPC trajectories in training time. To make this practical in massively parallel RL, we develop $π^n$MPC, a parallel-in-horizon and construction-free batched GPU MPC solver that operates directly on time-varying dynamics to avoid high memory usage and pre-compilation. Through a variety of comparative studies and hardware validations, we have found that MPC-RL achieves superior performance in locomotion and manipulation skills. The code base is available at https://github.com/junhengl/mpc-rl.

AI.tldr: Efficient training-time MPC guidance for humanoid RL using a parallel GPU MPC solver and centroidal-dynamics reward, enabling scalable MPC-assisted RL in locomotion and manipulation.
AI.motivation: Reduce the overhead of integrating MPC into RL for humanoid control while preserving performance, enabling scalable training on large RL workloads.
AI.method: Introduce a centroidal-dynamics MPC reward; propose π^nMPC, a parallel-in-horizon, construction-free batched GPU MPC solver operating on time-varying dynamics; MPC guidance is incorporated during training.
AI.result: Empirical comparisons and hardware validations show MPC-RL achieves superior locomotion and manipulation skills; code released at the authors' repository.
AI.conclusion: MPC-guided RL via π^nMPC provides practical, scalable training-time guidance for humanoid control with strong performance gains; approach is accessible for large-scale RL.

title: Disentangled Fine-Grained Prototype Learning for Incomplete Image-Tabular Classification
summary: The missing-modality problem poses a significant challenge in image-tabular multimodal learning across a wide range of multimedia applications, including product understanding, recommendation systems, and medical diagnosis. This challenge is particularly pronounced when the two modalities are highly heterogeneous, as images and tabular attributes differ substantially in their semantic granularity and data distributions. Existing methods learn modality-invariant representations through disentanglement and alignment over global token-averaged features, capturing only coarse cross-modal consistency and overlooking fine-grained semantic and distributional misalignment, which hampers the exploitation of complementary cues under missing modalities. To address this, we propose DFPL, a novel framework for fine-grained prototype learning. Specifically, Shared-Specific Prototype Modeling (SSPM) extracts compact and diverse shared and modality-specific prototypes, and further performs prototype-level disentanglement to suppress redundant intra-modality correlations. Additionally, we propose a Prototype-guided Fine-grained Alignment (PFA) module that jointly enforces prototype-level distribution matching and prototype-to-class semantic alignment within a unified prototype space, thereby preserving both fine-grained distributional and semantic consistency across modalities. We further introduce a Class-aware Multi-scale Aggregation (CMA) module to adaptively aggregate shared semantics and modality-specific characteristics from global and prototype levels for robust predictions. Extensive experiments on three diverse image-tabular benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of our method compared to the previous approaches under various missing-modality settings. Code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Presents DFPL for fine-grained prototype learning to tackle missing-modality in image-tabular multimodal tasks, using SSPM to learn shared/modality-specific prototypes, PFA for prototype-level distribution and semantic alignment in a common space, and CMA for multi-scale aggregation, achieving superior results on three image-tabular benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing cross-modal methods align representations at a coarse, global level and struggle with fine-grained mismatches between heterogeneous modalities (images vs. tabular data), especially when modalities are missing. There is a need for compact, diverse prototypes and directed alignment to exploit complementary cues.
AI.method: Introduce Shared-Specific Prototype Modeling (SSPM) to extract shared and modality-specific prototypes with prototype-level disentanglement to suppress intra-modality redundancy. Propose Prototype-guided Fine-grained Alignment (PFA) to enforce prototype-level distribution matching and prototype-to-class semantic alignment within a unified prototype space. Add Class-aware Multi-scale Aggregation (CMA) to adaptively fuse shared and modality-specific semantics from global and prototype levels for robust predictions under missing modalities.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three diverse image-tabular benchmarks shows superior performance of DFPL over prior methods under various missing-modality settings. Code will be released publicly.
AI.conclusion: DFPL integrates fine-grained prototypes with a unified alignment space and multi-scale aggregation to preserve both distributional and semantic consistency across modalities, offering robust performance in missing-modality image-tabular learning.

title: PiL-World: A Chunk-Wise World Model for VLA Policy-in-the-Loop Evaluation
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies operate in a closed loop in real-world robot tasks: a robot observes the scene, executes an action chunk, and conditions its next decision on the resulting observation. However, most existing world models for robot action evaluation are limited to open-loop prediction along pre-collected action trajectories. This prevents them from supporting closed-loop VLA evaluation, where each action chunk must be conditioned on the observation generated by the previous execution. To address this gap, we propose PiL-World, a chunk-wise world model designed for policy-in-the-loop VLA evaluation. Given the current observation and the action trajectory rolled out by a VLA policy, PiL-World generates multi-view future observations that are consistent with the VLA rollout and match the image inputs required by the policy. By alternating between VLA inference and world-model prediction, PiL-World enables closed-loop evaluation without real robot execution at every step. To improve rollout fidelity, PiL-World conditions video generation on action-derived visual control from head-view robot motion and latent histories that encode task execution context, while jointly predicting complementary multi-view observations. Beyond successful teleoperated demonstrations, it also learns from failed execution trajectories, helping the imagined rollouts better match the distribution of real policy executions. We evaluate PiL-World on three real dual-arm manipulation tasks. PiL-World generates imagined rollouts that are highly consistent with real robot executions. More importantly, compared with the baseline, it reduces the error between VLA success rates measured in real-world rollouts and those estimated through closed-loop world-model evaluation from 63.2% to 12.0%.

AI.tldr: PiL-World is a chunk-wise world model enabling closed-loop policy-in-the-loop vision-language-action (VLA) evaluation by generating multi-view future observations conditioned on the VLA rollout, reducing real robot execution requirements and improving fidelity; it incorporates action-derived visual control and latent task histories, learns from failed trajectories, and substantially tightens real-vs-imagined evaluation accuracy (63.2% error to 12.0%).
AI.motivation: Current world models for robot action evaluation are largely open-loop and limited to pre-collected trajectories, hindering closed-loop VLA evaluation where each action chunk must be conditioned on the agent's own prior observation; there is a need for a world model that can support closed-loop evaluation without running a real robot at every step and improve fidelity.
AI.method: PiL-World is a chunk-wise world model that, given the current observation and the VLA policy's action trajectory, generates multi-view future observations consistent with the rollout and the policy inputs. It alternates between VLA inference and world-model prediction, conditions video generation on action-derived visual control from head-view robot motion and latent histories that encode task context, and jointly predicts complementary multi-view observations. It also learns from failed execution trajectories to improve rollout distribution alignment.
AI.result: Imagined rollouts are highly consistent with real robot executions. Compared with a baseline, the method reduces the error between real-world VLA success rates and closed-loop evaluation estimates from 63.2% to 12.0% across three real dual-arm manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: PiL-World enables reliable closed-loop VLA evaluation without real robot execution at every step, improves rollout fidelity through action-informed video generation and task-context histories, and benefits from learning from failed trajectories to better match real policy executions.

title: Assessing the Carbon Emissions and Energy Consumption of U.S. Hyperscale Data Centers
summary: The rapid proliferation of hyperscale data centers (HDCs) in the US, mainly driven by the adoption of artificial intelligence, has raised concerns about this industry's environmental footprint. We compiled facility-level information on 403 US hyperscale data centers operating between May 2024 and April 2025 and estimated their electricity consumption, electricity sources, and attributable CO2 emissions. Across different facility-load scenarios, these HDCs consumed approximately 68-99 TWh of electricity and were associated with about 37-54 million metric tons of CO2. Under the central scenario, HDC electricity demand corresponded to approximately 1.8% of total US electricity consumption, with roughly 54% of attributed generation supplied by fossil-fuel sources. The HDC electricity-weighted average carbon intensity was approximately 545 gCO2/kWh, about 48% above the contemporaneous US national grid-average carbon intensity of 370 gCO2/kWh. Our approach provides an attributional tool for assessing the environmental footprint of hyperscale data centers using the most recent EPA eGRID plant-level data.

AI.tldr: A study estimates electricity use and CO2 emissions for 403 US hyperscale data centers (May 2024–Apr 2025). Under central assumptions, they consume ~68–99 TWh and emit ~37–54 Mt CO2, ~1.8% of US electricity demand with ~54% from fossil sources. The electricity-weighted carbon intensity is ~545 gCO2/kWh, ~48% above the US grid average (370 gCO2/kWh). Uses EPA eGRID plant-level data to attribute footprint.
AI.motivation: Address concerns about the environmental footprint of hyperscale data centers driven by AI and rapid data-center expansion; provide a transparent, facility-level attribution of electricity use and CO2 emissions to inform policy, procurement, and decarbonization strategies.
AI.method: Compile facility-level information for 403 hyperscale data centers operating between May 2024 and April 2025. Estimate electricity consumption, electricity sources, and attributable CO2 emissions under multiple facility-load scenarios. Attribute emissions using EPA eGRID plant-level data to derive generation mix and carbon intensity.
AI.result: Quantified footprint: 68–99 TWh electricity use; 37–54 Mt CO2 emissions. Central scenario: ~1.8% of US electricity consumption; ~54% of generated electricity from fossil sources. Carbon intensity ~545 gCO2/kWh, ~48% higher than US grid average (370 gCO2/kWh). Method yields an attributional footprint using eGRID data.
AI.conclusion: Provides an attributional tool to assess the environmental footprint of hyperscale data centers using up-to-date EPA eGRID plant-level data; enables benchmarking and informs decarbonization and energy-sourcing strategies for the HDC sector.

title: Zero knowledge verification for frontier AI training is possible
summary: Frontier AI governance frameworks increasingly use cumulative training compute as the primary criterion for designating high-impact models, but enforcement rests on self-reporting because no technical verification primitive for training exists. Any future international agreement on frontier AI faces the same problem at higher stakes: coordinated regulation of technologies with significant externalities has historically rested on technical verification, without which agreements are declaratory. Recent governance analyses judge zero-knowledge proofs a promising candidate but currently impractical at frontier scale [26, 4]. We argue the impracticality is paradigm-bound rather than fundamental, and propose a verification architecture for frontier dense pre-training combining a pre-committed training specification, inter-node network observations, and on-the-fly Merkle commitments of intermediate computation, verified through a zero-knowledge Virtual Machine (zkVM) with native BF16/FP32 precompiles. The proof checks the actual floating-point computation the GPU performed rather than a fixed-point approximation, and preserves model-architecture confidentiality through a private training specification. The protocol produces three proof types: a genesis proof at initialisation, in-training step proofs across the run, and ex-ante attestations enforcing policy-relevant claims as running invariants, turning the training record into a governance-enforceable artefact. We estimate a deployable proof of concept within approximately 36 months at single-digit-percent training-side overhead, against a six-to-ten-year cycle for verification-grade custom silicon. Thirteen open research and engineering problems are catalogued as a research agenda for external contribution

AI.tldr: A proposed verification architecture for frontier model training using pre-committed specs, inter-node observations, Merkle commitments, and zkVM; aims to move beyond self-reporting to governance-enforceable proofs with genesis/step/ex-ante attestations; estimated deployable PoC in 36 months with low overhead; outlines 13 research problems.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of a technical verification primitive for large-scale frontier AI training, which makes enforcement of governance standards difficult if not impossible. Current reliance on self-reporting is inadequate at frontier scale; the work explores a path to verifiable evidence that could underpin international governance agreements.
AI.method: Proposes an architecture combining a pre-committed training specification, inter-node network observations, and on-the-fly Merkle commitments of intermediate computation, verified via a zero-knowledge Virtual Machine (zkVM) with native BF16/FP32 precompiles. The proof types include a genesis proof at initialisation, in-training step proofs, and ex-ante attestations enforcing policy invariants. The training record becomes a governance artefact while preserving model-architecture confidentiality through a private training specification.
AI.result: Claims feasibility for a deployable PoC within ~36 months with single-digit percent training-side overhead, versus a 6–10 year cycle for verification-grade custom silicon. Presents a structured research agenda with 13 open problems to be tackled externally; aims to shift governance from declaratory to verifiable through a provable training record.
AI.conclusion: If realizable, the framework could dramatically improve verifiability of frontier AI training and enable enforceable governance artefacts. While ambitious and with notable engineering and cryptographic challenges, it offers concrete steps and a prioritized research agenda to address them.

title: Brick-Composer: Using MLLMs for Assembly with Diverse Bricks
summary: We dream of AI agents that can read arbitrary designs and construct real-world objects from reusable building blocks. As a first step toward this vision, we study whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) possess the visual grounding and spatial reasoning capabilities required for brick assembly. We formulate brick assembly as a sequential decision-making problem, where each step involves two subtasks: brick selection, identifying the target brick from candidate components, and brick pose estimation, predicting where and how the selected brick should be placed. To support this study, we introduce BC-Bench (Brick Construction Benchmark), the first benchmark for evaluating MLLMs on assembly with diverse bricks. Experiments show that current state-of-the-art MLLMs remain far from reliable builders, struggling with fine-grained brick selection and failing at precise pose estimation. To bridge this gap, we propose Brick-Composer, a learning framework that equips MLLMs with assembly skills through three complementary signals: Human Design Sparks, which provide affordance-rich construction demonstrations; World Feedback, which grounds predicted actions in visual and physical consequences; and Synthetic Experience, which scales learning beyond existing object designs. Brick-Composer improves brick selection accuracy by over three times, substantially reduces pose estimation errors, and raises strict step-level assembly success from less than 1% to around 15%. After training, a Qwen-3-8B can correctly compose up to 42% of the steps for a complete object, suggesting that MLLMs can acquire assembly capabilities through targeted, physically grounded learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes BC-Bench to evaluate multimodal LLMs on brick assembly and introduces Brick-Composer, a learning framework leveraging human demonstrations, world feedback, and synthetic experiences. Results show large gains in brick selection and pose estimation, with up to 42% complete-object composition on Qwen-3-8B.
AI.motivation: To enable AI agents to read designs and assemble real-world objects from reusable bricks by assessing and improving MLLMs' visual grounding and spatial reasoning in sequential assembly tasks.
AI.method: Formulate brick assembly as sequential decision making with two subtasks (brick selection and pose estimation). Create BC-Bench for evaluation. Develop Brick-Composer that uses three signals—Human Design Sparks (demonstrations), World Feedback (visual/physical grounding), and Synthetic Experience (scaling data)—to train MLLMs. Evaluate on evolving architectures (e.g., Qwen-3-8B).
AI.result: Brick-Composer triples brick selection accuracy, substantially reduces pose estimation error, and raises strict step-level assembly success from <1% to ~15%. A Qwen-3-8B model can correctly compose up to 42% of steps for a complete object.
AI.conclusion: Physically grounded, targeted learning can endow MLLMs with meaningful assembly capabilities, though reliable full-object assembly remains challenging; BC-Bench provides a suite for evaluating progress on multimodal reasoning and manipulation.

title: L-SDPPO: Policy Optimization of Spiking Diffusion Policy for Intra-vehicular Robotic Manipulation
summary: Intra-vehicular robots in spacecraft help reduce astronaut workload and improve mission efficiency. Recent research focuses on using deep learning methods to achieve the acute control required for operations in these complex environments. However, objects exhibit unpredictable, unconstrained drift without gravitational damping. These factors demand robustness against complex multimodal action distributions. Diffusion policies (DP) can model these complex actions, but their iterative sampling process consumes too much energy for the limited power budgets of spacecraft. We therefore propose a low-energy intra-vehicular robotic manipulation framework, L-SDPPO, in which the Spiking Diffusion Policy (SDP) is optimized with a reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm. Furthermore, to address the insufficient perception of dynamic spatiotemporal features in microgravity, we propose the statedependent latency injection (SDLI) mechanism, which mimics biological neural delays to dynamically regulate the timing of input information. Evaluation on five representative intra-vehicular daily tasks (e.g., hatch opening and precision container capping) shows that our method consistently achieves higher success rates and lower energy consumption, compared to the state-of-the-art robotic manipulation methods. These results demonstrate our method is a viable intra-vehicular robotic manipulation method.

AI.tldr: Low-energy intra-vehicular robotic manipulation using a spiking diffusion policy optimized via RL (L-SDPPO) and a biologically-inspired latency injection (SDLI) to handle microgravity dynamics; shows improved success and reduced energy in representative tasks.
AI.motivation: In microgravity, objects drift unpredictably; robust control under complex multimodal action distributions is needed; diffusion policies model this but are energy-intensive.
AI.method: Introduce L-SDPPO: SDP-based diffusion policy optimized with RL; introduce SDLI to emulate neural delays for dynamic spatiotemporal perception; evaluate on five intra-vehicular tasks; compare to state-of-the-art.
AI.result: Higher success rates and lower energy consumption than baselines across tasks; demonstrates viability of low-energy intra-vehicular manipulation.
AI.conclusion: SDP-based diffusion policy with RL optimization plus SDLI is an effective, energy-efficient approach for autonomous intra-vehicular manipulation in microgravity; suitable for space missions.

title: SciVisAgentSkills: Design and Evaluation of Agent Skills for Scientific Data Analysis and Visualization
summary: Recent advances in agentic visualization have enabled the translation of natural language into executable scientific visualization (SciVis) workflows. While general-purpose coding agents show strong capabilities, they often lack the tool-specific expertise required for SciVis tasks. In this work, we present SciVisAgentSkills, a collection of reusable agent skills that augment coding agents for scientific data analysis and visualization by encoding environment assumptions, tool usage patterns, and domain heuristics across scientific tools such as ParaView, napari, VMD, and TTK. We evaluate these skills on Codex and Claude Code using SciVisAgentBench, a benchmark of 108 expert-designed multi-step tasks. Results show that agent skills improve mean task scores across the evaluated suites, with token-efficiency benefits that depend on the agent harness and tool setting. These findings highlight the importance of structured procedural knowledge for enabling reliable, long-horizon SciVis workflows, while also showing that skills should be studied alongside the execution harness that loads and applies them. The skills are available at https://github.com/KuangshiAi/SciVisAgentSkills.

AI.tldr: SciVisAgentSkills provide reusable agent capabilities tailored to SciVis tools, improving long-horizon workflows by encoding tool usage patterns and domain heuristics, validated on Codex/Claude with SciVisAgentBench.
AI.motivation: General-purpose coding agents lack tool-specific expertise required for SciVis tasks; there is a need for structured procedural knowledge that aligns with scientific visualization tools and workflows to improve reliability and efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce SciVisAgentSkills that encode environment assumptions, tool usage patterns, and domain heuristics for tools like ParaView, napari, VMD, and TTK. Evaluate on Codex and Claude Code using SciVisAgentBench (108 multi-step tasks). Measure mean task performance and token efficiency across tool settings.
AI.result: Agent skills yield higher mean task scores across evaluated suites; token efficiency gains depend on the agent harness and tool configuration, indicating interactions between skill set and execution environment.
AI.conclusion: Structured procedural knowledge embedded as agent skills is crucial for reliable, scalable SciVis workflows; future work should study skills alongside the execution harness that applies them; code is released at the linked GitHub repository.

title: HDST-GNN: Heterogeneous Dynamic Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Networks for Multi-Object Tracking in UAV Aerial Imagery
summary: Multi-object tracking (MOT) from UAV imagery presents unique challenges: altitude varies across sequences, objects are small and densely packed, and frequent occlusion causes identity switches. Existing graph-based trackers assume fixed spatial context and treat all objects uniformly, ignoring the heterogeneous lifecycle states of detections, active tracklets, and lost targets. We propose HDST-GNN, a Heterogeneous Dynamic Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Network with three novel contributions. First, Altitude-Adaptive Edge Construction estimates a camera-altitude proxy from mean object area and adjusts the graph connectivity radius accordingly. Second, Heterogeneous Node Representation models detections (Type-D), confirmed tracklets (Type-T), and lost tracklets (Type-L) as distinct node types with dedicated projections and typed edge relations. Third, Occlusion-Gated Temporal Aggregation gates each node's attention contribution by its occlusion confidence, preventing occluded nodes from corrupting neighbour embeddings. HDST-GNN is trained end-to-end with a differentiable Sinkhorn head using joint cross-entropy and triplet loss. On VisDrone2019-MOT with oracle detections, HDST-GNN achieves 94.51% MOTA and 97.24% IDF1, outperforming SORT by +5.0 MOTA points and reducing identity switches by 81%. With real YOLOv8n detections, HDST-GNN reduces identity switches by 49% vs. SORT. Ablation studies confirm the independent contribution of each component.

AI.tldr: HDST-GNN introduces a heterogeneous dynamic spatiotemporal graph for UAV MOT, with altitude-adaptive edges, D/T/L node types, occlusion-gated aggregation, and a Sinkhorn-based training head; it achieves state-of-the-art results on VisDrone2019-MOT, outperforming SORT and reducing identity switches, with ablations validating each component.
AI.motivation: MOT from UAV imagery faces varying altitude, small dense objects, and occlusions, while existing graph trackers treat detections uniformly and assume fixed spatial context. There is a need to model heterogeneous detection/tracklet states and to adapt connectivity and attention to occlusion.
AI.method: Three contributions: (1) Altitude-Adaptive Edge Construction uses a camera-altitude proxy from mean object area to set the graph radius; (2) Heterogeneous Node Representation defines D, T, L node types with dedicated projections and typed edges; (3) Occlusion-Gated Temporal Aggregation gates a node's attention by its occlusion confidence; training uses differentiable Sinkhorn head with joint cross-entropy and triplet loss; end-to-end.
AI.result: On VisDrone2019-MOT with oracle detections, 94.51% MOTA and 97.24% IDF1; +5.0 MOTA vs SORT; 81% fewer identity switches. With YOLOv8n detections, identity switches reduced by 49% vs SORT. Ablations show each component contributes independently.
AI.conclusion: HDST-GNN effectively handles altitude variations, occlusion, and heterogeneous lifecycle states in UAV MOT, delivering substantial gains over strong baselines and offering a framework for end-to-end, differentiable graph-based MOT; potential for broader applicability with further efficiency optimizations.

title: Pattern Selectivity is Not Task-Causal Structure: A Cross-Architecture Mechanistic Study of Composed-Task Circuits in 1B-Class Language Models
summary:
We test whether a single screen-and-ablate recipe -- identify attention-head circuits by task-pattern selectivity, then verify by causal ablation against a matched-random null -- produces consistent mechanistic claims across model families. The recipe ports across pipelines; the specific circuit it identifies does not. Across four composed tasks (indirect-object identification, greater-than, successor sequences, variable binding) and three 1B-class language models from distinct training pipelines (Pythia 1B / Pile / dense; OLMo 1B / DCLM / dense; OLMoE 1B-7B / DCLM / mixture-of-experts), we run a unified protocol with the matched-random null sampled across ten seeds per cell. The resulting 12 (task, model) cells contain no two that share the same primary causal screen at comparable effect size: the same task, with the same behavioral capability, is implemented through different attention-pattern types across models.
  We introduce a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy -- primary cause, secondary cause, correlate, interferer, null -- with quantitative thresholds, and show that all five outcomes appear in the panel. We propose a falsifiable hypothesis: the MoE model in our panel builds composed-task circuits on top of a foundational previous-token positional substrate (the prev-token-circuit ablation is the strongest causal screen on 3 of 4 tasks for OLMoE 1B-7B), with the IOI exception consistent with IOI being a final-position name-copying task whose structure directly probes a different pattern. The hypothesis comes with explicit predictions for other MoE language models.
  We frame the methodology honestly: the spectral participation-ratio signal from the companion methodology paper is a general indicator of specialized computation; what makes a finding task-specific is the task-pattern screen plus a per-model causal verification.

AI.tldr: A single screen-and-ablate protocol is tested across four tasks and three 1B-class models to assess cross-model consistency of identified attention-based circuits. They find no shared primary causal screen for the same task across models, propose a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy, and offer a falsifiable MoE-specific hypothesis. The approach emphasizes per-model causal verification and suggests task-specificity of circuits rather than universal mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Assess whether a unified causal-screening recipe yields reproducible mechanistic claims across diverse model families, and evaluate whether same behavioral capabilities arise from different circuit types or shared substrates.
AI.method: Apply a single screen-and-ablate protocol (identify attention-head circuits by task-pattern selectivity; verify with matched-random causal ablation) across four tasks and three model families (Pythia, OLMo, OLMoE) with 1B-class sizes; use ten seeds per cell; introduce a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy (primary/secondary/correlate/interferer/null); supplement with a spectral participation-ratio signal from companion methodology; propose falsifiable MoE-specific hypotheses.
AI.result: Across 12 task-model cells, no two share the same primary causal screen at comparable effect size; the same task uses different attention-pattern types across models.
