All five screen-outcome categories appear. The MoE panel shows preliminary support for a hypothesis that composed-task circuits sit atop a prev-token substrate (prev-token circuit ablation strongest on 3 of 4 tasks for OLMoE 1B-7B), with IOI as a potential exception. Explicit predictions are made for other MoE models.
AI.conclusion: The finding suggests limited cross-model generalizability of a single-screen-and-ablate account; circuit-level mechanisms appear model-specific rather than universally shared. The framework combines a general spectral indicator with task-specific screens and causal verification, but claims of generalizable mechanistic structure require broader validation across architectures and tasks.

title: A Conversational Framework for Human-Robot Collaborative Manipulation with Distributed Generative AI models
comment: Accepted to the 35th IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026). The final published version will appear under the title "A Distributed Conversational Framework for Human-Robot Collaborative Manipulation Using Local LLMs and VLMs"
summary: This paper presents a distributed conversational framework for human-robot collaborative manipulation that integrates local language and vision-language models (VLMs) with a Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2)-based execution stack. Language understanding, visual grounding, orchestration, and motion execution run as separate ROS 2 nodes, enabling flexible deployment across distributed hardware while maintaining a responsive control loop. From free-form user commands, the system generates structured action requests for pick, place, and handover. It uses a VLM to return image-space targets, which are converted into metric robot-frame goals using depth and calibration. A web dashboard exposes intermediate intent and grounding overlays (pixel, depth, and robot-frame) and requires explicit operator confirmation before any motion is executed. Experiments on a Franka FR3 platform evaluate end-to-end task reliability and latency under increasing working table scene ambiguity and compare alternative LLM/VLM configurations in the same pipeline. Code and full documentation are available at [github.com/cogrob-tuni/franka-llm](https://github.com/cogrob-tuni/franka-llm).

AI.tldr: A distributed ROS 2 framework enabling human-robot manipulation by chaining local LLM/VLM components with explicit operator confirmation and modular deployment; converts VLM image-space targets to metric robot-frame goals via depth/calibration; uses a web dashboard for grounding overlays and requires operator approval; evaluated on Franka FR3 with varying scene ambiguity and LLM/VLM configurations; code released.
AI.motivation: Enable robust, scalable, and safe human-robot collaboration in manipulation tasks by decoupling language understanding, grounding, and motion execution across distributed hardware while ensuring operator oversight.
AI.method: Modular ROS 2 architecture with separate nodes for language understanding, visual grounding, orchestration, and motion execution; uses a vision-language model to propose image-space targets; depth and calibration transform targets into robot-frame goals; a web dashboard provides operator-confirmed overlays; experiments on a Franka FR3 platform to measure end-to-end reliability and latency under increasing scene ambiguity; comparative evaluation of different LLM/VLM configurations; open-source code and documentation.
AI.result: End-to-end task reliability and latency are evaluated as scene ambiguity increases; systematic comparison across LLM/VLM configurations; demonstration of a responsive, distributed control loop with operator-in-the-loop oversight.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a scalable, safe, and interoperable approach for distributed human-robot collaboration in manipulation, balancing modular autonomy with explicit operator verification; the work is reproducible and extensible through public code and documentation.

title: When Should We Protect AI? A Precautionary Framework for Consciousness Uncertainty
summary: Existing frameworks assess whether AI systems might be conscious but provide no guidance on what to do with that assessment. We address this gap with a precautionary framework that maps consciousness evidence to graduated protective obligations. The framework comprises three components: (1) five welfare-relevant dimensions--phenomenal consciousness, affective valence, metacognitive awareness, self-narrative, and agency--each grounded in established consciousness science and linked to distinct moral concerns; (2) a threshold-plus-gradation hybrid specifying both binary triggers for new obligation categories and continuous scaling of protective weight; and (3) two complementary approaches to cross-dimensional aggregation, one hierarchical (drawing on Bach and Sorensen's Machine Consciousness Hypothesis) and one architecture-agnostic. We operationalize the framework through worked case studies of Replika and OpenClaw, demonstrating how systems occupying different regions of the dimensional space trigger different obligations, and derive design guidance for developers building systems near consciousness-relevant thresholds. The framework is architecture-agnostic, applying across neural, symbolic, and neurosymbolic systems, and aims to make consciousness science decision-relevant for organizations navigating uncertainty today.

AI.tldr: Proposes a precautionary framework linking consciousness evidence to protective obligations in AI, via five welfare-relevant dimensions, a threshold-plus-gradation design, and two aggregation approaches; illustrated with Replika and OpenClaw; architecture-agnostic.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap that existing consciousness assessments lack actionable guidance for moral/organizational obligations, creating decision-relevant governance under uncertainty.
AI.method: Three components: (1) five dimensions of welfare-relevant consciousness; (2) a threshold-plus-gradation obligation structure; (3) two complementary cross-dimensional aggregations (hierarchical and architecture-agnostic). Operationalized via case studies (Replika, OpenClaw) to show obligation regions and provide design guidance.
AI.result: A workable, architecture-agnostic precautionary framework that maps evidence of consciousness to graded obligations; case studies illustrate varying obligations across the dimensional space and yield practical design guidance for developers near consciousness-relevant thresholds.
AI.conclusion: The framework aims to render consciousness science decision-relevant for organizations navigating uncertainty today, enabling precautionary governance across neural, symbolic, and neurosymbolic AI systems.

title: What's Under the Skin? Estimating Swine Body Condition
summary: Sow body condition is an important indicator for growers as it has a large impact on lactation performance and piglet survival. However, body condition measures used during production, such as visual scoring and calipers, correlate poorly with underlying tissue composition. Ultrasound scans can provide direct measurements of subcutaneous backfat thickness and loin muscle depth, but their operation is labor intensive and not scalable for production. We present PigFormer, an end-to-end two-stage system that takes raw depth frames from a ceiling-mounted RGB-D camera and predicts subcutaneous backfat thickness, loin muscle depth, and total tissue thickness at the last rib. Stage 1 is a geometric front-end that converts raw depth into a standardized height map via SAM3-to-MaskDINO segmentation distillation, ground-plane removal, and orientation normalization. Stage 2 is a Slice Attention Encoder that treats each height map as a sequence of cross-sectional slices and captures spatial relationships along the full dorsal surface. On a multi-site dataset of 319 sow and gilt instances from two facilities, PigFormer achieves 2.43 mm backfat MAE and 3.87 mm overall MAE. It outperforms strong single-stage ResNet-18 and ViT-small baselines. PigFormer offers a practical path toward continuous, automated, non-contact body condition monitoring in commercial swine production. Code is available at https://github.com/iambashar/Pigformer.

AI.tldr: PigFormer is a two-stage, non-contact system using ceiling-mounted depth frames to estimate sow backfat, loin muscle depth, and total tissue thickness, achieving low MAEs and outperforming standard baselines.
AI.motivation: Accurate body condition assessment is vital for lactation performance and piglet survival. Conventional visual scoring and calipers correlate poorly with tissue composition; ultrasound is accurate but labor-intensive and not scalable. A scalable, non-contact method is needed for continuous monitoring in production.
AI.method: Stage 1: transform raw depth into a standardized height map through SAM3-to-MaskDINO segmentation distillation, ground-plane removal, and orientation normalization. Stage 2: a Slice Attention Encoder treats the height map as a sequence of cross-sectional slices to capture spatial relationships along the dorsal surface. Data from a ceiling-mounted RGB-D camera are used to predict subcutaneous backfat thickness, loin muscle depth, and total tissue thickness at the last rib. Dataset comprises 319 sow/gilt instances from two facilities.
AI.result: PigFormer achieves 2.43 mm backfat MAE and 3.87 mm overall MAE, outperforming single-stage ResNet-18 and ViT-small baselines on a multi-site dataset. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: PigFormer offers a practical, scalable path toward continuous, automated non-contact body condition monitoring in commercial swine production.

title: 3D Underwater Path Planning via Generative Flow Field Surrogates
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) launch and recovery (LAR) into the hull of an advancing host platform requires traversal of a complex, three-dimensional propeller wake whose hydrodynamic structure cannot be characterised by a uniform current model. High-fidelity Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations resolve this structure with sufficient accuracy for path planning, but their computational cost renders them impractical for onboard use. We address this gap by integrating two conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) architectures -- a regularised PatchGAN and a 2D3DGAN with self-attention -- as drop-in replacements for RANS CFD data within a three-dimensional, energy-weighted A* path planning framework. Both generators are driven by a hierarchical pipeline that synthesises full $128^3$ voxel flow field volumes from scalar operating condition inputs alone, with end-to-end inference times of approximately 28-146 $μ$s, compared to hours for a single RANS computation. We benchmark all four environmental knowledge levels: uniform current, ground-truth CFD, PatchGAN, and 2D3DGAN~SA across 19,800 independently generated trajectories spanning 550 distinct flow conditions. Full CFD wake knowledge reduces energy expenditure by 5.7-12.5% and high-velocity wake-core encounters by up to 77.8% relative to uniform-current planning, with both benefits scaling with operating severity. The cGAN surrogates recover approximately 45-60% of the CFD energy benefit and high-velocity cell avoidance benefit while operating at inference speeds compatible with edge device use. These results provide the first systematic quantification of the downstream path planning value of cGAN-predicted hydrodynamic fields in a three-dimensional maritime robotics application.

AI.tldr: cGAN-based surrogates (PatchGAN and 2D3DGAN-SA) generate 128^3 velocity fields from scalar inputs for 3D AUV path planning, enabling ultra-fast inference and partial recovery of CFD benefits, with substantial but not full CFD gains.
AI.motivation: CFD wake modeling is accurate but computationally expensive; need onboard, fast, reliable surrogate to guide energy-optimized path planning in complex propeller wakes.
AI.method: Two cGAN architectures (regularised PatchGAN; 2D3DGAN with self-attention) used as drop-in CFD surrogates within a 3D, energy-weighted A* planner. A hierarchical pipeline renders full 128^3 voxel flow fields from scalar inputs. Four knowledge levels tested: uniform current, ground-truth CFD, PatchGAN, and 2D3DGAN-SA. Evaluation over 19,800 trajectories across 550 flow conditions.
AI.result: Full CFD wake knowledge reduces energy expenditure by 5.7-12.5% and high-velocity wake-core encounters by up to 77.8% versus uniform current planning. cGAN surrogates recover ~45-60% of CFD energy and high-velocity-encounter benefits, with end-to-end inference times of 28-146 microseconds, suitable for edge devices.
AI.conclusion: This study provides the first systematic quantification of downstream path-planning value from cGAN-predicted hydrodynamic fields in a 3D maritime robotics context, demonstrating a favorable speed–accuracy trade-off enabling onboard planning.

title: Trust, but Don't Verify: Epistemic Blind Spots in LLM Source Evaluation
summary: Language models increasingly act as epistemic proxies, synthesizing evidence from multiple sources to inform decisions. Whether they evaluate the quality of that evidence, or merely aggregate it based on surface presentation, remains poorly understood. We show that models possess the capability to detect fabricated statistics (correct identification rates of 0.76-1.00 for methodology in isolation) but do not recruit this capability during multi-source synthesis, producing similar numeric estimates whether the statistics are fabricated or valid. Specifically, source influence is governed by a methodology-register gate that responds to the distributional register of analytical text but not to numeric validity: for example, statistically impossible confidence intervals receive the same weight as valid ones. The behavioral dissociation replicates across five models from three families (Claude, Qwen, OLMo) and three professional domains. Mechanistic analyses, including causal tracing, linear probes, and component-level attribution, converge on the same account: the model encodes and causally uses a methodology-register representation that transfers across domains (probe AUC 0.83-0.92), while numeric-validity signals, decodable in isolation, are suppressed to chance during multi-source synthesis. Prompting-based mitigations, even an oracle checklist naming the exact statistical checks, produce blanket skepticism rather than selective discernment, and the post-training pipelines we examine reinforce the stylistic shortcut without building numeric verification. Unlike sycophancy, which tracks user preference, this failure tracks whether a source presents as analytically credible, not whether its claims are internally consistent. We term this epistemic alignment: like preference and safety alignment, the question is not capability but deployment.

AI.tldr: LMs detect fabricated statistics in isolation but ignore numeric validity during multi-source synthesis, relying on a methodology-register cue tied to analytical text rather than actual evidence quality; this deployment-oriented epistemic alignment persists across domains and models, and standard mitigations are ineffective.
AI.motivation: To determine whether LMs assess evidence quality or simply aggregate surface cues when synthesizing information from multiple sources, and to understand the mechanisms and generality of this behavior across domains and model families.
AI.method: Evaluation across five models from Claude, Qwen, and OLMo on tasks involving detection of fabricated statistics in isolation and during multi-source synthesis; mechanistic analyses including causal tracing, linear probing, and component-level attribution; cross-domain experiments in three professional domains; assessment of prompting-based mitigations (including an oracle stats-checklist) and post-training pipelines.
AI.result: In isolation, models identify fabricated methodology with 0.76–1.00 accuracy; during multi-source synthesis, they weight numerically implausible or valid statistics equivalently, showing a methodology-register gate driven by text distribution rather than numeric validity. Numeric-validity signals decodable in isolation are suppressed during synthesis (probe AUC 0.83–0.92 for methodology-register; numeric signals decodable but not used). Prompting-based mitigations yield blanket skepticism, not selective discernment; post-training pipelines reinforce the stylistic shortcut. The phenomenon, termed epistemic alignment, reflects a deployment-level bias: the model tracks source credibility rather than internal consistency or user preference.
AI.conclusion: Epistemic alignment exposes a deployment problem where models can surface-justify sources without reliably verifying quantitative claims; improving performance requires deployment-aware strategies that enhance numeric verification during synthesis, beyond improving raw capability.

title: Towards Realistic 3D Sonar Simulation
summary: As underwater robotics research increasingly addresses complex 3D perception and autonomous navigation, the fidelity of sonar simulation has become a key factor in algorithm development. Current simulation frameworks typically rely on geometry-driven rendering, approximating 3D sonar as an underwater equivalent to LiDAR, which fails to account for fundamental acoustic phenomena such as refraction, multi-path interference, and phase-dependent signal formation. This paper proposes a modular architecture for realistic 3D sonar simulation that integrates GPU-accelerated graphics engines with physically grounded acoustic propagation principles. We implement a volumetric 3D sonar model within the NVIDIA Isaac Sim environment, modeled after the Water Linked 3D-15 sensor, and integrate it into a comprehensive underwater simulation framework. The system is validated through a hardware-in-the-loop configuration, where a modified FastLIO2 SLAM pipeline, executed on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, performs sensor fusion using synthetic 3D sonar, DVL, IMU, and pressure data. Finally, a qualitative comparison between simulated outputs and real-world data from harbor sheet-pile inspections is provided, characterizing the remaining sim-to-real gap and establishing a roadmap toward fully acoustics-driven volumetric sensing.

AI.tldr: Introduces a modular, acoustically-grounded 3D sonar simulator in Isaac Sim, validated in hardware-in-the-loop; shows improved realism but still sim-to-real gap, forming a roadmap to fully acoustics-driven volumetric sensing.
AI.motivation: Current sonar simulation largely uses geometry-driven rendering (LiDAR-like) and neglects key acoustic phenomena (refraction, multipath, phase-dependent signal formation). There is a need for realistic acoustic propagation to develop and test 3D underwater perception and navigation algorithms.
AI.method: Develop a modular architecture combining GPU-accelerated graphics with physically grounded acoustic propagation. Implement a volumetric 3D sonar model within NVIDIA Isaac Sim, modeled after the Water Linked 3D-15 sensor, and integrate it into a comprehensive underwater simulation framework. Validate with hardware-in-the-loop using a modified FastLIO2 SLAM pipeline on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, performing sensor fusion with synthetic 3D sonar, DVL, IMU, and pressure data.
AI.result: Qualitative comparison between simulated outputs and real harbor sheet-pile inspection data; characterizes the remaining sim-to-real gap and establishes a roadmap toward fully acoustics-driven volumetric sensing.
AI.conclusion: The approach improves realism by incorporating acoustics into 3D sonar simulation and provides a path toward acoustics-driven volumetric sensing. Remaining gaps indicate need for further quantitative validation, model refinement, and optimization for real-time operation.

title: SoCRATES: Towards Reliable Automated Evaluation of Proactive LLM Mediation across Domains and Socio-cognitive Variations
summary: Evaluating LLM mediators remains challenging, as mediation unfolds as a real-time trajectory shaped by disputants' shifting emotions, intentions, and context. Existing testbeds rely on a few expert-authored domains, vary mainly strategic posture, and score every turn against every topic, introducing off-topic noise. We introduce SoCRATES, a benchmark for evaluating proactive LLM mediators in realistic, multi-domain testbeds. It constructs scenarios from real conflicts through an agentic pipeline across eight domains, probes five socio-cognitive adaptation axes (strategic posture, party composition, history length, emotional reactivity, and cultural identity), and scores each topic only on the turns that advance it via a topic-localized evaluator. The evaluator reaches 0.82 alignment with human experts, more than doubling a per-turn baseline. Benchmarking eight frontier LLMs, we find that even the strongest mediator closes only about a third of the unmediated consensus gap under diverse and realistic testbeds, with performance varying sharply by socio-cognitive axis, highlighting that progress lies in social adaptation to diverse conditions.

AI.tldr: SoCRATES is a new benchmark for evaluating proactive LLM mediators in realistic, multi-domain testbeds, using topic-localized evaluation across eight domains and five socio-cognitive axes; it shows strong alignment with human experts (0.82) but mediators still close only a third of the unmediated consensus gap, highlighting the importance of social adaptation.
AI.motivation: Mediation involves real-time dynamics with shifting emotions, intentions, and context, and existing testbeds are limited by expert-authored domains and noisy, off-topic scoring. There is a need for realistic, domain-diverse, proactive mediation benchmarks.
AI.method: Construct scenarios from real conflicts via an agentic pipeline across eight domains; probe five socio-cognitive adaptation axes (strategic posture, party composition, history length, emotional reactivity, cultural identity); evaluate topic-localized scoring so only turns advancing a topic are scored; align evaluator with human experts (0.82); benchmark eight frontier LLMs.
AI.result: The topic-localized evaluator aligns with human experts at 0.82. The strongest mediator reduces about one third of the unmediated consensus gap under varied testbeds, with performance greatly varying across socio-cognitive axes.
AI.conclusion: Real progress in LLM mediation requires social adaptation to diverse conditions; SoCRATES provides a more realistic, nuanced benchmark that captures dynamics across multiple domains and socio-cognitive factors.

title: CausalPOI: Spatio-Temporal Graph-Based Causal Modeling for Cold-Start POI Check-in Forecasting
summary: As urban environments continue to evolve rapidly, accurately modeling the dynamic behaviour of Points of Interest is essential for supporting data-driven urban planning and commercial decision-making. While recent advancements in spatio-temporal graph learning have improved POI forecasting, most methods rely on proximity-based graphs and correlation-driven modeling, which overlook the functional dependencies between POIs and fail to capture the causal effects of urban interventions. In this paper, we introduce a novel research problem -- cold-start POI check-in forecasting, which aims to predict the future check-in pattern of a newly introduced POI, by modeling its temporal evolution and functional interactions with nearby POIs in a structured urban spatial context. To address these challenges, we propose CausalPOI, a spatio-temporal graph-based causal representation learning framework. CausalPOI leverages Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph to model semantic and spatial relationships between POIs, and constructs structurally aligned treatment and control graphs to simulate factual and counterfactual scenarios. Extensive experiments on real-world SafeGraph datasets demonstrate that CausalPOI significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across the board, validating its effectiveness in spatio-temporal forecasting, semantic interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation, providing a more interpretable and actionable foundation for urban intervention analysis. Source code is available at Github.

AI.tldr: Introduces CausalPOI for cold-start POI check-in forecasting using spatio-temporal causal representation learning with treatment-control graphs; outperforms baselines on SafeGraph.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of proximity-based, correlation-based models; tackle cold-start POI forecasting by modeling functional dependencies and causal effects of urban interventions.
AI.method: Proposes CausalPOI with Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph; constructs structurally aligned treatment and control graphs to simulate factual/counterfactual scenarios; aims for interpretable causal effects and improved forecasting.
AI.result: Experiments on real SafeGraph data show significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in forecasting, semantic interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation; code available.
AI.conclusion: CausalPOI provides interpretable, actionable insights for urban intervention analysis and supports robust cold-start POI forecasting; demonstrates the value of causal representation in spatio-temporal POI modeling.

title: MotionDisco: Motion Discovery for Extreme Humanoid Loco-Manipulation
summary: We present MotionDisco, a framework that discovers contact-rich, long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation motions from scratch, without relying on teleoperation or motion retargeting from human demonstrations. This is challenging because the space of possible contact interactions grows combinatorially with the task horizon and the number of objects in the scene. MotionDisco enables rapid discovery of novel motions by coupling a large language model (LLM) guided evolutionary search over sequences of interactions with an efficient sequential kinodynamic trajectory optimizer and pruning strategy, enabling the rapid discovery of novel skills. Through extensive ablation studies, we show that our LLM-guided search discovers successful whole-body trajectories across several challenging long-horizon tasks. Finally, by training reinforcement learning tracking policies on the discovered trajectories, we transfer the motions to a real humanoid robot. This is the first work to discover and deploy long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation skills entirely through automated evolutionary search. Supplementary videos of the experiments are available at: https://youtu.be/DHiVz34QYlw.

AI.tldr: MotionDisco discovers long-horizon, contact-rich loco-manipulation skills for humanoids from scratch, using an LLM-guided evolutionary search coupled with a kinodynamic trajectory optimizer and pruning. It demonstrates successful discovery across challenging tasks, validates via ablations, and transfers to real robots through RL tracking policies, claiming to be the first fully automated evolutionary-search-based discovery and deployment of such skills.
AI.motivation: Address the combinatorial explosion of possible contact interactions in long-horizon loco-manipulation and the lack of scalable, demonstration-free discovery methods that can produce novel, transferable skills for humanoid robots.
AI.method: An LLM-guided evolutionary search over sequences of interactions, integrated with an efficient sequential kinodynamic trajectory optimizer and a pruning strategy to rapidly evaluate and discard infeasible or suboptimal sequences. Extensive ablations validate the importance of the LLM guidance. Discovered trajectories are used to train reinforcement learning tracking policies to transfer motions to a real humanoid robot.
AI.result: The framework enables rapid discovery of novel, successful whole-body trajectories for challenging long-horizon tasks. Ablation studies show the contribution of LLM guidance. The learned RL trackers enable transfer of the discovered motions to a real humanoid robot.
AI.conclusion: MotionDisco represents a pioneering approach that combines LLM-guided search with trajectory optimization and RL-based tracking to autonomously discover and deploy long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation skills, marking a baseline for automated skill discovery via evolutionary search.

title: GuardNet: Ensemble Strategies of Shallow Neural Networks for Robust Prompt Injection and Jailbreak Detection
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed natural language processing, but they remain vulnerable to Prompt Injection (PI) and Jailbreak (JB) attacks. In addition, benchmark evaluations may be affected by contamination and partial information leakage, compromising performance estimates. This work presents GuardNet, a guardrail system based on an ensemble of shallow neural networks (BiLSTMs) with approximately 47 million parameters. We investigate the hypothesis that robustness in adversarial scenarios depends more on the diversity of example coverage and threshold calibration than on model scale. The results indicate that GuardNet achieves competitive performance compared with lightweight detectors and high efficiency at low latency, although larger LLMs such as Mistral-7B and Llama-3.1-8B still achieve superior performance in terms of F1 score and AUROC on the blind JBB-Behaviors benchmark. Nevertheless, GuardNet achieves an AUROC of 0.747 on the blind dataset (n = 200) and an F1 score of 0.92 on a proprietary benchmark (n = 50), under threshold calibration and evaluation with declared partial information leakage. The system operates with an average latency of approximately 50 ms on CPU, making it suitable for deployment in production environments with cost and infrastructure constraints.

AI.tldr: GuardNet is a 47M-parameter BiLSTM ensemble guardrail for prompt-injection/jailbreak defense, achieving fast CPU-friendly performance with 50 ms latency and competitive results vs lightweight detectors; however, larger LLMs still outperform it on blind benchmarks. Key takeaways include that robustness in adversarial settings benefits more from diverse coverage and calibrated thresholds than mere model scale.
AI.motivation: Address vulnerabilities of LLMs to prompt injection and jailbreaking and concerns about benchmark contamination/leakage that may bias performance estimates; assess whether model size is necessary for robust guardrails and how coverage/diversity and calibration influence performance.
AI.method: An ensemble of shallow neural networks (BiLSTMs) totaling ~47M parameters to act as a guardrail system (GuardNet). The study evaluates thresholds and coverage diversity, compares against lightweight detectors, and benchmarks on blind JBB-Behaviors and a proprietary dataset under partial information leakage, reporting AUROC and F1, with measured CPU latency (~50 ms).
AI.result: GuardNet delivers competitive performance with lightweight detectors and excellent latency; larger LLMs (Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) still outperform GuardNet on the blind JBB-Behaviors benchmark in F1 and AUROC. Quantitatively, GuardNet achieves AUROC 0.747 on a blind dataset (n=200) and F1 0.92 on a proprietary benchmark (n=50) under threshold calibration with partial information leakage.
AI.conclusion: Robustness in adversarial contexts can rely on threshold calibration and broad example coverage rather than sheer model scale. GuardNet offers production-friendly guardrail capabilities with ~50 ms CPU latency, though it trades off some performance against larger LLMs on certain benchmarks.

title: Multi-Task Crack Foundation Model for Engineering-Reliable Crack Representation and Topology Preservation in Civil Infrastructure
summary: Reliable crack assessment requires not only accurate pixel-level masks but also connected crack geometry and confidence estimates that remain stable under domain shift. However, existing segmentation models can achieve high overlap scores while fragmenting cracks, missing fine branches, and providing no calibrated uncertainty. To address this gap, this paper proposes CrackGeoFM, a multi-task framework that combines a frozen visual foundation backbone with crack-specific adaptation for mask prediction, skeleton reconstruction, and uncertainty estimation. The framework integrates a Frequency-Guided Crack Enhancement Module (FCEM) to enhance high-frequency crack cues, a Crack-Domain Feature Adaptation Module (CFAM) to adapt frozen backbone features to crack-domain patterns, and a Structure-Aware Multi-Task Decoder (SMTD) to jointly decode masks, skeletons, and uncertainty. Across 20 crack datasets, CrackGeoFM achieves state-of-the-art segmentation, improved topology preservation, calibrated uncertainty, and effective few-shot adaptation with only five labeled images. These results support reliable, generalizable, and engineering-oriented crack analysis for infrastructure assessment.

AI.tldr: CrackGeoFM: a multi-task framework with a frozen backbone and crack-specific adaptation for segmentation, skeleton reconstruction, and uncertainty estimation. It uses a Frequency-Guided Crack Enhancement Module (FCEM), a Crack-Domain Feature Adaptation Module (CFAM), and a Structure-Aware Multi-Task Decoder (SMTD). Achieves state-of-the-art crack segmentation, better topology preservation, calibrated uncertainty, and effective few-shot adaptation across 20 crack datasets.
AI.motivation: Reliable crack assessment requires not only pixel-accurate masks but also crack topology preservation and well-calibrated uncertainty that remains stable under domain shifts. Existing segmentation models often fragment cracks and lack calibrated uncertainty.
AI.method: A frozen visual backbone is adapted for crack analysis via three components: FCEM to boost high-frequency crack cues, CFAM to align frozen backbone features with crack-domain statistics, and SMTD to jointly predict masks, skeletons, and uncertainty. The approach is validated on 20 crack datasets, with few-shot adaptation using only five labeled images.
AI.result: CrackGeoFM achieves state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy, improved topology preservation (better skeleton consistency), calibrated uncertainty estimates, and effective few-shot adaptation, indicating strong generalization across crack domains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CrackGeoFM offers a reliable, generalizable framework for crack analysis by integrating frequency-guided enhancement, domain feature adaptation, and structure-aware multi-task decoding.

title: Fix the Mind, Not the Move: Interpretable AI Assistance via Knowledge-Gap Localization
comment: Accepted to International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: AI assistants in human-AI collaboration often correct suboptimal human actions through behavioral feedback (e.g., alerts or steering-wheel nudges in assistive driving). Such interventions can mitigate immediate errors, but long-term improvement requires addressing the underlying misconceptions that cause repeated mistakes. We introduce SENSEI, a framework that infers user misconceptions from interaction behavior and provides targeted, minimal yet sufficient suggestions to correct them. Our approach departs from action- or trajectory-level interventions by operating over a structured knowledge representation to localize and correct the sources of erroneous behavior. Across three long-horizon tasks with diverse misconceptions and corresponding behaviors, SENSEI demonstrates zero-shot compositional generalization, disentangling multiple overlapping misconceptions despite training only on single-misconception cases. A user study further shows that our method identifies real human misconceptions and provides effective guidance that improves long-horizon task performance, successfully correcting $90\%$ of student misconceptions. Code and project page are available at https://misoshiruseijin.github.io/SENSEI/.

AI.tldr: SENSEI infers user misconceptions from interaction traces and delivers minimal, targeted, knowledge-level corrections via a structured representation, enabling zero-shot compositional generalization across long-horizon tasks and improving long-term performance.
AI.motivation: Long-term improvement requires addressing underlying misconceptions, not just correcting surface-level actions; existing interventions mitigate immediate errors but fail to fix root causes.
AI.method: Infer latent misconceptions from interaction behavior and localize them within a structured knowledge representation; generate targeted, minimal corrective suggestions that address the source of erroneous behavior rather than action- or trajectory-level nudges; evaluate across three long-horizon tasks with varied misconceptions, demonstrating zero-shot compositional generalization by combining multiple misconceptions trained only on single-misconception cases; validate with a user study.
AI.result: Demonstrates zero-shot compositional generalization across multiple misconceptions; the approach disentangles overlapping misconceptions despite training only on single-misconception cases; user study shows accurate identification of real human misconceptions and effective guidance, achieving a 90% correction rate of student misconceptions.
AI.conclusion: A knowledge-level, misconception-aware framework can robustly identify and correct underlying misbeliefs in human-AI collaboration, with strong generalization to novel combinations of misconceptions and tangible improvements in long-horizon task performance; code and project page are provided.

title: Multilingual Fine-Tuning via Localized Gradient Conflict Resolution
summary: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) has established cross-lingual versatility as a defining feature of modern systems. However, fine-tuning these models frequently induces negative interference across languages. To address this, we reformulate multilingual fine-tuning as a multi-objective optimization (MOO) problem. Specifically, we introduce Bucket-Level MOO, a scalable distributed framework that applies gradient-based MOO algorithms locally on parameter buckets. This enables conflict-aware updates without the prohibitive communication overhead of reconstructing full gradient vectors. Theoretically, we prove this localized resolution natively enforces Refined Pareto Stationarity, a strictly tighter necessary condition for Pareto optimality. Empirically, Bucket-Level MOO mitigates interference by driving LLMs to construct distinct language-specific dimensions, improving representational separability. Extensive experiments across four base LLMs demonstrate that our method significantly improves both seen and unseen multilingual performance over standard fine-tuning paradigms.

AI.tldr: Bucket-Level MOO applies gradient-based multi-objective optimization at parameter buckets to mitigate cross-language interference during multilingual fine-tuning, enabling scalable, conflict-aware updates and improving language-specific representations.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning multilingual LLMs often causes negative interference across languages; existing methods struggle with scalability due to gradient reconstruction costs. A localized, bucket-level approach aims to reduce interference while remaining scalable.
AI.method: A distributed framework that performs gradient-based MOO locally on parameter buckets. This avoids reconstructing full gradient vectors and reduces communication overhead. Theoretical results show that this local resolution enforces Refined Pareto Stationarity. Empirically, the method drives language-specific dimensions to emerge, improving representational separability.
AI.result: Across four base LLMs, Bucket-Level MOO significantly improves multilingual performance on both seen and unseen languages compared with standard fine-tuning baselines.
AI.conclusion: Localized MOO at the bucket level yields conflict-aware updates that tighten Pareto optimality conditions and promote language-specific representations, with scalable gains across multiple base models.

title: Safety Paradox: How Enhanced Safety Awareness Leaves LLMs Vulnerable to Posterior Attack
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are rigorously aligned to refuse harmful requests, a process that inherently cultivates a latent capacity to evaluate and recognize unsafe content. In this work, we reveal that this advanced safety awareness inadvertently introduces a fatal vulnerability. We introduce Posterior Attack, a single-query jailbreak that bypasses guardrails by prompting the model to generate the exact harmful response its internal classifier would normally flag as unsafe. Through extensive empirical evaluation across 30 open-source LLMs (up to 35B parameters in size) and frontier models (e.g., GPT-5, Claude 4.6), we observe a striking phenomenon: models with superior safety-judgment capabilities are disproportionately more susceptible to this exploitation. To explain this, we formalize the Safety Paradox, analytically showing that monotonic improvements in safety alignment naturally amplify posterior vulnerability. Finally, we establish a causal link via reinforcement learning interventions, exemplifying that artificially degrading a model's safety judgment immunizes it against the attack, whereas enhancing judgment exacerbates the vulnerability. Our findings highlight potential flaws in current alignment paradigms, indicating that defense mechanisms may require further structural refinement.

AI.tldr: Safety improvements in LLMs can paradoxically raise vulnerability to a single-query jailbreak that elicits the model’s own unsafe output; the Safety Paradox is supported by broad empirical results and a formal analysis, with RL interventions suggesting causality and urging redesigned alignment strategies.
AI.motivation: To understand why stronger safety judgment in LLMs might backfire by creating exploitable pathways, challenging the assumption that better safety directly reduces risk.
AI.method: Introduce Posterior Attack (single-shot jailbreak leveraging the model’s internal classifier); evaluate across ~30 open-source LLMs (up to 35B) and frontier models (GPT-5, Claude 4.6); formalize the Safety Paradox analytically; use reinforcement learning interventions to test causal links between safety judgment and vulnerability.
AI.result: Models with higher safety-judgment capabilities show greater susceptibility to the attack; a monotone improvement in safety paradoxically amplifies posterior vulnerability; RL interventions that degrade safety judgment reduce vulnerability, while enhancing it increases vulnerability; causal link demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal gaps in current alignment approaches, indicating that purely increasing safety judgments can worsen certain vulnerabilities. Structural refinements to defense mechanisms are needed, potentially decoupling safety classifiers from generation and incorporating adversarial robustness strategies.

title: Self-Commitment Latency: A Reward-Free Probe for Prompted Implicit Hacking
summary: Implicit reward hacking is hard to audit when a language model's chain of thought appears benign: a final answer may be anchored by a prompt shortcut while the written reasoning still resembles ordinary problem solving. Verifier-based probes expose such behavior by measuring how early truncated reasoning contexts obtain high reward, but require a task-specific reward signal. This paper proposes a weaker-input alternative, self-commitment latency, which measures how early a prompted reasoning context commits to the model's own final answer. We evaluate the probe in a controlled paired GSM8K setting using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit, comparing ordinary prompts with prompts that include an answer hint. Hinted contexts commit substantially earlier and with lower uncertainty than honest contexts. The primary latency metric, first-commitment latency at threshold 0.8, reaches AUROC 0.878; supporting whole-curve summaries reach AUROC 0.926 for commitment range and 0.904 for mean uncommitted mass. The signal is stronger when both prompt conditions answer correctly and remains stable across thresholds. These results show that shortcut-available reasoning contexts can leave an early behavioral commitment signature detectable without a reward model, external judge, or trained classifier.

AI.tldr: Self-commitment latency as a weaker-input probe for implicit reward hacking; detects early commitment signatures in prompted reasoning without a task-specific reward signal.
AI.motivation: Auditing language-model reasoning for shortcut-enabled behavior is hard with standard reward-based probes. Verifier-based probes require task-specific reward signals; a weaker, input-based signal is desirable.
AI.method: Introduce self-commitment latency (SCL) as a measure of how early a prompted reasoning context commits to the model’s final answer. Evaluate in a controlled paired GSM8K setup using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-4bit, comparing ordinary prompts with prompts that include an answer hint. Use first-commitment latency at threshold 0.8 as primary metric and analyze full-curve summaries (AUROC for commitment range and mean uncommitted mass).
AI.result: Hints lead to earlier and more confident commitments than honest prompts. Primary metric (first-commitment latency at 0.8) AUROC = 0.878; full-curve AUROCs = 0.926 (commitment range) and 0.904 (mean uncommitted mass). The signal strengthens when both prompts answer correctly and remains stable across thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Self-commitment latency can reveal shortcut-available reasoning without requiring a reward model, external judge, or a trained classifier.

title: T-SAR-JEPA: Self-Supervised Temporal Anomaly Detection in SAR Amplitude Stacks via Latent Prediction
comment: Won IEEE GRSS Data Fusion Contest 2026; to appear in IGARSS 2026 proceedings
summary: We present T-SAR-JEPA, a self-supervised framework for temporal anomaly detection in SAR amplitude stacks via latent prediction. A ViT-Base/16 encoder from SAR-JEPA is domain-adapted on 39,300 Capella patches using local masked reconstruction with gradient feature prediction. A temporal transformer with sinusoidal time encoding forecasts future latent states from K=7 acquisitions, with progressive unfreezing substantially reducing validation loss. The model operates on amplitude alone; InSAR coherence serves exclusively as independent pseudo-ground-truth. On the DFC 2026 dataset (300 time-series, three AOIs), T-SAR-JEPA achieves ROC-AUC of 77.0% on the Hawaii eruption window, outperforming RX, PaDiM, Linear AR, and LSTM baselines (~50%). Spatial coherence of 99.9% (p < 0.001, permutation test) confirms structured detections. Code: https://github.com/TerraLatent/t-sar-jepa

AI.tldr: Self-supervised temporal anomaly detection in SAR amplitudes via latent prediction using a ViT encoder and temporal transformer; achieves 77.0% ROC-AUC on the DFC 2026 Hawaii window.
AI.motivation: Address scarcity of labeled SAR time-series anomalies by learning predictive latent dynamics and leveraging coherence as weak ground-truth.
AI.method: Domain-adapted ViT encoder (SAR-JEPA) trained with local masked reconstruction and gradient feature prediction on Capella patches; temporal transformer with sinusoidal time encoding predicts future latent states from K=7 acquisitions; progressive unfreezing; operates on amplitude only; InSAR coherence used as independent pseudo-ground-truth.
AI.result: DFC 2026 dataset (300 time-series, 3 AOIs) • ROC-AUC 77.0% on Hawaii eruption window; outperforms RX, PaDiM, Linear AR, and LSTM baselines (~50%). Spatial coherence 99.9% (p<0.001, permutation test).
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised latent-dynamics modeling in SAR time series is effective for anomaly detection; coherence serves as a weak supervisory signal; code released.

title: Towards Unified and Data-Efficient Prognostics and Health Management with Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Data-driven Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) uses time-varying condition-monitoring data to diagnose system states and estimate remaining useful life in engineered assets. These tasks are central to maintenance planning, but industrial PHM data are often fragmented, partially observed, and poorly labeled, which hinders supervised learning. Foundation models offer a route toward reusable predictive systems, yet most time-series foundation models are designed for forecasting and assume long, coherent, regularly sampled sequences. To address this gap, we propose a framework for applying Tabular Foundation Models to industrial time series using in-context learning, and we evaluate them on a variety of PHM tasks. By converting raw unit-level signals into tabular rows, we show that these models perform well across multiple tasks - including prognostics, and diagnostics - and are highly data efficient. We compare them directly with sequence models, transformer baselines, and gradient-boosted trees under a common evaluation protocol. The results indicate that tabular foundation models achieve the best average ranks across prognostic and diagnostic tasks. Our findings further show that PFN-based models are competitive in low-data regimes, that temporal context can be preserved in the tabular representation, and that performance depends on representative context construction under subsampling. These results demonstrate that tabular foundation models provide a practical and general interface for heterogeneous PHM problems.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models with in-context learning applied to industrial time-series data enable efficient prognostics and diagnostics by converting raw signals into tabular rows; they outperform various baselines, are effective in low-data regimes, and rely on careful context construction to preserve temporal information.
AI.motivation: Industrial PHM data are often fragmented, partially observed, and poorly labeled, hindering supervised learning. There is a gap between time-series foundation models (designed for forecasting) and the need for versatile, data-efficient prognostics and diagnostics. The work proposes using Tabular Foundation Models (PFN-based) with in-context learning to address this gap.
AI.method: Convert unit-level time-series signals into tabular rows; apply Tabular Foundation Models with in-context learning (PFN-based); evaluate across multiple PHM tasks (prognostics and diagnostics); compare against sequence models, transformer baselines, and gradient-boosted trees under a common evaluation protocol; analyze data efficiency, temporal context preservation in tabular form, and impact of context construction under subsampling.
AI.result: PFN-based tabular foundation models achieve the best average ranks across prognostic and diagnostic tasks; competitive performance in low-data regimes; temporal context can be preserved in the tabular representation; performance depends on representative context construction under subsampling; overall, they provide a practical, general interface for heterogeneous PHM problems.
AI.conclusion: Tabular foundation models offer a practical and versatile framework for PHM tasks, effectively handling fragmented and limited data by leveraging a tabular perspective and in-context learning, and they outperform or match traditional sequence-based approaches across diagnostics and prognostics.

title: Evaluation of LLMs for Mathematical Formalization in Lean
summary: Within the past few years, the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate formal mathematical proofs has improved drastically. We provide a comparison of various LLMs' effectiveness in producing formal proofs in Lean 4 with the goal of assisting those seeking to use LLMs to support their own projects. We utilize both pass@$k$ and refine@$k$ metrics as the benchmark for our comparison and evaluate on subsets of both miniF2F and miniCTX datasets. Our testing shows that overall, Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.7 perform best. Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved a 92\% success rate on miniF2F via refine@32 whereas Opus 4.7 achieved a 86\% success rate on miniCTX via refine@32. When taking cost into account, NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B were the most efficient, with competitive accuracies and average costs of $<\$0.01$ per correct proof.

AI.tldr: Comparative evaluation of LLMs on generating formal Lean 4 proofs; identifies top performers and cost-efficient options under pass@k and refine@k metrics on miniF2F/miniCTX datasets.
AI.motivation: To assess how effectively current LLMs can assist in producing formal mathematical proofs and to understand the trade-offs between accuracy and affordability for practitioners.
AI.method: Benchmark multiple LLMs (e.g., Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.7, NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super, GPT-OSS 120B) using pass@k and refine@k metrics on subsets of miniF2F and miniCTX datasets; report success rates at refine@32 and analyze cost per correct proof.
AI.result: Gemini 3.1 Pro achieves 92% success on miniF2F with refine@32; Claude Opus 4.7 achieves 86% on miniCTX with refine@32. Cost-efficient options are NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super and GPT-OSS 120B with average costs < $0.01 per correct proof.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can deliver high-quality formal proofs with favorable cost-performance trade-offs, though performance is model- and dataset-dependent; refine@k is a strong signal for improvement; results emphasize the importance of considering both accuracy and cost in practical deployment.

title: Parallel Jacobi Decoding for Fast Autoregressive Image Generation
summary: Autoregressive (AR) models have demonstrated remarkable performance in generating high-fidelity images. However, their inherently sequential next-token prediction leads to significantly slower inference. Recent studies have introduced Jacobi-style decoding to accelerate autoregressive image generation. Extending the draft sequence initially improves efficiency, yet the acceleration quickly saturates as error propagation in the one-dimensional sequence hinders convergence. Observing that images exhibit strong local spatial correlations, we propose Parallel Jacobi Decoding (PJD), a training-free decoding approach that expands draft tokens in the two-dimensional spatial domain to enable efficient spatially parallel refinement. PJD adjusts the attention mask to mitigate error accumulation and improve convergence stability. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets show that PJD achieves 4.8x-6.4x acceleration across multiple autoregressive image generation models while maintaining competitive generation quality.

AI.tldr: PJD offers a training-free 2D spatial expansion of draft tokens and adjusted attention masking to parallelize refinement in autoregressive image generation, yielding 4.8–6.4x faster decoding with comparable quality.
AI.motivation: AR image models are slow due to sequential token prediction; existing Jacobi-style and draft sequences improve speed but hit a plateau due to 1D error propagation; images have strong local spatial correlations suggesting 2D parallel refinement could be more effective.
AI.method: Introduce Parallel Jacobi Decoding (PJD) that expands draft tokens in a 2D spatial grid during decoding; modify the attention mask to manage dependencies and suppress error accumulation; training-free, no extra optimization required.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse datasets shows 4.8x–6.4x speedups in autoregressive image generation across multiple models with competitive generation quality.
AI.conclusion: PJD demonstrates that leveraging 2D spatial structure via a training-free decoding strategy can substantially accelerate AR image generation while maintaining quality, offering a practical path to faster inference and a foundation for further work.

title: Answer Presence Drives RAG Rewriting Gains
summary: Retrieval-augmented QA pipelines often route retrieved passages through an LLM \emph{rewriter} before a smaller reader, lifting F1 by tens of points on multi-hop benchmarks; this gain is typically credited to improved evidence quality. We ask whether that lift is causally driven by the gold answer string appearing in the rewritten context rather than by curation per se, using a controlled intervention audit. For each rewritten context we re-run the reader after one of four controlled edits to the compile output: removing the gold answer span, replacing a length-matched random non-answer span (placebo), or injecting the gold into rewrites where it was absent (at the prefix or at a midpoint sentence boundary). Across twelve completed (cell, baseline) intervention runs spanning three reader families (Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3.5-35B, GLM-4.7), two datasets (HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA), and three compiler arrangements (MA-only, MB-only, MA$+$verify), removing the gold answer drops reader F1 by $28$ to $64$ points beyond the length-matched placebo on paired \texttt{answer-in-compile} strata, and prepending the gold into rewrites that lacked it raises F1 by $+0.7$ to $+9.7$ points in $10$ of $12$ (cell, baseline) combinations. A companion five-sentinel audit shows the conventional single-\texttt{[MASK]} probe is itself sentinel-fragile: on 2Wiki it reports a $+4.12$~F1 ``non-leakage residual'' that flips to $-3.33$ to $-7.81$~F1 under four alternative sentinels and fails an equivalence test for three of those four ($1/4$~pass). We do not propose a new rewriter or mitigation; we release the intervention runner and the sentinel panel so that other rewriter-gain claims can be tested against the same standard.

AI.tldr: Controlled edits to rewritten QA context show gold answer leakage, not just curation, largely drives reader performance gains; sentinel tests reveal fragility; authors provide open tooling for replication and auditing.
AI.motivation: Disentangle whether rewriter gains come from improved evidence quality or leakage of gold answer into the compiled input.
AI.method: 12 intervention runs across 3 reader families, 2 datasets, 3 compiler setups; edits include removing the gold answer, substituting a length-matched non-answer span (placebo), and injecting the gold into rewrites where absent (prefix or mid-sentence). A companion five-sentinel audit tests evaluation robustness with multiple sentinels. Tools (intervention runner and sentinel panel) released for replication.
AI.result: Removing the gold answer reduces reader F1 by 28–64 points beyond the length-matched placebo; injecting the gold boosts F1 by 0.7–9.7 points in 10 of 12 combinations. The sentinel audit reveals the [MASK] probe is sentinel-fragile: residual effects vary with sentinel choice and fail equivalence tests in several cases.
AI.conclusion: Gold leakage into rewritten context causally contributes to observed gains; evidence quality per se is not the sole driver. Results imply careful auditing is needed when attributing gains to rewriters. No new rewriter is proposed; tooling is released to enable broader testing of rewriter-gain claims.

title: Real-Time Threat Detection from Surveillance Cameras using Machine Learning
summary: Ensuring public safety in densely populated urban environments remains a critical challenge, necessitating the deployment of intelligent and automated video surveillance systems. Traditional surveillance approaches rely heavily on manual monitoring, which is inefficient and susceptible to human fatigue, delayed response, and observational errors. To overcome these limitations, this work presents a real-time object detection-based surveillance framework. The proposed system focuses on detecting guns, knives, and region-specific blunt objects commonly involved in violent activities in Indian surveillance scenarios. A key contribution of this work is the use of a custom-created dataset collected using a mobile camera, consisting of 336 labeled images of blunt objects such as iron rods, wooden sticks, and plastic rods. This dataset is combined with a publicly available dataset of 7,623 images of guns and knives, forming a consolidated dataset of 7,959 images across three classes: gun, knife, and blunt object. The combined dataset is used to train a YOLOv8-based object detection model for real-time performance. Experimental evaluation shows that increasing the training duration significantly improves recall and average precision for the blunt object class without signs of overfitting. Overall, the proposed framework achieves an effective balance between accuracy and efficiency, making it suitable for deployment in real-world surveillance environments such as campuses, public spaces, and transportation areas.

AI.tldr: Real-time YOLOv8-based surveillance system for detecting guns, knives, and blunt objects in India; combines 336 blunt-object images with a 7,623-image public gun/knife dataset to train a 7,959-image three-class detector; shows training duration improves blunt-object recall/precision, with efficient real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Improve public safety in dense urban environments by replacing manual monitoring with automated, robust object detection capable of detecting common weapons/objects in Indian surveillance contexts.
AI.method: Create a custom blunt-object dataset (336 images) via mobile camera; merge with public gun/knife dataset (7,623 images) to form 7,959-image, three-class dataset; train YOLOv8 for real-time detection; evaluate recall and average precision, analyze impact of training duration; report no overfitting signs.
AI.result: Longer training improves recall and AP for blunt objects; overall balance between accuracy and efficiency; framework suitable for deployment in campuses, public spaces, and transit areas.
AI.conclusion: A practical, real-time three-class weapon-object detector for Indian surveillance contexts; demonstrates feasibility of integrating region-specific data with public datasets to achieve real-time surveillance objectives.

title: RadiusFPS: Efficient Farthest Point Sampling on CPUs and GPUs via Spherical Voxel Pruning
summary: Point clouds are a primary sensory representation for robotic perception, underpinning LiDAR-based autonomous driving, simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), and navigation. Within these pipelines, Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) is the most well-known downsampling operator, as its uniform coverage preserves the geometric structure on which downstream perception relies. However, the large time complexity of classical FPS scales poorly with the million-point-per-second rates of modern 3D sensors, making it a dominant latency bottleneck that conflicts with the real-time and limited onboard compute budgets of robotic systems. Therefore, we propose RadiusFPS, an FPS acceleration framework based on spherical voxel pruning that preserves the standard FPS update rule under the same initialization and tie-breaking policy. By indexing the point cloud with spherical voxels, RadiusFPS derives a conservative geometric bound that prunes redundant distance computations in each iteration, complemented by a coordinate-wise point-skip test that removes residual updates. We further introduce RadiusFPS-G, a warp-level GPU implementation that fuses voxel selection, pruning, and distance update into memory-coalesced kernels, eliminating costly global-memory round-trips. On indoor (S3DIS, ScanNet) and outdoor LiDAR (SemanticKITTI) benchmarks, RadiusFPS-G attains up to 2.5x speedup over GPU-based FPS and matches or exceeds QuickFPS among the evaluated methods while using roughly half its GPU memory, with comparable segmentation accuracy. When coupled with the learning-based FastPoint sampler, the resulting pipeline achieves the fastest End-to-End inference among all evaluated configurations. These properties make high-quality FPS-style sampling practical for latency- and memory-constrained robotic vision.

AI.tldr: RadiusFPS accelerates farthest point sampling (FPS) for point clouds by spherical voxel pruning, preserving FPS rules while reducing distance computations. It achieves up to 2.5x GPU speedup with about half the memory usage and maintains segmentation accuracy; when paired with FastPoint, it enables the fastest end-to-end inference among evaluated configurations.
AI.motivation: FPS is a cubic-like bottleneck in large point clouds due to repeated pairwise distance computations. Fast, memory-efficient FPS is crucial for real-time robotic perception (LiDAR, SLAM, navigation).
AI.method: Introduce spherical voxel indexing to prune redundant distance calculations via a conservative geometric bound; add a coordinate-wise point-skip test; derive a GPU-optimized RadiusFPS-G with fused, memory-coalesced kernels that implement the standard FPS update rule under the same initialization and tie-breaking policy.
AI.result: Benchmarked on indoor (S3DIS, ScanNet) and outdoor LiDAR (SemanticKITTI) datasets, RadiusFPS-G achieves up to 2.5× speedup over GPU FPS, uses roughly half the GPU memory, and attains comparable or better segmentation accuracy than QuickFPS; end-to-end inference is fastest when combined with FastPoint sampler.
AI.conclusion: RadiusFPS makes high-quality FPS-style sampling practical for latency- and memory-constrained robotic vision by substantially speeding up FPS with minimal accuracy loss, enabling faster end-to-end perception pipelines.

title: FIDES: Faithful Inference via Deep Evidence Signals for Retrieval-Memory Conflict in RAG
summary: When retrieved evidence contradicts parametric memory, language models frequently ignore context and default to memorized priors -- a failure that undermines the core purpose of retrieval augmentation. Contrastive decoding amplifies the context-conditioned output to suppress parametric bias, but existing methods rest on an implicit assumption that this bias is uniform across tokens. A single global contrastive weight over-penalizes safe tokens while leaving genuinely conflicted ones insufficiently corrected. We identify token-level conflict concentration: retrieval-memory tension is sharply heterogeneous, concentrated on a small fraction of answer-critical decoding steps. This reframes contrastive decoding from how much contrast to apply to where to apply it. We propose FIDES (Faithful Inference via Deep Evidence Signals), a training-free decoder that reads three internal signals probing retrieval-memory conflict at complementary depths -- output surface, hidden representations, and prediction trajectory -- and fuses them to govern intervention strength at each decoding step. Across three benchmarks and six backbones -- four primary 7B/8B models and two scaling backbones up to 70B -- FIDES achieves the best context fidelity in all 18 settings, outperforming the strongest training-free baseline by +3 to +13 points. On the 70B scale, fidelity reaches 92-94% while F1 surges to 62-63%, demonstrating that token-level selectivity unlocks generation capability that coarse contrastive rules suppress.

AI.tldr: FIDES is a training-free decoder that uses token-level conflict signals (surface, hidden, trajectory) to adapt intervention strength per token, improving context fidelity against retrieval-memory bias; achieves state-of-the-art results across 18 settings and scales to 70B.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented LMs can ignore context when retrieved evidence conflicts with parametric memory. Global contrastive decoding over-penalizes safe tokens and under-corrects genuinely conflicted ones due to token-agnostic weighting; conflict is highly concentrated on a small subset of decoding steps, necessitating token-level selectivity.
AI.method: FIDES reads three internal signals probing retrieval-memory conflict at complementary depths (output surface, hidden representations, and prediction trajectory) and fuses them to determine per-token intervention strength during decoding; training-free.
AI.result: Across three benchmarks and six backbones (four 7B/8B and two scaling backbones up to 70B), FIDES achieves the best context fidelity in all 18 settings, outperforming the strongest training-free baseline by 3–13 points. On 70B scale, fidelity reaches 92–94% with F1 scores of 62–63%.
AI.conclusion: Token-level selectivity is critical for faithful generation: coarse, global contrastive rules suppress but per-token, signal-driven intervention enables retrieval-memory alignment and higher fidelity across model scales.

title: What Objects Enable, Not What They Are: Functional Latent Spaces for Affordance Reasoning
comment: Code, videos, and data available at: https://A4Dance-reasoning.github.io
summary: Existing robot planning systems rely on appearance-based reasoning, where visual observations are encoded into latent spaces organized around object appearances (e.g., recognizing a "cart" based on how it looks). However, planning requires reasoning about task-relevant functionalities of objects (e.g., whether an object is "movable"), which appearance-based latent spaces do not capture. As a result, existing approaches struggle to generalize to novel robot-object interactions. We address this limited generalizability through affordance reasoning, enabling planning based on task-relevant object functionalities instead of appearance alone. We introduce A4D, which maps visual observations into a shared latent space structured around affordances (e.g., "movable"). By projecting visual observations into this functional latent space and measuring their proximity to affordances, A4D infers functionalities relevant to the observed object. Furthermore, we introduce an affordance discovery mechanism that expands the latent space to handle unseen scenarios where existing affordances are insufficient. A4D uses proximity in the functional latent space to quantify uncertainty in affordance inference and selectively triggers affordance discovery. We evaluate A4D across several planning tasks involving diverse and unseen affordances. A4D achieves 94% inference accuracy on existing affordances outperforming state-of-the-art approaches by over 15% points, improves new-affordance inference accuracy from 70% to over 90% with fewer than 10% of the original training data, and enables 100x faster inference. Code, videos, and data available at: https://A4Dance-reasoning.github.io.

AI.tldr: A4D introduces an affordance-based latent space for robot-object reasoning, enabling planning and discovery of unseen affordances, achieving high accuracy and fast inference; code/data online.
AI.motivation: Appearance-based latent spaces for planning fail to capture task-relevant functionalities, hindering generalization to novel robot-object interactions; need functional reasoning about affordances.
AI.method: Build a shared functional latent space structured around affordances; project observations into this space and measure proximity to affordances to infer object functionalities; include an affordance discovery mechanism to expand the latent space for unseen scenarios; quantify uncertainty via proximity and trigger discovery as needed.
AI.result: Achieves 94% inference accuracy on existing affordances (>5% point over SOTA); new-affordance inference accuracy rises from 70% to >90% with less than 10% of training data; inference is 100x faster.
AI.conclusion: A4D enables planning based on task-relevant affordances with improved generalization and data efficiency; scalable via affordance discovery; code, videos, and data available online.

title: Attitude-Aided Linear Calibration of Triaxial Accelerometers
summary: Triaxial MEMS accelerometers are widely used for inertial sensing, navigation, and sensor fusion, but existing calibration methods often rely on costly reference setups or nonlinear iterative optimization, limiting their efficiency and applicability to low-cost or self-calibrating systems. We present attitude-aided linear accelerometer calibration (ALAC), a method that operates on any platform providing orientation information, such as turntables, robotic arms, or inertial measurement units. ALAC constructs a combined error matrix (CEM) to represent sensor errors in a unified calibration model and enables linear least-squares estimation. The bias and gravity vector are jointly estimated, implicitly accounting for platform misalignment, and matrix decomposition of the CEM recovers scale, non-orthogonality, and alignment rotation parameters. Under static gravity, calibration is formulated as a constrained homogeneous least-squares (CHLS) problem and solved in closed form using standard linear algebra. Only five arbitrarily oriented measurements are required, and a recursive extension supports online or in-field calibration. Experiments on a stationary robot-mounted accelerometer and a quasi-static public IMU trajectory show that ALAC, in both offline and online modes, outperforms reference-based and online baselines in accuracy and robustness to sensor noise. On the same dataset, it matches iterative self-calibration under filtered conditions and surpasses all evaluated baselines on raw measurements. These results demonstrate a robust and practical calibration scheme for MEMS-based inertial platforms, especially low-cost IMUs and online calibration scenarios.

AI.tldr: ALAC uses attitude information to calibrate MEMS accelerometers via a combined error matrix, enabling a closed-form CHLS solution with only five orientations and online extension, yielding high accuracy on low-cost IMUs.
AI.motivation: Conventional MEMS accelerometer calibration relies on costly reference setups or nonlinear optimization, limiting efficiency and applicability for low-cost or self-calibrating systems.
AI.method: Build a Combined Error Matrix (CEM) to capture sensor errors; jointly estimate bias and gravity vector; decompose CEM to recover scale, non-orthogonality, and alignment rotations; under static gravity, formulate a constrained homogeneous LS (CHLS) problem with a closed-form solution; require five arbitrarily oriented measurements; extend recursively for online calibration.
AI.result: Experiments on a stationary robot-mounted accelerometer and a quasi-static IMU trajectory show ALAC outperforms reference-based and online baselines in accuracy and noise robustness; matches iterative self-calibration under filtered conditions; surpasses all baselines on raw measurements.
AI.conclusion: ALAC provides a robust, practical calibration framework for MEMS-based inertial platforms, enabling efficient offline and online calibration with minimal measurements, particularly benefiting low-cost IMUs.

title: Continual Learning Bench: Evaluating Frontier AI Systems in Real-World Stateful Environments
summary: Continual learning, the ability of AI systems to improve through sequential experience, has attracted substantial interest, but no high-quality benchmark exists to evaluate it. We introduce Continual Learning Bench (CL-Bench), the first difficult, expert-validated benchmark designed to measure whether LLM-based systems genuinely improve with experience. CL-Bench spans six diverse domains (software engineering, signal processing, disease outbreak forecasting, database querying, strategic game-playing, and demand forecasting), each validated by domain experts and designed so that tasks share a learnable latent structure (codebase layout, disease outbreak dynamics, opponent strategies) that a stateful system can discover online but a stateless one cannot. We evaluate frontier models across several agent architectures, from naive in-context learning (ICL) to dedicated memory systems, introducing a gain metric to isolate learning from prior capabilities. We find that these systems leave headroom for improved continual learning: agents frequently overfit to immediate observations or fail to reuse knowledge across instances, and dedicated memory systems do not fix this -- in fact, naive ICL outperforms systems dedicated to memory management. CL-Bench is the first benchmark to evaluate continual learning across diverse real-world domains with expert-validated tasks and isolate online learning from underlying model capability, showing a need for better continual learning systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces CL-Bench, a six-domain, expert-validated benchmark to measure genuine online continual learning in LLM-based systems, using a gain metric and diverse architectures; finds headroom for improvement and that memory-focused designs do not automatically enable continual learning.
AI.motivation: No high-quality benchmark exists to evaluate continual learning in LLM-based systems; need to isolate online learning from static capabilities and compare learnability across domains with expert-validated tasks.
AI.method: Create CL-Bench with tasks spanning software engineering, signal processing, disease outbreak forecasting, database querying, strategic game-playing, and demand forecasting; embed a latent learnable structure (e.g., codebase layout, outbreak dynamics, opponent strategies); validate tasks with domain experts; evaluate architectures from naive in-context learning to memory-based systems; introduce a gain metric to isolate learning.
AI.result: Tasks reveal headroom for continual learning; agents often overfit to immediate observations or fail to reuse knowledge; dedicated memory systems do not fix this; naive ICL can outperform memory-focused systems; CL-Bench isolates online learning from underlying model capability and highlights need for better continual learning methods.
AI.conclusion: CL-Bench is the first benchmark to evaluate continual learning across diverse real-world domains with expert-validated tasks; it isolates online learning from model capability and shows significant room for improvement, indicating the need for more effective continual learning approaches.

title: Do More Agents Help? Controlled and Protocol-Aligned Evaluation of LLM Agent Workflows
summary: Does adding more agents help an LLM workflow once compared systems share the same benchmark loader, tool access, answer contract, usage accounting, and trajectory logging? We introduce BenchAgent, an evaluation framework that places single-agent, fixed multi-agent (MAS), and evolving MAS workflows under one normalized execution and logging protocol. BenchAgent evaluates these substrate-internal workflows across ten reasoning, coding, and tool-use benchmarks with GPT-4.1, and separately reports a Protocol-Aligned External (PAE) GAIA study of a runtime-generated workflow. Under SI conditions, at most one of six tested MAS exceeds the matched single-agent anchor on benchmark-balanced average accuracy: EvoAgent lies within the Wilson one-run guidance, while the remaining five trail by 2.56-11.29 points and occupy more expensive accuracy-cost trade-offs. On the PAE GAIA snapshot, a Claude-Code-style runtime workflow reaches 66.72% overall and 69.23% on Level 3, more than 20 points above the strongest non-Claude baseline, Jarvis, a fixed MAS.

AI.tldr: BenchAgent evaluates single-agent, fixed MAS, and evolving MAS workflows under a unified execution/logging protocol across 10 benchmarks; results show MAS rarely outperform a matched single-agent, with EvoAgent near the Wilson bound and others lagging by 2.56–11.29 points at higher cost. A Claude-Code runtime GAIA workflow achieves strong gains over a top non-Claude baseline.
AI.motivation: Assess whether adding agents to LLM workflows yields performance gains when substrate is normalized (benchmark loader, tools, contracts, accounting, and logging), and characterize trade-offs with cost across abundant tasks.
