AI.method: Normalize execution/logging; compare single-agent, fixed MAS, evolving MAS workflows; evaluate across ten reasoning, coding, and tool-use benchmarks using GPT-4.1; include a Protocol-Aligned External (PAE) GAIA study of a runtime-generated workflow; analyze under SI conditions; use Wilson one-run guidance for EvoAgent.
AI.result: Under SI, at most one MAS exceeds the matched single-agent anchor on benchmark-balanced average accuracy; EvoAgent lies within Wilson one-run guidance; the other five MAS lag by 2.56–11.29 points and incur higher accuracy-cost trade-offs. In the PAE GAIA snapshot, a Claude-Code-style runtime workflow reaches 66.72% overall and 69.23% on Level 3, >20 points above the strongest non-Claude baseline (Jarvis, a fixed MAS).
AI.conclusion: Adding more agents under a unified protocol yields limited gains; evolving MAS offers marginal improvement at best, while Claude-Code-like runtime workflows can deliver substantial gains in external evaluation. The framework enables fair comparisons but results depend on models, tasks, and cost considerations.

title: Balancing Image Compression and Generation with Bootstrapped Tokenization
summary: Despite progress in image tokenization, standard methods encode redundant information by mixing all granularities within each token, thus redundancy persists between tokens. The mix of information of different granularity also complicates the training of generators. This paper introduces SelfBootTok, a method that resolves this by cleanly decomposing information into global and local token groups. Through self-bootstrapped learning, the model predicts local details exclusively from global tokens, shifting the burden of visual details from the generator to the tokenizer. Consequently, our generator is far more efficient, requiring only global tokens and reducing computation by approximately 40%, while delivering superior reconstruction and generation. Moreover, this paradigm scales elegantly: by leveraging more data or parameters to self-supervise local representation learning, SelfBootTok achieves a new state-of-the-art gFID score of 1.56 using only 64 tokens.

AI.tldr: SelfBootTok cleanly separates global and local information in image tokens, enabling the generator to use only global tokens while the tokenizer handles local details. This reduces computation by ~40% and yields better reconstruction/generation, achieving a new state-of-the-art gFID of 1.56 with only 64 tokens.
AI.motivation: Reduce redundancy and training difficulty in image tokenizers that mix multiple granularities within tokens, which leads to inefficiencies and burdens on the generator.
AI.method: Decompose token information into distinct global and local groups; employ self-bootstrapped learning where local details are predicted exclusively from global tokens; shift visual detail modeling from the generator to the tokenizer; train to reconstruct/generate using global tokens only.
AI.result: Significantly more efficient generator (~40% less computation) with improved reconstruction and generation quality; achieves state-of-the-art gFID of 1.56 using 64 tokens.
AI.conclusion: The approach scales with more data or parameters to improve local representation learning, establishing SelfBootTok as a scalable paradigm for tokenized image generation and suggesting broader applicability of clean granularity separation in tokenizers.

title: Beyond Output Matching: Preserving Internal Geometry in NVFP4 LLM Distillatio
summary: Demand for low-precision inference, including NVFP4-based approaches, has grown as large language models are increasingly deployed in latency and cost constrained production environments. Quantization-aware distillation (QAD) helps recover accuracy lost under low bit quantization by training a quantized student to match the output distribution of a frozen higher precision teacher via a KL-divergence loss. In this work, we first provide a representation level diagnosis of QAD: output matching alone can mask internal degradation, because many intermediate activation geometries can yield similar teacher-aligned logits. Using CKA, we show that KL-only QAD can reduce layerwise representational similarity relative to the BF16 teacher, with especially severe drift in RL-post-trained models. This drift correlates with downstream bottlenecks on reasoning and coding tasks, suggesting that low bit recovery requires preserving internal geometry rather than matching outputs alone. Motivated by this finding, we propose \textbf{CKA-QAD}, a CKA-guided representational alignment method for NVFP4 QAD and low bit LLM accuracy recovery. The method adds a lightweight regularizer that preserves internal representational geometry during distillation by aligning layerwise Gram matrices through CKA. Across Nemotron 3 Nano and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, CKA-QAD substantially improves representational alignment and improves downstream reasoning and coding accuracy with modest training overhead. Our findings position CKA-guided representational alignment as a practical complement to output matching for quantized LLM recovery.

AI.tldr: CKA-guided representational alignment improves low-bit quantized LLM recovery beyond KL-only output matching by preserving internal geometry.
AI.motivation: Quantization-aware distillation (QAD) often emphasizes output matching (KL loss) but misses internal geometric degradation of representations, which can limit downstream reasoning and coding tasks in low-bit LLMs.
AI.method: Introduce CKA-QAD, a lightweight regularizer that aligns layerwise Gram matrices via Centered Kernel Alignment during NVFP4 QAD distillation, preserving internal representational geometry while matching outputs.
AI.result: CKA-QAD improves representational alignment and downstream reasoning and coding accuracy on LLMs (Nemotron 3 Nano and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507) with modest training overhead, reducing drift relative to BF16 teacher.
AI.conclusion: CKA-guided representational alignment complements output matching, offering a practical enhancement for recover­ing accuracy in low-bit quantized LLMs by preserving internal representations.

title: Cosine Misleads: Auxiliary Losses Reshape Vision Language Models, Not Their Latents
summary: Latent visual reasoning (LVR) inserts supervised latent tokens between perception and answer generation in vision-language models (VLMs). The field uses alignment between these latents and their visual targets, i.e., cosine similarity or mean squared error (MSE), as both the training loss and the quality metric, assuming that better alignment yields a better answer. We test this with a designed matrix of five LVR variants and find the assumption inverted: cosine alignment is negatively correlated with accuracy across all five (r=-0.94). To explain this, we introduce PRISM, a pair of inference-time diagnostics: a linear probe that asks where the answer is decodable, and a corruption test that asks whether the latent is load-bearing. The supervised latents are largely bypassed. Corrupting them shifts accuracy by at most four points. The answer is decodable downstream of the latent but not at it, and the size of this decodability gap predicts how much each variant relies on its latent under perturbation. Consistent with an Information Bottleneck reading of the loss, the auxiliary objective reshapes the language model via shared parameters rather than via the latent variable it nominally optimizes.

AI.tldr: Cosine alignment between latent tokens and visual targets (used as loss/metric) is negatively correlated with VLM accuracy (r = -0.94) across five LVR variants. PRISM diagnostics show latents are largely bypassed: perturbing latent tokens affects accuracy little; answers are decodable downstream but not from latent itself. The decodability gap predicts latent reliance. An Information Bottleneck view suggests the auxiliary objective mainly reshapes the language model parameters, not the latent variable.
AI.motivation: Challenge the common assumption that better alignment between supervised latent tokens and their visual targets improves answer quality in vision-language models; investigate where the information for answers actually resides.
AI.method: Create a matrix of five LVR variants and evaluate alignment (cosine similarity/MSE) versus accuracy. Introduce PRISM at inference time: (1) a linear probe to assess where the answer is decodable from latent representations, (2) a corruption test to determine if the latent is load-bearing. Test perturbations on latents, measure accuracy changes, and relate decodability gaps to latent reliance. Interpret results through an Information Bottleneck lens, examining whether the auxiliary objective reshapes the LM via shared parameters rather than the latent variable itself.
AI.result: Across five LVR variants, cosine alignment is negatively correlated with accuracy (r = -0.94). Corrupting supervised latents shifts accuracy by at most four points, indicating limited latent dependence. The answer is decodable downstream of the latent but not at the latent itself; the size of the decodability gap predicts how much each variant relies on its latent under perturbation. These findings align with an Information Bottleneck view: the auxiliary objective primarily reshapes the language model via shared parameters rather than the latent variable it nominally optimizes.
AI.conclusion: The assumed benefit of tighter latent-target alignment is not supported; inference-time diagnostics reveal the latents are largely bypassed. The auxiliary objective appears to influence the LM parameters more than the latent, consistent with an Information Bottleneck interpretation.

title: Representation Learning Enables Scalable Multitask Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Scaling reinforcement learning (RL) to diverse multitask settings remains a central challenge. While recent advances in model-based RL achieve strong performance, they rely on planning and complex training pipelines, making it unclear which components are essential for scalability. We revisit this question and argue that the primary driver of scalable multitask RL is not model-based control, but \emph{representation learning}. In particular, we show that combining predictive, model-based representations with high-capacity value function approximation is sufficient to achieve strong performance, even without planning. We evaluate a simple model-free algorithm, MR.Q, coupled with auxiliary predictive objectives into a scalable actor-critic architecture. This approach outperforms a recent world-model-based method and a range of deep RL baselines across a diverse suite of multitask continuous control tasks, while significantly reducing computational overhead and improving wall-clock efficiency. We observe consistent improvements with increased model capacity and show through ablations that predictive representation learning is critical for performance.

AI.tldr: Predictive, model-based representations with high-capacity value-function in a model-free MR.Q architecture enable scalable multitask RL without planning, outperforming world-model baselines and improving efficiency.
AI.motivation: Identify what enables scalable multitask RL and whether representation learning alone suffices, challenging the emphasis on model-based planning.
AI.method: Implement MR.Q with auxiliary predictive objectives within a scalable actor-critic framework; evaluate on multitask continuous-control tasks; compare against a world-model-based method and standard baselines; perform ablations on model capacity to assess the role of predictive representations.
AI.result: MR.Q with predictive objectives achieves strong performance across diverse multitask tasks, surpassing a recent world-model-based method and baselines; achieves lower computational overhead and faster wall-clock time; performance improves with larger model capacity; ablations indicate predictive representation learning is critical for performance.
AI.conclusion: Predictive representation learning is a key driver of scalable multitask RL. Model-based planning is not strictly necessary when predictive representations are combined with high-capacity value-function approximation; this approach yields scalable performance with reduced compute.

title: AdaMEM: Test-Time Adaptive Memory for Language Agents
summary: A central challenge for language agents is utilizing past experience to adapt to dynamic test-time conditions. While recent work demonstrates the promise of agentic memory mechanisms, most systems restrict retrieval to episode initiation. Consequently, agents are forced to rely on static guidance that becomes increasingly misaligned as long-horizon tasks unfold. To address this rigidity, we propose the Adaptive Memory Agent (AdaMEM), a novel framework for agent test-time adaptation. Without updating model parameters online, AdaMEM adapts agent behavior via a hybrid memory architecture: it maintains a long-term trajectory memory of raw experiences collected offline while generating dynamic short-term strategy memory on-the-fly to guide decision-making. This mechanism enables the trade-off between token efficiency and adaptability across varying inference-time compute levels. Empirically, AdaMEM significantly outperforms static memory baselines, achieving relative gains of up to 13% on ALFWorld and 11% on WebShop, with consistent leading performance extending to agentic search on HotpotQA. To further enhance this adaptation, we develop STEP-MFT, a Step-wise Memory Fine-Tuning technique that trains the policy to synthesize high-quality strategies from retrieved experiences, yielding additional performance gains. Our work establishes a new scaling dimension for agentic memory, supporting continuous reasoning and self-evolution post-deployment in real-world environments. Our code is available at https://github.com/yunx-z/AdaMEM.

AI.tldr: AdaMEM introduces a hybrid long-term trajectory memory and short-term strategy memory for agentic adaptation at test time, enabling continuous reasoning without online parameter updates and achieving strong gains across ALFWorld, WebShop, and HotpotQA. It is complemented by STEP-MFT, which tunes policies to synthesize strategies from retrieved experiences. Code is released.
AI.motivation: To overcome the rigidity of episode-initiation memory retrieval in language agents and to enable dynamic adaptation to long-horizon tasks without online parameter updates.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-memory architecture: a long-term offline-accumulated trajectory memory for raw experiences, plus a dynamically generated short-term strategy memory used to guide decisions; introduces STEP-MFT (Step-wise Memory Fine-Tuning) to train the policy to synthesize high-quality strategies from retrieved experiences; analyzes trade-offs between token efficiency and adaptability across varying inference compute.
AI.result: AdaMEM yields relative improvements of up to 13% on ALFWorld and 11% on WebShop over static-memory baselines; shows consistent gains in agentic search on HotpotQA; STEP-MFT provides additional performance improvements.
AI.conclusion: This work defines a new scaling dimension for agentic memory, enabling continuous reasoning and self-evolution post-deployment in real environments, with open-source code available.

title: DRIFT: A Residual Flow Adapter for Decoding Continuous Outputs in Vision-Language Models
summary: Many modern vision-language models (VLMs) build on autoregressive decoding of discrete tokens. While text-based output interfaces enable scalable pretraining and strong zero-shot generalization across diverse tasks, they are poorly suited for problems that require precise continuous outputs, such as localizing temporal boundaries of events or generating robotic control actions. To address this challenge, we propose DRIFT, a general framework for adapting pretrained VLMs to continuous decoding tasks. DRIFT combines a base predictor, which provides a coarse estimate of the target output, with a generative refinement module based on flow matching that iteratively improves the prediction. This residual formulation transforms the generative modeling problem from learning a global output distribution to modeling a localized residual distribution around a strong prior, substantially simplifying optimization. We evaluate DRIFT on both perception and planning tasks, including visual grounding and robotic control. Across multiple tasks and architectures spanning MLLMs, VLAs, and WAMs, DRIFT consistently outperforms a strong set of regression- and generative-based solutions.

AI.tldr: DRIFT introduces a residual refinement framework to adapt pretrained vision-language models to continuous decoding tasks by coupling a base predictor with a flow-matching refinement module, modeling a local residual around a strong prior; it improves continuous outputs across perception and planning tasks.
AI.motivation: Discrete, autoregressive outputs from text-based VLMs struggle with precise continuous outputs (e.g., temporal localization, robotic control). A method to leverage pretrained VLMs for continuous decoding is needed.
AI.method: A base predictor yields a coarse target; a generative refinement module based on flow matching iteratively improves the prediction. This residual formulation shifts modeling from a global output distribution to a localized residual around a strong prior, simplifying optimization. Applicable across architectures (MLLMs, VLAs, WAMs) and tasks (perception and planning).
AI.result: DRIFT consistently outperforms strong regression- and generative-based baselines across multiple tasks and architectures, including visual grounding and robotic control.
AI.conclusion: DRIFT is a general, effective framework for converting pretrained VLMs to continuous decoders via a residual, flow-matching refinement, enabling more accurate continuous outputs with simplified optimization.

title: Ensuring Interaction Safety in Multitask Exoskeleton Control: A Simulation-Trained Variable Impedance Framework
summary: Wearable exoskeletons can augment human phys ical capabilities during complex activities. However, ensuring adaptation across diverse tasks while guaranteeing interaction safety remains a critical challenge. To address this, a simulation trained variable impedance control approach with stability guarantees is proposed. First, a simulation-based human exoskeleton motion data generation pipeline is established, utilizing Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to synthesize human muscle activations while the exoskeleton provides direct compensation for human biological joint torques. Subsequently, the generated dataset is used to train a dual modality policy that fuses semantic instructions with proprioceptive history, enabling the prediction of reference trajectories and variable impedance gains for nine different motion tasks. To guarantee safety, the network outputs are constrained by a stability criterion derived from Lyapunov stability theory, which bounds stiffness variations to ensure the asymptotic stability of the coupled system. Experimental results indicate that the proposed framework reduces metabolic cost in real-world scenarios com pared with standard baseline methods. These findings suggest the feasibility of the proposed framework for safe, multitask exoskeleton control.

AI.tldr: A simulation-driven, Lyapunov-stable, variable-impedance control framework for exoskeletons that learns reference trajectories and impedance gains across nine tasks using a PPO-generated dataset.
AI.motivation: To achieve safe, adaptable control of wearable exoskeletons across diverse tasks while ensuring interaction safety.
AI.method: 1) Generate human-exoskeleton interaction data with PPO-synthesized muscle activations and exoskeleton torque compensation. 2) Train a dual-modality policy that combines semantic instructions and proprioceptive history to predict reference trajectories and variable impedance gains for nine tasks. 3) Impose a Lyapunov-based stability constraint to bound stiffness changes and guarantee asymptotic stability of the coupled system.
AI.result: Experimentally, the framework reduces metabolic cost relative to baseline methods in real-world scenarios, indicating improved efficiency and safety for multitask exoskeleton control.
AI.conclusion: The approach is feasible for safe, multitask exoskeleton control and demonstrates a viable path toward adaptive, stable assistance across tasks.

title: Physics-Guided Deep Unfolding for Blind Cross-Sensor Spectral Super-Resolution via Learning the Spectral Transformation Function
summary: Hyperspectral imaging provides rich spectral information for quantitative remote sensing, yet hyperspectral sensors remain costly and thus unavailable in many UAV deployments. Spectral super-resolution (SSR) seeks to reconstruct hyperspectral images (HSIs) from multispectral images (MSIs). Most existing SSR methods assume a fixed and known spectral response function (SRF) and are therefore limited to single-sensor settings. In practical cross-sensor scenarios, the spectral degradation from HSI to MSI is unknown and varies with sensor characteristics and scene content, which renders HSI reconstruction ill-posed. This paper proposes a physics-guided deep unfolding network, termed PGU-Net, to address blind cross-sensor SSR by jointly estimating the HSI and a learnable spectral transformation function (STF). PGU-Net unrolls an alternating optimization procedure into an end-to-end trainable architecture with stages, where each stage sequentially updates the HSI and the STF. Both modules combine learnable proximal networks with differentiable closed-form solvers, enabling physical interpretability while retaining strong representation capacity. Experiments on benchmark datasets (CAVE and NTIRE 2022) with multiple SRFs demonstrate accurate recovery of the STF (degradation operator) and improved reconstruction performance over state-of-the-art SSR methods. Furthermore, evaluations on a real UAV cross-sensor dataset (Headwall Nano HSI and DJI P4 Multispectral MSI) verify the effectiveness and robustness of PGU-Net under truly blind conditions, and suggest that the estimated STF may exhibit land-cover-related differences.

AI.tldr: Physics-guided deep unfolding network for blind cross-sensor spectral super-resolution (PGU-Net) that jointly estimates HSI and a learnable STF, achieving state-of-the-art results and robust UAV cross-sensor performance.
AI.motivation: Cross-sensor SSR is ill-posed because HSI-to-MSI degradation is unknown and sensor-dependent; a method that simultaneously estimates the HSI and a learnable spectral transformation function (STF) improves robustness and physical interpretability.
AI.method: Unfolds an alternating optimization into a multi-stage network. Two modules estimate the HSI and the STF, respectively, combining learnable proximal networks with differentiable closed-form solvers. The STF is learnable, enabling blind degradation estimation within an end-to-end framework (PGU-Net).
AI.result: Achieves accurate STF recovery and improved SSR over state-of-the-art methods on CAVE and NTIRE 2022 across multiple SRFs; validated on real UAV cross-sensor data (Headwall Nano HSI and DJI P4 MSI), showing robustness under blind conditions and suggesting land-cover-related STF variations.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable, interpretable, physics-guided SSR framework for blind cross-sensor deployment; STF estimation captures sensor/scene dependencies and enhances practical cross-sensor HSI reconstruction.

title: Autoregressive Diffusion World Models for Off-Policy Evaluation of LLM Agents
summary: Evaluating large language model (LLM) agents in multi-turn interactive environments is expensive and risky, as it requires online environment interaction. We propose ADWM (Autoregressive Diffusion World Model), an evaluation framework that estimates the performance of a new LLM agent policy purely from pre-collected trajectories. The core idea is to learn a latent diffusion world model that simulates how the environment responds to the evaluation policy, without ever executing it in the real environment. Existing diffusion-based OPE methods guide full trajectories in a single pass by jointly diffusing states and actions, an assumption that breaks down for LLM agents whose actions are discrete text that must be sampled from the policy after observing the environment. Unlike autoregressive world models that suffer from compounding errors, ADWM models each transition as an independent denoising process, enabling reliable step-by-step rollouts where the world model and agent alternate in causal order. Crucially, the LLM agent under evaluation directly guides the diffusion generation at each step via a policy-conditioned score function, ensuring that simulated trajectories accurately reflect its decision-making patterns. Empirically, ADWM achieves accurate value estimates and evaluation reliability across diverse multi-turn agent tasks, demonstrating its promise as a practical framework for offline LLM agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: ADWM enables offline evaluation of new LLM agent policies by learning a latent diffusion world model from pre-collected trajectories and using the policy-conditioned diffusion to generate simulated, step-by-step interactions without real-world execution.
AI.motivation: To reduce cost and risk of evaluating LLM agents in interactive environments, and to enable offline, reliable evaluation despite discrete text actions and potential compounding errors in autoregressive models.
AI.method: Train a latent diffusion world model that predicts environment transitions; model each step as an independent denoising process; use the evaluation policy to condition the score function at each step; run stepwise rollouts alternating between world model and agent in causal order; avoid joint diffusion of states and actions; rely on pre-collected trajectories.
AI.result: The framework yields accurate value estimates and reliable evaluation across diverse multi-turn tasks.
AI.conclusion: ADWM offers a practical offline evaluation framework for LLM agents, mitigating online interaction costs and enabling more reliable benchmarking.

title: RiskFlow: Fast and Faithful Safety-Critical Traffic Scenario Generation
summary: Safety-critical traffic scenario generation is essential for evaluating autonomous driving systems under rare but high-risk interactions. Existing diffusion-based methods offer strong controllability in closed-loop generation, but their iterative denoising process is computationally expensive and may accumulate sampling and guidance errors over long rollouts, causing unrealistic motion artifacts such as jitter, abnormal acceleration, and off-road behavior. To address these issues, we propose RiskFlow, a closed-loop safety-critical multi-agent traffic generation framework that formulates future trajectory generation as transport in the action space. Instead of relying on iterative denoising, RiskFlow learns an average velocity field over a finite interval to transform Gaussian action sequences into future acceleration and yaw-rate commands with a single forward pass, using a JVP-based objective for efficient and stable training. At test time, RiskFlow applies output-space guidance to the generated actions, steering selected critical agents toward risky interactions while regularizing off-road behavior, and reconstructs physically feasible trajectories through vehicle dynamics. Experiments on nuScenes with tbsim closed-loop evaluation show that RiskFlow achieves a strong adversariality-realism trade-off across multi-agent and long-horizon settings. Compared with representative baselines, RiskFlow consistently improves realism while maintaining competitive safety-critical generation capability, and substantially reduces inference time for evaluation.

AI.tldr: One-shot, action-space transport model for closed-loop multi-agent traffic generation (RiskFlow) that replaces diffusion with a single forward pass to generate acceleration and yaw-rate, improving realism and reducing inference time.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based, iterative methods yield realistic trajectories but are computationally heavy and accumulate errors in long-horizon, unsafe scenarios; need efficient, stable generator for safety-critical evaluation.
AI.method: Train a model that learns an average velocity field over a finite horizon to transform Gaussian action sequences into vehicle commands; uses Jacobian-vector-product (JVP) objective to enable efficient training; at test time apply output-space guidance to steer critical agents toward risky interactions while regularizing off-road, and reconstruct trajectories with vehicle dynamics.
AI.result: On nuScenes with tbsim closed-loop evaluation, RiskFlow achieves strong adversariality-realism trade-offs in multi-agent, long-horizon settings; improves realism vs baselines while preserving safety-critical generation; significantly reduces inference time.
AI.conclusion: RiskFlow delivers a computationally efficient, stable closed-loop traffic generator enabling realistic, adversarial scenarios for autonomous vehicle evaluation with favorable safety-performance balance.

title: Seeing Time: Benchmarking Chronological Reasoning and Shortcut Biases in Vision-Language Models
summary: Recent advancements in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have significantly enhanced their ability to interpret complex visual semantics, yet their capacity for chronological reasoning remains under-explored. In this paper, we introduce a novel benchmark specifically designed to evaluate how VLMs perceive and reason about chronological information within and across images. Unlike existing video-based benchmarks that focus on frame sequencing, our work delves into the underlying logic of chronological judgment and the expansion toward multimodal integration. To facilitate this, we construct three specialized datasets: one containing visually similar objects spanning long historical durations, another categorized by diverse event and object types, and a third pairing images with time-sensitive news text for cross-modal alignment. Through extensive experiments, we analyze whether models exhibit performance disparities across categories and, crucially, explore whether they rely on ``incorrect shortcuts'', such as image color rather than genuine chronological features. Our results reveal that while VLMs show promise, they frequently exploit superficial cues like grayscale versus color filters to bypass authentic chronological reasoning. By providing these high-quality datasets and a rigorous evaluation framework, we offer a diagnostic tool to identify current limitations and guide the development of more robust, logically grounded multimodal models. The source code is shown in https://github.com/LuoRenqiang/ChronoVision.

AI.tldr: Introduces ChronoVision, a benchmark suite to evaluate chronological reasoning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) across three datasets, revealing reliance on superficial cues and providing a diagnostic framework with open-source code.
AI.motivation: VLMs excel at static visual semantics but struggle with temporal/chronological reasoning. Existing video-based benchmarks fail to capture cross-modal, logical chronological understanding; a robust diagnostic tool is needed to guide development of truly temporally grounded multimodal models.
AI.method: Constructs three datasets: (1) visually similar objects spanning long historical durations; (2) diverse event/object-type categories; (3) image-text pairs with time-sensitive news for cross-modal alignment; conducts extensive experiments to compare category performance and to detect shortcuts (e.g., color vs grayscale) that bypass true chronological reasoning.
AI.result: VLMs show limited genuine chronological reasoning and often rely on superficial cues to bypass temporal logic; the benchmark exposes these weaknesses and serves as a diagnostic tool; source code is provided.
AI.conclusion: Datasets and the evaluation framework offer a valuable diagnostic resource to identify current limitations and steer the development of more robust, logically grounded multimodal models that reason about chronology rather than superficial cues.

title: ExpSpeech-Net: Multimodal Fusion of Expression and Speech for Deepfake Detection
summary: Deepfake videos are increasingly challenging the credibility of online content. Many existing detection methodology relies on complex, resource-intensive models, which limit their practical use. The study introduces the ExpSpeech-Net deepfake detection (SqN-R-DFD) model, which utilizes SqueezeNet and RNN (Recurrent Neural Network) as its backbone, providing a lightweight and efficient deepfake detection framework that simultaneously analyzes facial expressions and speech patterns. The approach incorporates advanced feature extraction, such as ISLBT-based features for image and MPNCC for signals, along with a smart feature-selection strategy using SASMA (Sandpiper-Assisted Slime Mould Algorithm), ensuring optimal and balanced input to the detection models. By combining SqueezeNet and an RNN, subtle inconsistencies in deepfake videos are captured effectively. The framework achieves 94.5% accuracy, precision of 99.3%, and F-measure of 96.8%, outperforming conventional methods. This demonstrates that integrating multiple modalities with intelligent preprocessing and feature selection enables practical, real-time deepfake detection suitable for everyday applications.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, multi-modal deepfake detector (ExpSpeech-Net: SqN-R-DFD) using SqueezeNet + RNN with ISLBT image features and MPNCC audio features, plus SASMA feature selection, achieving high performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of resource-intensive deepfake detectors and enable practical, real-time detection using lightweight architectures and intelligent preprocessing.
AI.method: Proposes ExpSpeech-Net (SqN-R-DFD) with a SqueezeNet backbone coupled with an RNN. Feature extraction includes ISLBT-based image features and MPNCC-based audio signals. A Sandpiper-Assisted Slime Mould Algorithm (SASMA) selects and balances features for the model.
AI.result: Achieves 94.5% accuracy, 99.3% precision, and 96.8% F-measure, outperforming conventional methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multi-modal data with efficient preprocessing and feature selection yields practical real-time deepfake detection suitable for everyday use.

title: CLaaS: Continual learning as a service for sample efficient online learning
summary: Deployed large language model agents must adapt to distribution shift in dynamic environments. Ideally, adaptation can be performed from accumulated agent experiences and retain prior capabilities while transferring to future tasks. However, agent actions and environmental transitions can only be sampled once per scenario, as real-world environments cannot be trivially reset. To this end, we investigate an experiential and online continual learning setting in which agents learn from a stream of scenarios. We propose continual learning as-a-service (CLaaS), a system which enables agents to improve during deployment, abstracted behind a chat API. To increase sample efficiency, CLaaS stores rollouts in an experience replay buffer for gradient reuse during asynchronous training. We evaluate CLaaS on an adversarial task, demonstrating that parametric updates lead to superior forward transfer and less forgetting than in-context learning, with replay being a critical choice for sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: Proposes CLaaS, a deployment-time continual learning framework for LLM agents in dynamic environments, leveraging experience replay to reuse past rollouts; shows parametric updates outperform in-context learning with replay crucial for sample efficiency on an adversarial task.
AI.motivation: Agents deployed in dynamic environments face distribution shift and limited ability to reset environments; there is a need to learn continuously from stream experiences while preserving prior capabilities and transferring to future tasks.
AI.method: Introduce continual learning as-a-service (CLaaS), a system accessible via a chat API; collects agent rollouts into an experience replay buffer and performs asynchronous gradient updates (parametric) to adapt online.
AI.result: In experiments on an adversarial task, parametric updates achieved better forward transfer and reduced forgetting compared with in-context learning; replay was essential for improving sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: CLaaS enables deployment-time adaptation with efficient sample usage, showing that replay-based continual learning can outperform purely in-context approaches in dynamic, non-resettable environments.

title: Flow-based Policy Adaptation without Policy Updates
summary: Leveraging prior knowledge from pretrained policies, foundation models, or human operators offers an efficient alternative to learning robot skills from scratch. However, these agents often provide actions that are suboptimal, noisy, or misaligned with task-specific expert behavior. We propose GLOVES, a family of flow-based adaptation methods that correct non-expert actions by transporting them toward an expert action distribution. Rather than replacing agentic control with full autonomy, GLOVES performs selective action-level adaptation, improving task success while preserving agent intent. The learned flow also provides a natural in-distribution scoring mechanism through reverse flow evaluation. We use this signal as an intervention gate: actions that appear consistent with the expert distribution are passed through unchanged, while anomalous or out-of-distribution (OOD) actions are corrected. In this way, assistance is only provided when necessary. GLOVES requires only limited expert supervision, using a small number of demonstrations or reusable successful skill segments. By learning local expert action patterns and stitching them during execution, GLOVES provides a lightweight shared-control module for robust action adaptation across tasks and environments. Code and demos are available at ripl.github.io/GLOVES_web.

AI.tldr: GLOVES adapts non-expert actions toward expert distributions using flow-based local corrections, enabling selective, in-distribution aligned intervention with limited supervision.
AI.motivation: Leverage pretrained or human-derived policies while avoiding widespread suboptimal, noisy, or misaligned actions; enable lightweight, selective assistive control that preserves agent intent.
AI.method: Train flow-based adapters that transport actions toward an expert action distribution; use reverse-flow evaluation as an in-distribution score to gate interventions; apply local, stitchable patterns from limited demonstrations to cover multiple tasks/environments.
AI.result: Claims improved task success and robustness while preserving agent intent; introduces an in-distribution scoring mechanism to decide when to intervene; supports limited supervision and reusable skill segments; code available.
AI.conclusion: GLOVES offers a lightweight, shared-control module for robust action adaptation across tasks by selectively correcting actions with a flow-based transport toward expert behavior, using a natural gating signal.

title: Critic-Guided Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Reasoning for Reliable Mathematical Problem Solving
summary: Recent Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive reasoning abilities; but they are still susceptible to hallucinations, intermediate reasoning mistakes, and unreliable reasoning results in complex mathematical reasoning problems. In this study, we introduce a critic-based heterogeneous multi-agent approach to improve the dependability of mathematical reasoning. This framework incorporates several LLM agents of different specialties and employs a critic-driven adaptive learning system to assess and guide the reasoning process based on intermediate feedback. The system adopts a generator-validator framework, with the validator not only determining correctness but also offering critiques to guide regeneration of solutions. This allows for adaptive error correction and prevents error cascading. Our experiments on the GSM8K benchmark show that the proposed method achieves up to 13% accuracy improvement over single-shot and non-critic models. Additionally, findings suggest that heterogeneity and critique reduce the need for large models, allowing smaller models to perform on par. Ablation studies reveal the main performance gains are due to the critic-based feedback loop and not model size. In summary, the proposed approach showcases the benefits of combining heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration and critique to obtain reliable and interpretable reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: A critic-driven heterogeneous multi-agent framework improves mathematical reasoning in LLMs via generator-validator with intermediate critiques; achieves 13% GSM8K accuracy gain over non-critic models; heterogeneity and critique reduce reliance on large models; critical feedback loop is the main performance driver.
AI.motivation: LLMs frequently produce hallucinations and incorrect intermediate steps in mathematical reasoning. There is a need for dependable, interpretable reasoning. Leveraging multiple specialized agents and a critic to guide regeneration can mitigate error propagation.
AI.method: Employs a generator-validator framework with multiple heterogeneous LLM agents. A critic-based adaptive learning system provides intermediate feedback and critiques to guide solution regeneration. The validator not only checks correctness but also offers critiques to steer subsequent reasoning, enabling adaptive error correction and preventing error cascades. Empirical evaluation on GSM8K with ablation studies.
AI.result: Reported up to 13% accuracy improvement over single-shot and non-critic baselines. Heterogeneity and critique enable smaller models to perform comparably to larger ones. Ablations indicate the main gains stem from the critic-based feedback loop rather than model size.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the value of combining heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration with critique to achieve more reliable and interpretable mathematical reasoning; the critic-guided loop appears essential for performance gains, suggesting directions for scalable, robust reasoning systems.

title: Imagine Before You Predict: Interleaved Latent Visual Reasoning for Video Event Prediction
summary: Video event prediction (VEP) requires models to infer unobserved future states from partial video evidence. Existing video MLLMs usually verbalize intermediate future reasoning in text space: once visual evidence is verbalized, fine-grained motion, geometry, and interaction cues can be lost, leading to plausible but visually ungrounded hallucinations. We introduce Future-L1, an interleaved latent visual reasoning framework that lets an MLLM alternate between language tokens and continuous latent visual spans during autoregressive decoding. To train this capability, we construct Future-L1-50K by selecting examples where future visual hints help prediction and align latent states to future-frame embeddings, then further optimize sampled latent trajectories with LA-DAPO, a latent-aware RL objective with outcome-contrastive and temporal-diversity rewards. Future-L1 achieves new state-of-the-art results on both benchmarks: on FutureBench, it improves Qwen3-VL-8B from 61.0 to 85.4 and exceeds the previous best Video-CoE by 10.4 points; on TwiFF-Bench, it improves the average score from 2.44 to 3.04. These results suggest that future-oriented video reasoning benefits from preserving intermediate visual semantics in latent space rather than translating every reasoning step into text.

AI.tldr: Future-L1 introduces interleaved latent visual reasoning for video event prediction by alternating language tokens and continuous latent visual spans during autoregressive decoding; trained on a specialized Future-L1-50K dataset aligned with future-frame embeddings and optimized with latent-aware RL (LA-DAPO) to guide latent trajectories, achieving state-of-the-art on FutureBench and TwiFF-Bench.
AI.motivation: Conventional video MLLMs verbalize intermediate steps, risking loss of fine-grained visual cues and visually grounded predictions, leading to plausible yet ungrounded hallucinations. Preserving latent visual semantics could improve grounding and predictive accuracy.
AI.method: Use interleaved latent visual spans and language tokens during decoding. Build Future-L1-50K by selecting examples where future visual hints aid prediction and align latent states to future-frame embeddings. Further optimize latent trajectories with LA-DAPO, a latent-aware RL objective with outcome-contrastive and temporal-diversity rewards.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on FutureBench: Qwen3-VL-8B from 61.0 to 85.4; ahead of Video-CoE by 10.4. On TwiFF-Bench: average score from 2.44 to 3.04.
AI.conclusion: Preserving intermediate visual semantics in latent space, rather than translating every reasoning step into text, improves future-oriented video reasoning and reduces the risk of visually ungrounded hallucinations.

title: TempoVLA: Learning Speed-Controllable Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Robot manipulation alternates between low-risk transit phases that call for fast execution and high-risk contact stages that demand slow, precise motion. Yet existing Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) only inherit a single fixed speed from training demonstrations. Prior efforts to accelerate VLAs through model compression, KV-cache reuse, or reinforcement learning only shift the policy from one fixed speed to another, and leave deceleration almost unexplored. We observe that the magnitude of each predicted action already governs how fast the robot moves, opening a direct route to controllable execution speed. We turn this observation into TempoVLA, a single VLA whose execution speed is controlled by an explicit condition. TempoVLA combines two coupled components. (1) A data-side Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation (VSTA) that re-times demonstration to any target speed by merging or splitting actions while preserving its motion semantics. (2) A model-side conditioning mechanism that feeds the speed to the policy. Statistics show that VSTA reaches the requested speed with negligible motion error. Experiments in simulation and on real-world tasks demonstrate that TempoVLA achieves flexible speed control in both directions, while VSTA additionally boosts the default $1\times$ performance via better data utilization. Furthermore, by cooperating with a large multimodal model, TempoVLA realizes dynamic speed control, accelerating through low-risk phases and decelerating for high-risk ones.

AI.tldr: TempoVLA enables explicit, bidirectional speed control for Vision-Language-Action models via data-side variable-speed augmentation and model conditioning, achieving flexible execution speeds and improved data efficiency, especially when paired with a large multimodal model.
AI.motivation: VLAs are constrained by a fixed execution speed learned from demonstrations, which hinders safe, efficient robot manipulation during low-risk and high-risk phases. A method to dynamically control speed without sacrificing semantics is needed.
AI.method: Two coupled components: (1) VSTA, a data-side Variable-Speed Trajectory Augmentation that retimes demonstrations to any target speed by merging or splitting actions while preserving motion semantics; (2) A model-side conditioning mechanism that feeds the speed to the policy, enabling the VLA to execute at the commanded speed. The approach demonstrates speed control in simulation and real robots, and synergizes with a large multimodal model for dynamic speed adaptation across risk phases.
AI.result: VSTA reaches the requested speed with negligible motion error. TempoVLA demonstrates flexible speed control in both directions. VSTA improves default 1x performance via better data utilization. With a large multimodal model, TempoVLA enables dynamic speed control, accelerating in low-risk phases and decelerating in high-risk ones.
AI.conclusion: TempoVLA provides a practical framework for controllable execution speed in VLAs, combining data augmentation and policy conditioning to achieve bidirectional speed control and improved efficiency, with potential benefits when integrated with larger multimodal systems.

title: DiG-Plan: Mitigating Early Commitment for Tool-Graph Planning via Diffusion Guidance
comment: Accepted at IJCAI-ECAI 2026. This is an author preprint; the final version will appear in the IJCAI Proceedings
summary: Generating executable tool plans requires selecting appropriate subsets from tool libraries, a combinatorial search problem with an exponentially large solution space. However, we identify a critical misalignment in predominant approaches: standard autoregressive (AR) decoding suffers from early commitment, where initial token choices rigidly constrain the search trajectory. A controlled study shows that masked denoising raises Pass@10 solution coverage from 0.320 to 0.943 over AR sampling under matched compute. Motivated by this, we propose DiG-Plan, a framework that decouples combinatorial exploration from structural refinement. DiG-Plan employs a diffusion-based proposer to generate diverse tool sets via iterative refinement, followed by an AR refiner for dependency prediction. On TaskBench, DiG-Plan improves over AR baselines by a 10% relative margin, with the largest gains on complex compositional tasks; API-Bank results show that the propose-refine-select design remains effective across domains. Code is available at https://github.com/puddingyeah/DiG-Plan.

AI.tldr: DiG-Plan decouples combinatorial exploration from structural refinement, using a diffusion-based proposer to generate diverse tool sets and an AR refiner for dependencies, addressing autoregressive early-commitment and achieving improved solution coverage and task performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive decoding in tool-plan generation suffers from early commitment, limiting search and solution coverage in large combinatorial spaces. A method that separates exploration from structural refinement can better navigate the solution space and improve success rates.
AI.method: Introduce a diffusion-based proposer to iteratively refine diverse candidate tool sets, followed by an autoregressive refiner that predicts dependencies. This propose-refine-select workflow decouples tool-set generation from plan assembly, aiming to broaden search and then enforce correctness constraints.
AI.result: Masked denoising significantly boosts Pass@10 coverage (0.320 to 0.943) compared to AR sampling under matched compute. On TaskBench, DiG-Plan yields ~10% relative improvement over AR baselines, with largest gains on complex tasks. API-Bank results indicate the design is robust across domains. Code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling exploration from refinement via a diffusion-based proposer and an AR refiner mitigates early-commitment bias in AR decoding, enabling more diverse and accurate executable tool plans and demonstrating solid gains across benchmarks and domains.

title: LiAuto-GeoX: Efficient Grounded Driving Transformer
summary: Dense 3D reconstruction has demonstrated immense potential for spatial understanding, yet its viability as a real-time, onboard representation for autonomous driving remains an open challenge. Existing large-scale visual geometry models typically require substantial computational resources and lack the long-range geometric fidelity, surround-view consistency, and real-time efficiency demanded by dynamic driving environments. To bridge this gap, we present \textbf{LiAuto-GeoX}, an efficient grounded driving transformer designed for deployable, ego-centric 3D scene understanding. Our approach begins by learning a high-capacity driving geometry model from large-scale surround-view data, utilizing sparse LiDAR priors to provide robust geometric grounding in distant, ambiguous, or structure-sparse regions. We then instantiate this capability into a highly compact 155M-parameter onboard model through a novel geometry-preserving distillation framework. This framework employs mask-guided depth-aware distillation to retain fine-grained metric structures by emphasizing geometrically informative regions, and relative-pose relational distillation to enforce cross-view spatial consistency through pose-induced geometric relations. Extensive evaluations reveal that \textbf{LiAuto-GeoX} runs at 220 FPS on KITTI while maintaining high-fidelity dense reconstruction, enabling real-time deployment. The learned geometry transfers seamlessly to downstream autonomy tasks, achieving 90.6 PDMS in trajectory prediction, 24.63 mIoU in occupancy prediction, and 47.67 IoU in future-frame prediction. These all demonstrate that efficient dense 3D reconstruction can transcend its traditional role as a perception target to serve as a scalable, foundational geometric representation for next-generation autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: LiAuto-GeoX: an efficient grounded driving transformer that distills a large-scale surround-view geometry model into a compact 155M-parameter onboard model using mask-guided depth-aware distillation and relative-pose relational distillation, achieving real-time dense 3D reconstruction (220 FPS on KITTI) and benefiting downstream autonomy tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-time, ego-centric dense 3D reconstruction is essential for autonomous driving, but existing large-scale models are compute-intensive and struggle with long-range geometric fidelity and surround-view consistency.
AI.method: Train a high-capacity driving geometry model from surround-view data with sparse LiDAR priors for grounding; distill to a compact 155M parameter onboard model via (1) mask-guided depth-aware distillation emphasizing geometrically informative regions, and (2) relative-pose relational distillation enforcing cross-view spatial consistency through pose-induced geometric relations.
AI.result: The approach runs at 220 FPS on KITTI with high-fidelity dense reconstruction; geometry transfers to downstream tasks achieving 90.6 PDMS in trajectory prediction, 24.63 mIoU in occupancy prediction, and 47.67 IoU in future-frame prediction.
AI.conclusion: Efficient dense 3D reconstruction can be a scalable, foundational geometric representation for autonomous driving, bridging perception and downstream autonomy tasks with real-time performance.

title: When AI Says It Feels
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are generally constrained from expressing feelings through human-preference alignment in post-training processes. This policy is designed using a top-down approach and may conflict with the goal of training models to exhibit human-like intelligence using human-generated texts. Here, we performed an experiment called Human-like Model eXpressions of Feeling (HMX-feel), in which LLMs were encouraged to express feelings, intentions, and self-awareness through self-rewarded reinforcement learning. We successfully enhanced these capabilities using a rubric-based self-rewarding training scheme with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). By comparing the trained models with contrastively trained models, we investigated the effects of this approach on performance across various tasks. Overall, we conducted a broad assessment from various perspectives and identified capabilities that were enhanced, degraded, or showed no significant change. The human-like-trained models showed robustness to sycophancy-inducing questions and bias in disambiguated conditions, whereas degradation in truthful question-answering capability was observed. The results of this experiment suggest the possibility of developing AI systems that can express feelings in the future, provided that appropriate measures are taken.

AI.tldr: Explores self-rewarded RL to induce LLMs to express feelings (HMX-feel) using a rubric-based GRPO scheme, comparing to contrastive training. Finds mixed effects: improvements in some human-like expressions and robustness to certain prompts, but degradation in truthful QA, suggesting potential with safeguards.
AI.motivation: To challenge conventional top-down alignment that suppresses emotional expression in LLMs and to investigate whether self-reward mechanisms can cultivate human-like expressiveness without sacrificing core capabilities; evaluate trade-offs against standard contrastive training.
AI.method: Rubric-based self-rewarding training with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to encourage LLMs to express feelings, intentions, and self-awareness. Compare trained models to contrastively trained baselines across a diverse task suite and assess performance along multiple dimensions.
AI.result: Enhanced expressivity-related capabilities in the trained models; improved robustness to sycophancy-inducing prompts and bias under disambiguated conditions; yet degradation in truthful question answering. Overall, mixed outcomes with some capabilities strengthened and others weakened; broader assessment identifies domains of improvement, degradation, and neutrality.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of training LLMs to express feelings via self-reward schemes, but highlights significant trade-offs. Future work could explore safeguards, evaluation metrics, and alignment strategies to balance expressivity with truthfulness, reliability, and safety.

title: From Prediction to Self: Developmental Conditions for Agency in Minimal Neural Systems
summary:
How does a system that merely predicts the world come to distinguish its own causal influence from everything else? We trace this transition in a minimal 192-dimensional GRU through 40 controlled experiments arranged as a developmental sequence, adding components one at a time and tracking whether the system can distinguish self-caused from world-caused changes.
  The developmental path reveals four conditions that must be satisfied in strict order: (1) persistent state forming stable attractors, (2) a causal action loop linking output to input, (3) proprioceptive feedback that makes implicit causal knowledge explicit, and (4) asynchronous awakening - perceptual learning must consolidate before action learning begins. We propose agency gain (A = Err_world - Err_self), the predictive advantage of knowing one's own action, as a metric to track this process. The self-aware predictor consistently outperforms the self-blind predictor across periodic (sinusoidal) and chaotic (Lorenz) environments, and the metric survives ablation of all auxiliary components. Only forward-sampled action selection produces meaningful agency gain; two gradient-based alternatives degenerate.
  Equally significant are 12 falsified hypotheses mapping where development stalls: predictive coding alone does not produce self-represent

AI.tldr: A developmental study of a 192-dimensional GRU exploring how a predictive system distinguishes its own causal influence from world-driven changes. It identifies four prerequisites that must be satisfied in strict order, introduces an agency gain metric, and shows that forward-sampled action selection (not gradient-based methods) yields a meaningful self-agency signal. 12 hypotheses about developmental failures are falsified.
AI.motivation: To understand how a primarily predictive system can acquire a representation of its own causal influence and delineate the learning sequence (developmental path) that yields self-awareness in agents.
AI.method: 40 controlled experiments arranged as a developmental sequence, incrementally adding components to a minimal 192-dimensional GRU; track whether the system differentiates self-caused vs world-caused changes; test in periodic (sinusoidal) and chaotic (Lorenz) environments; perform ablations of auxiliary components; compare forward-sampled action selection against gradient-based alternatives; report falsified hypotheses where development stalls.
AI.result: Four conditions must be satisfied in strict order: (1) persistent state forming stable attractors, (2) a causal action loop linking output to input, (3) proprioceptive feedback making implicit causal knowledge explicit, and (4) asynchronous awakening—perceptual learning must consolidate before action learning begins. The authors propose an agency gain A = Err_world − Err_self as the predictive advantage of knowing one’s own action. The self-aware predictor consistently outperforms the self-blind predictor across sinusoidal and Lorenz environments, and the metric survives ablations of all auxiliary components. Only forward-sampled action selection produces meaningful agency gain; two gradient-based alternatives degenerate. Additionally, 12 falsified hypotheses map where development stalls: predictive coding alone does not produce self-represent
AI.conclusion: The study articulates a concrete developmental sequence and a measurable criterion (agency gain) for the emergence of self-aware prediction in a predictive system. It emphasizes the necessity of an explicit action loop, proprioception, and asynchronous consolidation, while showing that predictive coding alone is insufficient for self-representation. Forward-action-based policies appear essential for realizing agency in this framework.

title: Flash-WAM: Modality-Aware Distillation for World Action Models
summary: World-action models (WAMs) jointly generate future video and robot actions through iterative diffusion, achieving strong performance on manipulation benchmarks but requiring tens of denoising steps, a cost that precludes real-time control. Step distillation has emerged as the natural remedy, but off-the-shelf methods break down in the joint video-action setting because video and action streams use different SNR-shifted noise schedules and reach training with substantially different marginal noise distributions, an asymmetry that single-modality distillation methods cannot accommodate. We introduce \textbf{Flash-WAM}, a modality-aware step-distillation framework inspired by consistency distillation that selects the consistency function for each modality to match its noise regime: a linear-gradient-scaling parametrization for the action stream's low-noise regime, paired with a variance-preserving parametrization for the video stream's high-noise regime, grounded in a structural analysis of the consistency-function family that characterizes the achievable gradient scaling under the consistency boundary condition. Instantiated on LingBot-VA, Flash-WAM compresses inference to a single step in each modality. On RoboTwin 2.0, this reduces per-chunk latency from $8.1$ seconds to $348$ ms on NVIDIA L40S, a $23{\times}$ speedup that enables real-time inference. Flash-WAM preserves task success on simulation benchmarks ($85.5\%$ RoboTwin 2.0, $95.7\%$ LIBERO) and substantially recovers real-world performance ($60\%$ average on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot), while naive consistency distillation drops to $24\%$ at the same step budget.

AI.tldr: Flash-WAM enables real-time joint video-action diffusion by modality-aware step distillation, using tailored consistency functions per modality to align with their noise regimes, achieving large latency reductions with preserved performance.
AI.motivation: Tens of denoising steps in joint world-action models hinder real-time control, and single-modality distillation cannot handle the asymmetric noise schedules of video and action streams.
AI.method: Propose a modality-aware consistency distillation framework (Flash-WAM) that selects a consistency function per modality: linear-gradient-scaling for the low-noise action stream and variance-preserving for the high-noise video stream, grounded in a structural analysis of the consistency-function family under the boundary condition. Instantiate on LingBot-VA; evaluate on RoboTwin 2.0 and LIBERO; compare to naive distillation.
AI.result: Compress inference to a single step per modality; latency on RoboTwin 2.0 drops from 8.1 seconds to 348 ms (23x speedup on NVIDIA L40S); preserves task success: 85.5% (RoboTwin 2.0) and 95.7% (LIBERO); real-world 60% average on Unitree G1; naive distillation underperforms with ~24% at the same step budget.
AI.conclusion: Modality-aware step distillation effectively accelerates joint WAMs by aligning per-modality noise regimes with appropriate consistency functions, enabling real-time control while maintaining performance; highlights the importance of per-modality design for multi-stream diffusion models.

title: Class-Specific Branch Attention for Mitigating Gradient Interference under Class Imbalance
summary: Deep neural networks trained under severe class imbalance often exhibit degraded performance, typically attributed to statistical bias. In this work, we identify a complementary optimization-level pathology: inter-class gradient interference within shared representations, where gradients from majority classes suppress minority-class learning. To analyze this phenomenon, we introduce a diagnostic framework based on layer-wise gradient flow analysis and a Gradient Conflict Matrix, which quantifies interference using cosine similarity between class-specific gradients. Using this framework, we study multi-branch convolutional architectures and propose a lightweight modification, Class-Specific Branch Attention (CSBA), that enables branch-specific channel reweighting to reduce gradient coupling. This mechanism promotes implicit feature decoupling across branches while preserving architectural simplicity. Empirically, CSBA improves minority-class performance, increasing the F1 score for the Physical-Damage class from 0.261 to 0.522 under severe imbalance, while maintaining comparable overall accuracy. Validation on CIFAR-10-LT confirms that this behavior generalizes across imbalanced visual recognition settings, with Macro-F1 improving from 0.595 to 0.655. More broadly, our findings highlight the importance of considering optimization dynamics alongside statistical methods when designing architectures for imbalanced learning.

AI.tldr: Identifies inter-class gradient interference under severe imbalance and proposes a lightweight fix: Class-Specific Branch Attention (CSBA) with a Gradient Conflict Matrix to reduce gradient coupling in multi-branch networks, boosting minority-class metrics while preserving overall accuracy.
AI.motivation: To understand why performance degrades beyond statistical bias by examining optimization dynamics—specifically how gradients from majority classes interfere with learning for minority classes within shared representations.
AI.method: Layer-wise gradient flow analysis; construction of a Gradient Conflict Matrix using cosine similarity between class-specific gradients; study of multi-branch CNNs; introduce Class-Specific Branch Attention (CSBA) to reweight channels per branch and reduce cross-class gradient coupling.
AI.result: CSBA yields substantial gains for minority classes (e.g., Physical-Damage F1 from 0.261 to 0.522) with overall accuracy largely preserved; Macro-F1 on CIFAR-10-LT increases from 0.595 to 0.655, indicating improved performance across imbalanced settings.
AI.conclusion: Optimization dynamics, not just data statistics, shape learning under imbalance. Architecture-level interventions that decouple gradients across branches can mitigate inter-class interference and improve minority-class performance without sacrificing overall accuracy.

title: Unpaired RGB-Thermal Gaussian-Splatting Using Visual Geometric Transformers
comment: Accepted at ICRA 2026's Workshop MM-SpatialAI: Multi-Modal Spatial AI for Robust Navigation and Open-World Understanding
summary: Multi-modal novel view synthesis (NVS) combining RGB and thermal imagery enables precise 3D scene reconstruction with visual and thermal information. However, existing methods typically rely on precisely calibrated RGB-thermal image pairs or stereo setups, limiting scalability and practical deployment. To address this, we introduce a framework for unpaired RGB-thermal NVS that leverages VGGT, a 3D feed-forward transformer architecture, to independently estimate camera poses for each modality. The pose sets are then aligned using the Procrustes algorithm with a cross-modal feature matcher, enabling joint registration without paired calibration. Building on this alignment, we further propose a multi-modal 3D Gaussian Splatting approach that learns directly from unpaired RGB and thermal images. Experiments on diverse scenes demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance in thermal view synthesis while maintaining RGB fidelity. Moreover, we show that existing reconstruction approaches can produce modality-specific reconstructions that lack cross-modal consistency. We thus introduce a benchmarking framework to rigorously evaluate both per-modality image synthesis and the multi-modal coherence of reconstructed scenes.

AI.tldr: An unpaired RGB–thermal NVS framework using VGGT for per-modality pose estimation, aligned via Procrustes with a cross-modal matcher, followed by multi-modal 3D Gaussian Splatting and a benchmarking suite to assess per-modality quality and cross-modal coherence.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable RGB–thermal NVS without calibrated or paired data, addressing practical deployment where paired scans are hard to obtain.
AI.method: 1) Use VGGT, a 3D feed-forward transformer, to estimate camera poses independently for RGB and thermal modalities. 2) Align pose sets with Procrustes using a cross-modal feature matcher to achieve joint registration without paired calibration. 3) Train a multi-modal 3D Gaussian Splatting model that learns from unpaired RGB and thermal images. 4) Propose a benchmarking framework to evaluate per-modality image synthesis and multi-modal scene coherence.
AI.result: Empirical results show competitive thermal view synthesis while preserving RGB fidelity. Demonstrates that existing reconstruction methods yield modality-specific reconstructions with poor cross-modal consistency; introduces a framework to comprehensively evaluate both per-modality fidelity and cross-modal coherence across scenes.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables unpaired RGB–thermal NVS with independent pose estimation, robust cross-modal alignment, and coherent multi-modal reconstruction, coupled with a benchmarking protocol. Further work could focus on alignment robustness, real-time performance, and broader cross-modal generalization.

title: SubtleMemory: A Benchmark for Fine-Grained Relational Memory Discrimination in Long-Horizon AI Agents
summary: Persistent AI assistants, such as OpenClaw, accumulate large collections of related memories over long-term interactions. As these memories grow, they may reinforce one another, diverge across contexts, or directly conflict, making correct assistance depend on memory relations rather than isolated recall. Existing long-term memory benchmarks rarely probe how agents preserve and utilize such relations during downstream tasks. To address this gap, we introduce SubtleMemory, a benchmark for fine-grained relational memory discrimination in long-running AI agents. SubtleMemory constructs relation-controlled latent semantic artifacts whose variants instantiate complementary, nuanced, or contradictory relations, and embeds them into realistic user-agent histories, requiring agents to recover distributed relational structures during later queries and instructions. The benchmark contains 1,522 evaluation instances over 10 long histories, grounded in 1,090 relation-controlled memory-variant sets and spanning user-related and non-user-related queries. Evaluating six standalone memory systems, two Claw-style agents with native memory modules, and three Claw-style agents with plugin memory modules, we find that current systems remain weak on fine-grained relational memory discrimination. We further introduce diagnostic protocols that reveal distinct capability profiles across memory preservation, retrieval, and downstream reasoning stages.

AI.tldr: SubtleMemory is a relational-memory benchmark for long-running AI agents that tests preservation, retrieval, and downstream reasoning of memory relations. It reveals that current memory systems struggle with fine-grained relational discrimination and uncovers distinct capability profiles across memory stages.
AI.motivation: Long-term AI memories can reinforce, diverge, or contradict across contexts; existing benchmarks rarely test how agents preserve and leverage relational memory structures in downstream tasks.
AI.method: Construct relation-controlled latent artifacts with complementary/nuanced/contradictory relations, embed them into realistic histories to form 1,522 evaluation instances over 10 histories from 1,090 relation-controlled sets, covering user-related and non-user-related queries; evaluate six standalone memory systems, two Claw-style agents with native memory, and three Claw-style agents with plugin memory; add diagnostic protocols to profile memory preservation, retrieval, and downstream reasoning.
AI.result: Current systems underperform on fine-grained relational memory discrimination; diagnostic protocols reveal distinct capability profiles across memory stages and highlight weaknesses in preserving and utilizing relational structures.
AI.conclusion: SubtleMemory provides a challenging, fine-grained benchmark for relational memory in long-running AI agents and a diagnostic framework to guide improvements in memory preservation, retrieval, and reasoning.

title: Emotion-Aware Image Generation from Korean Diary Text via LLM-based Prompt Translation and LoRA Fine-Tuning
summary: T2I models cannot effectively capture sentiment from various types of text, including diaries, as they primarily focus on visual object-related patterns rather than contextual emotional understanding. This paper proposes an emotion-aware text-to-image pipeline that generates children's hand drawing style images from short Korean diary entries. The proposed pipeline employs Qwen3-8B for recognising implicit sentiment from short diaries, and Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium fine-tuned with LoRA on children's drawing images with emotion-based trigger words for image generation. Additionally, this paper presents experiments examining the effect of emotion trigger words on generated images and discusses the limitations of CLIP Score as an evaluation metric for emotion-aware image generation.

AI.tldr: Emotion-aware T2I: generate kids-doodle images from Korean diary entries using Qwen3-8B for sentiment and SD 3.5 with LoRA on child drawings; studies emotion trigger words and CLIP-score limitations.
AI.motivation: Conventional T2I models focus on object-level patterns and neglect contextual sentiment, failing to capture emotional content from diaries.
AI.method: Recognize implicit sentiment in diaries with Qwen3-8B; fine-tune Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium with LoRA on children's drawing images using emotion-based trigger words to steer generation.
AI.result: Experiments analyze the impact of emotion trigger words on generated doodles and reveal limitations of CLIP Score for assessing emotion-aware image generation.
AI.conclusion: Emotion-aware T2I requires targeted language cues and domain-specific fine-tuning; CLIP-based evaluation may be inadequate for emotional content.

title: StableRCA: Robust Graph-Agnostic Mechanism-Level Root Cause Analysis
summary: Root-Cause Analysis (RCA) seeks to identify the variables responsible for abnormal system behavior in complex domains such as manufacturing, cloud computing, and healthcare. Existing approaches face a critical bottleneck: graph-based causal methods can identify intervention targets but typically require a known or accurately estimated causal graph, while graph-free statistical methods either localize marginal anomalies rather than structural causes, or rely on restrictive assumptions about graph structure or functional form. We propose StableRCA, a local mechanism-level RCA framework that avoids global graph discovery by estimating local Markov boundaries and detecting conditional distribution shifts within them. Leveraging the Independent Causal Mechanism principle, we show that intervention targets can be identified with probability converging exponentially in sample size under faithful Markov boundary recovery and non-degenerate mechanism shifts. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and five real-world datasets demonstrate that StableRCA is robust to graph misspecification, effective under multiple intervention targets, scalable to large systems, and reliable across diverse application domains. Code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/StableRCA-E362

AI.tldr: StableRCA is a local, graph-free RCA method that identifies intervention targets by estimating local Markov boundaries and detecting distribution shifts, with exponential convergence guarantees under faithful boundary recovery and non-degenerate shifts.
AI.motivation: Graph-based causal methods require known or accurately estimated graphs; graph-free methods either miss structural causes or rely on restrictive assumptions. There's a need for a scalable, mechanism-level RCA that works without global graph discovery.
AI.method: For each variable, estimate its local Markov boundary, test for within-boundary distribution shifts, and infer intervention targets via Independent Causal Mechanism (ICM) principles. The method avoids full causal graph learning, provides probabilistic guarantees as sample size grows, and handles multiple targets.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic data and five real-world datasets shows robustness to graph misspecification, effectiveness across multiple intervention targets, scalability to large systems, and cross-domain reliability.
AI.conclusion: StableRCA offers a principled, scalable local RCA framework that identifies intervention targets without full graph discovery, backed by exponential-convergence guarantees under suitable assumptions.

title: TAPO: Tool-Aware Policy Optimization via Credit Transfer for Multimodal Search Agents
summary: We identify and formally characterize credit misassignment as a systematic failure mode of GRPO in tool-augmented multimodal search agents: its uniform broadcast of trajectory-level advantages to all tokens causes valuable tool-use steps in failing trajectories to be penalized no differently from valueless ones. We further empirically quantify the scale of this phenomenon. Over half of failing trajectories and failing tool-use actions exhibit correctable credit misassignment, demonstrating that the wasted training signal is both substantial and structurally exploitable. Building on this insight, we propose Tool-Aware Policy Optimization (TAPO), which exploits the parameter-determinism property of information-acquisition tools: similar call parameters define equivalent information-acquisition actions and should therefore share comparable action credit. TAPO constructs counterfactual witnesses within the current training batch and compensates misassigned negative credit via confidence-gated conservative advantage correction. It requires no additional annotation, models, or sampling, and introduces negligible computational overhead. Across multiple multimodal search benchmarks, TAPO delivers consistent, plug-and-play improvements over strong baselines for three mainstream RL algorithms (GRPO, GSPO, and SAPO). Our code and models will be publicly released upon acceptance.
