AI.tldr: Credit misassignment in GRPO-based tool-augmented multimodal search is a prevalent, correctable failure; TAPO counteracts it via counterfactuals and conservative correction, yielding consistent gains across RL algorithms.
AI.motivation: Systematic misassignment arises from uniform broadcast of trajectory-level advantages to all tokens, wasting training signal and hindering tool-use steps; empirical evidence shows that over half of failing cases are correctable, indicating exploitable inefficiency.
AI.method: Introduce Tool-Aware Policy Optimization (TAPO) that leverages the parameter-determinism of information-acquisition tools: identify equivalent information-acquisition actions with similar call parameters; construct in-batch counterfactual witnesses; apply confidence-gated conservative advantage correction to compensate misassigned negative credit; requires no extra annotations/models/sampling and adds negligible computational overhead.
AI.result: TAPO delivers consistent, plug-and-play improvements over strong baselines for three mainstream RL algorithms (GRPO, GSPO, SAPO) across multiple multimodal search benchmarks; empirical evidence includes substantial scale of correctable credit misassignment (over 50%).
AI.conclusion: Credit-aware training via TAPO is a practical, low-cost enhancement for tool-augmented RL, with broad applicability across algorithms and benchmarks; code/models will be released upon acceptance.

title: Gender Artifacts from Art History to Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Artistic styles are rooted in specific socio-historical contexts that encode social hierarchies, including distinct constructions of gender. Yet in AI research, style has long been treated as a surface-level visual property: a filter of color, brushstroke, and texture applied to otherwise content-neutral scenes. We introduce the first dataset to investigate the interplay between gender representation and style in both historical and generated images. StyleGender comprises 74k images spanning 19 artistic styles, comprising art historical images with style and gender annotations, T2I-generated images under controlled style and gender prompts, and a semantically aligned set enabling direct art history-to-generation comparison. By proposing two Set Gender Artifact (SGA) metrics (PixelSGA and MaskSGA), capturing gender signals at the pixel level and in compositional structure, we show that (1) gender representation shapes visual features across artistic styles, (2) style keywords carry these patterns into T2I generation, and (3) generative models tend to amplify gender artifacts beyond what is observed in historical sources.

AI.tldr: Introduces StyleGender, a 74k-image dataset across 19 art styles with gender annotations, plus T2I-generated images and a direct history-to-generation comparison; proposes PixelSGA and MaskSGA metrics to measure gender signals; finds that gender representations influence style features, style prompts convey gender cues to generation, and models amplify gender artifacts relative to historical sources.
AI.motivation: To move beyond viewing style as a surface-level property and investigate how gender representations interact with artistic style both in historical art and in AI-generated images.
AI.method: Assemble and annotate StyleGender combining historical art images (with style and gender annotations) and T2I-generated images under controlled prompts; define PixelSGA (pixel-level gender signal) and MaskSGA (structural/compositional gender signal) metrics; conduct cross-dataset analyses comparing historical vs generated data.
AI.result: Evidence that gender representations affect visual features across styles; style keywords bias T2I outputs toward gendered cues; generation tends to amplify gender artifacts beyond historical levels.
AI.conclusion: The interplay between gender and style is pervasive in both historical and AI-generated art; caution in AI research about amplifying gender artifacts; StyleGender enables direct comparisons and more nuanced evaluation of stylized generation.

title: Let It Be Simple: One-Step Action Generation for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Diffusion-based vision-language-action (VLA) models often inherit the image-generation view: actions are generated by iterative denoising. We argue that VLA action generation has a different condition-target structure: the policy is conditioned on rich observations, language, and state, but predicts only a compact, low-dimensional action chunk. Under this asymmetry, strong one-step action generation should not necessarily require the advanced one-step methods developed for image synthesis. We keep standard velocity prediction and add no teacher model, distillation stage, or auxiliary objective; in our main recipe, we simply bias the training time distribution toward high-noise states. We first isolate the effect in a controlled MNIST grid-to-sequence task, then test it with extensive robot-policy experiments. Across standard LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and LIBERO-Pro, one-step policies trained with high-noise biased schedules generally match ten-step decoding under the same recipe, and on standard LIBERO can exceed ten-step policies trained with a uniform time distribution. A real-robot bimanual YAM RSS evaluation gives a small-sample cross-architecture check of the same sampler trend. On a 1.4B VLM model with a 30M action head, one-step decoding reaches 95.6\% on LIBERO-Long. These results show that strong one-step VLA action generation can emerge from standard diffusion training, without importing the full few-step diffusion machinery developed for image generation.

AI.tldr: High-noise biased diffusion training enables strong one-step action generation for VLA models, matching or surpassing multi-step decoding without extra objectives or distillation.
AI.motivation: VLA action generation has asymmetric conditioning (rich observations and language to a compact action). This suggests simpler one-step generation may suffice, challenging the need for image-diffusion-style multi-step inference.
AI.method: Use standard velocity prediction without teachers/distillation/auxiliary losses; bias training-time diffusion schedules toward high-noise states; evaluate on MNIST grid-to-sequence and robot-policy tasks across LIBERO variants and a real robot; test on a 1.4B VLM with 30M action head.
AI.result: One-step policies trained with high-noise biased schedules generally match ten-step decoding under the same recipe; can exceed ten-step policies trained with uniform time distribution; real-robot evaluation corroborates trend; achieves 95.6% one-step decoding on LIBERO-Long with the large model.
AI.conclusion: Strong one-step VLA action generation can emerge from standard diffusion training, without adopting full few-step diffusion machinery used in image generation.

title: T-FunS3D: Task-Driven Hierarchical Open-Vocabulary 3D Functionality Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation enables robots to localize functional object components in 3D scenes. It is a challenging task that requires spatial understanding and task interpretation. Current open-vocabulary 3D segmentation methods primarily focus on object-level recognition, while scene-wide part segmentation methods attempt to segment the entire scene exhaustively, making them highly resource-intensive and time consuming. Balancing segmentation performance in terms of granularity, accuracy, and speed remains a challenge. As one step towards alleviating this, we introduce T-FunS3D, a task-driven hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation method that provides actionable perception for robotic applications. Our method takes as input the 3D point cloud and posed RGB-D images of an indoor scene. We construct an open-vocabulary scene graph by extracting instances and their visual embeddings in the environment. Given a task description, T-FunS3D identifies the most relevant instances in the scene graph and locates their functional components leveraging a vision-language model. Experiments on the SceneFun3D dataset demonstrate that T-FunS3D is comparable to state-of-the-art in open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation, while achieving faster runtime and reduced memory usage.

AI.tldr: T-FunS3D introduces a task-driven hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation framework that builds an open-vocabulary scene graph and uses vision-language models to locate functional components of relevant instances, achieving competitive accuracy with faster runtime and lower memory usage on SceneFun3D.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation is needed for robotic perception but existing methods face tradeoffs: object-level recognition vs exhaustive scene-wide segmentation with high resource costs; thus a method balancing granularity, accuracy, and speed is needed.
AI.method: Construct an open-vocabulary scene graph from 3D point clouds and posed RGB-D images; extract instance-level visual embeddings; given a task description, identify relevant instances in the scene graph and locate their functional components leveraging a vision-language model.
AI.result: On SceneFun3D, T-FunS3D achieves competitive open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation performance while offering faster runtime and reduced memory usage relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: T-FunS3D provides a scalable, task-driven approach for open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation by integrating scene graphs with vision-language reasoning to focus on task-relevant components.

title: From Risk Classification to Action Plan Remediation: A Guardrail Feedback Driven Framework for LLM Agents
summary: LLM-based guardrails typically safeguard agents by evaluating proposed actions or inputs before execution, producing safety signals such as binary allow/deny decisions, risk categories, and/or explanatory rationales about potential policy violations. However, agent risks often arise when otherwise benign tasks are contaminated by untrusted external content, unsafe instructions, or risky tool use. Existing guardrails often flag the entire task uniformly as unsafe, thereby blocking the threat but sacrificing the benign part. Moreover, existing work largely evaluates guardrails in isolation, leaving unclear whether their interventions lead to safer downstream agent behavior. To address this, we introduce TRIAD (Tripartite Response for Iterative Agent Guardrailing), a guardrail-integrated agent framework that leverages guardrail-generated verbal feedback as a guiding signal to keep the agent aligned with benign objectives at each planning step. We finetune a language model on a self-curated training dataset to output one of three decisions: proceed, refuse, or update, together with structured natural-language feedback. Rather than merely allowing or blocking execution, update guides the agent to revise its plan, avoid harmful components, and preserve the benign task where possible. TRIAD injects this feedback into the agent's context, enabling subsequent plan revision and forming a closed loop between guardrail feedback and agent planning. Extensive experiments on ASB and AgentHarm show that TRIAD reduces the average attack success rate to 10.42%, while achieving the best safety-utility trade-off among guardrail-integrated baselines. Our code is available at: https://github.com/YUHAOSUNABC/TRIAD.

AI.tldr: TRIAD is a guardrail-integrated agent framework that uses guardrail-generated verbal feedback to iteratively revise plans, rather than simply allowing or blocking actions. It finetunes an LM to output proceed/refuse/update with feedback, creating a closed loop between guardrails and planning. It reduces attack success rate (to 10.42%) and achieves a strong safety-utility trade-off on ASB and AgentHarm, with code released open-source.
AI.motivation: Existing guardrails often flag entire tasks as unsafe, harming benign components and failing to impact downstream behavior. TRIAD aims to align agent planning with benign objectives by integrating guardrail feedback at each planning step, enabling safer downstream behavior.
AI.method: Finetune a language model to output one of three decisions (proceed, refuse, update) plus structured natural-language feedback. The update option guides plan revision to avoid harmful components and preserve benign tasks. Feedback is injected into the agent’s context to revise the plan, forming a closed loop between guardrail feedback and planning.
AI.result: TRIAD reduces average attack success rate to 10.42% and achieves the best safety-utility trade-off among guardrail-integrated baselines on ASB and AgentHarm benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Guardrail feedback can be effectively integrated into planning to reduce risk while preserving benign objectives, demonstrated by TRIAD’s closed-loop approach and competitive performance; code is publicly available.

title: Geometry-Aware Dataset Condensation for Diffusion Model Training
summary: Dataset condensation aims to construct compact datasets from real data via synthesis or selection. However, existing approaches are ill-suited for diffusion model training: synthetic data generation often yields low-fidelity samples unsuitable for authentic modeling, while real subset selection typically fails to preserve the distributional geometry required by diffusion likelihood objectives. To address this, we propose to reformulate real subset selection as a geometry-aware distribution alignment problem. By incorporating one-sided partial optimal transport, our method selectively aligns a compact subset with the full data distribution while allowing unmatched mass in low-density regions, ensuring the preserved geometric structure necessary for effective diffusion model training. To further ensure distributional fidelity, we complement geometric alignment with lightweight feature-statistics and semantic consistency regularization. An efficient two-stage discrete optimization strategy is proposed to achieve this alignment objective. Extensive experiments across diffusion variants, subset sizes, image resolutions, and training rounds show that our method achieves superior fidelity and distributional coverage in diffusion model training. Codes are available at https://github.com/2018cx/GADC.

AI.tldr: Geometry-aware dataset condensation for diffusion modeling using one-sided partial optimal transport to align a compact subset with the full data distribution, preserving geometric structure while allowing unmatched mass in low-density regions; augmented with feature-statistics and semantic consistency regularization and a two-stage discrete optimization, achieving better fidelity and coverage; code released.
AI.motivation: Diffusion model training demands a faithful representation of the data distribution. Existing condensation approaches either produce low-fidelity synthetic data or fail to maintain the distributional geometry essential for diffusion likelihood objectives.
AI.method: Reformulate real subset selection as a geometry-aware distribution alignment problem using one-sided partial optimal transport. The subset selectively aligns with the full distribution while leaving unmatched mass in low-density regions to preserve geometry. Augment with lightweight feature-statistics and semantic consistency regularization. Employ a two-stage discrete optimization strategy to realize the alignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diffusion variants, subset sizes, image resolutions, and training rounds demonstrate superior fidelity and distributional coverage of the condensed subset for diffusion model training.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware subset selection framework based on one-sided partial OT, coupled with regularization and a two-stage optimization procedure, improves diffusion model training by preserving the distributional geometry and fidelity of the data; code is available.

title: PLAN-S: Bridging Planning with Latent Style Dynamics for Autonomous Driving World Models
summary: Latent world models (LWMs) have strengthened end-to-end autonomous driving by forecasting compact scene dynamics for downstream planning. However, existing LWM-based planners usually generate trajectories directly from entangled latent representations. This compact latent-to-planner pathway lacks explicit modeling of risk, drivability, and diverse style preferences, making driving-style dynamics difficult to supervise, inspect, or modulate before a final trajectory is selected. We propose PLAN-S (PLANning with latent Style dynamics), a planner-facing bridge that addresses this compactness-controllability dilemma by decoding a style-conditioned, four-channel semantic cost map from the latent representation. The cost map is conditioned on ego state and driving style and is consumed up-stream of the planning decision through two host-side interfaces: attention-level fusion for regression planners and reward-level fusion for anchor-score planners. We validate PLAN-S on two architecturally distinct hosts, ResWorld on nuScenes and WoTE on NAVSIM, while keeping the host backbones frozen to isolate the contribution of the proposed bridge. On nuScenes, PLAN-S reduces L2 at every horizon over the baseline, with 0.55 m average L2 and a 42% relative reduction in the 3 s collision rate. On NAVSIM, the rule-cost variant reaches 89.4 Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS), while the learned cost variant provides complementary gains on baseline-challenging scenes. Ablations show that the cost pathway contributes most directly to safer trajectory selection. Qualitative results further show that PLAN-S can produce diverse cost maps, with spatially consistent variations aligned to different driving styles.

AI.tldr: PLAN-S introduces a style-conditioned latent-to-cost-map bridge for latent world model–based planners, enabling explicit risk, drivability, and driving-style control via a four-channel semantic cost map decoded from latent representations. It interfaces with regression and anchor-score planners to improve safety and diversity, validated on two distinct platforms (nuScenes/ResWorld and NAVSIM/WoTE) with frozen backbones; shows consistent L2 and collision-rate improvements and interpretable, style-aligned cost maps.
AI.motivation: End-to-end LWM planners rely on entangled latent representations to generate trajectories, lacking explicit supervision over risk, drivability, and driving style. This hampers supervision, inspection, and modulation of driving behavior before final trajectory selection.
AI.method: Decode a style-conditioned, four-channel semantic cost map from the LWM latent representation. Conditioned on ego state and driving style, the cost map is consumed upstream via two host-side interfaces: attention-level fusion for regression planners and reward-level fusion for anchor-score planners. Validate across two architecturally distinct hosts (ResWorld on nuScenes and WoTE on NAVSIM) with frozen backbones to isolate PLAN-S contribution.
AI.result: On nuScenes, PLAN-S reduces L2 error at every horizon; average L2 = 0.55 m and 42% relative reduction in 3 s collision rate. On NAVSIM, the rule-cost variant achieves 89.4 PDMS; learned-cost variant offers gains on baseline-challenging scenes. Ablations show the cost pathway directly enhances safe trajectory selection. Qualitative results show diverse, spatially consistent cost maps aligned to different driving styles.
AI.conclusion: PLAN-S provides a practical, interpretable bridge to inject controllable, risk-aware style dynamics into LWM-based planners without retraining backbones, improving safety metrics and enabling style-driven planning across heterogeneous planning architectures.

title: When Tools Fail: Benchmarking Dynamic Replanning and Anomaly Recovery in LLM Agents
summary: Existing benchmarks evaluate Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) in LLMs on idealized ''happy paths'', largely overlooking real-world tool failures. We introduce ToolMaze, a benchmark for dynamic path discovery and error recovery in TIR agents. To separate systematic replanning from blind trial-and-error, ToolMaze adopts a two-dimensional design: DAG-based topological complexity and a $2 \times 2$ taxonomy of tool perturbations (explicit/implicit, transient/permanent). Evaluations show that perturbations degrade performance across nearly all models, with the sharpest drops under implicit semantic failures. Driven by systemic over-trust in corrupted outputs, Perturbation Recovery Rate (PRR) plummets by around 37\% in these scenarios, while complex topologies trap agents in futile trial-and-error loops. Crucially, agentic fault-tolerance improves with model scale $3.66\times$ slower than basic task execution, highlighting dynamic replanning as a distinct bottleneck unaddressed by model scaling or prompting. Data and code are available at https://github.com/Zhudongsheng75/ToolMaze.

AI.tldr: ToolMaze introduces a benchmark for dynamic path discovery and error recovery in Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) agents, using a 2D design (DAG complexity and a 2x2 perturbation taxonomy). It shows that real-world perturbations degrade performance across models, with implicit semantic failures causing the largest drops; Perturbation Recovery Rate declines ~37%, complex topologies induce futile trial-and-error loops, and scaling improves fault-tolerance only slowly, indicating a distinct replanning bottleneck not solved by scaling or prompting. Data/code available at the project URL.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of TIR focus on idealized, happy-path scenarios and overlook dynamic failures and recovery. ToolMaze targets real-world tool failures and the agent's ability to recover and replan under perturbations.
AI.method: A two-dimensional benchmark design: (1) DAG-based topological complexity, and (2) a 2×2 taxonomy of perturbations (explicit vs implicit; transient vs permanent). Evaluations span multiple models to measure performance, Perturbation Recovery Rate (PRR), and looping behavior under perturbations, emphasizing dynamic replanning as a bottleneck.
AI.result: Perturbations degrade performance across nearly all models, with the largest drops under implicit semantic failures. PRR falls by about 37% in corrupted-output scenarios. Complex topologies push agents into futile trial-and-error loops. Model-scale improves agentic fault-tolerance but much more slowly than basic task execution (~3.66× slower), highlighting dynamic replanning as a distinct bottleneck not solved by scaling or prompting.
AI.conclusion: ToolMaze reveals a distinct bottleneck in dynamic replanning and fault-tolerance for TIR agents under tool perturbations. Scaling and prompting alone do not address this issue; robust recovery strategies are needed. The dataset and code enable replication and further study.

title: Resonant Minds: Closed-Loop Social Avatars with Theory of Mind
summary: Creating lifelike digital humans with genuine social intelligence requires unifying cognitive reasoning and multimodal generation within a coherent framework. Current approaches treat these as separate tasks: Large Language Models excel at dialogue but lack embodied expression, while diffusion-based talking head models achieve visual fidelity but ignore social cognition. To bridge this gap, we propose a closed-loop dual-agent framework integrating perception, social reasoning, and expression into a continuous interaction cycle. The perception module analyzes partners' multimodal behaviors from video, while the social reasoning module infers hidden mental states through Theory of Mind and selects responses via an ensemble mechanism. The expression module then generates emotion-controllable dual-agent videos synthesizing both speaker speech and expression alongside listener reactive behaviors, capturing bidirectional dynamics absent in prior work. We construct a hierarchical Persona-Scenario dataset with psychologically grounded personas and private social goals to support evaluation under information asymmetry. Experiments on this dataset demonstrate competitive or superior performance on both dialogue quality and video generation metrics. Notably, our method surpasses even the full-information Script mode on key dialogue quality dimensions, suggesting that explicit mental state inference under uncertainty can elicit more thoughtful dialogue than unrestricted information access.

AI.tldr: Closed-loop dual-agent framework integrating perception, Theory-of-Mind social reasoning, and emotion-driven expression to create lifelike digital humans; evaluated on a Persona-Scenario dataset with information asymmetry; achieves competitive or superior dialogue and video generation metrics, surpassing Script mode in dialogue under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between cognitive reasoning and multimodal generation for social intelligent agents, addressing the separation of dialogue (LLMs) and embodied visual systems.
AI.method: A three-module closed-loop system: (1) perception analyzes partners' multimodal behaviors from video; (2) social reasoning infers hidden mental states via Theory of Mind and selects responses through an ensemble mechanism; (3) expression generates emotion-controllable dual-agent videos incorporating speaker speech, expression, and listener reactive behaviors. Evaluated on a hierarchical Persona-Scenario dataset designed for information asymmetry.
AI.result: Experiments show competitive or superior performance on both dialogue quality and video generation metrics; notably, the method surpasses full-information Script mode on key dialogue dimensions, suggesting benefits of explicit mental-state inference under uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Explicit mental-state inference under uncertainty, integrated in a bidirectional perception–reasoning–expression loop, can yield more thoughtful dialogue and more coherent social dynamics in digital humans; the dataset enables evaluation under information asymmetry.

title: Benchmarking Counterfactual Prediction in Epidemic Time Series with Time-Varying Interventions
summary: Deep learning has enabled significant advances in time-series causal inference, yet progress remains constrained by the lack of realistic benchmarks with observable counterfactual outcomes. Existing datasets either rely on real-world observations without ground-truth counterfactuals or on simplified simulations that fail to capture complex causal dynamics. To address this gap, we develop a large-scale benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series under dynamic interventions. Unlike existing benchmarks, it supports static and time-varying treatments, as well as both single-policy and multi-policy intervention settings, enabling evaluation of causal inference methods across a broad range of causal inference scenarios. Leveraging a calibrated agent-based model grounded in real-world demographic, mobility, epidemiological, and policy data, we generate realistic counterfactual trajectories across more than 150 U.S. counties. Using this benchmark, we evaluate widely used and state-of-the-art causal inference methods, revealing substantial performance differences and highlighting the challenges of realistic time-series causal reasoning.

AI.tldr: A large-scale, realistic benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series under dynamic interventions, using an agent-based model to simulate trajectories for 150+ counties and evaluate causal inference methods across static/time-varying and single/multi-policy settings.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of realistic benchmarks with ground-truth counterfactuals for time-series causal inference; existing datasets are either real with no ground-truth counterfactuals or simple simulations with limited dynamics.
AI.method: Calibrated agent-based model built on real-world demographic, mobility, epidemiological, and policy data; generates counterfactual trajectories for more than 150 U.S. counties; supports static and time-varying treatments; allows single-policy and multi-policy intervention configurations.
AI.result: Benchmark reveals substantial performance differences among standard and state-of-the-art causal inference methods; identifies challenges in realistic time-series causal reasoning under dynamic interventions.
AI.conclusion: Provides a versatile platform for evaluating causal inference methods across diverse epidemic intervention scenarios, highlighting gaps and guiding future methodological advances.

title: Synthetic Data Generation and Vision-based Wrinkle and Keypoint Detection for Bimanual Cloth Manipulation
summary: Robotic manipulation of textiles remains challenging because continuous deformation and self-occlusions hinder the robust visual perception required to estimate the cloth's state. To address the lack of annotated real-world data, we developed a Blender-based synthetic pipeline exporting auto-annotated keypoints, and combined manually labeled renders with real-world data to train a wrinkle detector. We present a perception framework integrating a CNN for permutation-invariant keypoint detection and a YOLOv8-OpenCV pipeline to extract grasping points from structural wrinkles. A proposed bimanual algorithm uses this system to stretch fully folded garments via wrinkles, transitioning to keypoint-based ironing once corners emerge. The keypoint model achieves a Mean Position Error (MPE) of 1.7615 pixels. The perception system transfers to physical fabrics without fine-tuning, outperforming baselines that fail in high-occlusion states or yield false positives on severe folds.

AI.tldr: A synthetic-data–augmented perception pipeline for robotic textile manipulation that detects keypoints and wrinkle-based grasp points to enable a bimanual ironing-like workflow, achieving precise keypoint localization and real-world transfer without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Textile manipulation is hard due to continuous deformation and self-occlusions, and annotated real data is scarce. A robust perception system is needed to estimate cloth state for manipulation tasks such as ironing.
AI.method: A Blender-based synthetic pipeline exports auto-annotated keypoints and combines manually labeled renders with real data. A CNN detects permutation-invariant keypoints; YOLOv8-OpenCV extracts grasp points from structural wrinkles. A bimanual algorithm stretches garments via wrinkles and switches to keypoint-based ironing once corners emerge.
AI.result: Mean Position Error (MPE) of 1.7615 pixels for the keypoint model. The perception system transfers to real fabrics without fine-tuning and outperforms baselines that struggle with high occlusion or produce false positives on severe folds.
AI.conclusion: Integrating synthetic, labeled data with a permutation-invariant keypoint detector and wrinkle-based grasp detection yields a robust perception framework capable of guiding efficient garment manipulation on real fabrics without substantial domain adaptation.

title: Agentic Molecular Recovery via Molecule-Aware Exploration
summary: Text-guided molecular generation with LLMs often yields invalid SMILES. We argue that invalid drafts should be addressed through a shift from validity-oriented repair to identity-preserving molecular recovery: the objective is not only to restore chemical validity, but also to preserve target-relevant structural cues and recover the molecular identity implied by the description. This perspective reveals the limitations of existing correction strategies. Post-hoc repair can recover validity while distorting key structures, LLM-only correction can introduce unintended global drift, and generic agentic correction remains constrained by greedy single-candidate trajectories even when equipped with executable RDKit edit tools. To address these limitations, we propose AMREC, which couples molecule-aware mismatch tracking with expanded candidate exploration and trajectory-level selection. On invalid ChEBI-20 drafts from three backbone models, AMREC achieves the strongest overall recovery profile across structural, exact-match, and string-level metrics.

AI.tldr: AMREC shifts molecular generation from validity repair to identity-preserving recovery, improving recovery on invalid drafts by tracking mismatches and exploring more candidates, achieving strong results on ChEBI-20 across models.
AI.motivation: Validity-focused repairs in text-guided molecule generation often distort target structural cues and the molecular identity implied by the description; there is a need to preserve identity while ensuring validity.
AI.method: Introduce AMREC: molecule-aware mismatch tracking, expanded candidate exploration, and trajectory-level selection; evaluate on invalid ChEBI-20 drafts from three backbone models; compare against post-hoc repair and generic correction baselines.
AI.result: AMREC yields the strongest overall recovery across structural, exact-match, and string-level metrics on the tested prompts.
AI.conclusion: Identity-preserving molecular recovery with enhanced search and trajectory-level decisions can outperform existing correction strategies in text-guided molecular generation.

title: CamFlow+: Hybrid Motion Bases for 2D Camera Motion Estimation with Stabilization Applications
summary: Estimating 2D camera motion is fundamental to computer vision and computational photography. Existing homography-based methods work well for planar scenes or pure rotation, but struggle with camera translation, depth variation, and local parallax; local homography and mesh-based models improve flexibility but still rely on piecewise planar assumptions. We introduce CamFlow+, a hybrid-basis framework that represents 2D camera motion directly in dense-flow space. CamFlow+ combines homography-derived physical bases, stochastic bases sampled from homography flows, and depth-translational bases derived from depth and camera intrinsics, relaxing the single-plane constraint while preserving camera-motion regularity. A depth-aware smoothness term further regularizes translation-induced parallax in continuous-depth regions while preserving motion changes near depth boundaries. We evaluate CamFlow+ on GHOF-Cam, a camera-motion benchmark that masks out dynamic objects and ill-posed occlusion regions in an optical-flow benchmark to isolate camera-induced motion. Experiments show that CamFlow+ improves sparse and dense camera-motion estimation. In digital video stabilization, CamFlow+ also improves global and local stability, achieving the best top-1 preference rate in a blind user study. Code and datasets will be available on the project page: https://lhaippp.github.io/CamFlow+.

AI.tldr: CamFlow+ is a dense-flow, hybrid-basis model for 2D camera motion that fuses homography-based, stochastic, and depth-derived bases to handle depth variation and parallax, with a depth-aware smoothness term.
AI.motivation: Standard homography-based motion estimation fails under translation and depth variation; local or mesh models still assume piecewise planes and struggle with continuous depth; a dense-flow representation with physically and data-driven bases can regularize motion while allowing depth-induced parallax.
AI.method: CamFlow+ represents 2D camera motion in dense-flow space using a hybrid basis: (1) homography-derived physical bases, (2) stochastic bases sampled from homography flows, and (3) depth-translational bases derived from depth maps and camera intrinsics. A depth-aware smoothness term regularizes translation-induced parallax in continuous-depth regions while preserving changes near depth boundaries.
AI.result: On GHOF-Cam, CamFlow+ improves both sparse and dense camera-motion estimation; in digital video stabilization, it enhances global and local stability and achieves the best top-1 preference rate in a blind user study.
AI.conclusion: CamFlow+ relaxes the single-plane constraint while preserving camera-motion regularity via a dense-flow hybrid basis and depth-aware regularization, yielding improved camera-motion estimation and stabilization. Code and datasets will be publicly available.

title: Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents: A Lightweight Framework for Measuring Behavioral Patterns
summary: AI agents are commonly evaluated using task success, reward, latency, and cost. These metrics are useful, but they often miss important aspects of agent behavior: whether an agent explores too much, repeats itself too rigidly, uses tools effectively, reduces uncertainty over time, or remains robust across repeated runs. This paper proposes Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents (EEA), a lightweight framework for measuring agent behavior through entropy. Rather than treating intelligence as only final task completion, EEA studies the structure of the agents decision process. The framework introduces action entropy, trajectory entropy, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, and robustness entropy. These metrics are intended to complement, not replace, traditional evaluation methods. We also present a practical Python implementation designed to integrate with agent frameworks such as LangChain, Google ADK, custom agent loops, and stored observability traces.

AI.tldr: Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents (EEA): a lightweight framework to quantify agent behavior via entropy metrics (action, trajectory, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, robustness entropy) to complement task-based metrics; includes a practical Python implementation for LangChain/ADK/custom loops and stored traces.
AI.motivation: Conventional metrics (task success, reward, latency, cost) overlook behavioral qualities such as exploratory diversity, rigidity, tool usage effectiveness, uncertainty reduction, and cross-run robustness. An entropy-based perspective aims to reveal the decision-process structure driving agent behavior.
AI.method: Define and compute multiple entropy-based metrics (action entropy, trajectory entropy, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, robustness entropy) from agent traces/data. Provide a lightweight Python implementation designed to integrate with agent frameworks (e.g., LangChain, Google ADK) and stored observability traces to measure these metrics alongside traditional evaluators.
AI.result: Proposes a set of entropy-based metrics and a practical Python implementation for integrating with common agent frameworks and traces; designed to complement, not replace, traditional evaluation methods.
AI.conclusion: EEA offers deeper behavioral insights by quantifying structure in the agent’s decision process, enabling better assessment of exploration, tool usage, and robustness across runs, and is ready for integration into existing evaluation pipelines.

title: Unveiling the Unknown: Open Vocabulary Object Detection with Scene Graphs
summary: Open-vocabulary object detection seeks to identify novel object categories that were not part of the training data. Many knowledge distillation-based approaches have shown promising performance by transferring knowledge from pre-trained vision-language models to object detection. However, these methods often overlook structured, image-specific relationships between objects, such as interactions and spatial arrangements. This oversight can significantly restrict the effectiveness of detecting novel categories. To address this issue, we propose a Scene-guided Relational Modeling detection framework. This framework utilizes scene graphs to capture structured semantic and spatial relationships between candidate regions and their contextual objects. It explicitly models interactions among neighboring regions and incorporates a Relation Attention Module to implicitly amplify the key relational cues extracted from the scene graph. Furthermore, we present a scene-based textual alignment branch that distills category knowledge from captions to guide relational alignment. This approach facilitates a seamless integration of visual relations with semantic information for enhanced detection performance. Comprehensive experiments show that our model achieves superior performance compared to other OVOD methods, improving the AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.

AI.tldr: Proposes Scene-guided Relational Modeling for open-vocabulary object detection using scene graphs, relation attention, and caption-based textual alignment to leverage structured relational cues for novel categories; reports AP gains on COCO and LVIS.
AI.motivation: OVOD methods using knowledge distillation from vision-language models miss image-specific object relationships (interactions and spatial arrangements) that can help detect novel categories.
AI.method: Use scene graphs to model structured semantic and spatial relations among candidate regions and context objects; include a Relation Attention Module to amplify key relational cues; add a scene-based textual alignment branch that distills knowledge from captions to guide relational alignment.
AI.result: Experiments show superior performance compared with other OVOD methods, with improved average precision for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.
AI.conclusion: Integrating visual relational information with scene-guided cues and textual alignment enhances open-vocabulary detection, enabling better recognition of novel categories.

title: Intercomparison of Machine Learning Algorithms for Remote Sensing-based In-season Crop Mapping
summary: In-season crop type mapping is critical for food security in the face of increasingly extreme climate-related threats to crops. Currently, the USDA Cropland Data Layer provides crop type labels at 30m resolution and is available the February after harvest, but no product exists that maps crop types before harvest with satisfactory accuracy that would allow emergency managers to respond to crop threats in near real time. Furthermore, the relative advantages of a wide range of algorithms have not been evaluated in a way that accounts for interannual variability, until this study. Here, Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel surface reflectance imagery time series and crop rotation history information are combined to map corn in Iowa and almonds in California at 30m resolution accurately by early June in unseen years, with robust quantification of uncertainty due to phenology and crop distribution. Thousands of model configurations across ten machine learning algorithms were compared using a year-wise cross-validation and a suite of metrics. Hyperparameter search revealed Support Vector Machines to be the most successful algorithm overall, with a mean F1 score of 0.74 (0.59) across five unseen validation years for almonds by early June in California (corn by early June in Iowa). Interannual variation was a large source of uncertainty, but patterns showed the potential to further improve performance with ensemble approaches or ancillary data. Future work may extend these methods to include multiclass maps of all crop types, CONUS-wide maps, and in-season crop yield forecasting.

AI.tldr: Pre-harvest crop-type mapping for almonds in California and corn in Iowa using Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel time series; Support Vector Machines (SVM) perform best across many configurations with mean F1 ~0.74 for almonds and ~0.59 for corn in unseen years, indicating feasible near real-time mapping with notable interannual uncertainty; ensemble approaches and additional data could improve accuracy.
AI.motivation: Provide pre-harvest, near real-time crop type maps to support emergency management and food security; address the lack of satisfactory pre-harvest mapping methods and evaluate a broad set of algorithms under interannual variability.
AI.method: Combine Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel surface reflectance time series with crop rotation history; evaluate thousands of model configurations across ten machine learning algorithms; use year-wise cross-validation with an emphasis on unseen validation years; perform hyperparameter optimization; quantify uncertainty due to phenology and crop distribution.
AI.result: SVM emerged as the most successful algorithm overall; mean F1 ~0.74 for almonds in California and ~0.59 for corn in Iowa across five unseen validation years; substantial interannual variation constitutes a major source of uncertainty; ensemble methods or inclusion of ancillary data could further improve performance; the approach provides robust uncertainty quantification related to phenology and crop distribution.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of pre-harvest, 30 m crop-type maps for at least two crops with reasonable accuracy; future work could extend to multiclass maps, CONUS-wide coverage, and in-season yield forecasting, with potential gains from ensemble approaches and additional data sources.

title: QCFuse: Query-Aware Cache Fusion via Compressed View for Efficient RAG Serving
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves large language model (LLM) answer quality by grounding generation in external evidence, but processing retrieved contexts makes the prefill stage a dominant serving cost. RAG cache fusion reduces this cost by reusing precomputed key-value (KV) caches for retrieved chunks and selectively recomputing tokens under the current prompt. Existing selectors, however, face a dilemma between quality and efficiency: fast query-agnostic or final-layer query-to-context selectors can miss request-relevant evidence, whereas full-view query-aware selectors require broad context and layer visibility before recomputation and therefore stall the layer-wise cache-fusion pipeline. We present QCFuse, a compressed-view query-aware selector for RAG cache fusion. QCFuse uses chunk-anchor query probing to condition user-query states on compact per-chunk anchors and critical-layer profiling to identify recomputation tokens without all-layer inspection. We implement QCFuse in SGLang and evaluate it on four open-weight LLMs across six datasets. QCFuse reaches full-prefill-level quality. At matched quality, QCFuse achieves an average prefill-time speedup of 1.7x over full prefill and 1.5x over ProphetKV, the strongest quality-preserving baseline.

AI.tldr: QCFuse is a compressed-view, query-aware selector for RAG cache fusion that preserves full prefill quality while delivering speedups (1.7x vs full prefill, 1.5x vs ProphetKV) across 4 open-weight LLMs and 6 datasets.
AI.motivation: RAG prefill costs are dominated by processing retrieved contexts. Fast, query-agnostic selectors can miss request-relevant evidence, while full-view selectors impede the cache-fusion pipeline. A lightweight, quality-preserving selector is needed.
AI.method: Introduce QCFuse with chunk-anchor query probing to condition user-query states on compact per-chunk anchors and critical-layer profiling to identify recomputation tokens without inspecting all layers. Implemented in SGLang and evaluated on four open-weight LLMs across six datasets.
AI.result: QCFuse achieves full-prefill-level quality. At matched quality, it yields average prefill-time speedups of 1.7x over full prefill and 1.5x over ProphetKV (a strong quality-preserving baseline).
AI.conclusion: Compressed-view, query-aware selectors can match full prefill quality while accelerating prefill, enabling more efficient RAG cache fusion without sacrificing evidence grounding.

title: MemoryCard: Topic-Aware Multi-Modal Clue Compression for Long-Video Question Answering
summary: Long-video question answering remains challenging for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), as answer-relevant evidence is often sparse, transient, and temporally dispersed across lengthy video contexts. Existing frame-centric approaches improve efficiency through uniform sampling, query-aware frame selection, visual-token compression, and adaptive resolution strategies. However, they still rely on isolated and fragmented frames as the fundamental evidence units, limiting VLMs' ability to effectively capture coherent event-level semantics. To address this limitation, we propose MemoryCard, a video-memory-based augmentation framework that organizes long videos into self-contained Memory Cards. Specifically, MemoryCard first performs a self-reading process over videos and aligned utterances to segment the video into semantically coherent units, each corresponding to a distinct topic or event. For each unit, it generates an event-level video gist and selects representative visual moments, which are then rendered into unified Memory Cards for retrieval and question answering. Experimental results demonstrate that MemoryCard consistently improves long-video QA performance under comparable visual-token budgets, achieving up to a 21.8% relative improvement in accuracy. All code is available at https://github.com/NEUIR/MemoryCard.

AI.tldr: MemoryCard proposes a memory-card-based augmentation for long-video QA: segment videos into semantically coherent units (Memory Cards) via self-reading of videos and aligned utterances, generate event-level gist and representative moments, enabling retrieval and QA with improved accuracy (up to 21.8% relative).
AI.motivation: Long videos yield sparse, transient, and dispersed evidence; frame-centric sampling and token compression fail to capture event-level semantics; need unit-level memory representations aligned to events/topics.
AI.method: Self-reading to segment video and aligned utterances into Memory Cards; for each unit generate an event-level gist and select representative visual moments; render into Memory Cards for retrieval-augmented QA; comparable visual-token budgets.
AI.result: Across experiments, MemoryCard improves long-video QA accuracy by up to 21.8% relative; results under comparable visual-token budgets; code released.
AI.conclusion: MemoryCard effectively captures coherent event-level semantics in long videos and enhances QA performance; demonstrates the value of memory-card-based organization for long-video understanding.

title: Retry Policy Gradients in Continuous Action Spaces
summary: Retry-based objectives such as pass@K and max@K optimize the best return obtained from multiple sampled trajectories, and recent work has shown that they can promote exploration without explicit exploration bonuses. In discrete action spaces, ReMax was shown to do so by adapting to return uncertainty. In this work, we introduce pathwise derivative estimators for retry objectives and use them to extend ReMax to continuous action spaces. We study the resulting learning dynamics and show that, even with deterministic rewards, ReMax can encourage stochastic exploration by reshaping the policy-gradient landscape. In particular, it alters gradients both in direction, biasing updates toward higher policy entropy, and in magnitude, damping gradients and slowing convergence. We further show that Adam's adaptive normalization can mitigate this damping, depending on its numerical stabilization parameter. Empirically, we instantiate this objective as ReMax Actor-Critic (ReMAC), an off-policy actor--critic algorithm that optimizes the ReMax objective using a pathwise derivative estimator. Our experiments show that ReMAC can promote higher policy entropy without entropy regularization and achieves performance comparable to SAC.

AI.tldr: Pathwise-derivative retry objectives (ReMax) are extended to continuous actions via ReMAC, showing that retry-based objectives reshape policy-gradients to encourage exploration and higher entropy, yielding competitive performance with SAC without entropy regularization.
AI.motivation: Tackle exploration in reinforcement learning by leveraging retry-based objectives beyond discrete actions, and understand their learning dynamics in continuous action spaces, including optimization effects like gradient damping and entropy biases.
AI.method: Derive pathwise derivative estimators for retry objectives, adapt ReMax to continuous action spaces, analyze how the gradient landscape changes (direction and magnitude), and implement ReMAC as an off-policy actor–critic that optimizes the ReMax objective with the estimator; study the role of Adam's normalization.
AI.result: ReMAC promotes higher policy entropy without relying on entropy regularization, and achieves performance comparable to SAC; Adam's adaptive normalization can mitigate gradient damping depending on its stabilization parameter.
AI.conclusion: Retry-based objectives can effectively promote exploration in continuous-action RL when combined with appropriate optimization (e.g., Adam); ReMAC provides a competitive alternative to SAC and highlights how gradient shaping from retry objectives impacts learning dynamics.

title: Beyond Soft Masks: Hard-Perturbation Mixup Explainer for Robust GNN Explainability
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across a range of applications involving graph-structured data, particularly in high-stakes domains. However, the opaque nature of their decision-making processes limits their trustworthiness and broader adoption. Existing post-hoc explanation methods aim to improve explainability by identifying subgraphs that influence GNN predictions and adopt mixup strategies to alleviate the out-of-distribution (OOD) issue caused by using subgraphs for prediction. Yet, these approaches typically rely on soft masks, which are inherently unable to fully eliminate label-irrelevant information, allowing redundant structures to leak into the mixup process and hindering the resolution of the OOD problem, thereby degrading explanation fidelity. In this work, we propose HPME, a Hard-Perturbation Mixup Explanation framework grounded in a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck, which leverages graph pooling to extract discrete explanatory subgraphs and to yield an information-capacity bound to thoroughly compress label-irrelevant components. Furthermore, we introduce a novel mixup strategy built upon structure-level replacement, generating in-distribution explanations to effectively mitigate the distribution shift. Extensive experiments on diverse tasks demonstrate that HPME achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating robust and interpretable explanations across both synthetic and real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: Proposes HPME: a Hard-Perturbation Mixup Explanation framework for GNNs that uses graph pooling to extract discrete subgraphs under a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck, providing an information-capacity bound to prune label-irrelevant content, and introduces a structure-level replacement mixup to yield in-distribution explanations; claims state-of-the-art robustness and interpretability across synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Increase trustworthiness and reliability of post-hoc explanations for GNNs by fully eliminating label-irrelevant information and mitigating distribution shift in mixup-based explanations, addressing the limitations of soft masks.
AI.method: Use graph pooling to derive discrete explanatory subgraphs; apply a generalized Graph Information Bottleneck to bound information flow and compress label-irrelevant components; introduce a hard-perturbation mixup with structure-level replacement to keep the explanations in-distribution.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show HPME achieves state-of-the-art performance in generating robust and interpretable explanations on both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: HPME effectively produces robust, faithful explanations while mitigating OOD issues in mixup-based explanations via hard perturbation and structure-level replacement; outperforms existing baselines in explainability quality and robustness.

title: Retrospective Harness Optimization: Improving LLM Agents via Self-Preference over Trajectory Rollouts
summary: AI agents rely on a harness of skills, tools, and workflows to solve complex problems. Continually improving this harness is essential for adapting to new tasks. However, existing optimization methods typically require ground-truth validation sets, yet such labeled data is difficult to acquire in practical deployment settings. To address this problem, we introduce Retrospective Harness Optimization (RHO), a self-supervised method that optimizes the agent harness using only past trajectories. Specifically, RHO selects a diverse coreset of challenging tasks from past trajectories and re-solves them in parallel. The agent analyzes these rollouts using self-validation and self-consistency, then generates candidate harness updates and selects the most effective one by its own pairwise self-preference. We evaluate RHO across three diverse domains, spanning software engineering, technical work, and knowledge work. Notably, a single optimization round improves the pass rate on SWE-Bench Pro from 59% to 78% without any external grading. Furthermore, our analysis demonstrates that RHO effectively targets prior failure modes. As a result, the optimized harness alters the agent's behavior patterns and sustains higher accuracy during long-horizon sessions.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised harness optimization (RHO) uses retrospective challenging-task coresets from past trajectories to re-solve and select harness updates, improving task success without labeled data (e.g., SWE-Bench Pro pass rate 59%→78%).
AI.motivation: Ground-truth validation data is scarce in deployment; a self-supervised, data-efficient method is needed to iteratively improve the agent's harness and long-horizon performance.
AI.method: Build a diverse coreset of hard tasks from past rollouts, re-solve them in parallel, analyze rollouts via self-validation and self-consistency, generate candidate harness updates, and select the best update using pairwise self-preference.
AI.result: Demonstrated across three domains; a single optimization round raises SWE-Bench Pro pass rate from 59% to 78% without external grading; analysis shows targeted prior failure modes and sustained higher accuracy in long-horizon sessions; harness behavior shifts accordingly.
AI.conclusion: RHO provides a domain-general, self-supervised approach to harness optimization, reducing reliance on labeled data while improving robustness and long-horizon performance.

title: Domain-Adapted Small Language Models with Hybrid Post-Processing: Achieving Cost-Efficient, Low-Latency Multi-Label Structured Prediction via LoRA Fine-Tuning on Scarce Data
summary:
Deploying frontier large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific structured evaluation tasks often incurs substantial latency, cost, and data privacy overhead. We present a hybrid framework that combines a fine-tuned small language model (LLaMA 3.1 8B, with only 2.05% trainable parameters via LoRA) and a deterministic rule-based post-processing layer. Trained on just 219 curated examples, the system is applied to multi-label compliance evaluation of conversational transcripts spanning 18 heterogeneous output fields. In blind evaluation on 53 previously unseen production transcripts, it achieves 100% JSON structural validity, 83.0% human-validated overall accuracy, and 100% accuracy on the most critical classification field. The proposed approach formalizes a hybrid neural-symbolic decomposition and introduces targeted hard-negative augmentation to improve performance on critical decision boundaries. Running on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, inference completes in approximately 2 seconds, which is 2-5x faster than frontier-model APIs. The system costs only $0.013 per evaluation compared with $0.025-$0.055 for proprietary alternatives, resulting in 46-76% cost savings. These results demonstrate that domain-adapted small language models, when combined with deterministic post-processing, can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while substantially reducing operational cost, latency, and privacy risk.
  Keywords: small language models, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, LoRA, domain adaptation, hybrid inference, compliance evaluation, structured output.

AI.tldr: Hybrid neural-symbolic system using a LoRA-tuned small LLM (LLaMA 3.1 8B) plus a rule-based post-processor for 18-field multi-label compliance; trained on 219 examples; achieves strong accuracy with low latency and cost on unseen data.
AI.motivation: Reduce latency, cost, and privacy risks of deploying frontier LLMs for domain-specific structured evaluation, while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.method: Fine-tune a small LLM (LLaMA 3.1 8B) with LoRA (2.05% trainable) using 219 curated examples; apply a deterministic rule-based post-processing layer; perform multi-label classification across 18 fields; augment with targeted hard-negative samples to improve boundary performance.
AI.result: On 53 unseen transcripts: 100% JSON structural validity; 83.0% overall accuracy (human-validated); 100% accuracy on the critical field; ~2 seconds inference on NVIDIA A100; 2–5x faster than frontier-model APIs; cost per evaluation $0.013 vs $0.025–$0.055 for proprietary options; 46–76% cost savings.
AI.conclusion: Domain-adapted small LLMs with deterministic post-processing can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while significantly reducing latency, cost, and privacy risk.

title: Towards World Models in Biomedical Research
summary: A central goal of biomedicine is to understand, predict and ultimately control the dynamic mechanisms by which biological systems respond to perturbations, disease progression and therapeutic intervention. Although foundation models and large language models have accelerated biomedical data interpretation, most current systems remain focused on static pattern recognition rather than prospective simulation of biological futures. Here we propose biomedical world models as a paradigm for AI-driven discovery. These models learn latent representations of molecular, cellular, tissue and clinical states, together with intervention-conditioned dynamics that allow future trajectories to be simulated before actions are taken. We discuss how biomedical world models could function as data engines, environment simulators and scientific planning substrates across applications including virtual cells, organoids, virtual patients and surgical simulation. We outline the data infrastructure, evaluation benchmarks, safety constraints and governance frameworks required. Biomedical world models may provide a foundation for simulation-guided, closed-loop and experimentally actionable biomedical discovery.

AI.tldr: Proposes biomedical world models that learn latent, intervention-conditioned dynamics to simulate future biological trajectories and guide discovery; outlines data, benchmarks, safety, and governance for simulation-driven biomedicine.
AI.motivation: Current AI focus on static pattern recognition falls short of prospective, actionable biomedical forecasting; need tools to simulate future states under perturbations to accelerate discovery and planning.
AI.method: Conceptual framework: learn latent representations across molecular to clinical states; model intervention-conditioned dynamics; treat models as data engines, environment simulators, and scientific planners; discuss infrastructure and benchmarks.
AI.result: Not empirical; outlines potential capabilities and a roadmap including data pipelines, evaluation metrics, safety constraints, governance.
AI.conclusion: Biomedical world models could enable simulation-guided, closed-loop, experimentally actionable discovery in biomedical domains, enabling virtual cells, organoids, patients, and surgical planning.

title: Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks for Counterfactual Outcome Prediction
summary: Longitudinal treatment decisions require predicting potential outcomes under future treatment sequences in the presence of time-varying confounding, heterogeneous patient dynamics, and limited domain-specific data. Existing longitudinal causal estimators typically train a new model for each cohort or simulator. We introduce Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks (CausalLongPFN), a prior-fitted in-context predictor for longitudinal causal prediction. The model is pretrained entirely on synthetic episodes sampled from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, exposing it to treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear state evolution, delayed effects, and cumulative treatment responses. At test time, CausalLongPFN is frozen: it conditions on support trajectories, a query history, and a proposed future treatment sequence, and returns a predictive distribution over future outcomes without gradient updates or propensity-model fitting. Multi-step predictions are obtained by recursively applying the one-step predictor under the specified treatment sequence. We evaluate on branchable cancer, HIV, and warfarin benchmarks with ground-truth counterfactual labels, and on factual-only rolling-origin prediction in MIMIC-III ICU trajectories. CausalLongPFN is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines on counterfactual benchmarks and performs strongly on factual MIMIC-III prediction, suggesting that broad synthetic causal pretraining can provide a useful frozen alternative when repeated domain-specific training is costly or impractical.

AI.tldr: A frozen, prior-fitted in-context predictor for longitudinal causal forecasting trained on synthetic temporal SCM episodes, enabling multi-step predictions without fine-tuning; competitive on counterfactual benchmarks and strong on factual MIMIC-III data.
AI.motivation: To address time-varying confounding and heterogeneous dynamics in longitudinal causal prediction while avoiding per-cohort model training, by leveraging broad synthetic priors to pretrain a reusable predictor.
AI.method: Pretrain CausalLongPFN on synthetic episodes sampled from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, capturing treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear dynamics, delays, and cumulative effects. At test time, freeze the model, condition on support trajectories, query history, and a proposed future treatment sequence, and output a predictive distribution over future outcomes without further training. Multi-step forecasts are obtained by recursive one-step predictions.
AI.result: CausalLongPFN is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines on counterfactual benchmarks (branchable cancer, HIV, warfarin) and performs strongly on factual MIMIC-III prediction, indicating that broad synthetic causal pretraining can serve as a viable frozen alternative when domain-specific retraining is costly.
AI.conclusion: Broad synthetic causal pretraining yields a useful frozen predictor for longitudinal causal forecasting, enabling flexible future-treatment evaluation without ongoing gradient updates or propensity-model fitting, and is effective across diverse biomedical benchmarks.

title: A Pre-Registered Causal Partition of Self-Consistency Elicitation and Reward Design in RLVR
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves reasoning even when the reward signal is spurious -- assigning credit to the group-plurality answer rather than a ground-truth verifier. Practitioners commonly interpret naive = acc(TRUE) - acc(RANDOM) as the reward-design effect. We prove this estimand is systematically biased: it conflates self-consistency elicitation (sharpening the policy toward its modal answer via majority pseudo-reward) with genuine reward-design signal. Using a controlled tabular-GRPO simulator we derive an exact telescoping decomposition total = null + elicit + rd and measure each term across five prior-strength levels. The reward-design fraction of the naive estimator ranges from 0.139 at weak prior (ps=0.20) to 0.05 at strong prior (ps=0.80), with the elicitation term flipping sign at the self-consistency crossover. A pre-registered 2x2x2 factorial confirms non-additivity (interaction ratio 0.385; AxC effect -0.089). A points-vs-bounds pilot gate shows strong-prior regimes are point-identified while near-crossover regimes are only bounded. Re-audits of two named published results yield ELICITATION DOMINATED (elicitation share 0.98) and REWARD DESIGN DOMINATED (rd share 1.18) verdicts respectively, demonstrating the diagnostic value of the partition. We pre-commit to submit regardless of flip outcome; a non-flip is a finding of equal standing. We release a reusable one-command harness for any alignment paper to run the same audit.

AI.tldr: A structured audit framework for RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) that decomposes the naive reward-design signal into null, elicitation, and reward-design components. It demonstrates that the common acc(TRUE)−acc(RANDOM) estimate is biased, shows non-additivity between elicitation and reward-design effects, and provides a controlled simulator with a pre-registered factorial design, plus re-audits of published results. It also delivers a reusable audit harness.
AI.motivation: Clarify what is being measured when assessing reward-design effects in alignment/reward learning, and separate genuine signal from self-consistency elicitation. Provide diagnostic tooling to audit and quantify contributions of different mechanisms.
AI.method: Use a controlled tabular-GRPO simulator to derive an exact telescoping decomposition total = null + elicit + rd. Evaluate across five prior-strength levels. Pre-registered 2x2x2 factorial to test non-additivity. A points-vs-bounds pilot gate to assess identifiability. Re-audits of two published results to attribute dominance (elicitation vs reward-design). Release a reusable one-command audit harness.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the naive reward-design estimator is biased due to conflating self-consistency elicitation with true reward-design signal; the reward-design fraction varies with prior strength (0.139 to 0.05), and the elicitation term can flip sign at crossover. The factorial reveals non-additivity (interaction ratio 0.385; AxC = -0.089). Strong-prior regimes are point-identifiable; near-crossover regimes are bounded. Re-audits indicate elicitation-dominated and reward-design-dominated outcomes in two cases. A reusable audit harness is released.
AI.conclusion: The proposed null+elicitation+rd decomposition provides a diagnostically valuable, robust framework to audit alignment claims about rewards. It exposes biases in naive estimators, demonstrates non-additivity, and offers practical tooling for rigorous evaluation of reward-design versus elicitation effects.

title: Multimodal Sexism Identification and Characterization using Large Language Models and Gradient Boosting
summary: We present the AILS-NTUA submission to the EXIST 2026 Lab at CLEF, addressing multimodal sexism identification and characterization in memes (Task 2) and short-form videos (Task 3). Our system follows a feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline built around gradient-boosted regression models and hierarchical post-processing. For memes, we combine visual, textual, demographic, biometric, and LLM-derived semantic indicators designed to capture high-level cues such as stereotyping, objectification, irony, and misogyny. For videos, we investigate the effect of feature selection, frame-based visual representations, OCR-based textual features, acoustic descriptors, and sensor-derived metadata. Development results show that focused LLM-derived semantic cues improve meme sexism identification, while video performance is highly sensitive to feature dimensionality and cross-modal noise. For videos, development results favor compact feature selection, but official test results show that this conclusion does not fully transfer to unseen data, where the unfiltered representation generalizes better. Overall, our findings highlight the usefulness of targeted semantic feature engineering for static memes and the need for more robust temporal modeling in noisy short-form video settings.

AI.tldr: A feature-engineered, late-fusion system for multimodal sexism detection in memes and short-form videos. Memes benefit from LLM-derived semantic cues; videos require careful feature dimensionality management and more robust temporal modeling.
AI.motivation: Address challenging multimodal sexism identification in memes (Task 2) and short-form videos (Task 3) under the EXIST 2026 Lab, focusing on cross-modal cues, noise handling, and generalization.
AI.method: Feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline using gradient-boosted regression models. For memes: combine visual, textual, demographic, biometric, and LLM-derived semantic indicators to capture stereotyping, objectification, irony, and misogyny. For videos: examine feature selection, frame-based visual representations, OCR-based textual features, acoustic descriptors, and sensor-derived metadata; apply hierarchical post-processing.
AI.result: Development results show that LLM-derived semantic cues improve meme sexism identification. Video performance is highly sensitive to feature dimensionality and cross-modal noise. In development, compact feature selection helps; on official test data, this does not fully transfer, with unfiltered representations generalizing better to unseen data.
AI.conclusion: Semantic feature engineering is useful for static memes, and robust temporal modeling is needed to handle noisy short-form videos.

title: Edit-R2: Context-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Turn Image Editing
summary: Text-guided image editing has advanced rapidly with diffusion models and unified multimodal foundation models. However, most existing methods remain confined to single-turn settings, overlooking the more realistic scenario of multi-turn in-context editing, where users iteratively refine an image through a sequence of instructions. In this setting, a model must follow each new instruction while preserving accumulated session-level constraints, challenged by two coupled failure modes: long-context dilution, where sparse textual constraints become difficult to recover from growing interleaved image-text histories, and state contamination, where earlier editing mistakes degrade subsequent generations. We introduce Edit-R2, a novel reinforcement learning post-training framework for unified multimodal models. Edit-R2 reconstructs the operative session intent, which effectively consolidates scattered historical constraints into an explicit reasoning trace before each editing turn. It further enables multi-turn RL over both reasoning and generation through a unified objective that jointly optimizes intent reconstruction generation in discrete text space and flow-matching image generation in continuous latent space, while a trajectory filtering mechanism suppresses corrupted rollouts to stabilize training under state contamination. To support systematic evaluation, we introduce MICE-Bench, a large-scale benchmark for multi-turn in-context editing with automated metrics for instruction following (IF), content consistency (CC), and global awareness (GA) over accumulated session constraints. Experiments show that Edit-R2 substantially improves multi-turn in-context editing and achieves competitive performance compared against strong baselines.

AI.tldr: Edit-R2 introduces an RL post-training framework for unified multimodal models to enable robust multi-turn in-context editing. It reconstructs explicit session intent before each edit and optimizes both discrete intent generation and continuous image generation, with trajectory filtering to handle state contamination. It also introduces MICE-Bench, a large-scale benchmark with automated metrics for instruction following, content consistency, and global awareness.
AI.motivation: To address long-context dilution and state contamination in multi-turn in-context image editing, where cumulative session constraints are difficult to recover and can be degraded by prior edits.
AI.method: Edit-R2 reconstructs the operative session intent as an explicit reasoning trace before each editing turn. It performs multi-turn RL over both reasoning (discrete text space for intent reconstruction) and generation (continuous latent space for image editing) with a unified objective that combines these signals. A trajectory filtering mechanism suppresses corrupted rollouts to stabilize training against state contamination. MICE-Bench provides automated metrics for instruction following, content consistency, and global awareness.
AI.result: Empirical results show substantial improvements in multi-turn in-context editing and competitive performance compared with strong baselines. MICE-Bench enables systematic evaluation of session-level constraints across accumulated editing history.
AI.conclusion: Edit-R2 offers a principled framework for reliable multi-turn in-context editing by explicit intent reconstruction and joint optimization over text and image generations, and the MICE-Bench benchmark enables robust, automated assessment of instruction following and constraint preservation.

title: Deep Learning-based 3D Oral Cavity Reconstruction Using 2D Intraoral Images
comment: 4 pages, 5 figures. English version of a paper presented at the Korea Multimedia Society Conference, November 2025
summary: Oral 3D modelling is one of the most essential stages in dentistry, and many different approaches, such as impression taking and intraoral scanning, are commonly used for this phase, each with notable limitations. Impression taking, which involves placing alginate or silicone material in a tray and inserting it into the patient's oral cavity to form a negative mold, suffers from significant patient discomfort, material deformation errors, and difficulties in storage and transportation. Intraoral scanners, which directly scan oral structures in real time using structured light or laser technology, produce state-of-the-art results but are associated with substantially high equipment costs. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a software-based approach that reconstructs a 3D oral model using only ten 2D intraoral images captured from different angles, requiring no dedicated hardware devices. The proposed method reduces cost, eliminates the need for physical scanning equipment, minimises patient discomfort, and enables automated 3D reconstruction. The model is trained on the publicly available Dental3DS dataset, comprising 950 upper jaw samples, and employs MobileNetV2 as the image encoder combined with Multi-head Attention for multi-view feature fusion. The proposed model achieves an accuracy of 77.49%, measured by nearest-neighbor matching with a distance threshold of 0.035. However, predicted vertices tend to concentrate in high-density regions of the ground truth, resulting in uneven point distribution across the reconstructed model.

AI.tldr: Software-based 3D oral reconstruction from ten 2D intraoral images using MobileNetV2 and multi-head attention; evaluated on Dental3DS with 77.49% accuracy (threshold 0.035); final models show uneven point distribution with high-density concentration.
AI.motivation: Lower costs and patient discomfort by avoiding physical impression-taking and expensive intraoral scanners; enable automated 3D modeling with a software-only approach using publicly available data.
AI.method: Use ten 2D intraoral images from different angles. Encoder: MobileNetV2. Fusion: Multi-head attention for aggregating multi-view features. Dataset: Dental3DS (950 upper jaw samples). Evaluation: nearest-neighbor accuracy with distance threshold 0.035.
AI.result: Achieves 77.49% accuracy. Predicted vertices concentrate in high-density regions of ground truth, leading to uneven point distribution across the reconstructed model.
