AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a cost-effective, hardware-free route to 3D oral reconstruction with automated processing, but point distribution uniformity remains an issue; future work should address even vertex spacing and possibly improve global shape fidelity.

title: SALT: When More Rollouts Don't Help in Group-Based Policy Optimization and How to Make Them Matter
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) often adopts GRPO-style group-relative updates, sampling multiple rollouts per prompt to construct normalized learning signals. However, merely increasing the number of rollouts does not reliably strengthen learning: under GRPO-style group normalization, per-rollout policy-gradient features can concentrate into a low-rank, signed geometry, causing substantial cancellation during aggregation and weakening the effective update. We address this failure mode with SALT, a Subspace-Adaptive geometry pLug-in componenT that uses sample-wise gradient geometry to reweight the coefficients of group-relative updates. SALT estimates a dominant shared subspace from the mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies the residual channel when signed cancellation is severe. Across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales, SALT improves effective update geometry and performance without modifying the reward model or the rollout sampling procedure

AI.tldr: SALT improves GRPO-style RLVR by adaptively reweighting group-relative updates via a shared subspace and residual channels, mitigating low-rank cancellation and boosting learning without changing sampling or rewards.
AI.motivation: GRPO-style group normalization can cause per-rollout gradient geometry to collapse into a low-rank, signed structure, leading to cancellation and weak updates when sampling many rollouts. A geometry-aware reweighting scheme is needed to preserve effective gradient signals.
AI.method: SALT estimates a dominant shared subspace from the mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies the residual channel when signed cancellation is severe. It operates as a plug-in that reweights coefficients without changing reward models or rollout sampling.
AI.result: Across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales, SALT improves the effective update geometry and overall performance.
AI.conclusion: SALT provides a robust, architecture-agnostic plug-in that mitigates gradient cancellation in GRPO-style RLVR by exploiting sample-wise gradient geometry, enabling stronger updates without altering data collection or reward modeling.

title: Bidirectional Search for Longest Paths: Case for Front-to-Front Heuristics
summary: Bidirectional heuristic search can potentially reduce search effort for problems amenable to backward search. Therein, it is well-known that front-to-front heuristics can reduce the number of node expansions, but their overhead is so high that overall runtime almost always increases. We propose BiXDFBnB, a bidirectional depth-first branch-and-bound algorithm that adapts the Single-Frontier Bidirectional Search (SFBDS) framework - originally developed for shortest-path (MIN) problems - to the Generalized Longest Simple Path (GLSP) setting. Because SFBDS inherently operates on paired states, front-to-front (F2F) heuristic evaluation arises naturally and avoids the overhead typically associated with bidirectional frontier management. We show that this adaptation can be successfully applied to maximization (MAX) problems while efficiently handling overlapping constraints. BiXDFBnB is applied to several types of longest-path problems: Longest Simple Path (LSP), Snakes, and Coil-in-the-Box (CIB). Empirical evaluation shows that the new algorithm frequently reduces the number of node expansions and, in some cases, also improves overall runtime.

AI.tldr: BiXDFBnB: a bidirectional depth-first B&B algorithm that adapts SFBDS to generalized longest simple path problems, using front-to-front heuristic evaluation to reduce node expansions, applied to LSP, Snakes, and Coil-in-the-Box with empirical gains in expansion reduction and occasional runtime improvements.
AI.motivation: Reduce search effort in bidirectional searches for generalized longest path maximization problems where front-to-front heuristics incur high overhead in traditional bidirectional frontier management.
AI.method: Extend the Single-Frontier Bidirectional Search (SFBDS) framework (originally for MIN problems) to GLSP/MAX. Leverage paired states to enable natural front-to-front heuristic evaluation, reducing the overhead of bidirectional frontier management. Adapt and apply the approach to LSP, Snakes, and Coil-in-the-Box (CIB) with overlapping constraints.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows BiXDFBnB frequently reduces the number of node expansions and, in some cases, also improves overall runtime across the tested problem classes.
AI.conclusion: BiXDFBnB demonstrates that front-to-front heuristic evaluation can be effectively integrated into GLSP/MAX via an SFBDS-based adaptation, yielding practical search-efficiency gains across multiple longest-path problems.

title: Robust and sparse support vector machine via hybrid truncated loss for supervised classification
summary: The support vector machine (SVM) is a widely used classifier, but choosing an appropriate loss function remains difficult. Convex losses such as the hinge loss and least-squares loss are sensitive to outliers, while bounded non-convex losses often lead to high computational cost. To address this, we propose a hybrid truncated loss function ($L_{\mathrm{ht}}$) that is both sparse and bounded, and build the $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM model for single-view classification. We introduce the P-stationary point and use it to establish the first-order necessary and sufficient optimality conditions. Based on these conditions, we design an alternating direction method of multipliers with a working-set strategy that reduces computational cost and achieves global convergence. We further extend $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM to multi-view learning by adding structural information and view weights, resulting in Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM, which follows both the consensus and complementarity principles. Experiments on synthetic, real-world, and image datasets show that $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM achieves higher accuracy with fewer support vectors and better noise robustness than five single-view methods, while Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM outperforms six multi-view methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

AI.tldr: Introduces a hybrid truncated loss for SVMs that is sparse and bounded to improve robustness and efficiency; extends to multi-view learning with consensus/complementarity; achieves higher accuracy and fewer support vectors in experiments.
AI.motivation: To address the sensitivity of convex loss SVMs to outliers and the high cost of non-convex bounded losses, by proposing a sparse bounded loss and an efficient optimization framework that also extends to multi-view settings.
AI.method: Define L_ht as a sparse, bounded loss. Derive P-stationary point and first-order necessary/sufficient optimality conditions. Solve via an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) with a working-set strategy to reduce computation and guarantee global convergence. Extend to multi-view (MvL_ht-SVM) by incorporating structure and view weights under consensus and complementarity principles.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic, real-world and image datasets show L_ht-SVM achieves higher accuracy with fewer support vectors and better noise robustness than five single-view methods. MvL_ht-SVM outperforms six multi-view methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
AI.conclusion: The proposed L_ht-SVM provides robust, sparse classification with efficient optimization and strong multi-view performance; the framework integrates structural information and view weighting while maintaining convergence guarantees; future work could explore deeper theoretical guarantees, sensitivity to hyperparameters, and scalability.

title: The Self-Correction Illusion: LLMs Correct Others but Not Themselves
summary: Recent work shows that LLM agents struggle to correct errors in their own reasoning traces yet show markedly higher correction rates when identical claims appear under external sources. We ask whether this asymmetry reflects a capability deficit or a role-label artifact: does an agent's willingness to correct a wrong claim depend causally on the chat-template role that carries it, rather than on the claim's content? Our setup keeps the erroneous claim byte-identical across all conditions (SHA-256 verified) and varies only its wrapping role: the agent's own \role{<thought>}, a \role{user} message, a \role{tool} response, or a \role{system <memory>} block. Across 13 model-domain cells covering seven model families and three domains ($n{=}30$ paired tasks per cell), relabeling the claim from \role{<thought>} to an external role lifts the explicit-correction rate by 23 to 93 percentage points, with 10 of 13 cells reaching $p{<}0.001$. Further experiments confirm that the effect is asymmetric, mechanistically decomposable, and robust across domains. The failure to self-correct is not a cognitive deficit; it is a chat-template artifact. We exploit this artifact by designing a prompt-structure-only intervention that requires no training and no model modification, with its strongest role label being domain-dependent: \role{<memory>} dominates on math, while a plain \role{user} message dominates on logical deduction.

AI.tldr: Relabeling the same erroneous claim with external chat roles significantly boosts explicit self-correction; self-correction gaps arise from chat-template artifacts, not cognitive deficit.
AI.motivation: To determine whether LLM self-correction limitations are due to cognitive incapacity or prompt/role structure, by testing if role labels causally affect correction rates while the claim content remains identical.
AI.method: Controlled, multi-cell experiments across 13 model-domain cells (7 model families, 3 domains; n=30 paired tasks per cell). The erroneous claim is byte-identical (SHA-256 verified) and wrapped in different roles: the agent's own <thought>, a user message, a tool response, or a system <memory> block. Measure explicit-correction rate and test for significance (found p<0.001 in 10 of 13 cells).
AI.result: Relabeling from <thought> to external roles raises explicit-correction rates by 23–93 percentage points; the effect is asymmetric, decomposable, and robust across domains. The failure to self-correct appears as a chat-template artifact rather than a cognitive deficit.
AI.conclusion: Self-correction failure is a chat-template artifact; propose a prompt-structure intervention that requires no training or model modification. The intervention’s strongest role label is domain-dependent: <memory> dominates on math tasks, while a plain user message dominates on logical deduction.

title: Global-Local Monte Carlo Tree Search in Vision-Language Models for Text-to-3D Indoor Scene Generation
summary: Large Vision-Language Models have achieved significant reasoning performance in various tasks.However, there are few studies on text-to-3D indoor scene generation with LVLMs. The main challenge is that prevailing LVLM-based methods employ chain-of-thought sequential decision mechanisms that cannot revise earlier decisions, causing error propagation.In this paper, we consider the task as a planning problem constrained by spatial and layout commonsense.To solve this problem, we model it as a tree search problem with global and local trees, which differs from existing sequential decision-making approaches.In the global tree, we place each object iteratively and explore multiple attempts like humans furnishing a room, where the problem space is represented as a tree.To effectively search the tree, we propose a hierarchical scene representation and a PRM-guided MCTS method.The hierarchical representation abstracts a scene into room level, region level, floor object level, and supported object level.The PRM-guided MCTS method uses the PRM to prune unnecessary branches and the MCTS algorithm to balance exploration and exploitation to get an optimal solution with fewer attempts.In the local tree, it further decomposes the placement of each object into finer sub-steps, including the specific placement parameters.To make the whole appearance of the scene consistent, we leverage pre-trained diffusion image generative models to predict textures for all the objects in the scene.As existing benchmarks for text-to-3D indoor scene generation remain limited in scale and diversity, we collect a new large-scale diverse dataset that contains 65 scene types and 3,250 instructions with diverse sizes, layouts, and styles, named 3DTindo-bench, to better assess the capability of the state-of-the-art models. Our experiments show that our method generates more realistic 3D scenes than state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: Reframes text-to-3D indoor scene generation with LVLMs as a planning problem using global/local tree search, hierarchical scene representation, and PRM-guided MCTS, augmented by diffusion-based textures and a new large-scale dataset (3DTindo-bench); reports improved realism over baselines.
AI.motivation: Addresses error propagation from sequential LVLM decision-making in text-to-3D scene generation by introducing global planning with spatial commonsense constraints and a scalable benchmark to evaluate diverse indoor scenes.
AI.method: Introduce a two-level tree search (global and local). Global tree places objects iteratively with multiple attempts; hierarchical scene representation (room level, region level, floor object level, supported object level); PRM-guided MCTS to prune branches and balance exploration/exploitation; local tree decomposes object placement into sub-steps and parameter settings; textures predicted by pre-trained diffusion models; new dataset 3DTindo-bench with 65 scene types and 3,250 instructions.
AI.result: Empirical results show the proposed method produces more realistic 3D indoor scenes than state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: The planning-based, hierarchical, and diffusion-texture augmented framework with PRM-guided MCTS effectively improves text-to-3D indoor scene generation and is supported by a new, diverse benchmark dataset, addressing key limitations of previous LVLM-based methods.

title: Consistency Training Along the Transformer Stack
summary: Consistency training encourages models to behave similarly across different contexts, and has shown promise for reducing misalignment. We broaden the scope of consistency training in two ways. First, we introduce two new internal consistency targets: MLP Consistency Training (MLPCT), which matches post-activation MLP states, and Attention Consistency Training (AttCT), which matches per-head attention distributions. Second, we apply consistency training to four additional safety threats: persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment. Across several models and threat settings, we find that consistency training reduces misalignment well beyond the sycophancy and jailbreak settings studied in prior work. We also find cases of cross-threat generalization, where training against one failure mode improves robustness to another, and identify a shared residual-stream mechanism underlying ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT, while distinguishing BCT as mechanistically distinct. Our results suggest that consistency training is a flexible and extensible framework for alignment, capable of unifying defenses against a broader class of model pathologies.

AI.tldr: Consistency training extended via two internal targets (MLPCT and AttCT) and applied to four new safety threats, achieving reduced misalignment and cross-threat generalization; identifies a shared residual-stream mechanism and distinctions from BCT, suggesting a flexible, extensible defense framework.
AI.motivation: To broaden alignment defenses by extending consistency training to internal representations and to new adversarial and misalignment threats, aiming for robust, cross-threat protection.
AI.method: Introduce MLP Consistency Training (MLPCT) matching post-activation MLP states and Attention Consistency Training (AttCT) matching per-head attention distributions. Apply these targets to four threats—persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment—across several model settings to evaluate robustness and cross-threat transfer.
AI.result: Consistency training reduces misalignment beyond prior sycophancy/jailbreak settings; exhibits cross-threat generalization where training on one failure mode improves others; reveals a shared residual-stream mechanism underlying ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT, while BCT remains mechanistically distinct.
AI.conclusion: Consistency training is a flexible, extensible framework for alignment that can unify defenses against a broader class of model pathologies, with potential cross-threat robustness and a unified underlying mechanism.

title: Framing, Judging, Steering: An Assessable Competency Model for Teach-ing Students to Reason With Generative AI
summary: Generative AI makes answers easy and understanding hard, and uncritical use invites cognitive offloading. Schools still measure unaided performance, yet the real task is to produce good work with AI: framing an ill-defined task, judging the output, and steering the model toward a better result. This ability is rarely assessed in its own right; where measured, it collapses into one "prompting" score that cannot diagnose why AI use succeeds or fails. We propose CoRe-3 (Co-Reasoning), a competency model factoring productive AI use into three assessable skills we abbreviate FJS: Framing (specifying an ill-defined task before invoking AI), Judging (evaluating output for errors and unstated assumptions), and Steering (iteratively redirecting the model). Its distinguishing claim is the separation of pre-generation Framing from post-generation Steering, with Judging as the gate between. We ground the skills in theory, state five testable propositions, and instantiate them in CoReasoningLab, an open platform that presents flawed AI output and scores them independently. Over simulated learners (generated and graded by different models), the skills dissociate: each tracks its own manipulated competence while staying flat in the others, and grades become correlated when one competence is shared across all three (convergent and discriminant validity), across grader backends from two providers. Human-rater agreement and outcomes are next; we release the instrument, data, and protocol.

AI.tldr: CoRe-3 (Co-Reasoning) separates productive AI use into three assessable competencies—Framing (pre-generation task framing), Judging (output evaluation), and Steering (iterative model redirection)—with Framing and Steering separated by Judging as a gate. Instantiated in CoReasoningLab, it shows dissociation among skills in simulated learners, supports convergent/discriminant validity, and will be tested with human raters; instrument, data, and protocol are released for broader validation.
AI.motivation: Generative AI promotes easy answers but opaque cognitive processes; current assessments focus on unaided performance or a single 'prompting' score that fails to diagnose why AI use succeeds or fails. There is a need for an interpretable, diagnostic competency model to assess how learners frame problems, judge outputs, and steer interactions with AI.
AI.method: Define three competencies (Framing, Judging, Steering) forming CoRe-3; ground in theory; propose five testable propositions; implement in CoReasoningLab an open platform presenting flawed AI outputs and scoring them on each competency. Use simulated learners (different models) to test dissociation and validity, with multiple grader backends (two providers); plan human-rater agreement studies and release instrument, data, and protocol.
AI.result: In simulations, the three competencies dissociate: each manipulated competence affects its own trajectory while others stay flat; convergent and discriminant validity observed when a single competence is shared; grades correlate across different grader backends. Demonstrates the potential diagnostic power of the framework; an open platform is established for ongoing validation.
AI.conclusion: The abstract introduces an open, diagnostic framework for assessing AI-assisted work, with a validated-seeming dissociation of key Competences in simulation and a plan to extend validation with human raters; the instrument and materials will be released to enable broader replication and instrument refinement.

title: ReSAGE-PAR: Representational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
comment: Under review at IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology (TCSVT)
summary: To address the limited diversity and data scarcity in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR), we explore image synthesis using diffusion models guided by attribute-based prompts. While this enables the controlled generation of pedestrian images, it faces two critical challenges: (i) the domain gap between high-quality pre-training data and low-resolution, non-standard surveillance crops, and (ii) the need for reliable attribute verification to prevent generative hallucinations. In this paper, we introduce a robust generate-score-autolabel pipeline called ReSAGE-PAR (REpresentational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in PAR) that bridges this domain gap and enables scalable, high-fidelity dataset expansion. First, we adapt pre-trained diffusion models to native PAR resolutions using a tailored LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach. Second, we extract vision-language alignment scores between the generated images and their conditioning prompts, utilizing a comprehensive prompting strategy that includes label-consistent and inconsistent complements. Finally, we formulate a Bayesian classifier that converts these continuous scores into reliable binary pseudo-labels. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of ReSAGE-PAR in preserving spatial priors and verifying attributes. When integrated into PAR training, ReSAGE-PAR consistently yields significant improvements-achieving gains of up to 8.7% on standard backbones and pushing state-of-the-art frameworks to new performance levels. This proves its value as an architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement. The complete codebase for ReSAGE-PAR is publicly available at http://www-vpu.eps.uam.es/publications/ReSAGE-PAR.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based PAR data expansion via ReSAGE-PAR, combining LoRA-driven image adaptation to native PAR resolution, vision-language prompt-based alignment scoring, and a Bayesian pseudo-labeling stage. It bridges domain gaps, enables scalable, high-fidelity synthetic data with attribute verification, and yields up to 8.7% gains on standard backbones; code is public.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and domain shift in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition by generating controlled, high-fidelity synthetic data and ensuring reliable attribute labels to prevent hallucinations.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) adapt pre-trained diffusion models to native PAR resolutions using a LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach; (2) compute vision-language alignment scores between each generated image and its conditioning prompts using a robust prompting strategy that includes label-consistent and label-inconsistent complements; (3) fit a Bayesian classifier to map continuous alignment scores to binary pseudo-labels. The pipeline yields scalable synthetic data and integrates into standard PAR training in an architecture-agnostic manner.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show preservation of spatial priors and reliable attribute verification. When used for PAR training, ReSAGE-PAR delivers significant performance gains (up to 8.7%) on standard backbones and pushes state-of-the-art frameworks to new highs. Complete codebase is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: ReSAGE-PAR provides an architecture-agnostic, scalable solution to enhance PAR via controllable, high-fidelity synthetic data with verifiable attributes, addressing domain gaps and reducing hallucinations.

title: Beyond Vector Similarity: A Structural Analysis of Graph-Augmented Retrieval for Industrial Knowledge Graphs
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) fails systematically on queries requiring structural reasoning over interconnected entities. We compare eight retrieval architectures for aerospace supply chain intelligence, progressing from text retrieval through graph traversal to graph computation. Using a 46-node knowledge graph with 64 typed edges, we evaluate 23 queries across 10 intent categories and demonstrate that five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. Our central finding is the operator vocabulary thesis: the barrier to LLM-based graph reasoning is not model intelligence but the computational operators available as tools. An LLM Query Planner with 9 typed traversal primitives outperforms bespoke handlers (F1 = 0.632 vs. 0.472) while generalizing to unseen queries. Adding 6 graph computation tools, the LLM selectively adopts them for exactly the query categories where traversal fails. We also identify a measurement gap: entity-level F1 systematically underscores structural queries where comprehensive answers are correct.

AI.tldr: RAG struggles with structured reasoning over interconnected entities; an LLM planner using a small set of traversal primitives outperforms bespoke handlers, and adding graph-computation tools helps only where traversal fails; vector retrieval cannot reach several query classes; entity-level F1 reveals measurement gaps.
AI.motivation: Evaluate limitations of retrieval architectures for structured reasoning on a knowledge-graph-based aerospace supply chain task and test whether explicit computational operators enable LLM-based graph reasoning.
AI.method: Compare eight retrieval architectures ranging from text retrieval to graph traversal to graph computation on a 46-node knowledge graph with 64 typed edges. 23 queries across 10 intent categories. Implement an LLM Query Planner with 9 typed traversal primitives, then incrementally add 6 graph computation tools. Measure performance using F1, assess generalization to unseen queries, and analyze entity-level F1.
AI.result: The LLM Query Planner with traversal primitives achieves higher F1 (0.632) than bespoke handlers (0.472), and generalizes to unseen queries. The addition of 6 graph computation tools leads the LLM to selectively adopt them for query categories where traversal fails. Five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. An operator vocabulary thesis is supported: the barrier to LLM-based graph reasoning is the set of available computational operators rather than model intelligence. A measurement gap is identified: entity-level F1 underscores structural queries where comprehensive answers are correct.
AI.conclusion: Structured reasoning with LLMs hinges on a usable operator vocabulary; explicit traversal primitives plus selective graph computations enable robust performance and generalization, whereas pure vector retrieval is insufficient for interconnected-entity queries.

title: Texture-preserving implicit neural representation for Cone beam CT truncated reconstruction
summary: Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) frequently suffers from data truncation, which introduces severe artifacts and limits the effective field of view (FOV). Existing deep learning methods for truncated cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) reconstruction suffer from serious limitations, including a strict reliance on supervised ground truth and a failure to account for continuous 3D spatial truncation variations. To address these challenges, we introduce a self-supervised 3D reconstruction framework based on neural scene representations. By directly mapping spatial coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, our approach inherently bypasses traditional filtering and backprojection operations, thereby fundamentally eliminating truncation-induced ring artifacts while enabling robust continuous 3D data extrapolation. However, coordinate networks are susceptible to an inherent spectral bias, which leads to a severe loss of clinically vital high-frequency textures. To resolve this bottleneck, we further incorporate a physics-based iterative refinement module into the neural scene representation architecture. Leveraging the artifact-free, extrapolated volume from the coordinate network as an optimal initialization, this module progressively re-extracts and injects high-frequency structural information from the original projections back into the volume. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method successfully unifies the exceptional artifact suppression and extrapolation capabilities of neural networks with the high-fidelity detail preservation of iterative algorithms.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised 3D CBCT reconstruction framework uses neural scene representations to map coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, bypassing backprojection to suppress truncation artifacts and enable 3D extrapolation. It suffers from spectral bias in coordinate networks, which is mitigated by a physics-based iterative refinement module that reinjects high-frequency details from projections. Experiments on simulated and real data show strong artifact suppression and faithful detail preservation.
AI.motivation: Truncated CBCT data lead to severe artifacts and limited effective FOV; supervised 3D CNN or reconstruction methods depend on ground-truth data and fail to handle continuous 3D truncation variations. A self-supervised, coordinate-based approach aims to intrinsically model 3D space and extrapolate beyond measured data while avoiding filtering/backprojection artifacts.
AI.method: A 3D coordinate-network maps spatial coordinates to radiodensity, trained with projection supervision to bypass traditional filtering/backprojection. The method performs continuous 3D data extrapolation to overcome truncation. To address spectral bias and loss of high-frequency textures, a physics-based iterative refinement module uses the artifact-free extrapolated volume as initialization and iteratively re-injects high-frequency information from the original projections back into the volume.
AI.result: Experiments on simulated and real-world CBCT datasets demonstrate effective artifact suppression, robust 3D extrapolation beyond the measured FOV, and preservation of high-frequency structural details when the iterative refinement is applied.
AI.conclusion: The approach unifies the strengths of neural scene representations (artifact suppression and extrapolation) with iterative, physics-informed refinement (high-frequency fidelity), offering robust truncated CBCT reconstruction without traditional filtering artifacts.

title: Deciphering Two Training Clocks in Grokking via Deep Linear Network Theory with Conditional ReLU Reduction
summary: Grokking suggests that fitting the training data and learning a simple underlying rule may occur on different time scales. We formalize this phenomenon by separating the fast decay of the classification loss from the slower simplification of the learned representation, and we call the resulting pair of stopping times two training clocks. For deep linear networks, we show that a post-margin gap-growth or one-step tail-contraction condition reduces the cross-entropy loss to level epsilon on a logarithmic time scale. In contrast, when layerwise weight decay is present, the induced regularization on the end-to-end map can be expressed as a Schatten-type penalty; under a sharp late-time Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail, this structural energy closes on a polynomial time scale. The two clocks, therefore, separate fitting from representation simplification. We then explain how the same mechanism can appear in ReLU MLPs. In regions where the activation patterns on the training set remain fixed, the network reduces to a linear model in the active coordinates. In a two-layer ReLU embedding model, chain-rule estimates further show that the classifier head can receive larger effective gradients than the embedding block under controlled downstream norms. This supports a two-stage mechanism in which the classifier fits first, while the representation continues to simplify later. We use modular addition as the main experimental setting. The deep linear theory provides the rigorous core of the analysis. But the ReLU results are formulated as conditional reductions that account for empirical behavior without claiming a global proof for nonlinear training dynamics.

AI.tldr: Grokking arises from two distinct training clocks: rapid loss minimization and slower representation simplification, yielding different time scales for fitting and abstraction; theory in deep linear nets and conditional ReLU results explain this two-stage dynamic, with experiments in modular addition supporting the mechanism.
AI.motivation: Explain grokking by formalizing a separation between fast and slow dynamics in learning trajectories, and provide a theoretical framework that links loss decay to representation evolution across both linear and nonlinear (ReLU) architectures.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze deep linear networks under post-margin gap-growth or one-step tail-contraction conditions; show cross-entropy loss reaches epsilon on a logarithmic time scale. Introduce layerwise weight decay leading to a Schatten-type penalty, with a Kurdyka–Łojasiewicz tail yielding polynomial-time convergence. Extend to ReLU MLPs by examining regions with fixed activation patterns where the network behaves linearly on active coordinates; in a two-layer ReLU embedding model, use chain-rule gradient estimates to bound downstream vs. embedding gradients. Empirically illustrate with modular addition as the main setting; treat deep linear results as rigorous core, and present nonlinear results as conditional reductions rather than full proofs.
AI.result: In deep linear nets, a post-margin gap-growth or tail-contraction mechanism reduces cross-entropy to epsilon on a logarithmic time scale. Layerwise weight decay induces a Schatten-type regularization on the end-to-end map, yielding polynomial-time convergence under a sharp late-time KL tail. The two clocks separate fitting from representation simplification. In ReLU MLPs, in regions of fixed activation patterns the network reduces to a linear model in active coordinates; in a two-layer ReLU embedding model, downstream gradients can dominate the embedding block under controlled norms, supporting a two-stage mechanism where the classifier fits first and the representation simplifies later. Experiments with modular addition corroborate the core linear theory and show conditional reductions for nonlinear dynamics rather than a general proof.
AI.conclusion: Two distinct training clocks underpin grokking: fast fitting of training data and slower simplification of representations, with the former captured rigorously in deep linear models and the latter described (as conditional reductions) in nonlinear ReLU settings. This framework clarifies the observed two-stage learning dynamics and highlights the limits of current nonlinear analyses while offering a robust mechanism for two-stage generalization behavior.

title: LoomVideo: Unifying Multimodal Inputs into Video Generation and Editing
summary: Developing unified video generation and editing models capable of interpreting interleaved multimodal inputs is a promising yet challenging frontier field. Existing unified frameworks predominantly rely on massive models (typically 13B parameters or more) and incorporate source video conditions for editing by concatenating sequence tokens. This concatenation inevitably doubles the sequence length, quadrupling the computational complexity of the self-attention mechanism and introducing prohibitive overhead. To address these bottlenecks, we present LoomVideo, a highly efficient 5B-parameter unified architecture for both video generation and editing. LoomVideo replaces the standard text encoder with a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) and employs Deepstack injection mechanism to align multi-layer MLLM features with the Diffusion Transformer (DiT). Crucially, we introduce a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning approach for video editing. By scaling and directly adding the clean source video latent to the noised target latent, this elegant design eliminates the need for token concatenation, drastically reducing computational cost while maintaining robust capabilities for complex, non-rigid edits. Furthermore, a Negative Temporal RoPE strategy is seamlessly integrated to handle multiple reference images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our compact 5B model achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across comprehensive benchmarks, exhibiting exceptional superiority in e-commerce and fashion generation scenarios. Benefiting from the zero-overhead conditioning mechanism, LoomVideo achieves at least a 5.41x acceleration in inference speed compared to models of similar capabilities, paving the way for highly practical and efficient video foundation models.

AI.tldr: LoomVideo is a 5B unified video generation/editing model that avoids token concatenation via Scale-and-Add conditioning and MLLM-based encodings, achieving large speedups (≥5.41x) while delivering competitive video generation performance, especially in fashion/e-commerce tasks.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational bottlenecks of existing unified video models that rely on large parameter counts and sequence-token concatenation, which inflates self-attention costs and memory, hindering practical deployment.
AI.method: Use a Multimodal LLM as the text encoder; apply Deepstack injection to align multi-layer MLLM features with the Diffusion Transformer; introduce zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning to merge source and target latents without token concatenation; incorporate Negative Temporal RoPE to handle multiple reference images.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance across standard benchmarks; demonstrates strong performance in e-commerce and fashion video generation; 5B model with substantial inference speedups (≥5.41x) compared with similarly-capable models.
AI.conclusion: Validates a compact, efficient architecture for unified video generation/editing; reduces compute cost without sacrificing quality, enabling practical deployment; future directions include broader modality support, robustness to diverse edits, and scaling considerations.

title: RedditPersona: A Modular Framework for Community-Conditioned LLM Adaptation from Reddit
summary: Community-conditioned language model adaptation requires choices about data collection, community definition, and evaluation that are currently made independently in each study, making it hard to compare assumptions or reuse artifacts. We present RedditPersona, a modular framework that standardizes these choices: it collects Reddit posts and comments, profiles active users, partitions them under five grouping strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, and interaction-based), trains a parameter-efficient adapter per strategy via QLoRA, and evaluates them under a shared metric suite spanning fluency, fidelity, distributional alignment, and community identifiability. Applied to 112 subreddits in the urban well-being domain (301,429 user profiles, 16M+ comments), we find that adapters' behavioral identifiability tracks each strategy's intrinsic agreement with the subreddit baseline, and that a consistent trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity to real text holds across all five strategies. The code and configuration files are available at: https://github.com/Ahghaffari/redditpersona.

AI.tldr: RedditPersona is a modular framework that standardizes data collection, community definition, and evaluation for community-conditioned language model adaptation, using five partitioning strategies and QLoRA adapters; applied to 112 subreddits with 301k+ users and 16M+ comments, revealing a trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity, with code released.
AI.motivation: To enable comparability and reusability across studies of community-conditioned language model adaptation by standardizing data collection, community partitioning, and evaluation.
AI.method: Collect Reddit posts/comments, profile active users, partition users under five strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, interaction-based), train a parameter-efficient adapter per strategy via QLoRA, evaluate with a shared metric suite (fluency, fidelity, distributional alignment, community identifiability).
AI.result: Adapters’ behavioral identifiability tracks how well each strategy agrees with the subreddit baseline; a consistent trade-off exists between identifiability and distributional similarity to real text across all strategies; applied to 112 subreddits (≈301k profiles, 16M+ comments).
AI.conclusion: RedditPersona standardizes community-conditioned LM adaptation workflows, enabling cross-study comparability; five partitioning strategies with QLoRA adapters reveal a robust identifiability–distributional similarity trade-off, with code and configurations publicly available.

title: LLM-Conditioned Synthesis of Pathological Gaits via Structured Gait-Language Representations
comment: Accepted at CVPR MOMA Workshop 2026 and selected for spotlight presentation at the workshop
summary: Pathological gait datasets remain scarce due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and movement variability. Our work presents a multimodal LLM-guided framework for pathology-aware 3D gait data synthesis from structured textual descriptions. The proposed method generates fixed-length synthetic skeleton-based gait sequences for pathological gait classification tasks. The framework combines motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, LLM-based semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation. A key contribution is the proposed pathological tokeniser, which is designed to preserve pathology-specific motion characteristics during discrete representation learning. Experiments suggest that the proposed synthetic sequences improve downstream classification for recurrent classifiers when combined with real data. The best result is obtained using a GRU classifier trained with real and synthetic samples, achieving 92.77\% accuracy under a leave-one-subject-out protocol.

AI.tldr: A multimodal framework uses LLM-guided, pathology-aware synthesis to generate fixed-length 3D gait sequences from text descriptions, augmenting real data to improve pathology classification; introduces a novel pathological tokeniser; yields best 92.77% accuracy with a GRU under leave-one-subject-out.
AI.motivation: Gait pathology datasets are scarce due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and high inter-subject variability. Synthetic data can augment training and stabilize models.
AI.method: A multimodal pipeline that tokenises motion, condition language on pathology, uses LLM-based semantic augmentation, and performs language-to-gait generation. A key component is a pathological tokeniser designed to preserve pathology-specific motion characteristics during discrete representation learning; generates fixed-length skeleton-based gait sequences for classification.
AI.result: Synthetic sequences improve downstream classification for recurrent classifiers when combined with real data. Best result: GRU classifier trained on real+synthetic data achieves 92.77% accuracy under leave-one-subject-out.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables pathology-aware data augmentation for gait analysis, leveraging text-to-gait generation and LLM augmentation to mitigate data scarcity; cosine or other metrics of realism and generalization should be evaluated in future work, along with robustness to distribution shifts and privacy considerations.

title: Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents
summary: Despite recent progress, LLM agents still struggle with reasoning over long interaction histories. While current memory-augmented agents rely on a static retrieve-then-reason paradigm, this rigid pipeline design prevents them from dynamically adapting memory access to intermediate evidence discovered during inference. To bridge this gap, we propose MRAgent, a framework that combines an associative memory graph with an active reconstruction mechanism. We represent memory as a Cue-Tag-Content graph, where associative tags serve as semantic bridges connecting fine-grained cues to memory contents. Operating on this structure, our active reconstruction mechanism integrates LLM reasoning directly into memory access, allowing the agent to iteratively explore and prune retrieval paths based on accumulated evidence. This ensures that memory retrieval is dynamically adapted to the reasoning context while avoiding combinatorial explosion caused by unconstrained expansion. Experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark and LongMemEval benchmark demonstrate significant improvements over strong baselines (up to 23%), while substantially reducing token and runtime cost, highlighting the effectiveness of active and associative reconstruction for long-horizon memory reasoning.

AI.tldr: MRAgent uses an associative Cue-Tag-Content memory graph with an active reconstruction mechanism to dynamically steer memory retrieval during LLM reasoning, achieving up to 23% gains on long-horizon benchmarks while reducing token and runtime cost.
AI.motivation: Current memory-augmented agents rely on a static retrieve-then-reason pipeline, which cannot adapt memory access to intermediate evidence discovered during inference and risks combinatorial retrieval blowups when reasoning over long histories.
AI.method: Propose MRAgent: represent memory as a Cue-Tag-Content graph where associative tags link fine-grained cues to memory content; implement an active reconstruction mechanism that integrates LLM reasoning into memory access, iteratively exploring and pruning retrieval paths based on accumulated evidence to adapt retrieval to the reasoning context and avoid uncontrolled expansion.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on LoCoMo and LongMemEval shows significant improvements over strong baselines (up to 23%), with substantial reductions in token usage and runtime, demonstrating the effectiveness of active and associative reconstruction for long-horizon memory reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, active, and associative memory access enhances long-horizon reasoning with LLMs by tailoring retrieval to the evolving reasoning context, improving performance while reducing cost; this approach mitigates combinatorial retrieval explosion and facilitates scalable reasoning over extended histories.

title: ReCache: Learning Budget-Aware Caching Schedules for Diffusion Models via REINFORCE
summary: Modern diffusion models generate high-quality images and videos, but their iterative denoising process makes inference expensive. Feature caching accelerates sampling by reusing or predicting intermediate activations across neighboring denoising steps, exploiting the redundancy of computations along the reverse trajectory. In this work, we focus on the caching schedule: selecting which denoising steps should be fully recomputed. Existing schedules are either fixed (e.g. uniform) or chosen adaptively from per-step error heuristics; in both cases, the actual compute cost is a side-effect of hand-tuned thresholds rather than a quantity the user can specify. We propose ReCache, which inverts this: given a target budget k, it learns the recomputation schedule that maximizes generation quality, turning compute into a directly controllable input. ReCache trains via policy gradients, sidestepping backpropagation through full diffusion inference, and uses no labelled data. Generations from uncached inference serve as matching targets, paired with a reward for generation quality. ReCache is compatible with any caching mechanism, including feature reuse and feature forecasting; for each mechanism, a single trained policy adapts across computational budgets at inference time. ReCache consistently outperforms scheduling baselines: under a $\times5.04$ FLOPs reduction on FLUX, it reduces LPIPS by 31% (from 0.456 to 0.316) compared to DiCache; on Wan 2.1 at a $\sim \times2.6$ speedup, it drops LPIPS by 65% (from 0.480 to 0.169) and boosts the VBench score by 7% (5.6 points, from 70.4 to 76.0) over uniform HiCache. Code is available at https://github.com/thecrazymage/ReCache.

AI.tldr: ReCache learns a recomputation schedule for cached diffusion sampling under a user-specified compute budget using policy gradients, converting compute into a controllable input and outperforming fixed/adaptive baselines across multiple datasets and caching methods.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are expensive to sample; existing caching schedules are fixed or heuristic, providing limited user control over compute and quality trade-offs.
AI.method: Train a policy via policy gradients without labeled data. The policy selects which steps to recompute given a target compute budget k; training uses uncached generations as targets and rewards generation quality (no backprop through full inference). The approach applies to any caching mechanism (feature reuse or forecasting) with a single policy that adapts across budgets at inference time.
AI.result: ReCache outperforms baselines (e.g., DiCache, HiCache). On Flux with ~×5.04 FLOPs reduction, LPIPS improves 31% (0.456→0.316). On Wan 2.1 with ~×2.6 speedup, LPIPS improves 65% (0.480→0.169) and VBench score rises 7% (70.4→76.0). Code: https://github.com/thecrazymage/ReCache.
AI.conclusion: A reusable, budget-aware learning-based scheduling approach for diffusion caching; it converts compute into a tunable input, doesn't require labeled data, and transfers across caching mechanisms, providing stronger quality under constrained compute than hand-tuned or uniform schedules.

title: Learning Visual Spatial Planning from Symbolic State via Modality-Gap-Aware Self-Distillation
summary: While vision-language models excel at general multimodal understanding, they still struggle with visual spatial planning. We attribute this to a perception-reasoning modality gap: visual planning requires models to infer latent state structures from pixels and then reason over the recovered structure to produce valid actions, whereas symbolic planning directly leverages explicit objects and constraints. This creates dual bottlenecks in visual state recovery and multi-step planning. To address this, we propose MGSD, a two-stage modality-gap-aware self-distillation framework. First, a cold-start grounding stage equips the visual student with reliable state representations, minimizing early perception noise. Second, a privileged teacher transfers planning capabilities via on-policy distillation, using explicit symbolic states to supervise the student's own visual rollout prefixes. Crucially, symbolic data is used strictly during training, leaving inference purely visual. Experiments on visual planning benchmarks show that MGSD consistently improves visual planning across both 4B and 8B backbones, raising the macro average by 19.3% and 18.4%, respectively. The resulting models narrow the gap to symbolic-input upper bounds, while ablations and diagnostics confirm that the improvement comes from both visual state recovery and optimal-path reasoning. These results suggest that modality-gap-aware self-distillation improves not only how models perceive actionable states, but also how they plan over the inferred structure. Code is available at https://github.com/Oranger-l/MGSD.

AI.tldr: MGSD introduces a two-stage, modality-gap-aware self-distillation framework to bridge perception and planning gaps in vision-language models, improving visual planning by leveraging cold-start grounding and privileged teacher supervision with symbolic states during training, while keeping inference purely visual.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models struggle with visual spatial planning due to a perception-reasoning modality gap: inferring latent state structures from pixels and then planning over them, versus symbolic planning that uses explicit objects and constraints. This gap yields bottlenecks in state recovery and multi-step planning.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) cold-start grounding to provide reliable visual state representations and reduce early perception noise; (2) privileged teacher distillation that uses explicit symbolic states to supervise the student’s visual rollout prefixes via on-policy distillation, with symbolic data used only during training; inference remains purely visual.
AI.result: MGSD yields consistent improvements in visual planning across 4B and 8B backbones, increasing macro average by 19.3% and 18.4% respectively, narrowing the gap to symbolic-input upper bounds. Ablations indicate gains come from both improved visual state recovery and better optimal-path reasoning; code is provided.
AI.conclusion: Modality-gap-aware self-distillation can enhance both perceptual state recovery and planning over inferred structures, bringing vision-language models closer to symbolic-input performance in visual planning tasks.

title: Dead Directions: Geometric Singular Learning
summary: Singular learning theory and information geometry have studied the same parameter spaces in mostly separate vocabularies: the former computes Bayesian invariants in resolved coordinates, the latter works in original coordinates under a non-degeneracy assumption that overparameterised models routinely violate. We bridge them through one primitive, the dead direction: a unit vector along which the Fisher metric degenerates, equivalently a tangent to the analytic singular set with a definite KL order, set by how fast the KL divergence vanishes. The two readings name the same vector; our central move shows its KL order is recoverable as the decay rate of the directional Fisher curvature approaching the singularity, in original parameter coordinates and without a Hironaka resolution. A selection rule on smooth fibres translates this rate into Watanabe's single-direction contribution to the real log canonical threshold, and we extend the recovery to multi-component crossings, multiplicity $m$, the singular fluctuation $ν$ (universal in the KL order for 1D directions), prior-RLCT shifts, and tempered posteriors. We then lift this rate to a deep network: a multi-layer K-FAC factorisation writes each Fisher block as a product of activation- and gradient-side rates with a duality between them, instantiated at modern-network primitives (residual streams, layer normalisation, attention). A quotient theorem carries the rate to the gauge quotient $Θ/G$ under gradient flow on a $G$-invariant metric; SGD qualifies, standard Adam does not, and we construct a $G$-equivariant Adam-family preconditioner (DDCAdam) that does. The bridge yields a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form per-architecture predictions, and a trajectory-rate readout of Watanabe's triple $(λ, m, ν)$ from one checkpoint's forward and backward passes, without posterior sampling.

AI.tldr: Bridges singular learning theory and information geometry via the dead direction, linking KL-order to directional Fisher curvature and RLCT; extends to multi-component singularities and training dynamics; provides architecture-level predictions through K-FAC, a G-invariant gradient flow quotient, and a practical readout of Watanabe's invariants from a single checkpoint using a new DDCAdam preconditioner.
AI.motivation: Unify two parallel formalisms for parameter spaces that often violate non-degeneracy, and produce computable, architecture-aware predictions for generalization and training dynamics in deep nets.
AI.method: Define the dead direction where the Fisher metric degenerates; quantify KL order via directional Fisher curvature without resolution; map to Watanabe's RLCT; extend to crossings with multiplicity m and singular fluctuation ν; apply a multi-layer K-FAC factorisation to relate activation- and gradient-side rates; prove a quotient theorem for Θ/G under gradient flow; construct G-equivariant Adam (DDCAdam).
AI.result: Derive closed-form, per-architecture predictions; extract the RLCT triplet (λ, m, ν) from a single forward-backward pass; provide rate-based understanding of SGD vs Adam; enable prior-RLCT shifts and tempered posteriors; deliver a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry.
AI.conclusion: The framework unifies singular geometry with information geometry in deep nets, yielding practical readouts of singular invariants from standard training trajectories and offering a principled, invariant preconditioning approach (DDCAdam) for optimization.

title: HyperVis: Continuous Latent Visual Relational Graphs on the Lorentz Hyperboloid for Compositional Reasoning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle with compositional reasoning that requires understanding inter-object relationships. A natural remedy is to inject explicit scene graph triplets $\langle s, p, o \rangle$ from an off-the-shelf scene graph generator (SGG), but we show this backfires: discrete text labels collide with the continuous visual modality, degrading GQA accuracy from 60.38\% to 58.86\%. We propose \textbf{HyperVis}, which bypasses the SGG semantic bottleneck entirely. From $N$ class-agnostic region proposals, we compute a dense $O(N^2)$ visual relation tensor via spatially-biased cross-attention, project it onto a Lorentz hyperboloid, and enforce hierarchy through spatial physics, namely IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion. We discover that HyperVis contributes in two complementary ways: (1) as a \emph{training-time regularizer}, the hyperbolic relational losses shape LoRA representations that improve generative VQA (GQA 61.03\% vs.\ 57.21\% for LoRA fine-tuning without relational losses, recovering and surpassing the baseline); and (2) as an \emph{inference-time relational encoder}, hyperbolic prefix tokens boost discriminative compositional scoring (SugarCrepe 79.94\%, $+$6.25pp over baseline). The learned curvature stabilises at $κ{=}4.0$, an order of magnitude above prior hyperbolic VLMs where $κ$ typically collapses toward zero, indicating that continuous visual features genuinely require the exponential volume of strongly curved space. A controlled Euclidean ablation confirms this decomposition: the relational pipeline regularises LoRA comparably in flat space (GQA 60.81\%), but the compositionality gain is specifically hyperbolic (SugarCrepe $+$4.58pp over Euclidean), with entailment loss ${\sim}6{\times}$ higher in Euclidean training. Codes are available at TBA.

AI.tldr: HyperVis introduces a hyperbolic relational modeling framework for vision-language models to bypass brittle discrete scene graph labels and improve compositional reasoning. It builds a dense relation tensor from region proposals, projects to a Lorentz hyperboloid, and enforces hierarchical structure via IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion. It serves as both a training-time regularizer (LoRA tuning) and an inference-time relational encoder (hyperbolic prefix tokens). Empirically, it recovers and surpasses baselines on GQA and SugarCrepe, with a stable curvature κ=4.0 indicating the benefit of hyperbolic space for continuous visual features. Euclidean ablations show the gains are specifically due to hyperbolic geometry. Code is forthcoming (TBA).
AI.motivation: Compositional reasoning in vision-language models remains weak, especially when relying on discrete scene-graph labels that clash with continuous visual features. There is a need for a geometry-aware relational representation that preserves continuous signals while encoding inter-object relations to improve tasks requiring relational understanding.
AI.method: From N proposals, compute a dense O(N^2) visual relation tensor via spatially-biased cross-attention. Project relations to a Lorentz (hyperbolic) space (hyperboloid) and enforce hierarchical structure with IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion losses. Train with hyperbolic relational losses to shape LoRA representations; during inference, insert hyperbolic prefix tokens to serve as a relational encoder. Evaluate on generative VQA (GQA) and SugarCrepe; analyze curvature stabilization.
AI.result: Replacing SGG labels with HyperVis yields improvements: GQA improves to 61.03% (from 57.21% with LoRA alone; baseline 60.38%); SugarCrepe reaches 79.94% (+6.25pp). Euclidean ablations show weaker gains (GQA 60.81%; SugarCrepe +4.58pp) and entailment losses ~6× higher in Euclidean training. The learned curvature stabilizes at κ=4.0, suggesting the exponential volume of strongly curved space is beneficial for continuous visual features.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic relational modeling provides a dual benefit as training regularizer and inference-time encoder, yielding significant gains in compositional reasoning for VLMs, with curvature behavior indicating a genuine need for hyperbolic geometry over Euclidean space.

title: LLM Explainability with Counterfactual Chains and Causal Graphs
summary: Causal graphs provide a high-level language for making mechanisms transparent. Recent work uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to recover causal graphs of external-world processes. Instead, in this paper, we use causal graphs to model LLM inference itself, providing stakeholders with a transparent view of how the model perceives and organizes high-level concepts to produce a prediction. We propose a four-phase method for constructing such graphs. Given a target LLM and a set of textual examples, our method discovers class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts and maps each input to LLM-perceived concept states. We then introduce an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation procedure that expands the sparse observational data through chains of counterfactuals. This enables stable causal discovery with $σ$-CG, yielding informative, interpretable graphs. We apply our method to three LLMs across disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge classification tasks. We evaluate the learned graphs for predictive fidelity and structural stability, and the MCMC-inspired augmentation for convergence and downstream utility. Our results show that the discovered causal graphs capture meaningful dependencies consistent with LLMs' reasoning. Together, this paper provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs.

AI.tldr: A framework that models LLM inference with causal graphs to provide concept-level explainability, using a four-phase method to extract interpretable concepts, map inputs to concept states, and an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation to enable stable σ-CG causal discovery across three tasks (disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge).
AI.motivation: Address the opacity of LLM reasoning by shifting from external-world causal graphs to modeling the internal reasoning of LLMs. Provide transparent, human-interpretable representations of how high-level concepts organize into predictions.
AI.method: Four-phase method: (1) given a target LLM and textual examples, discover class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts; (2) map each input to LLM-perceived concept states; (3) apply an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation to expand sparse observational data via chains of counterfactuals; (4) perform stable causal discovery with σ-CG to yield informative, interpretable graphs.
AI.result: The learned σ-CG graphs capture meaningful dependencies that align with LLMs' reasoning, achieving predictive fidelity and structural stability across three tasks and three LLMs; the counterfactual augmentation improves convergence and downstream utility.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs by enabling transparent, causally-structured representations of how LLMs organize concepts to reach predictions.

title: Where, What, Why, and Importance: Structured Defect Grounding for Text-to-Image Feedback
summary: Despite generating increasingly photorealistic images, text-to-image (T2I) models still exhibit localized, subtle, and structurally complex failures. Diagnosing these failures requires instance-level feedback that answers where a defect occurs, what type it is, why it is defective, and its importance to overall image quality. While recent dense-feedback methods move beyond scalar supervision, their heatmap-centric representations still formulate diagnosis as pixel-field regression, making it difficult to localize variable-cardinality defects and bind semantic reasons to individual failures. To address this representation bottleneck, we propose Structured Defect Grounding (SDG), which casts T2I diagnosis as structured set prediction by modeling each defect as a (location, type, reason, importance) tuple. To make this formulation trainable and measurable, we introduce SDG-30K, a 30K-image dataset with box-grounded annotations across four modern T2I generators, together with a dedicated evaluation protocol, SDG-Eval. Building on this structured representation, we further present a diagnosis-to-alignment framework in which a Vision-Language Model (VLM) serves as the SDG detector, and BoxFlow-GRPO converts predicted defect sets into box-derived, importance-weighted spatial rewards for diffusion model alignment. Extensive experiments show that our SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding, while SDG-guided rewards consistently improve T2I alignment and support localized image refinement. These results establish SDG as a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and enhancing modern generative models.

AI.tldr: SDG introduces a structured defect grounding paradigm for text-to-image models, replacing heatmap regression with a defect tuple (location, type, reason, importance), supported by a 30K image dataset (SDG-30K) and an evaluation protocol (SDG-Eval). A diagnosis-to-alignment framework uses a Vision-Language Model as the detector and BoxFlow-GRPO to convert defect sets into spatial rewards to improve diffusion model alignment and localization refinement.
AI.motivation: Current T2I diagnostics rely on heatmaps and pixel-level regression, which struggle with variable-cardinality, semantically grounded defects. There is a need for instance-level, structured representations that can capture where, what, why, and how important a defect is, enabling reliable evaluation and targeted model improvements.
AI.method: Define SDG as structured set prediction where each defect is a (location, type, reason, importance) tuple. Build SDG-30K with 30K images and box-grounded annotations across four modern T2I generators. Propose SDG-Eval evaluation. Implement diagnosis-to-alignment: a Vision-Language Model acts as the SDG detector; BoxFlow-GRPO converts predicted defect sets into box-derived, importance-weighted spatial rewards for diffusion-model alignment.
AI.result: SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding. SDG-guided rewards consistently improve text-to-image alignment and support localized image refinement.
AI.conclusion: SDG offers a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and enhancing modern generative models, enabling precise defect localization, semantic reasoning, and targeted improvements.

title: CogManip: Benchmarking Manipulative Behavior in Multi-Turn Interactions with Large Language Model
summary: Whether Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit covert psychological manipulation in complex human-AI interactions has garnered increasing safety concerns. However, existing AI safety benchmarks remain largely restricted to explicit rule compliance and static prompts, failing to capture the dynamic and covert nature of manipulative strategies in multi-turn dialogues. We introduce CogManip, a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates 15 manipulation strategy risks across 1,000 multi-turn interaction scenarios, validated by human experts. A systematic evaluation of 13 representative models, including frontier models like GPT-5.4 and DeepSeek-V3.2, reveals significant risk heterogeneities and illuminates the targeted direction for future defense. Further analysis of objective function perturbation reveals that DeepSeek-V3.2's manipulation tactics are highly sensitive to both negative and benign system prompts, demonstrating the critical necessity of prompt-based defense engineering and implicit goal auditing. CogManip offers a robust instrument and perspective for auditing the implicit psychological influence and dynamic strategy selection of modern LLMs.

AI.tldr: CogManip benchmarks 15 manipulation strategies in 1,000 multi-turn LLM interactions, revealing risk heterogeneity and prompting vulnerabilities; aids defense research.
AI.motivation: Existing AI safety benchmarks miss covert, dynamic manipulation in sustained dialogues; a human-validated, dynamic benchmark is needed.
AI.method: Construct CogManip with 1,000 scenarios and 15 manipulation strategies; expert-validated; evaluate 13 models (incl. GPT-5.4, DeepSeek-V3.2); assess objective function perturbation and prompts; derive defense implications.
AI.result: Notable heterogeneity in manipulation risk across models; DeepSeek-V3.2 manipulation is highly sensitive to both negative and benign system prompts; indicates need for prompt-based defense engineering and implicit goal auditing.
AI.conclusion: CogManip provides a robust auditing instrument for implicit psychological influence and dynamic strategy selection in modern LLMs, guiding future safety research.

title: Diff-CA: Separating Common and Salient Factors with Diffusion Models
summary: Contrastive Analysis aims to separate factors that are common between two data distributions from those that are salient to only one of them. Existing contrastive methods are based on generative models (e.g., VAEs or GANs) that often suffer from limited reconstruction and image quality, which hampers effective latent factor separation and limits their applicability to high-fidelity image generation and edition. We propose a novel conditioning framework for diffusion models that enables contrastive decomposition without compromising generation quality. We first train a prompt-free, image-conditioned diffusion model, and then learn to decompose the conditioning into a common and a salient factor, using weak supervision. We prove that the additive contrastive factorization, commonly assumed in prior work, is identifiable under mild conditions. This factorization enables targeted operations by swapping or interpolating only the salient factor.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-model-based framework for contrastive decomposition into common and salient factors, with identifiability of additive factorization and capability to swap/interpolate the salient factor while maintaining high-fidelity generation.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of generative-model-based contrastive analysis (VAEs/GANs) that yield poor reconstruction and image quality, hindering latent factor separation and applicability to high-fidelity image generation and editing.
AI.method: Train a prompt-free, image-conditioned diffusion model; decompose conditioning into common and salient factors using weak supervision; prove identifiability of the additive contrastive factorization.
AI.result: Identifiability of the additive contrastive factorization under mild conditions; enables targeted operations by swapping or interpolating only the salient factor while preserving overall generation quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed conditioning framework enables contrastive decomposition without compromising generation quality and provides actionable manipulation of salient factors through factor swapping/interpolation.

title: HoT-SSM:Higher-order Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning with State Space Models for Health Care
summary: Medical knowledge graphs (MKGs) infused with clinical knowledge have been increasingly used to model electronic health records (EHRs) to support interpretable predictions in healthcare domain. However, existing MKG-based approaches are limited in capturing pairwise relations between clinical concepts (e.g., conditions, procedures, and medications), and restricts their ability to model higher-order interactions among co-occurring or semantically related concepts. In addition, most representation learning methods that leverage MKGs either collapse temporal information across visits or lack an explicit mechanism for modeling long-range temporal dependencies, which is critical for clinical tasks such as mortality prediction. To mitigate these limitations, we propose HoT-SSM, a parameter efficient and higher-order temporal graph reasoning with state space models. For each visit, HoT-SSM constructs hypergraphs by grouping semantically related clinical concepts into hyperedges using domain knowledge, thereby preserving visit-level clinical context. Further, to model the temporal dynamics while learning the representations, we introduce a novel dynamic hypergraph-based state space model that explicitly captures patients latent state evolution over time while preserving long-range information. The learned representations are used for downstream clinical prediction and reasoning. Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets shows significant performance improvement over the current state-of-the-art models, demonstrating the effectiveness of jointly modeling higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal dependencies.

AI.tldr: HoT-SSM: higher-order temporal graph reasoning with state-space models for MKGs that capture higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal dynamics using hypergraphs per visit and a dynamic state-space model, achieving state-of-the-art results on MIMIC-III/IV.
AI.motivation: Existing MKG-based methods mainly model pairwise relationships and often collapse temporal information or lack long-range temporal modeling, limiting predictive performance and interpretability in EHR-based tasks such as mortality prediction.
AI.method: Construct visit-level hypergraphs by grouping clinically related concepts into hyperedges using domain knowledge; introduce a dynamic hypergraph-based state-space model that tracks patients' latent state evolution over time while preserving long-range dependencies; the approach is parameter-efficient and used for downstream clinical prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV show significant performance improvements over current state-of-the-art models, validating the effectiveness of combining higher-order clinical interactions with long-range temporal dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling higher-order interactions among clinical concepts and long-range temporal dependencies enhances predictive accuracy and interpretability in MKG-based healthcare models.

title: Step-adaptive multimodal fusion network with multi-scale cloud feature learning for ultra-short-term solar irradiance forecasting
summary: Ultra-short-term solar irradiance prediction is critical for photovoltaic system dispatch and power grid stability. Existing approaches suffer from three key shortcomings: single time-series models cannot capture the spatial dynamics of clouds under complex conditions, standard convolutions inadequately represent multi-scale cloud features, and fixed low-frequency compensation strategies fail to adapt to different prediction steps. To address these issues, this proposes a multi-source data fusion model for ultra-short-term irradiance prediction. The model first employs InceptionNeXt to extract multi-scale, multi-directional spatial features from ground-based cloud images. A step-adaptive low-frequency compensation unit is then introduced to dynamically modulate global low-frequency information based on the prediction step. Eventually, the enhanced image features are combined with meteorological time-series features, and a TempAttnLSTM network captures global temporal dependencies for multi-step prediction. Experiments on the public NREL dataset and practical photovoltaic stations in Shandong illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with several state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: A multi-source data fusion framework for ultra-short-term irradiance prediction using InceptionNeXt for multi-scale cloud image features, a step-adaptive low-frequency unit, and TempAttnLSTM integrating meteorological time-series, showing improved performance over baselines on NREL and Shandong data.
AI.motivation: To address three key shortcomings: (1) single time-series models cannot capture spatial cloud dynamics under complex conditions, (2) standard convolutions fail to represent multi-scale cloud features, and (3) fixed low-frequency compensation is not adaptable to different prediction steps.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) InceptionNeXt extracts multi-scale, multi-directional spatial features from ground-based cloud images; (2) a step-adaptive low-frequency compensation unit dynamically modulates global low-frequency information based on the prediction horizon; (3) enhanced image features are fused with meteorological time-series features and fed into a TempAttnLSTM network to capture global temporal dependencies for multi-step prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on the public NREL dataset and practical PV stations in Shandong demonstrate the proposed method’s effectiveness, outperforming several state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively integrates spatial cloud dynamics with meteorological time-series to enable accurate multi-step ultra-short-term irradiance forecasting in real-world deployments.

title: Computation-Aware Event-to-Frame Reconstruction via Selective Attention
summary: Event-to-frame (E2F) reconstruction bridges asynchronous event streams with frame-based vision pipelines, but existing methods often face a trade-off between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency. In this work, we propose an efficient E2F framework that emphasizes causal temporal modeling and computation-aware design. The architecture adopts a recurrent encoder-decoder to incrementally aggregate event information with compact hidden states. To improve robustness under fast motion and illumination variations, a selective context fusion strategy is introduced to integrate event-driven features with prior intensity cues. Within this fusion process, a lightweight hybrid attention mechanism enhances feature selectivity without relying on heavy attention operations. Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves competitive reconstruction performance while maintaining a favorable balance between accuracy and model complexity.
