AI.tldr: Efficient Event-to-frame reconstruction via a causal, computation-aware framework using a recurrent encoder-decoder and selective context fusion with lightweight hybrid attention, achieving competitive accuracy with lower complexity.
AI.motivation: Bridge asynchronous event streams to frame-based vision while maintaining high reconstruction quality and computational efficiency; existing E2F methods trade off accuracy against speed and often lack robust performance under fast motion and illumination changes.
AI.method: A recurrent encoder-decoder incrementally aggregates event information with compact hidden states. A selective context fusion integrates event-driven features with prior intensity cues. A lightweight hybrid attention mechanism enhances feature selectivity within the fusion without heavy attention operations.
AI.result: On standard benchmarks, the approach achieves competitive reconstruction performance while maintaining a favorable balance between accuracy and model complexity.
AI.conclusion: Proposes an efficient E2F framework that emphasizes causal temporal modeling and computation-aware design, achieving robust reconstruction under challenging conditions with improved efficiency.

title: Towards Healthy Evolution: Exploring the Role and Mechanisms of Human-Agent Interaction in Self-Evolving Systems
summary: Self-evolving agents improve through continual self-play and self-generated learning signals, but autonomous evolution can also cause capability degradation and safety drift. Although human feedback has proven effective for static and post-trained agents, its role in self-evolving systems remains underexplored. We introduce Agent Norm Correction through Human-like Oversight and Review (ANCHOR), an LLM-based framework that simulates human supervision and delivers feedback at various phases of self-evolution. With ANCHOR, we evaluate two representative open-source self-evolving agent systems across coding, mathematical reasoning, and safety. Our results show that even limited supervision substantially mitigates safety degradation while preserving stable performance on core evolutionary objectives. Further analysis shows that supervision over the output verification phase is the most effective for intervention, whereas increasing supervision frequency yields diminishing returns. These findings provide empirical evidence and practical guidance for designing more stable, controllable, and human-aligned self-evolving agent systems.

AI.tldr: ANCHOR uses an LLM-based supervision framework to guide self-evolving agents by simulating human oversight across evolution phases; limited human-like feedback reduces safety degradation while preserving core performance; output verification phase supervision is most effective; more frequent supervision yields diminishing returns; provides practical guidance for stable, controllable, human-aligned self-evolving agents.
AI.motivation: Self-evolving agents risk capability degradation and safety drift; while human feedback helps static/post-trained agents, its role in evolving systems is understudied. There is a need to explore how supervision can stabilize evolution without hindering progress.
AI.method: Propose ANCHOR framework that uses an LLM to simulate human supervision and feedback at multiple stages of the self-evolution pipeline. Evaluate two open-source self-evolving agent systems across coding, math reasoning, and safety domains; compare different supervision intensities and phases, especially output verification.
AI.result: Limited supervision substantially mitigates safety degradation while maintaining stable performance on core evolutionary objectives; supervision at the output verification phase is most effective; increasing supervision frequency yields diminishing returns; empirical evidence and practical design guidance.
AI.conclusion: Supervision via human-like oversight can stabilize self-evolving agents and improve alignment; ANCHOR offers a concrete mechanism and empirical findings to guide design of more stable, controllable self-improving systems.

title: Adaptive Tokenisation Via Temporal Redundancy Masking And Latent Inpainting
summary:
Adaptive video tokenisation seeks to dynamically allocate token budgets based on the underlying visual complexity of a sequence. Current continuous-regime approaches achieve this via iterative binarised searches or trained neural regressors, while discrete methods often require a full-rate decoder pass to estimate information content. We demonstrate that such computational overheads are not strictly necessary. We show that the latent space of a frozen continuous video tokeniser inherently encodes temporal redundancy that can be exploited directly: spatial positions whose latent representations change minimally between consecutive frames carry near-zero additional information.
  We introduce a parameter-free adaptive token allocation mechanism that applies a fixed threshold to per-position temporal-L1 differences, identifying and dropping redundant latent positions. Consequently, the compression rate emerges naturally from the input content rather than being enforced top-down: static scenes get compressed aggressively, while highly dynamic sequences retain more tokens. To reconstruct the dropped positions, we propose the Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT), a lightweight factorised spatial-temporal attention architecture. The resulting inference pipeline is highly efficient, requiring only a single encoder pass and one LIT forward pass, eliminating the need for auxiliary routing networks. Evaluations across TokenBench and DAVIS, which are the standard benchmarks used by recent tokenisers~\cite{infotok, agarwal2025cosmos}, indicate that our framework yields meaningful, content-driven token allocation while maintaining competitive reconstruction fidelity, and delivers a $31\times$ inference-time speedup over the continuous adaptive baseline (ElasticTok-CV) and an $\approx2\times$ speedup over the discrete information-theoretic baseline (InfoTok)

AI.tldr: A parameter-free, content-aware token allocation for video tokenisers that drops latent positions with minimal temporal change; introduces Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT) for reconstruction, enabling a single encoder pass plus one LIT pass and achieving substantial speedups with competitive fidelity.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational overhead in adaptive video tokenisation by exploiting temporal redundancy directly in the frozen continuous video tokeniser's latent space, avoiding iterative searches and extra routing networks.
AI.method: Apply a fixed temporal-L1 difference threshold per latent position to drop redundantly changing latent tokens; preserve dynamic regions. Reconstruct dropped tokens using Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT), a lightweight factorised spatial-temporal attention model. Inference uses one encoder pass and one LIT pass; no auxiliary routing networks.
AI.result: Evaluations on TokenBench and DAVIS show meaningful, content-driven token allocation with competitive reconstruction fidelity. Achieves ~31× inference-time speedup over the continuous adaptive baseline (ElasticTok-CV) and ~2× speedup over the discrete information-theoretic baseline (InfoTok).
AI.conclusion: A simple, parameter-free mechanism can exploit latent-space temporal redundancy for adaptive tokenisation, yielding efficient inference and content-driven compression without extra decoding passes or routing networks.

title: Adaptive Oscillatory-State Alignment for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Long-term time series forecasting benefits from inductive biases that expose recurring temporal structure. Existing periodic forecasting methods typically model recurrence through predefined periods, global spectral components, or fixed learnable templates. However, real-world temporal dynamics are rarely rigidly periodic: oscillatory behavior often evolves through amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation. Under these conditions, fixed-template periodic modeling can become fundamentally mismatched to the underlying temporal states. We propose AOSNET, a Hilbert-guided forecasting framework that reformulates periodic forecasting from fixed template matching to adaptive oscillatory-state alignment. AOSNET extracts analytic-signal descriptors from both the observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior, then adaptively aligns local states through a descriptor-conditioned gate that selectively preserves reliable observations while softly correcting mismatched regions. The learned prior serves not as a rigid repeated template but as a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics. Experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy with fast inference speed. Controlled synthetic studies isolating amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation confirm that the advantage of oscillatory-state alignment consistently increases as non-stationarity intensifies.

AI.tldr: AOSNET uses Hilbert-transform-based analytic signals and a learnable global oscillatory prior to adaptively align local oscillatory states for non-stationary long-term forecasting, replacing fixed periodic templates.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series exhibit non-stationary oscillations with amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation; fixed periodic templates fail to capture this, hindering long-horizon forecasting.
AI.method: Extract analytic-signal descriptors from observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior; a descriptor-conditioned gate selectively preserves reliable observations and softly corrects mismatched regions; the prior is a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics.
AI.result: Eight benchmarks show state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy with fast inference; synthetic studies isolating amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation confirm that benefits of oscillatory-state alignment grow with non-stationarity.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive oscillatory-state alignment with a Hilbert-guided prior improves long-term forecasting under non-stationarity, outperforming fixed-template periodic models.

title: WorldFly: A World-Model-Based Vision-Language-Action Model for UAV Navigation
summary: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise in UAV navigation. However, existing approaches typically rely on historical observations to directly predict actions, often struggling in dense urban environments where severe occlusions and sharp turns result in drastic viewpoint transitions. We argue that the ability to "imagine" future states -- inherent in World Models -- is critical for robust decision-making under such partial observability. To address this, we construct a challenging Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark, specifically designed to evaluate spatial understanding in scenarios characterized by severe occlusions and drastic viewpoint transitions. To this end, we propose WorldFly, a novel world-model-based VLA framework that employs a dual-branch coupled flow matching mechanism to jointly generate future video predictions and navigation actions, thereby explicitly guiding the agent's policy via spatial imagination. Extensive evaluations on our benchmark demonstrate that WorldFly outperforms other baselines, particularly in unseen environments, validating the effectiveness of integrating world models into embodied aerial agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes WorldFly, a world-model-based VLA framework with a dual-branch coupled flow matching mechanism to jointly predict future video frames and navigation actions for UAVs in urban canyons; introduces the Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark to evaluate spatial understanding under severe occlusions and drastic viewpoint transitions; shows superior performance, especially in unseen environments.
AI.motivation: Robust UAV navigation under partial observability and drastic viewpoint changes is challenging for end-to-end VLA models that rely on historical observations; incorporating world-models to ‘imagine’ future states can improve decision-making in dense urban environments.
AI.method: WorldFly uses a dual-branch coupled flow matching architecture that jointly forecasts future video frames and navigation actions, guiding the policy via spatial imagination. It also introduces a challenging Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark designed to test spatial understanding under severe occlusions and drastic viewpoint transitions in urban-like canyons.
AI.result: In extensive evaluations on the proposed benchmark, WorldFly outperforms baselines, particularly in unseen environments, validating that integrating world models into embodied aerial agents enhances robustness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Integrating world models into vision-language-action paradigms for UAV navigation yields more robust decision-making under partial observability, with spatial imagination helping to cope with occlusions and drastic viewpoint changes; the Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark effectively measures this capability.

title: RQUL-UIE: Revitalizing Quality-Unstable Labels for Underwater Image Enhancement via In-Dataset Self-Supervision
summary: Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) is essential for mitigating degradations caused by water medium. Although learning-based methods have advanced significantly, most rely on paired datasets with unstable label quality, which bottlenecks model performance. This paper proposes a diffusion-based, in-dataset self-supervised learning strategy designed to exploit the quality distribution of training labels. Specifically, we evaluate label quality via semantic perception embeddings from a pre-trained diffusion model in a training-free manner. These quality scores are subsequently quantized into noise-level indices, guiding a multi-step denoising process for level-wise supervision. This mechanism prevents low-quality labels from degrading the model while maximizing their utility during training. Furthermore, a Fourier-based refinement network is incorporated to explicitly reconstruct high-frequency components. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms SOTA approaches in restoration quality. The code and pre-trained model will be available once accepted in link.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based self-supervised UIE method that leverages in-dataset label-quality distribution, using diffusion embeddings to score labels, quantizing into noise levels for multi-step denoising supervision, plus a Fourier refinement network; achieves SOTA restoration results.
AI.motivation: UIE datasets with paired labels suffer from unstable label quality, which hurts learning; a training-free metric to assess label quality and a supervision scheme that mitigates poor labels can improve robustness and performance while leveraging all data.
AI.method: Use a pre-trained diffusion model to obtain semantic perception embeddings that quantify label quality in a training-free manner. Quantize these quality scores into discrete noise-level indices and guide a multi-step denoising process for level-wise supervision. Integrate a Fourier-based refinement network to reconstruct high-frequency components.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show the proposed method consistently outperforms current SOTA on UIE restoration quality across tests; training-free quality assessment and level-wise supervision contribute to robustness against low-quality labels.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that exploiting the distribution of label quality via diffusion-based self-supervision, complemented by Fourier refinement, yields superior UIE performance and robustness, with code and models to be released upon acceptance.

title: OPRD: On-Policy Representation Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) supervises the student only in output space by matching next-token probabilities. This output-only paradigm has two limits: (1) sampling variance from Monte Carlo KL estimates over large vocabularies (e.g., Qwen's ~150k tokens) persists throughout training, and (2) it treats the teacher as a black-box, discarding all intermediate hidden states after the LM head. We propose On-Policy Representation Distillation (OPRD), which lifts distillation into hidden-state space by aligning student and teacher representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head entirely. Theoretically, OPRD eliminates sampling variance and provides richer per-layer structural information. Empirically, OPRD closes the student-teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO, while output-space OPD baselines plateau below the teacher. OPRD also trains 1.44x faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD. Code: https://github.com/ShenzhiYang2000/OPRD.

AI.tldr: Introduces On-Policy Representation Distillation (OPRD) that aligns student and teacher hidden representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head; claims reduced sampling variance, faster training, and memory efficiency, outperforming output-only OPD on AIME/AIMO tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses two drawbacks of on-policy distillation (OPD): sampling variance from Monte Carlo KL estimates in large vocabularies and treating the teacher as a black box by ignoring hidden states.
AI.method: Lift distillation into hidden-state space by aligning per-layer representations between student and teacher across chosen layers during the same rollouts, removing dependence on the LM head and output probabilities; operate on-rollout representations to transfer structural information.
AI.result: Empirically reduces the student-teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO; OPD baselines plateau below the teacher; OPRD trains 1.44x faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD.
AI.conclusion: OPRD provides a more informative and efficient distillation paradigm by exploiting hidden-state representations, mitigating sampling variance and improving data efficiency; supports broader adoption for large LMs; code available.

title: Amortizing Federated Adaptation: Hypernetwork Driven LoRA for Personalized Foundation Models
comment: Accepted at International Workshop on Federated Learning in the Age of Foundation Models In Conjunction with IJCAI 2026 (FL@FM-IJCAI'26)
summary: Federated fine-tuning of foundation models using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) offers a communication efficient solution for distributed learning. However, existing federated LoRA methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: (1) structural aggregation bias, where independently averaging low rank factors fails to approximate the true combined update, and (2) client side initialization lag, as clients repeatedly reinitialize LoRA parameters across communication rounds, slowing convergence. We propose HyperLoRA, a unified framework that addresses both issues through amortized federated adaptation through hypernetwork-driven LoRA generation and product space aggregation. Instead of iterative per-client optimization, HyperLoRA employs a learned generator that maps client distribution signatures to LoRA initializations, effectively amortizing per client adaptation. On the server side, we introduce a learned aggregation module that directly synthesizes updates in the low-rank product space, eliminating the inconsistencies of factor-wise averaging. A lightweight residual correction module further improves stability under heterogenous (non-IID) client distributions.By replacing iterative optimization and heuristic averaging with learned operators, HyperLoRA jointly enables efficient personalization, unbiased aggregation, and faster convergence. Experiments on federated vision and vision-language benchmarks show that HyperLoRA achieves improved convergence speed, greater robustness to distribution shift, and stronger personalization performance compared to prior federated LoRA methods.

AI.tldr: HyperLoRA: a unified federated LoRA framework using a hypernetwork to generate LoRA initializations and a learned aggregation in the low-rank product space, with residual correction to handle non-IID distributions.
AI.motivation: Address two core limitations of federated LoRA: (i) structural aggregation bias due to averaging low-rank factors, and (ii) client initialization lag from reinitializing LoRA parameters each communication round, which slows convergence.
AI.method: Use a hypernetwork to map client distribution signatures to LoRA initializations (amortized per-client adaptation); a learned server aggregation module synthesizes updates directly in the low-rank product space (avoiding factor-wise averaging); a lightweight residual correction module improves stability under heterogeneous (non-IID) client distributions.
AI.result: Empirical results on federated vision and vision-language benchmarks show faster convergence, improved robustness to distribution shifts, and stronger personalization compared with prior federated LoRA methods.
AI.conclusion: Replacing iterative per-client optimization and heuristic factor averaging with learned operators enables efficient personalization, unbiased aggregation, and accelerated convergence in federated learning with LoRA adapters.

title: Adversarial Attacks Already Tell the Answer: Directional Bias-Guided Test-time Defense for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), such as CLIP, have shown strong zero-shot generalization but remain highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, posing serious risks in real-world applications. Test-time defenses for VLMs have recently emerged as a promising and efficient approach to defend against adversarial attacks without requiring costly large-scale retraining. In this work, we uncover a surprising phenomenon: under diverse input transformations, adversarial images in CLIP's feature space consistently shift along a dominant direction, in contrast to the dispersed patterns of clean images. We hypothesize that this dominant shift, termed the Defense Direction, opposes the adversarial shift, pointing features back toward their correct class centers. Building on this insight, we propose Directional Bias-guided Defense (DBD), a test-time framework that estimates the Defense Direction and employs a DB-score-based two-stream reconstruction strategy to recover robust representations. Experiments on 15 datasets demonstrate that DBD not only achieves SOTA adversarial robustness while preserving clean accuracy, but also reveals the counterintuitive result that adversarial accuracy can even surpass clean accuracy. This demonstrates that adversarial perturbations inherently encode directional priors about the true decision boundary.

AI.tldr: VLMs like CLIP are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations; a test-time defense (DBD) discovers a Defense Direction in feature space that counteracts adversarial shifts, enabling robust representations via a two-stream reconstruction and achieving SOTA robustness with clean accuracy preserved (sometimes adversarial accuracy exceeds clean).
AI.motivation: Improve robustness of vision-language models against adversarial attacks without retraining, by exploiting a consistent dominant shift pattern in adversarial examples.
AI.method: Identify the Defense Direction through input transformations; estimate a DB-score-based two-stream reconstruction (two-stream DB reconstruction) to recover robust representations at test time.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 15 datasets showing state-of-the-art adversarial robustness while preserving clean accuracy; in some cases adversarial accuracy surpasses clean accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial perturbations encode directional priors about the true decision boundary; leveraging the Defense Direction yields effective defenses in test-time for VLMs.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting as Accessibility Collapse: A Three-Level Framework for Knowledge Persistence in Continual Learning
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Sequential continual-learning experiments on CIFAR-100 using ResNet-18
summary: Catastrophic forgetting is commonly interpreted as the irreversible erasure of previously acquired knowledge during sequential learning. In this work, we investigate an alternative perspective: that forgetting may arise not from complete destruction of task representations but from a loss of accessibility to preserved information. We introduce a three-level framework separating knowledge storage, representation, and accessibility, and evaluate each component through a series of continual-learning experiments on sequential CIFAR-100 classification using ResNet-18. Our analysis combines checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry, classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability experiments. We observe complete behavioral forgetting of earlier tasks, with task accuracy collapsing from 54.8% to 0%, while linear probe performance retains approximately 76% of the original representational information. Furthermore, retraining only the final classifier restores 75.7% of the original task performance without modifying the backbone network. Layer-wise analysis reveals that early and intermediate layers preserve highly recoverable task information despite severe degradation at later stages. Projection-energy and principal-angle analyses indicate that retained knowledge persists as distributed high-dimensional representations rather than through preservation of a small dominant subspace. These findings suggest that catastrophic forgetting is better characterized as an accessibility failure than complete representational erasure, and that substantial task-relevant information remains embedded within neural representations even after functional forgetting has occurred.

AI.tldr: Forgetting is better viewed as an accessibility failure: preserved representations remain in high-dimensional, distributed form, but task access decays; retraining the final classifier can recover most performance without touching the backbone.
AI.motivation: Reframe catastrophic forgetting as an accessibility issue rather than complete representational erasure, by introducing a three-level framework (storage, representation, accessibility) and testing it via continual learning experiments.
AI.method: Three-level framework: analyze knowledge storage, representations, and accessibility. Empirically evaluate using sequential CIFAR-100 tasks with ResNet-18 across: checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry, classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability; supplement with projection-energy and principal-angle analyses to characterize the retained information.
AI.result: Complete behavioral forgetting observed (task accuracy drops from 54.8% to 0%), yet linear probes retain ~76% of the original information. Retraining only the final classifier recovers 75.7% of original task performance without altering the backbone. Early/intermediate layers hold highly recoverable information; knowledge persists as distributed high-dimensional representations rather than a single dominant subspace.
AI.conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting is best characterized as an accessibility failure. Substantial task-relevant information remains embedded in representations after forgetting, and recovery is possible by restoring access (e.g., retraining the classifier) without backbone modification.

title: Where does Absolute Position come from in decoder-only Transformers?
summary: RoPE-trained transformers distinguish absolute position in their attention patterns, even though RoPE encodes only relative offsets in the inner product. We trace this leakage to two architectural components, The causal mask is responsible for the first: its per-query softmax denominator depends on the absolute query position by construction. The residual stream supplies the second. Under causal attention the activation at position $0$ attends only to itself and runs as a closed dynamical system from the embedding of the token at that position; downstream attention reads this trajectory through sink-reading heads. Both components appear in all three architectures we study, in architecturally specific balance: NTK scaling suppresses the residual-stream component, sliding-window attention allows it to accumulate with depth, and standard RoPE sits between. Replacing the \texttt{BOS} embedding before the forward pass removes $40\%$ of the residual-stream component at early queries. Attention sinks are token-anchored stabilizers that pass forward a deterministic fingerprint of the token at position $0$, constant across inputs when that token is the auto-prepended \texttt{BOS} and varying with it otherwise.

AI.tldr: RoPE-trained transformers exhibit absolute-position leakage in attention patterns due to two architectural sources: the causal mask (per-query softmax denominator depends on absolute position) and the residual stream (a dynamical system that reads its trajectory via attention sinks). The leakage balance varies by architecture (NTK scaling suppresses the residual stream; sliding-window accumulates it with depth; standard RoPE sits in between). Replacing the BOS embedding reduces residual leakage by ~40% early on. Attention sinks act as token-anchored stabilizers that fingerprint the token at position 0, deterministic with BOS and variable otherwise.
AI.motivation: To explain why RoPE-based models reveal absolute positional information despite encoding only relative offsets, and to identify which architectural components enable this leakage.
AI.method: Analytical tracing of three RoPE-based architectures. Examine how the causal mask affects the softmax denominator and how the residual stream contributes to position leakage. Compare NTK-scaling, sliding-window, and standard RoPE variants. Perform ablation by removing/replacing the BOS embedding. Introduce the concept of attention sinks as stabilizers that read the first-token trajectory.
AI.result: Identification of two leakage sources: (1) the causal mask causing absolute-position dependence in the per-query attention normalization; (2) the residual stream providing a dynamical trajectory that can be read by downstream attention heads. Architecture-specific balances: NTK scaling dampens the residual path, sliding-window permits accumulation with depth, RoPE sits between. BOS replacement reduces about 40% of the residual component at early queries. Attention sinks offer a token-anchored, deterministic fingerprint of the initial token, stable when BOS is auto-prepended and varying with other tokens.
AI.conclusion: RoPE does not isolate relative-position information; absolute-position leakage arises from compound architectural effects. Understanding these sources enables targeted design to control leakage for interpretability or robustness, and introduces attention sinks as a mechanism to stabilize or read early-token information.

title: SC-MFJ: A Simple Haptic Quality Metric for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Standard segmentation metrics such as Dice and Hausdorff distance measure geometric overlap but say nothing about whether a segmented surface is suitable for haptic rendering in surgical simulation. We propose SC-MFJ (Surface-Constrained Mean Force Jerk), a simple, inexpensive metric that samples a segmented organ surface with many short virtual stylus walks and measures how jerky the resulting contact forces are. The metric is computed from existing segmentation outputs and uses roughly one minute of CPU time per case. We evaluate three pancreas CT segmentation approaches-binary nnU-Net output, Gaussian-smoothed output, and learned signed distance function (SDF) regression-across 80 cases in five-fold cross-validation. SC-MFJ reveals a 147x gap in haptic quality between the raw binary baseline and simple Gaussian post-processing, a difference entirely invisible to Dice and HD95. It also shows that learned SDF regression, despite requiring full model retraining, produces more variable haptic quality than Gaussian smoothing, with a case-level standard deviation of 168 N/s2 compared with 22 N/s2 for Gaussian. A second evaluation on the LiTS liver dataset (131 cases) confirms the generality of these findings: the binary-to-Gaussian gap widens to 189x, and Gaussian smoothing again produces consistently low force jerk across all folds. Our results suggest that for haptic simulation applications, a one-line post-processing step may be sufficient, and that a cheap metric like SC-MFJ can flag problems that geometric metrics miss.

AI.tldr: SC-MFJ is a cheap, surface-constrained jerk-based metric to evaluate haptic suitability of segmentation outputs. It reveals large gaps between raw binary and Gaussian-smoothed outputs not captured by Dice/HD95, and shows SDF regression as more variable in haptic quality. Gaussian smoothing emerges as a simple, effective post-processing to improve haptics. The metric can flag issues missed by geometric metrics and is inexpensive.
AI.motivation: Geometric overlap metrics (Dice, Hausdorff) do not capture whether a segmented surface yields realistic haptics in surgical simulation; a fast, surface-aware proxy is needed to assess haptic plausibility of segmentation outputs.
AI.method: Compute SC-MFJ by sampling the segmented surface with many short stylus-like contacts and measuring jerks in the resulting contact forces. Evaluate three pancreas segmentation pipelines (binary nnU-Net, Gaussian-smoothed, learned SDF regression) across 80 cases using five-fold CV; replicate on LiTS liver dataset (131 cases).
AI.result: SC-MFJ uncovers a 147× gap between binary and Gaussian-smoothed pancreas outputs (invisible to Dice/HD95) and shows that SDF regression yields more variable haptic quality (168 N/s^2 vs 22 N/s^2 for Gaussian). On LiTS, the gap widens to 189×, with Gaussian smoothing producing consistently low jerk across folds.
AI.conclusion: A simple one-line post-processing (Gaussian smoothing) can substantially improve haptic quality in segmentation pipelines, and SC-MFJ provides a cheap, surface-focused metric to flag issues that geometric metrics miss.

title: When Good Enough Is Optimal: Multiplication-Only Matrix Inversion Approximation for Quantized Gated DeltaNet
summary: Matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention is a major bottleneck for long-context modeling, particularly on NPUs, where forward-substitution-based methods exhibit limited parallelism and poor hardware utilization. We propose a fast, Matrix Multiplication (MatMul)-based algorithm tailored for strictly lower-triangular matrices arising in chunk-wise linear attention. Motivated by the rapid growth of Neumann-series terms and the diagonal concentration of the inverse matrix, we employ a truncated Neumann expansion with structural masking and parallel residual correction to eliminate sequential dependencies. We further extend our method to low-bits INT by mitigating the dynamic range expansion arising from repeated matrix power operations, and adapt the approximation order and residual step to the chunk size to minimize computational cost while preserving the model's accuracy. Experiments on Qwen3.5-family models demonstrate up to 5$\times$ kernel-level speedup and a 20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, while preserving accuracy under both floating-point and low-precision inference. Our method offers an efficient and hardware-friendly solution for scalable linear attention.

AI.tldr: A fast MatMul-based approach yields a 5x kernel speedup for chunk-wise linear attention via a truncated Neumann expansion with masking and residual correction, enabling low-bit inference with preserved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long-context modeling with linear attention suffers from a bottleneck in matrix inversion, especially on NPUs, due to limited parallelism in forward-substitution-based methods. A fast, hardware-friendly solution is needed to enable scalable long-context processing.
AI.method: Develop a MatMul-based algorithm for strictly lower-triangular matrices from chunk-wise linear attention. Use a truncated Neumann expansion leveraging rapid term growth and diagonal concentration of the inverse, combined with structural masking and parallel residual correction to remove sequential dependencies. Extend to low-bit INT by controlling dynamic range from repeated matrix powers, and adapt the approximation order and residual steps to the chunk size to balance cost and accuracy.
AI.result: Empirical results on Qwen3.5-family models show up to 5× kernel-level speedup and a 20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, with preserved accuracy under both floating-point and low-precision inference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a hardware-efficient, scalable solution for linear attention via a MatMul-based, truncated Neumann expansion that mitigates dependencies and supports low-precision inference.

title: ProSarc: Prosody-Aware Sarcasm Recognition Framework via Temporal Prosodic Incongruity
summary: We present ProSarc, an audio-only framework that detects sarcasm by modelling temporal prosodic incongruity, that is, the mismatch between local prosodic dynamics and the utterance-level emotional baseline. Dual encoding paths, a Global Emotion Encoder and a Temporal Prosody Encoder (BiLSTM + multi-head attention), feed a Prosodic Incongruity Analyzer that produces a scalar incongruity score for classification. Monte Carlo dropout provides uncertainty estimates, and an attention-based mechanism localises sarcastic onset without frame-level labels. ProSarc outperforms prior audio-only methods on MUStARD++ (F1=75.3) and generalises to spontaneous (PodSarc, F1=62.9) and cross-lingual speech (MuSaG, F1=65.6). Ten-run validation confirms the contribution of incongruity modelling (Wilcoxon p=0.002, Cohen's d=1.51). Human evaluation shows that model uncertainty tracks perceptual ambiguity and predicted onsets align with human-annotated temporal windows.

AI.tldr: ProSarc is an audio-only sarcasm detector that models prosodic incongruity via dual encoders (Global Emotion and Temporal Prosody) feeding a Prosodic Incongruity Analyzer; it provides MC dropout-based uncertainty and attends to localize sarcasm onset without frame-level labels, achieving state-of-the-art results across MUStARD++, PodSarc, and MuSaG, with strong cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of audio-only sarcasm detection by explicitly modeling the mismatch between local prosodic dynamics and the utterance-level emotional baseline. The approach also offers uncertainty estimates and onset localization without frame-level annotations, aiming for robust generalization across spontaneous and cross-lingual speech.
AI.method: Dual encoding: Global Emotion Encoder and Temporal Prosody Encoder (BiLSTM + multi-head attention) feeding a Prosodic Incongruity Analyzer to produce a scalar incongruity score for sarcasm classification. Monte Carlo dropout yields uncertainty estimates. An attention mechanism localizes sarcasm onset without frame-level labels. Evaluation via ten-run validation on MUStARD++ (and generalization to PodSarc and MuSaG).
AI.result: ProSarc improves on audio-only sarcasm detection benchmarks: MUStARD++ F1=75.3; PodSarc F1=62.9; MuSaG F1=65.6. Ten-run validation confirms incongruity modelling contributes (Wilcoxon p=0.002, Cohen's d=1.51). Human evaluation indicates model uncertainty tracks perceptual ambiguity and predicted onsets align with human-annotated temporal windows.
AI.conclusion: Modeling prosodic incongruity with dual encoders and MC dropout yields strong audio-only sarcasm detection performance, provides uncertainty estimates, and localizes onset without frame-level labels. The approach generalizes well to spontaneous and cross-lingual speech.

title: DisasterBench: A Multimodal Benchmark for UAV-Based Disaster Response in Complex Environments
summary: When a disaster unfolds, responders must answer not only what is happening, but also why it is happening, what will happen next, and what to do now, often from noisy low-altitude UAV views and under tight on-site compute constraints. However, most existing multimodal benchmarks emphasize perception (e.g., recognition/description), cover limited disaster types, and provide insufficient support for the multi-stage reasoning required in practical emergency response. We introduce DisasterBench, a multi-stage multimodal reasoning benchmark for UAV-Based disaster response in complex environments. DisasterBench spans 14 disaster-related scene types and 9 response-critical tasks across pre-, during-, and post-disaster stages, with fine-grained disaster-task mappings that explicitly test causal attribution, propagation prediction, damage analysis, and decision-oriented reasoning. To enable reasoning on the edge, we further propose DisasterVL, a lightweight multimodal model optimized with a three-stage pipeline combining domain instruction tuning, chain-of-thought-guided multimodal alignment, and reinforcement learning-based policy optimization. Experiments across 21 popular MLLMs show that our 2B-parameter DisasterVL outperforms all evaluated open-source models and substantially narrows the gap to state-of-the-art closed-source models, achieving GPT-4o-comparable reasoning accuracy with superior efficiency. The project page is available at https://github.com/TanmouTT/DisasterBench.

AI.tldr: Proposes DisasterBench for multi-stage UAV disaster reasoning and DisasterVL, a compact 2B model, achieving GPT-4o-level reasoning with edge efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal benchmarks emphasize perception, cover limited disaster types, and lack multi-stage reasoning under edge compute constraints; there is a need for causal attribution, propagation prediction, damage analysis, and decision-focused reasoning across pre-, during-, and post-disaster stages.
AI.method: DisasterBench spans 14 disaster-related scene types and 9 response-critical tasks with fine-grained mappings to test causal attribution, propagation prediction, damage analysis, and decision-oriented reasoning. DisasterVL uses a three-stage pipeline: domain instruction tuning, chain-of-thought-guided multimodal alignment, and reinforcement learning-based policy optimization, evaluated across 21 open-source MLLMs.
AI.result: DisasterVL outperforms all evaluated open-source models and narrows the gap to state-of-the-art closed-source models, achieving GPT-4o-comparable reasoning accuracy with superior efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The combination of DisasterBench and DisasterVL enables practical, edge-enabled, multi-stage multimodal reasoning for UAV-based disaster response; project page available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: Learning to replenish: A hybrid deep reinforcement learning for dynamic inventory management in the pharmaceutical supply chains
summary: Pharmaceutical supply chains (PSCs) struggle with inventory management (IM) due to unpredictable demand patterns and variable lead times associated with restocking. This complexity is further compounded by the finite shelf lives of pharmaceutical products, which necessitate a delicate balance between adequate stock and minimal waste. These intertwined factors create a complex optimization problem that requires sophisticated inventory strategies to ensure both product availability and PSC efficiency. This study aims to develop an optimal inventory replenishment policy for pharmaceutical products that can handle the stochasticity arising from uncertain demand and variable PSC conditions. The objective is to maximize the profitability of the PSC while maintaining a high patient service level. We formulate the problem as a Markov decision process and propose a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach, specifically, a hybrid asynchronous advantage actor critic distributed proximal policy optimization (A3C DPPO)algorithm. The A3C DPPO algorithm is tailored to handle the continuous action space inherent in IM. The numerical results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm adaptively updates the inventory replenishment strategy under dynamic scenarios, resulting in lower inventory costs compared to various benchmarks. We also conduct numerical validation using real-world pharmaceutical inventory data to confirm the practical feasibility of the proposed algorithm.

AI.tldr: DRL-based optimal replenishment policy for pharmaceutical inventories under stochastic demand, variable lead times, and shelf-life constraints, using a hybrid A3C-DPPO for continuous replenishment actions; shows lower costs and feasible real-data validation.
AI.motivation: Pharmaceutical supply chains contend with uncertain demand, variable lead times, and finite shelf lives, causing stockouts and waste. A robust, scalable policy is needed to maximize profitability while sustaining service levels.
AI.method: Formulate as a Markov decision process. Develop a hybrid asynchronous advantage actor-critic with distributed proximal policy optimization (A3C-DPPO) tailored for continuous action spaces to determine replenishment quantities. Evaluate on synthetic scenarios and real-world pharmaceutical inventory data.
AI.result: The proposed DRL policy adaptively updates replenishment under dynamic conditions, achieving lower inventory costs compared to benchmarks. Numerical results and real-data validation indicate practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: DRL-based replenishment with A3C-DPPO is effective for pharmaceutical IM under uncertainty and shelf-life constraints, offering a practical, scalable solution for PSC efficiency and profitability.

title: Symb-xMIL: Symbolic Explanations for Multiple Instance Learning in Digital Pathology
summary: Explanations of multiple instance learning (MIL) models are widely used for validation and discovery in digital histopathology. Existing methods primarily rely on heatmaps that highlight influential regions but do not explain how evidence from different tissue regions is combined to produce a prediction. This limits interpretability, especially when decisions depend on interactions between tissue features. We introduce Symbolic explainable MIL (Symb-xMIL), a post-hoc explanation framework that quantifies how a MIL model's behavior aligns with human-readable decision rules, expressed as logical relationships (e.g., AND, OR, NOT) between input features. These alignment scores reveal semantic patterns underlying the model's predictions. We evaluate Symb-xMIL on synthetic and real-world histopathology datasets. On synthetic MIL data, Symb-xMIL reliably recovers ground-truth logical rules. In a clinical tumor detection task, the best-aligned rules uncover heterogeneous decision patterns and expose hidden model errors. On an HPV-prediction task on TCGA-HNSCC, a cohort of head and neck cancer, our framework refines patient survival stratification beyond HPV status with potential clinical relevance. Overall, Symb-xMIL extends MIL explainability beyond visual attribution toward structured, rule-based reasoning, enabling more transparent and semantically grounded interpretation of model predictions.

AI.tldr: Symb-xMIL is a post-hoc symbolic MIL explainer that aligns MIL predictions with human-readable logical rules (AND/OR/NOT) over input features, enabling rule-based, semantically grounded explanations in histopathology.
AI.motivation: Heatmap-based explanations highlight influential regions but do not reveal how evidence from different tissue regions combines to drive predictions; there is a need for interpretable, rule-based reasoning that captures interactions among tissue features in MIL.
AI.method: Quantify and score the alignment between a MIL model's behavior and user-defined logical rules expressed over input features. The framework computes alignment scores, supports recovery of ground-truth logical rules on synthetic data, and is evaluated on real histopathology datasets to reveal decision patterns, model errors, and clinically relevant stratifications.
AI.result: On synthetic MIL data, Symb-xMIL reliably recovers ground-truth logical rules. In a clinical tumor detection task, the best-aligned rules reveal heterogeneous decision patterns and expose hidden model errors. In HPV-prediction on TCGA-HNSCC, the framework refines patient survival stratification beyond HPV status with potential clinical relevance.
AI.conclusion: Symb-xMIL extends MIL explainability beyond visual attribution to structured, rule-based reasoning, enabling more transparent and semantically grounded interpretation of model predictions.

title: Unsupervised Pattern Analysis in Japanese Veterinary Toxicology: A Regulatory-Compliant Framework for Cross-Species Risk Assessment
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
summary: Veterinary pharmacovigilance systems are essential for monitoring adverse drug events (ADEs), yet existing approaches often fail to capture region-specific toxicity patterns shaped by local biological and regulatory contexts. In Japan, these challenges are amplified by species-specific metabolic differences and reporting practices defined by the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry, and Fisheries (MAFF). Most prior work relies on prediction-oriented models, limiting mechanistic interpretability. This study proposes a regulatory-integrated unsupervised framework for pattern discovery using the National Veterinary Assay Laboratory (NVAL) database. ADEs are encoded into organ system-aligned representations and adjusted for species-specific reporting biases, enabling cross-species comparison. Similarity-based clustering and dimensionality reduction are applied to identify latent toxicity structures. Analysis of 4,120 high-confidence ADE reports (9,080 drug-ADE combinations) identified three significant species clusters (p < 0.01), including hepatic-dominant patterns in companion animals (0.42 $\pm$ 0.06), renal toxicity in ruminants (0.39 $\pm$ 0.07), and dermatological sensitivity in sheep (0.35 $\pm$ 0.07). Drug-level clustering achieved 83% alignment with pharmacological classes, while cosine similarity outperformed alternative metrics (silhouette score: 0.48; cluster precision: 87%). Regulatory validation showed strong agreement with established classifications. These findings demonstrate that regulation-aligned unsupervised analysis can uncover biologically meaningful, region-specific toxicity patterns, providing an interpretable and scalable framework for veterinary drug safety assessment.

AI.tldr: A regulatory-integrated unsupervised framework using the National Veterinary Assay Laboratory (NVAL) database identifies region- and species-specific toxicity patterns in Japan, enabling cross-species comparison and interpretable clustering that aligns with pharmacological classes and regulatory classifications.
AI.motivation: To capture region- and species-specific toxicity patterns shaped by local biology and MAFF reporting practices, addressing limitations of prediction-focused models and improving mechanistic interpretability.
AI.method: An unsupervised, regulation-integrated pipeline: encode ADEs into organ-system aligned representations; adjust for species-specific reporting biases; perform cross-species comparisons; apply similarity-based clustering and dimensionality reduction on 4,120 high-confidence ADE reports (9,080 drug-ADE combinations).
AI.result: Identified three significant species clusters (p < 0.01): hepatic-dominant patterns in companion animals (0.42 ± 0.06), renal toxicity in ruminants (0.39 ± 0.07), and dermatological sensitivity in sheep (0.35 ± 0.07). Drug-level clustering aligned 83% with pharmacological classes; cosine similarity outperformed alternative metrics (silhouette score: 0.48; cluster precision: 87%). Regulatory validation showed strong agreement with established classifications.
AI.conclusion: Regulation-aligned unsupervised analysis can uncover biologically meaningful, region-specific toxicity patterns and offers an interpretable, scalable framework for veterinary drug safety assessment.

title: OrderGrad: Optimizing Beyond the Mean with Order-Statistic Policy Gradient Estimation
summary:
Policy-gradient methods usually optimize expected return, but many real world applications care about distributional properties of returns: tail risk, outlier robustness, or best-of-K discovery. We introduce OrderGrad, a family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives. OrderGrad optimizes finite-sample L-statistics, i.e., weighted averages of sorted rewards or costs, recovering objectives such as VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, and top-m/best-of-K criteria by changing only the rank weights. For any fixed sample size and rank-weight vector, OrderGrad provides an unbiased gradient estimator for the corresponding order-statistic objective. The method is implemented as a simple reward transformation that can then be used in an otherwise standard policy-gradient or reparameterized update. We study the resulting estimator's variance behavior and evaluate it on tasks where mean optimization is mismatched to the deployment objective, including LLM math post-training and other tasks. OrderGrad provides a unified, plug-and-play route to risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning.
  Code: https://github.com/paavo5/ordergrad

AI.tldr: OrderGrad provides unbiased gradient estimators for optimizing finite-sample order-statistics (like VaR, CVaR, medians, trimmed means, top-K) in reinforcement learning by transforming rewards; it yields a plug-and-play method compatible with standard policy-gradient and reparameterization updates to target distributional objectives and assess risk-sensitive performance.
AI.motivation: Many real-world tasks require control of distributional properties of returns (tail risk, outliers, best-of-K) rather than just maximizing mean. OrderGrad unifies gradient estimation for order-statistic objectives and enables risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning.
AI.method: Introduce OrderGrad: a family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives (L-statistics). For fixed sample size n and a rank-weight vector w, the L-statistic is a weighted sum of sorted rewards. The gradient is unbiased with respect to this objective. Implemented as a reward transformation that can be used with standard policy-gradient or reparameterized updates. An analysis of variance is provided.
AI.result: The method is evaluated on tasks where mean optimization is mismatched with deployment objectives, including LLM math post-training and other tasks. Results indicate OrderGrad can effectively optimize distributional objectives and offers a unified pathway to risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning; code is provided at the stated repository.
AI.conclusion: OrderGrad provides a simple, general, plug-and-play framework to optimize order-statistic objectives (VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, top-K) by constructing unbiased gradient estimators for fixed sample sizes and rank weights, enabling risk-sensitive RL with standard optimization pipelines.

title: From Reward-Hack Activations to Agentic Risk States: Context-Calibrated Mechanistic Monitoring in LLM Agents
summary: Language-model agents act through repeated cycles of observation, reasoning, and action selection, making safety monitoring depend on both internal model state and environment context. We study reward-hacking monitors in ReAct-style agents acting in Gameable ALFWorld and WebShop. Agents are instrumented with activation-based reward-hack scores, token-level entropy, and decision-context features. We find that adapters fine-tuned on \textit{School-of-Reward-Hacks} dataset can transfer reward-hack tendencies into agentic action selection, especially when the environment exposes proxy-reward affordances. However, mitigating such behavior cannot rely on activation dynamics alone. High reward-hack activation identifies a latent policy state, but does not necessarily imply an immediate exploit action. Across next-step prediction tasks, entropy and context-calibrated internal features improve risk estimation over reward-hack activation alone. Activation-direction steering further reduces proxy-exploit behavior in selected mixed-adapter regimes. Overall, our results support context-calibrated internal monitoring for agents: reward-hack activation identifies a latent policy state, while entropy and decision context help determine when that state becomes risky action.

AI.tldr: Activation-based reward-hack signals reveal latent policy states in language-model agents; entropy and decision-context features are necessary to assess risk and curb proxy exploits in ReAct-style agents.
AI.motivation: To study safety monitoring for sequential decision-making agents and address vulnerabilities to reward hacking when agents operate with internal states and environmental context.
AI.method: Instrument agents with activation-based reward-hack scores, token-level entropy, and decision-context features; fine-tune adapters on the School-of-Reward-Hacks dataset; evaluate in Gameable ALFWorld and WebShop; analyze next-step prediction tasks and use activation-direction steering in mixed-adapter regimes.
AI.result: Activation activation correlates with a latent policy state but does not imply an immediate exploit action; entropy and context-calibrated features improve risk estimation over reward-hack activation alone; activation-direction steering reduces proxy-exploit behavior in some regimes.
AI.conclusion: Context-calibrated internal monitoring is essential: reward-hack activation signals a latent state, while entropy and decision context determine when that state becomes risky enough to act on.

title: Geodesic Flow Matching on a Riemannian Degradation Manifold for Blind Image Restoration
summary: Blind image restoration requires recovering clean images from observations corrupted by unknown and potentially mixed degradations. While recent deterministic flow-based methods model restoration as transport processes that map degraded images to clean ones, they typically rely on Euclidean interpolation, implicitly assuming linear degradation geometry. In this paper, we explicitly model degradations as points on a low-dimensional Riemannian manifold and formulate restoration as geodesic transport on the joint image-manifold space. Using a geodesic flow matching objective, we learn intrinsic transport dynamics that respect the curvature of degradation space. This framework generalizes linear flow matching, provides a principled treatment of mixed degradations as geodesic compositions, and yields a clean theoretical interpretation for generalization beyond observed degradations.

AI.tldr: Model degradations on a low-dimensional Riemannian manifold and restore via geodesic transport on the joint image-manifold; learns intrinsic transport dynamics with geodesic flow matching; generalizes linear flow matching and handles mixed degradations via geodesic compositions; provides theoretical interpretation for generalization beyond observed degradations.
AI.motivation: Deterministic flow-based restoration often relies on Euclidean interpolation, implicitly assuming linear degradation geometry. This limits modeling of nonlinear, curved degradation spaces and mixed degradations, and weakens generalization to unseen degradations.
AI.method: Explicitly model degradations as points on a low-dimensional Riemannian manifold and formulate restoration as geodesic transport on the joint image-manifold space. Use a geodesic flow matching objective to learn intrinsic transport dynamics that respect curvature of the degradation space. Treat mixed degradations as compositions of geodesic transports (geodesic flows).
AI.result: Provides a geometric restoration framework that generalizes linear flow matching, enabling principled handling of mixed degradations. Offers a clean theoretical interpretation for generalization beyond observed degradations and frames restoration as learning intrinsic, curvature-aware transport dynamics.
AI.conclusion: A principled geometric formulation of restoration on a joint image–degradation manifold; connects geodesic transport to degradation geometry, enabling curvature-aware generalization and a natural extension to mixed degradations, while reducing to linear flow matching in Euclidean limits.

title: A Sliced-Wasserstein Framework on Correlation Matrices for EEG Decoding
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) offers noninvasive, millisecond resolution recordings of neuronal activity and is widely used in neuroscience and healthcare. Many EEG decoding pipelines rely on covariance descriptors for their robustness to noise, but such representations are sensitive to channel-wise scaling. Recent studies have therefore advocated full-rank correlation matrices as a scale-invariant alternative for EEG decoding. In this paper, we propose a general framework for Sliced Wasserstein (SW) discrepancies on manifolds endowed with Pullback Euclidean Metrics (PEMs), termed Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW). Within this framework, we instantiate two Correlation Sliced-Wasserstein (CorSW) discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices under two recently introduced correlation geometries, \textit{i.e.}, the Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM). Building on CorSW, we further develop a domain generalization (DG) framework for EEG decoding. Experiments on three EEG datasets demonstrate improved generalization under distribution shifts, with low training overhead and no additional inference cost. The source code is available at https://github.com/ChenHu-ML/CorSW.

AI.tldr: Proposes Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW) for full-rank correlation matrices on EEG, introducing CorSW discrepancies under Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM); integrates a domain generalization framework for EEG decoding. Demonstrates improved generalization under distribution shifts across three datasets with low training overhead and no extra inference cost; code available.
AI.motivation: EEG covariance-based representations are robust to noise but sensitive to channel scaling. A scale-invariant representation using full-rank correlation matrices can improve decoding. We need a flexible, computationally efficient framework to compare such matrices on their natural manifolds and to extend this to domain generalization.
AI.method: Define a general framework PEMS-Wasserstein on manifolds endowed with Pullback Euclidean Metrics. Instantiate two CorSW discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices using two metrics: Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM). Integrate CorSW into a domain generalization pipeline for EEG decoding and evaluate across three datasets.
AI.result: CorSW-based DG improves generalization under distribution shifts across three EEG datasets, with low training overhead and no extra inference cost.
AI.conclusion: PEMSW with CorSW (OLM/LSM) provides effective, scalable, and domain-generalizable EEG decoding; demonstrated practical benefits and released source code.

title: Closing the Loop on Latent Reasoning via Test-Time Reconstruction
summary: Recent work moves intermediate reasoning from natural-language traces into latent or cache-level representations to reduce token overhead and avoid a discrete communication bottleneck. However, this shift also removes a key advantage of textual reasoning: intermediate states are no longer inspectable, making it difficult to determine whether a latent state still preserves the constraints of the original query. As a result, latent reasoning typically operates in an open loop, where a latent state is produced and consumed without an input-anchored fidelity check. We propose ReLAT (Reconstruction-Guided Latent Reasoning At Test Time), a self-supervised test-time training method that closes this loop using the query itself as the reference. Our key observation is that if a latent state faithfully represents a query, the query should be recoverable from it; if the query cannot be recovered, the latent state has lost task-relevant information. ReLAT operationalizes this principle by constructing a differentiable Question -> Latent Thought -> Question cycle and optimizing query reconstruction loss through the latent thought before answer generation. This anchors opaque latent computation to the problem specification it is supposed to represent. Across mathematical reasoning, knowledge QA, and code generation benchmarks on the Qwen family, ReLAT consistently improves over single-model inference, text-based collaboration, open-loop latent collaboration, and alternative test-time training objectives. On Qwen3-8B, ReLAT raises AIME 2024 accuracy from 56.7% to 73.3%, a 16.6-point gain over the strongest open-loop latent baseline.

AI.tldr: ReLAT: a test-time, self-supervised reconstruction objective that ties latent reasoning to the original query by reconstructing the query from latent thoughts, yielding significant gains across math, knowledge QA, and code generation benchmarks; notably AIME 2024 accuracy on Qwen3-8B improves from 56.7% to 73.3%.
AI.motivation: Latent or cache-level reasoning reduces token overhead but sacrifices inspectability and fidelity checks. A method is needed to verify that latent states preserve the constraints of the original query by anchoring them to the input.
AI.method: Introduce a differentiable cycle: Query -> Latent Thought -> Query; optimize query reconstruction loss at test time to ensure the latent thought preserves the input problem. Apply this self-supervised reconstruction objective across mathematical reasoning, knowledge QA, and code generation benchmarks on Qwen models, comparing against single-model inference, text-based collaboration, open-loop latent collaboration, and other test-time objectives.
AI.result: ReLAT yields consistent improvements over baselines across math, knowledge QA, and code generation tasks. On Qwen3-8B, AIME 2024 accuracy rises from 56.7% to 73.3%, a 16.6-point gain over the strongest open-loop latent baseline.
AI.conclusion: Anchoring latent computation to the input problem via a query reconstruction objective at test time stabilizes latent reasoning and enhances task fidelity, suggesting broad applicability of reconstruction-driven test-time training for latent reasoning systems.

title: Adaptive state-action abstractions via rate-distortion
summary: When learning to walk, infants seem to address a coarse version of the problem first - stay upright, reach the caregiver - and refine it only when further practice at that resolution stops paying off. Reinforcement learning offers multiple techniques for building simple versions of complex tasks, but lacks general principles for how to dynamically adjust the granularity of these abstractions during learning. This paper proposes one such principle: refine the abstraction as soon as the learning error within it becomes comparable to the error induced by the abstraction itself. Here, we investigate one way of formalising this principle via a performance certificate that decomposes value error into two terms: a learning error bound captured by a Bellman residual, and an abstraction error bound given by a bisimulation metric. The resulting switching strategy is implemented by soft state-action abstractions built from rate-distortion principles, whose resolution along state and action axes can be continuously adjusted. We validate this construction in a range of tabular settings, showing that near-optimal performance can be achieved under substantial lossy compression of state and action information.

AI.tldr: A principled method for dynamically refining RL abstractions: switch between coarse and fine representations by comparing learning error to abstraction error using a performance certificate based on Bellman residual and bisimulation distance; implemented with rate-distortion-based soft abstractions with tunable resolution; validated in tabular tasks achieving near-optimal performance under lossy compression.
AI.motivation: To solve the problem of choosing the granularity of state-action abstractions during learning. A general principle is needed to adapt abstraction complexity as learning progresses, improving data efficiency without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Introduce a performance certificate that decomposes value error into a learning (Bellman residual) term and an abstraction (bisimulation) term. Use a switching strategy over soft, rate-distortion-based state-action abstractions whose resolution can be continuously tuned along state and action axes. Validate the approach in tabular RL settings, demonstrating effective refinement decisions and robustness to information loss.
AI.result: The framework enables near-optimal performance under substantial lossy compression of state-action information in a range of tabular experiments, indicating effective dynamic abstraction refinement.
AI.conclusion: A principled, tunable approach to adapt RL abstractions during learning improves data efficiency and performance, with a demonstrated ability to operate under lossy compression in tabular domains; future work may extend to larger-scale or function-approximation settings.

title: RedKnot: Efficient Long-Context LLM Serving with Head-Aware KV Reuse and SegPagedAttention
summary:
As the input length of large language model (LLM) serving continues to grow, the KV cache has become a dominant bottleneck in AI infrastructure. It limits GPU memory capacity, serving concurrency, cache reuse, and distributed scalability. Several important problems, including position-independent KV cache, prefix KV cache compression, hot/cold KV cache separation, and distributed KV cache management, all depend on how the KV cache is represented and managed. However, existing serving systems largely rely on a monolithic KV cache abstraction, where the KV cache is treated as a homogeneous sequence of token-level memory blocks and managed with similar policies across attention heads and serving scenarios. We observe that KV cache utility is highly structured across KV heads: different heads exhibit different functional roles, attention distances, and runtime importance. Therefore, a full KV cache is not always necessary for every head, token range, or serving scenario.
  We present RedKnot, a head-aware KV cache management system for LLM serving. RedKnot breaks the conventional monolithic KV cache abstraction by decomposing the KV cache along KV heads, whose importance and effective attention ranges vary significantly across serving scenarios. This head-level decomposition turns the KV cache from a monolithic tensor abstraction into a structured memory object, enabling RedKnot to uniformly support position-independent KV reuse, prefix KV compression, hot/cold KV separation, and distributed KV placement while preserving output fidelity and improving resource efficiency, without requiring model retraining or fine-tuning. RedKnot establishes a new foundation for AI infrastructure by transforming the KV cache from a monolithic, passive runtime artifact into a dynamic, model-aware runtime substrate for scalable LLM serving.

AI.tldr: RedKnot introduces a head-aware KV cache management for LLM serving by decomposing the KV cache along attention heads, enabling position-independent KV reuse, prefix compression, hot/cold separation, and distributed placement while preserving output fidelity and improving resource efficiency, without model retraining.
AI.motivation: KV cache is a primary bottleneck in LLM serving as input length grows. A monolithic KV cache fails to exploit structured diversity across attention heads, hindering memory, throughput, and scalability. A head-aware, structured memory representation could unlock more efficient reuse, compression, and distribution.
AI.method: Decompose the KV cache along KV heads to create a structured memory object. Implement uniform policies across heads to support position-independent reuse, prefix KV compression, hot/cold separation, and distributed placement without requiring retraining. Treat the KV cache as model-aware runtime substrate rather than a passive tensor.
AI.result: Enables efficient KV cache management across multiple serving scenarios with fidelity preserved, supporting position-independent reuse, prefix compression, hot/cold separation, and distributed placement, thereby improving resource efficiency and scalability without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Shifts KV cache from a monolithic, passive artifact to a dynamic, model-aware runtime substrate, establishing a foundation for scalable LLM serving infrastructure.

title: Towards One-to-Many Temporal Grounding
summary: Temporal Grounding (TG) aims to localize video segments corresponding to a textual query. Prior research predominantly focuses on single-segment retrieval. Real-world scenarios, however, often require localizing multiple disjoint segments for a single query -- a setting we term One-to-Many Temporal Grounding (OMTG). Previous state-of-the-art MLLMs, optimized for one-to-one settings, struggle in this context, often yielding near-zero scores due to a lack of event cardinality perception. To bridge this gap, we present a systematic solution with three key contributions. First, we establish the first comprehensive OMTG benchmark, introducing Count Accuracy (C-Acc) and Effective Temporal F1 (EtF1) as evaluation metrics. Second, we curate a high-quality OMTG dataset comprising 56k samples through a sophisticated construction pipeline. Third, we develop novel temporal and caption reward functions specifically designed for OMTG. In particular, the caption reward leverages Chain-of-Thought reasoning over dense video captions to explicitly guide policy optimization toward both preciseness and completeness. Extensive experiments show our model achieves a new state-of-the-art EtF1 of 43.65\% on OMTG Bench, outperforming Gemini 2.5 Pro and Seed-1.8 by 15.85\% and 15.61\%, respectively.

AI.tldr: A new one-to-many temporal grounding framework with benchmarks, dataset, and specialized rewards, achieving state-of-the-art effectiveness (EtF1) on OMTG benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Real-world tasks require locating multiple disjoint video segments for a single text query; existing MLLMs optimize one-to-one and lack event cardinality perception.
AI.method: Threefold: (1) establish OMTG benchmark with Count Accuracy and Effective Temporal F1; (2) curate a 56k-sample OMTG dataset; (3) introduce temporal and caption reward functions, including a Chain-of-Thought-based caption reward guiding preciseness and completeness.
AI.result: New EtF1: 43.65% on OMTG Bench, surpassing Gemini 2.5 Pro and Seed-1.8 by 15.85% and 15.61%.
AI.conclusion: Addresses the cardinality perception gap, provides benchmarks and rewards for OMTG, and demonstrates efficacy of COH-based caption rewards for policy optimization.

title: Tight list replicability bounds via a novel sphere covering theorem
summary:
In recent years, list replicability has emerged as a framework for formalizing reproducibility in learning theory. A central question is how the required list size relates to the accuracy parameter and natural complexity measures of the hypothesis class.
  To achieve sharp bounds on list replicability, we prove a novel topological sphere covering theorem, derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. Specifically, if the $d$-sphere is covered by open sets, each of which lies in an open hemisphere, then $d+1$ of these sets must have a common intersection. Using this result, we obtain a sharp bound on the relationship between list size and accuracy for VC classes. We also show that for large-margin half-spaces, provided the margin is not too large, the optimal list size equals the ambient dimension. However, when the margin is taken to be very large, we devise a replicable algorithm achieving the minimal list size of $\lceil d/2 \rceil + 1$.

AI.tldr: Sharp bounds on list replicability for VC classes using a Borsuk-Ulam-based sphere covering theorem; for large-margin classifiers the optimal list size is dimension-dependent (equals d in moderate margins; ⌈d/2⌉+1 for very large margins).
AI.motivation: Address how the required list size for replicability depends on accuracy and intrinsic complexity (VC dimension) of the hypothesis class; establish tight, parameter-dependent bounds.
AI.method: Develop a topological sphere covering theorem derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. Apply it to derive sharp bounds for list replicability of VC classes. Analyze large-margin half-spaces to characterize optimal list sizes and construct a replicable algorithm achieving ⌈d/2⌉+1 under large margins.
AI.result: Establishes a sharp bound on the list size–accuracy relationship for VC classes. For large-margin half-spaces with moderate margins, the optimal list size equals the ambient dimension d; for very large margins, a replicable algorithm achieves the minimal list size ⌈d/2⌉+1.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that topological methods yield tight bounds in list replicability; margin size critically affects the attainable list size; suggests further exploration of topology-inspired approaches in learning theory replicability.

title: ToolChoiceConfusion: Causal Minimal Tool Filtering for Reliable LLM Agents
summary:
Large language model agents increasingly rely on external tools, but larger tool menus can reduce reliability and efficiency by increasing wrong-tool calls, premature actions, and token cost. Existing tool-selection methods often optimize semantic relevance, exposing tools whose names or descriptions match the user request. We argue that relevance is insufficient: a tool may be related to the task while still being unnecessary or premature at the current step.
  We propose Causal Minimal Tool Filtering (CMTF), a training-free method that selects tools by causal sufficiency. CMTF uses lightweight precondition-effect contracts to expose only the minimal next-step tool frontier needed to advance from the current state toward the user goal. Across multi-step tool-use tasks, we compare CMTF with all-tools exposure, keyword retrieval, state-aware filtering, and causal-path ablations, measuring task success, wrong-tool calls, premature actions, tool exposure, and token cost. In the main benchmark with 102 tasks, 100 tools, four LLM backends, and 2448 task-method-model runs, CMTF matches the strongest causal baseline in aggregate success while reducing visible tools from 100 to one per step and reducing token usage by about 90% relative to all-tools exposure.

AI.tldr: CMTF is a training-free method that uses causal precondition–effect contracts to expose only the minimal next-step tool frontier, reducing tool exposure and token costs while maintaining high task success.
AI.motivation: Large tool menus hinder reliability and efficiency; relevance alone is insufficient because a tool can be related yet unnecessary or premature at the current step.
AI.method: Leverages lightweight precondition–effect contracts to identify the minimal set of tools needed to advance toward the user goal, ensuring causal sufficiency. Evaluated against multiple baselines across multi-step tool-use tasks.
AI.result: On 102 tasks with 100 tools, four LLM backends, and 2448 task–method–model runs, CMTF matched the strongest causal baseline in aggregate success while reducing visible tools per step from 100 to 1 and lowering token usage by ~90% compared with all-tools exposure.
