AI.conclusion: CMTF demonstrates that enforcing causal sufficiency yields reliable, efficient tool use by dramatically pruning the tool frontier without sacrificing performance.

title: RhymeFlow: Training-Free Acceleration for Video Generation with Asynchronous Denoising Flow Scheduling
summary: Video generation models based on Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have achieved remarkable performance in video synthesis, yet they suffer from high inference latency and computational costs due to the quadratic complexity of 3D attention. Existing acceleration methods primarily reduce computational complexity within each individual denoising steps through techniques such as sparse attention and KV-caching. However, they rigidly adhere to the inherent constraint of the standard diffusion pipeline: every frame in the target video sequence must be subjected to a complete, dense denoising process across all diffusion timesteps. We observe that due to the corresponding contents and motions among adjacent frames, when keyframes with critical semantic transitions are anchored, the intermediate states of others often follow more predictable trajectories, which indicates that such uniform, dense denoising process is inherently redundant for natural video data. To this end, we introduce \textbf{RhymeFlow}, a training-free framework that decouples the denoising trajectories of different frames. Specifically, we first identify a sparse set of pivotal key frames that dominate the latent semantic evolution. Then, only these keyframes undergo dense, step-by-step denoising to ensure structural integrity, while non-keyframes progressively skip denoising steps to minimize computational cost. Since skipped intermediate states of non-keyframes break the temporal coherence in keyframe denoising steps, leading to visual degradation, we further introduce a latent trajectory projection module, which enables keyframes to interact with a complete and temporally consistent sequence representation. Extensive experiments on current DiT-based video generation models demonstrate our method outperforms existing baselines with higher inference speed and better visual quality.

AI.tldr: Introduces RhymeFlow, a training-free approach to accelerate Diffusion Transformer video generation by selecting key frames for dense denoising and using a latent trajectory projection to maintain temporal coherence, achieving faster inference with improved quality over baselines.
AI.motivation: 3D attention in diffusion transformers yields high latency; existing per-step acceleration focuses on within-step sparsity and KV caching but enforces dense denoising across all diffusion timesteps for every frame, causing redundancy given natural frame-to-frame dynamics.
AI.method: Identify a sparse set of pivotal key frames; perform dense, step-by-step denoising only on key frames; non-key frames skip intermediate denoising steps to save computation; introduce a latent trajectory projection module to let key frames interact with the full sequence representation, preserving temporal coherence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on current DiT-based video generation models show RhymeFlow outperforms existing baselines in inference speed and visual quality.
AI.conclusion: A training-free strategy that decouples frame denoising trajectories via keyframe-centric processing and trajectory projection can significantly reduce computation while maintaining or improving video quality in DiT models.

title: Trust-Aware Predictive Emissions Monitoring for Gas Turbine Fleets with Limited Labelled Data
summary: Machine learning-based predictive emissions monitoring systems offer a practical alternative to direct emissions measurement, but their deployment across gas turbine fleets is challenging when emissions labels are available for only a small subset of assets. In this work, a trust-aware probabilistic framework is proposed for fleet-level gas turbine NOx prediction under limited labelled supervision. The framework combines a multi-head recurrent prediction model with learned confidence estimation, ensemble-based uncertainty quantification, auxiliary feature prediction, feature-space distance analysis, and operating-range diagnostics. These signals are calibrated on labelled data to produce interpretable per-sample trust scores, providing indicators of prediction reliability on unlabelled turbines, supporting the identification of predictions that should be treated with greater caution during fleet-level deployment. Confidence-based filtering reduces MAE from 0.202 at full coverage to 0.070 for the highest-confidence 10\% of predictions, demonstrating that confidence estimates are meaningfully related to prediction error. Unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples exhibit increased uncertainty and reduced confidence, indicating that the framework responds appropriately to distributional shift. The results show that the proposed trust framework provides actionable reliability information for emissions prediction on unlabelled turbines, supporting more transparent and trustworthy deployment of PEMS across industrial fleets.

AI.tldr: A trust-aware probabilistic framework for fleet-level NOx prediction under limited labeled data, combining multi-head recurrent prediction, confidence estimation, ensemble uncertainty, auxiliary features, and operating-range diagnostics to produce per-sample trust scores for unlabelled turbines; improves reliability and handles distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Emissions monitoring with predictive models is practical but labeling is scarce across whole gas-turbine fleets; need reliable, interpretable indicators of prediction trust to enable fleet-wide deployment.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-head recurrent prediction model with learned confidence; ensemble-based uncertainty quantification; auxiliary feature prediction; feature-space distance analysis; operating-range diagnostics; signals calibrated on labeled data to yield per-sample trust scores.
AI.result: Confidence-based filtering reduces MAE from 0.202 (full coverage) to 0.070 for top-10% highest-confidence predictions; unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples show increased uncertainty and lower confidence; framework provides actionable reliability information for unlabelled turbines.
AI.conclusion: The trust framework supports more transparent, trustworthy deployment of predictive emissions monitoring (PEMS) across industrial fleets by delivering interpretable reliability signals and robust performance under limited labels and distribution shifts.

title: TRACE: A Temporal Conditional Estimation for Multimodal Time Series Foundation Models
comment: 5 figures and 5 tables in the main paper, plus appendix
summary: Time series foundation models (TS-FMs) aim to learn generalizable temporal representations that can be adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks. In real-world multimodal settings, time series are frequently affected by temporal misalignment and partial modality missingness, where different modalities are observed at heterogeneous time scales or are partially absent. Existing approaches typically rely on naive imputation or masking strategies, which fail to account for cross-modal dependencies and often lead to misaligned or degraded representations. We propose TRACE, a conditional estimation paradigm for multimodal time series foundation model pipelines under missingness and irregular sampling, allowing incomplete target modalities to be systematically inferred from available auxiliary modalities. We evaluate TRACE on diverse multimodal benchmarks spanning healthcare and affective computing, including the MIMIC-IV clinical dataset and the CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI benchmarks for multimodal sentiment analysis. Across a range of downstream prediction tasks and missing-modality settings, TRACE consistently outperforms prior multimodal fusion approaches, demonstrating improved robustness to severe modality missingness and more reliable cross-modal representations.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a conditional estimation framework for multimodal time series that robustly handles missingness and irregular sampling, inferring missing modalities from available ones; it outperforms prior fusion methods on MIMIC-IV, CMU-MOSI, and CMU-MOSEI.
AI.motivation: Real-world multimodal time series suffer from temporal misalignment and missing modalities, and naive imputation/masking ignores cross-modal dependencies, leading to degraded representations.
AI.method: A conditional estimation paradigm (TRACE) that enables inference of incomplete target modalities from auxiliary modalities within multimodal time series foundation model pipelines, accommodating irregular sampling and missing data.
AI.result: Empirical results show TRACE beats prior multimodal fusion approaches across various downstream tasks and missingness levels on benchmarks including MIMIC-IV, CMU-MOSI, and CMU-MOSEI.
AI.conclusion: TRACE provides a robust, cross-modally aware solution for missing data in time series foundation models, improving reliability of representations and applicability to diverse domains like healthcare and sentiment analysis.

title: StoryVideoQA: Scaling Deep Video Understanding with a Large-Scale, Multi-Genre and Auto-Generated Dataset
summary: Video question answering (VideoQA) aims to answer questions about given videos. While existing approaches excel on factoid VideoQA, they struggle with deep video understanding (DVU), which requires the comprehension of complex storylines. This challenge arises from the inherent long-range video content, multi-faceted question types, and instance-level story elements, all of which constrain the scale and diversity of manually constructed DVU datasets.These difficulties constrain the scale and diversity of manually-constructed DVU dataset. To address these, we previously introduced StoryMind to automatically construct DVU datasets with balanced fine-grained topics. Though it can generate high-quality question-answer pairs (QAs) for TV series, it suffers significant performance degradation when handling longer and more complex movies. In this paper, we further design StoryMindv2, an enhanced multi-agent collaboration framework to generate high-quality DVU datasets for both TV series and movies. By integrating a novel supervisor-guided generation mechanism and a refined multi-reviewer voting strategy, the framework is utilized to construct StoryVideoQA, the largest DVU dataset to date, featuring over 363K QAs on 393.2 hours diverse story videos including TV series (avg. 1,635 seconds) and movies (avg. 7,878 seconds). Comprehensive evaluations of 20 state-of-the-art VideoQA methods on this large-scale benchmark reveal that they cannot fully maintain long-range character associations or construct a coherent understanding of complex storylines. To bridge this gap, we propose PlotTree, a novel video understanding agent, re-organizing long-range video content into a hierarchical plot structure, enabling efficient storyline reasoning on StoryVideoQA. Project page: https://github.com/nercms-mmap/StoryVideoQA/

AI.tldr: StoryMindv2 uses supervisor-guided, multi-agent generation with a voting strategy to build StoryVideoQA, the largest DVU dataset, and introduces PlotTree for hierarchical storyline reasoning.
AI.motivation: DVU datasets are scarce in scale/diversity and long-range understanding; prior StoryMind struggles with longer, complex movies; need scalable generation and better reasoning.
AI.method: Enhanced multi-agent collaboration with supervisor-guided generation and refined multi-reviewer voting to construct StoryVideoQA (363K QAs, 393.2 hours). Also introduces PlotTree video understanding agent to reorganize long-range content into hierarchical plots for storyline reasoning.
AI.result: StoryVideoQA is the largest DVU benchmark to date; evaluation of 20 state-of-the-art VideoQA methods shows limited long-range coherence; PlotTree enables efficient storyline reasoning on StoryVideoQA.
AI.conclusion: The approach advances DVU dataset creation for TV series and movies and provides a reasoning tool (PlotTree) to address long-range story comprehension; datasets and methods set a new benchmark for DVU.

title: On the training of physics-informed neural operators for solving parametric partial differential equations
summary: Physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) aim to learn solution operators for partial differential equations by using the governing physics as supervision, rather than relying solely on paired input-output simulation data. By incorporating physical constraints into the training objective, PINOs combine the cross-instance generalization of neural operators with the data efficiency of physics-informed learning. Despite this promise, how to train PINOs efficiently and robustly remains less well-understood than the training of either data-driven neural operators or physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). To bridge this gap, we examine key components of the PINO training pipeline, including architecture design, optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling strategy. We study three representative operator backbones, Deep Operator Network (DeepONet), Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), and Continuous Vision Transformer (CViT), across five diverse parametric PDE systems. Our results show that CViT provides consistently strong and stable performance across the considered benchmarks. Beyond architecture, we find that several optimization pathologies previously identified in PINN training naturally arise in PINOs, including gradient conflicts and causal violation. We also find that mitigation algorithms developed for PINNs remain effective in the PINO setting. We further compare physics-informed and data-driven training under different data regimes, revealing that a carefully designed physics-informed training pipeline can match, and in some cases, outperform purely data-driven neural operators. Taken together, these findings provide a systematic empirical understanding of the optimization challenges in PINO training and inform a practical pipeline for efficient and robust physics-informed operator learning. Code and data are available at https://github.com/NanxiiChen/PI-CViT.

AI.tldr: Systematic empirical study of physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) across three backbones (DeepONet, FNO, CViT) on five parametric PDEs; CViT is the most robust; physics-informed training can match or exceed data-driven training under limited data; optimization pathologies exist and transfer mitigation from PINNs; provides a practical, robust PINO training pipeline with code availability.
AI.motivation: Address the limited understanding of how to train PINOs efficiently and robustly, bridging the gap between data-driven neural operators and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) to enable practical, data-efficient operator learning.
AI.method: Empirical study across three operator backbones (DeepONet, FNO, CViT) and five parametric PDE systems. Analyze architecture design, optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling. Diagnose optimization pathologies such as gradient conflicts and causal violations. Apply PINN mitigation strategies to PINOs. Compare physics-informed versus data-driven training under varying data regimes. Provide a practical pipeline and release code/data.
AI.result: CViT provides consistently strong and stable performance across benchmarks. Optimization pathologies commonly seen in PINN training (gradient conflicts, causal violations) also arise in PINOs; mitigation algorithms from PINNs are effective here. Physics-informed training can match or outperform data-driven operators in certain data regimes. The work yields an empirically grounded pipeline for robust PINO training and highlights actionable guidance for practitioners.
AI.conclusion: The study offers a systematic empirical framework for PINO training, identifying CViT as a robust backbone and demonstrating the transferability of PINN mitigation techniques to PINOs. It guides practitioners toward efficient, robust physics-informed operator learning and provides code for replication.

title: Multi-ResNets for Subspace Preconditioning in Constrained Optimization
summary: We propose MResOpt, a staged residual neural network architecture for constrained optimization problems. Our architecture fits within predict-complete-correct pipelines and decomposes constraint satisfaction by priority through intermediate re-completion and stage-aware losses. The framework enables domain-informed ordered constraint satisfaction which allows the network to utilize ordinal structure when present. Under an idealized infinite-width regime, we show that our design behaves as sequential Gaussian Process regression. On synthetic QP, QCQP, and SOCP benchmarks, the staged architecture improves high-priority constraint satisfaction across convex and non-convex settings. On line-flow-constrained AC optimal power flow, we introduce a physics-motivated constraint ordering and show that MResOpt supports a learned division of labor that keeps iterates on the equality manifold, achieving substantially lower high-priority violation than reprojected baselines while remaining computationally efficient.

AI.tldr: A staged residual network, MResOpt, prioritizes constraint satisfaction via stage-aware losses; it exhibits sequential GP-like behavior in the infinite-width limit and improves high-priority constraint satisfaction on QP/QCQP/SOCP benchmarks and power-flow problems.
AI.motivation: To improve constrained optimization by exploiting a hierarchy of constraints and a predict-complete-correct (PCC) pipeline, enabling domain-informed ordered constraint satisfaction that leverages ordinal structure.
AI.method: Introduce a staged residual architecture with intermediate re-completion and stage-aware losses to enforce prioritized constraints. Analyze the infinite-width regime as sequential Gaussian Process regression. Apply to synthetic QP, QCQP, SOCP benchmarks and line-flow-constrained AC optimal power flow with physics-mmotivated constraint ordering.
AI.result: In synthetic benchmarks (QP, QCQP, SOCP), the staged architecture improves high-priority constraint satisfaction across convex and non-convex settings. In line-flow-constrained AC OPF, MResOpt enables a learned division of labor that keeps iterates on the equality manifold and substantially reduces high-priority violations versus reprojected baselines, with better computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MResOpt enables domain-informed ordered constraint satisfaction with a learned division of labor, yielding improved high-priority feasibility and computational efficiency, applicable within predict-complete-correct pipelines.

title: Comparison of Deep Learning Frameworks For Rice Disease Mapping From UAV Multispectral Imaging
comment: This paper has been accepted in IGARSS 2026. Copyright 2026 IEEE
summary: In this study, UAV multispectral imagery is used to segment the severity of bacterial leaf blight (BLB) in rice using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transformer-based models. The evaluated architectures include U-Net with a ResNet- 101 encoder, U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 and EfficientNetB7, DeepLabV3+, and SegFormer, all trained under a common pipeline with three input configurations (multispectral only, multispectral+NDVI, and multispectral+NDRE). Experiments are conducted using the publicly available BLB dataset with performance reported using mean IoU (mIoU), mean F1 (mF1), mean accuracy (mAcc), precision, and recall. U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 achieved the highest performance, with an mIoU of 97.62%. SegFormer obtained lower segmentation accuracy but comparable inference speed. Overall, the results indicate that lightweight CNN backbones remain more reliable for operational BLB monitoring while integration of vegetation indices provides small and consistent improvements. The study also highlights the value of standardised UAV datasets to compare disease mapping methods and encourages the use of CNN architectures for field implementation.

AI.tldr: U-Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 achieved the top BLB segmentation performance (mIoU ~97.6%) on UAV multispectral data; adding NDVI/NDRE indices yields small gains, while transformer-based SegFormer offers slower inference and lower accuracy; CNN backbones are preferred for field deployment and standardized UAV datasets aid method comparisons.
AI.motivation: Evaluate and compare CNN and transformer architectures for segmenting bacterial leaf blight severity in rice using UAV multispectral imagery, across different input configurations and vegetation indices.
AI.method: Weigh multiple architectures (U-Net with ResNet-101; U‑Net++ with EfficientNet-B3 and EfficientNet-B7; DeepLabV3+; SegFormer) trained under a unified pipeline with three input configurations: (1) multispectral only, (2) multispectral + NDVI, (3) multispectral + NDRE. Dataset: public BLB UAV dataset. Metrics: mean IoU, mean F1, mean accuracy, precision, recall.
AI.result: Highest mIoU achieved by U‑Net++ with EfficientNet-B3: 97.62%. SegFormer had lower segmentation accuracy but comparable inference speed. Incorporating vegetation indices (NDVI/NDRE) provided small, consistent improvements. Overall, lightweight CNN backbones are more reliable for operational BLB monitoring, and standardized UAV datasets are valuable for method comparison.
AI.conclusion: CNN-based architectures, particularly lightweight backbones, are favored for field-scale BLB monitoring with UAV imagery; adding vegetation indices yields modest benefits; standardized datasets enable fair comparisons and promote CNN-based field-ready solutions.

title: Learning to model pediatric asthma exacerbation from multiple risk factors: a case study in coastal Virginia
summary: Childhood asthma is a common illness exacerbated by air pollution as well as meteorological and neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors. Modeling asthma exacerbation (AE) in large spatiotemporal datasets requires disentangling impacts from multiple contributors. In this case study, we compared three techniques that balance predictive power with interpretability to predict AE in Hampton Roads, a coastal Virginia region comprising 7 cities and over 1.5 million people. After collating ambient air pollution measurements, weather data, and measures of neighborhood opportunity, we modeled zip code-level acute AE visits to a regional children's hospital and affiliated providers from 2018-2023. Generalized linear models (GLM) provided a baseline while neural networks (NN) served as a maximally predictive target. To bridge between statistical models and deep learning, we developed a framework based on sparse dictionary learning to identify and interpret parsimonious nonlinear interacting equations. After comparing each model's predictive performance, we estimated relative risks for AE due to input exposure variables and found consensus across frameworks. Our work links statistical and interpretable machine learning models to highlight possible synergistic interactions influencing AE, and may enable future studies to guide public health interventions in coastal Virginia.

AI.tldr: Three modeling approaches—GLM baseline, neural networks, and a sparse dictionary learning framework—are compared to predict pediatric asthma exacerbation (AE) in Hampton Roads (2018–2023) using air pollution, weather, and neighborhood opportunity data. All frameworks yield consistent relative risk estimates and reveal nonlinear interactions, linking interpretable statistics with data-driven ML to inform public health interventions.
AI.motivation: Disentangle multiple drivers of asthma exacerbation in large spatiotemporal datasets and balance predictive accuracy with interpretability to guide public health actions in a coastal urban region.
AI.method: Compare GLM (baseline), neural networks (maximally predictive), and a sparse dictionary learning framework that produces parsimonious nonlinear interacting equations. Data integrated at the zip-code level for AE visits to a regional children’s hospital and affiliated providers from 2018–2023, including ambient air pollution, weather, and neighborhood opportunity measures. Assess predictive performance and estimate relative risks for AE exposure variables; evaluate cross-model consensus on interactions.
AI.result: Neural networks achieved highest predictive power, GLMs provided baseline performance, and the sparse dictionary learning framework yielded interpretable nonlinear interactions. Relative risk estimates for AE due to input exposures showed consensus across all models, suggesting robust identification of key drivers and synergistic effects.
AI.conclusion: The study links statistical regression with interpretable ML to illuminate synergistic drivers of AE, offering a framework to guide public health interventions in coastal Virginia and potentially informing application to other regions.

title: AIS-Based Vessel Trajectory Prediction Using Memory-Augmented Neural Networks
summary: Accurate vessel trajectory prediction is essential for safe and efficient maritime operations, enabling collision avoidance and supporting route optimization. Although memory-augmented neural networks have recently shown strong performance in pedestrian and road-vehicle trajectory prediction by selectively retrieving relevant information from an external memory, their potential for vessel trajectory prediction remains underexplored. This paper presents an empirical investigation of memory-based trajectory prediction using Automatic Identification System (AIS) data. Experiments on data from the Gulf of Mexico and the New York Bight demonstrate consistent and substantial performance gains over a range of deep learning baselines that do not incorporate an external memory.

AI.tldr: Memory-augmented trajectory prediction improves vessel path forecasts using AIS data, outperforming memoryless baselines on Gulf of Mexico and NY Bight datasets.
AI.motivation: Assess whether memory-based retrieval from external memory can enhance maritime vessel trajectory prediction, addressing limitations of existing deep models that lack external memory.
AI.method: Train and evaluate memory-augmented neural networks for AIS-based vessel trajectory prediction; compare against several deep learning baselines; use datasets from the Gulf of Mexico and New York Bight; measure predictive accuracy.
AI.result: Memory-augmented models achieve consistent and substantial gains over baselines without external memory.
AI.conclusion: External memory is a beneficial inductive bias for maritime trajectory prediction, suggesting memory-based approaches can improve safety and efficiency in maritime operations; results motivate further exploration across domains and datasets.

title: Physics in 2-Steps: Locking Motion Priors Before Visual Refinement Erases Them
summary: Image-to-Video diffusion models leverage input images to generate visually stunning content, yet frequently produce motion that violates physical laws. We reveal a surprising finding: a 2-step generation often exhibits better physical consistency than a 50-step output from the same model. Through spectral analysis, we trace this to phase erosion during denoising; the phase degrades significantly (dropping by $\approx 18\%$ from step 2 to step 50), whereas the magnitude remains relatively stable. Building on this insight, we propose PhaseLock, a training-free framework that preserves the valid motion priors from few-step inference throughout the denoising trajectory. Rather than relying on full-step inference for physical consistency, PhaseLock extracts a motion prior from just 2 steps and enforces it onto high-fidelity generation via Latent Delta Guidance. Our approach effectively mitigates phase degradation, improving physical consistency by an average of 6.2 points across diverse models while largely maintaining visual fidelity, with negligible overhead ($1.06\times$ time, $1.02\times$ memory) and reduced reliance on expensive external guidance methods ($\sim5\times$ time).

AI.tldr: PhaseLock is a training-free method that preserves motion priors from early (2-step) inference in image-to-video diffusion, using Latent Delta Guidance to enforce these priors along the denoising trajectory, thereby improving physical consistency with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Image-to-video diffusion often produces physically inconsistent motion due to phase degradation during denoising; surprisingly, few-step generation can be more physically consistent than full-step outputs.
AI.method: Spectral analysis reveals phase erosion from step 2 to step 50 while magnitude remains stable. PhaseLock extracts a motion prior from just 2 steps and enforces it onto high-fidelity generation via Latent Delta Guidance, without additional training.
AI.result: PhaseLock yields an average improvement of 6.2 points in physical consistency across diverse models, while largely preserving visual fidelity. Overhead is small (≈1.06× time, ≈1.02× memory) and external guidance methods require ~5× more time.
AI.conclusion: PhaseLock provides an effective, efficient training-free approach to mitigate phase degradation in diffusion-based image-to-video generation by leveraging early-step motion priors and guiding later steps via latent deltas.

title: Learning to Route LLMs from Implicit Cost-Performance Preferences via Meta-Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) present a trade-off between performance and cost, where more powerful models incur greater expense. LLM routing aims to mitigate expenses while maintaining performance by sending queries to the most suitable model. However, existing methods cannot perform well for different user cost-performance preferences. To address this gap, we introduce a novel perceptive LLM routing paradigm for personalized and user-centric cost-performance optimization, which efficiently learns users' implicit preferences through little interaction. To handle the challenge of heterogeneous user needs, we formulate preference profiles as a set of distinct tasks in contextual bandit and propose MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework designed for preference-aware LLM routing. Experimental results show that MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks. Furthermore, it exhibits high efficiency in learning user preferences, robustness to changes in the routable LLMs, and scalability to multi-model routing.

AI.tldr: MetaRouter is a meta-learning framework for personalized LLM routing that learns user preferences efficiently via contextual-bandit tasks, enabling cost–performance optimization across multiple models with robustness to model changes.
AI.motivation: LLMs incur higher cost with better performance; current routing methods fail to capture varied, user-specific cost-performance preferences, necessitating personalized and data-efficient routing.
AI.method: Model preference profiles as tasks in a contextual bandit framework; introduce MetaRouter, a meta-learning approach that learns cross-task knowledge to quickly adapt routing decisions to new users with minimal interaction; supports heterogeneity and scalability to multiple models.
AI.result: MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks; demonstrates data-efficient learning of user preferences; robust to changes in routable LLMs; scalable to multi-model routing.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical, user-centric routing paradigm enabling efficient, personalized cost-performance optimization in dynamic LLM ecosystems; potential for deployment in real-world systems.

title: LLM Self-Recognition: Steering and Retrieving Activation Signatures
comment: To appear in Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Recent advances in interpretability suggest that large language models (LLMs) implicitly encode signals in their generated text that enable self-recognition of their outputs. We demonstrate that this capability is reliable, even in low-entropy scenarios, and that it can be amplified through targeted intervention. By steering the internal residual stream during generation with a random sparse vector, we create a detectable fingerprint that enables attribution of a given text to a specific LLM. This signal is recoverable from the activations of an LLM used as a detector, achieving over 98% accuracy across multiple detection settings while preserving the quality of generated text. As AI-generated content proliferates, this approach offers a practical alternative to traditional detectors by leveraging the model's natural representation structure for attribution rather than embedding a signal externally. Our contributions include: (i) establishing reliable self-recognition capabilities in LLMs, (ii) a simple steering mechanism enabling multi-LLM identification with no quality degradation, (iii) demonstrating that activation spaces contain exploitable structure for encoding signals without semantic interference.

AI.tldr: New method fingerprints LLM outputs by steering the residual stream with a sparse random vector, enabling high-accuracy attribution (>98%) to a specific model without hurting output quality; claims self-recognition and activation-space leakage as a practical attribution signal.
AI.motivation: As AI-generated content becomes widespread, need robust, model-internal attribution signals that do not depend on externally embedded watermarks, leveraging the model's own representations for attribution.
AI.method: During text generation, steer the internal residual stream using a random sparse vector to imprint a fingerprint; use a detector model to recover the fingerprint from activations; evaluate detection accuracy across settings while maintaining generation quality.
AI.result: Achieves over 98% attribution accuracy across multiple detection settings; preserves text quality; works even in low-entropy contexts; scalable to multi-LLM identification with a simple mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Activation spaces of LLMs contain exploitable structure for encoding signals without semantic interference; provides a practical, internal-attribution approach that complements or substitutes external detectors.

title: GMBFormer: An NDVI-Guided Global Memory Bank Transformer for Urban Green-Space Extraction from Ultra-High-Resolution Imagery
summary: Urban green-space extraction from ultra-high-resolution (UHR) imagery is commonly performed patch by patch, which limits semantic reuse among spatially separated but visually similar vegetation patterns. Directly injecting the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) into red-green-blue (RGB) backbones can also blur the roles of visual appearance learning and physical vegetation confidence. We propose GMBFormer, a SegFormer-based framework that replaces adjacency-driven feature propagation with selective, similarity-driven prototype retrieval. Only RGB channels enter the backbone and decoder, while NDVI is decoupled as a physics-informed gate that admits high-confidence vegetation descriptors into a compact global memory bank through momentum updates. During training and inference, the current patch queries stored prototypes through memory-mediated cross-attention, and the retrieved response is integrated with bounded overhead. Experiments use a self-constructed Chengdu UHR dataset with 7,700 labeled 512 x 512 patches and two reduced-label settings derived from the public International Society for Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing (ISPRS) Potsdam dataset. Under the same training and evaluation protocol, GMBFormer obtains mean intersection over union (mIoU)/mean Dice (mDice) scores of 89.25%/94.31%, 92.17%/95.92%, and 83.72%/90.86%, respectively, improving the controlled SegFormer-B4 baseline in each setting. Ablation studies indicate that decoupled NDVI admission, memory retrieval, capacity, and momentum jointly shape the final performance.

AI.tldr: GMBFormer reframes urban green-space segmentation by replacing adjacency-driven feature propagation with memory-based, similarity-driven prototype retrieval, decoupling NDVI as a physics-informed gate in a SegFormer backbone, to achieve state-of-the-art mIoU/mDice on UHR urban imagery.
AI.motivation: Patch-by-patch segmentation restricts semantic reuse across similar vegetation patterns and injecting NDVI into RGB features can blur appearance cues and vegetation confidence. A memory-augmented, physics-informed mechanism can improve global consistency and robust vegetation sensing.
AI.method: Introduce GMBFormer, SegFormer-based framework that uses selective similarity-driven prototype retrieval instead of adjacency propagation. RGB features pass through the backbone; NDVI is decoupled as a gate that updates a global memory bank via momentum. During training/inference, patches query stored prototypes through memory-mediated cross-attention, integrating retrieved responses with bounded overhead.
AI.result: On a self-constructed Chengdu UHR dataset (7,700 patches of 512x512) and two reduced-label settings from ISPRS Potsdam, GMBFormer achieves: mIoU/mDice of 89.25%/94.31%, 92.17%/95.92%, and 83.72%/90.86% respectively, outperforming the controlled SegFormer-B4 baseline in all settings. Ablations show contributions from decoupled NDVI, memory retrieval/capacity, and momentum.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled NDVI admission, memory-based retrieval, capacity tuning, and momentum collectively drive the gains, enabling effective semantic reuse and more reliable vegetation extraction from high-resolution imagery.

title: A Machine Learning-Based Framework for Discovering Huntington's Disease Stages: Integrating Graph Representation Learning and clustering to Uncover Progression Dynamics in Longitudinal Enroll-HD Dataset
comment: Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 10th International Conference on Medical and Health Informatics (ICMHI 2026), Association for Computing Machinery (ACM)
summary: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive brain disorder that gradually affects movement, cognitive function, and behavior. Identifying the stage of the disease accurately and consistently is important for understanding its course, grouping patients, personalized care, and discovering treatment. Existing clinical staging frameworks rely primarily on predefined clinical measurement thresholds and clinical expert decisions, yet these discrete cut-offs may obscure meaningful intra-stage variability and remain vulnerable to inter-rater differences, especially in motor and functional assessments. To address these limitations, we developed an unsupervised machine learning framework based on dynamic graph representation learning to capture temporal relationships within and across patients from longitudinal clinical measurements. Using the learned representations, we applied K-means++ clustering to identify well-separated groups. We then iteratively increased the number of clusters (k), using stability analysis to assess robustness and reveal additional meaningful clusters beyond the initial optimal solution. We applied the framework to 302 individuals from the Enroll-HD cohort (1,477 visits, 44 clinical variables per visit; 80% manifest participants), enabling data-driven discovery of HD stages reflecting natural clinical progression. Despite the limited cohort size, the proposed framework achieved robust clustering performance using a four-dimensional latent space, identifying four meaningful and statistically distinct disease stages through clustering stability analysis. Each stage corresponded to well-defined clinical measurement boundaries, with minimal overlap compared to previously established clinical staging methods.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised dynamic graph learning identifies four data-driven Huntington's disease (HD) stages from longitudinal clinical data, yielding well-separated, boundary-defined groups that outperform traditional staging thresholds.
AI.motivation: HD staging relies on predefined clinical thresholds and expert judgments, which can obscure intra-stage variability and suffer from inter-rater differences; a data-driven, temporally-aware approach may reveal more natural progression stages.
AI.method: Dynamic graph representation learning on longitudinal clinical measurements to capture temporal relationships within and across patients. Learned latent representations are clustered with K-means++, followed by stability analysis across increasing k to identify robust, meaningful clusters. Data from 302 Enroll-HD participants (1,477 visits, 44 variables/visit) were used, yielding a 4-dimensional latent space and four statistically distinct stages.
AI.result: The framework produced four meaningful HD stages with robust clustering in the latent space. Each stage aligned with well-defined clinical measurement boundaries, showing minimal overlap relative to existing clinical staging methods, despite the modest cohort size.
AI.conclusion: A data-driven, temporally aware staging framework can reveal natural HD progression stages with clearer boundaries and reduced reliance on fixed thresholds, supporting improved patient stratification and longitudinal understanding. External validation in larger cohorts would bolster generalizability.

title: DragOn: A Benchmark and Dataset for Drag-Based GUI Interactions
summary: GUI agents - vision-based models that control desktops, web browsers, and mobile devices through graphical user interfaces - promise to automate a wide range of digital tasks. While million-scale datasets have enabled substantial progress on click-grounding, drag grounding (e.g. drag-and-drop, swipe, highlight) data remains an order of magnitude smaller and current models fall short on complex drag-based interactions. We introduce DragOn, a drag grounding benchmark and training dataset covering four domains: text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing and slider manipulation. The dataset comprises 286K training screenshots and 3.5M training tasks, plus a 2000-example held-out evaluation suite. We evaluate proprietary (GPT, Claude) and open-weight (Qwen, Kimi, Holo) models, as well as a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on our training data. Results suggest that our dataset could improve performance of state-of-the-art models on downstream computer-use tasks.

AI.tldr: DragOn introduces a large-scale drag grounding dataset and benchmark for GUI tasks, spanning four interaction domains with 286k training screenshots, 3.5M training tasks, and a 2k-example held-out evaluation set, evaluated across proprietary and open-weight models, showing potential improvements on downstream GUI-use tasks.
AI.motivation: Click-grounding data is abundant, but drag-based interactions (drag-and-drop, swipe, highlight) are far less represented. There is a gap for training and evaluating models on complex drag-based GUI actions.
AI.method: Constructed a drag grounding dataset across four domains (text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing, slider manipulation). Collected 286k training screenshots with 3.5M training tasks and a 2k-example held-out evaluation suite. Evaluated both proprietary (GPT, Claude) and open-weight (Qwen, Kimi, Holo) models, including a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on the training data.
AI.result: Indications that the dataset could improve performance of state-of-the-art models on downstream computer-use tasks; the abstract does not provide numerical metrics.
AI.conclusion: DragOn provides a new benchmark and dataset to advance drag-grounding research, addressing data scarcity in drag-based GUI interactions and enabling better training and evaluation of vision-language models for GUI manipulation.

title: Visual Commonsense Driven Knowledge Refinements for Scene Graph Generation
summary: Learning-driven Scene Graph Generation (SGG) models excel on frequent relation types but degrade sharply under annotation sparsity, failing to capture reliable visual commonsense knowledge. We propose a model-agnostic, semantically-guided knowledge refinement framework that systematically mines commonsense-grounded constraints from training data - capturing spatial, functional, and qualitative relational regularities - and uses general declarative commonsense reasoning to correct and refine ranked SGG predictions at inference time. The framework requires no manual rule authoring, no model retraining, and transfers across datasets and architectures. On three standard benchmarks, we obtain consistent improvements over strong baselines, demonstrating that structured visual commonsense reasoning over deep scene semantics is a practical and effective complement to purely learning-based scene graph generation.

AI.tldr: Model-agnostic, semantically-guided knowledge refinement for scene graph generation; mines commonsense-grounded constraints (spatial, functional, qualitative) from data and uses declarative reasoning at inference to refine predictions; no retraining; improves on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address annotation sparsity and limited visual commonsense in learning-based SGG; enable structured, commonsense reasoning to complement deep visual semantics without retraining.
AI.method: Mine commonsense constraints from training data ensuring spatial/functional/qualitative regularities; apply general declarative reasoning at inference to correct/refine SGG predictions; model-agnostic and rule-mining-based, no manual rule authoring or retraining; transfers across datasets and architectures.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over strong baselines on three standard benchmarks across datasets/architectures.
AI.conclusion: Structured visual commonsense reasoning is a practical, effective complement to purely learning-based SGG, enabling better handling of annotation sparsity without additional training.

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization for Event Data
summary: Continuous-time event data, in which entities emit instantaneous events over time, arises naturally across many domains such as neuroscience, seismology, and social networks. Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is a natural tool to uncover interpretable structure in such data, but it has so far only been applied after binning or smoothing the entity-level counting measures. This preprocessing step comes with the risk of erasing entity-level heterogeneities and fine-grained temporal features. In this paper, we introduce EventNMF, a continuous-time non-negative factorization model that operates directly on event times: each entity's events are modeled as a Poisson process whose intensity factorizes through a non-negative B-spline basis, and a simple estimation procedure recovers interpretable temporal templates shared across entities. The resulting method is mathematically principled, easy to implement, and computationally efficient. We further show that standard binned-count approaches arise as the special case of degree-zero splines, explore bias-variance tradeoffs and compare against existing methods on a synthetic latent factor model, and demonstrate the effectiveness of EventNMF on several real-world applications.

AI.tldr: EventNMF introduces a continuous-time non-negative factorization for event times by modeling each entity's events as a Poisson process with intensity that factorizes through a non-negative B-spline basis. This avoids binning, yields interpretable temporal templates shared across entities, and is computationally efficient.
AI.motivation: To analyze continuous-time event data without discretizing into counts, avoiding loss of temporal heterogeneity and fine-grained patterns that binning/smoothing imposes. Extend NMF to the continuous-time domain with principled probabilistic modeling.
AI.method: Model each entity's event times as a Poisson process with intensity λ_e(t) that is non-negatively factorized via a B-spline basis: λ_e(t) = sum_r a_{er} B_r(t), with a_{er} ≥ 0. The B-spline basis imposes smooth, non-negative temporal templates shared across entities. Estimation uses a simple, likely nonnegative constrained optimization (MLE-like) to recover the templates, enabling interpretable factors and efficient computation.
AI.result: Demonstrates that standard binned-count methods emerge as the degenerate case with degree-zero splines. analyzes bias-variance tradeoffs inherent to spline-based intensity modeling. shows competitive performance against existing methods on synthetic latent-factor data and real-world applications, validating interpretability and efficiency of EventNMF.
AI.conclusion: EventNMF provides a principled, scalable continuous-time factorization for event data that preserves temporal granularity and entity heterogeneity. It subsumes bin-count approaches as a special case and offers a practical tool for discovering shared temporal templates across entities.

title: TokenMizer: Graph-Structured Session Memory for Long-Horizon LLM Context Management
comment: 12 pages, 10 figures. Code and benchmark available at https://github.com/Shweta-Mishra-ai/tokenmizer
summary: Large language model (LLM) deployments for long-horizon tasks face a fundamental constraint: context windows are finite while productive work sessions are not. When history exceeds the Maximum Effective Context Window (MECW), critical structured information - architectural decisions, task transitions, file histories - is silently discarded. Existing mitigations treat history as flat text, destroying the relational structure that makes sessions resumable. We present TokenMizer, an open-source proxy system that models LLM session history as a typed knowledge graph. The schema defines 14 node types and 7 edge types. A hybrid extraction pipeline populates the graph incrementally, while a three-tier checkpoint system serializes it into compact resume blocks. An 8-layer compression pipeline reduces context overhead, and a semantic cache reduces repeated-query latency. Evaluated on a controlled benchmark of 21 sessions spanning 5 domains, TokenMizer demonstrates significant token economy. It produces resume blocks averaging 78 tokens (range: 42-124) - 2x smaller than evaluated baselines (159-170 tokens) - while achieving higher decision recall (+9-17 percentage points). Crucially, baselines only preserve that a technology was mentioned; TokenMizer preserves the rationale. Across all sessions, TokenMizer achieves mean task recall 51.0%, decision recall 46.6%, and file recall 58.7%. Variance reflects domain heterogeneity: explicit imperative phrasing (software engineering) scores higher than implicit reasoning (research). Ablation studies show fuzzy label matching is the dominant improvement factor (+33 pp task recall). The heuristic compression achieves 47.3% token reduction with zero external dependencies. TokenMizer provides a queryable alternative to text-retention baselines at half the token cost.

AI.tldr: TokenMizer is a proxy system that treats LLM session history as a typed knowledge graph to reduce context load while preserving rationale, enabling resumable long-horizon tasks with improved recall and lower token costs.
AI.motivation: Finite context windows hinder long-horizon task productivity. Preserving structured history and rationale, not just flat text, enables resumable sessions and faster responses.
AI.method: Build a typed knowledge graph with 14 node types and 7 edge types to capture session history; incremental graph population via a hybrid extraction pipeline; three-tier checkpointing to serialize resume blocks; 8-layer compression; semantic cache; evaluated on 21 sessions across 5 domains.
AI.result: Resume blocks average 78 tokens (42-124), about 2x smaller than baselines (159-170). Higher decision recall (+9–17 pp). Across sessions, mean task recall 51.0%, decision recall 46.6%, file recall 58.7%. Ablation shows fuzzy label matching as dominant improvement factor (+33 pp task recall). TokenMizer achieves token economy with no external dependencies and preserves rationale.
AI.conclusion: TokenMizer provides a queryable alternative to text-retention baselines at about half the token cost, preserving rationale and improving recall; performance varies with domain complexity.

title: EasyLens: A Training-Free Plug-and-Play Subtle-Lesion Representation Amplifier for Medical Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) have shown increasing potential for clinical image interpretation, including lesion detection and report generation. However, their practical utility remains limited by insufficient sensitivity to subtle lesions, whose visual evidence is often sparse, low-contrast, and embedded within complex anatomical context. As local visual tokens are aggregated, these weak lesion cues can become underrepresented in global image representations, making them difficult for medical VLMs to recognize. Existing efforts to improve lesion sensitivity mainly rely on medical-domain vision-encoder pre-training, clinical-term-guided alignment, or trainable pathological representation enhancement. Although effective, these approaches usually require additional training or model-specific adaptation and may overfit to particular disease morphologies, limiting their applicability to frozen medical VLMs. To address these limitations, we propose EasyLens, a training-free plug-and-play subtle-lesion representation amplifier for medical VLMs. EasyLens first constructs EasyBank, a pathology-anatomy prototype space that provides lesion-related prototypes and anatomy-aware normal references for comparing suspicious patches against both pathological and normal anatomical patterns. To avoid blindly amplifying normal tissues, EasyTag selects lesion-relevant patches through counterfactual prototype reasoning. To counteract the dilution of subtle lesion cues in global image representations, EasyAmplifier strengthens the selected lesion-relevant patch representations through morphology-guided residual enhancement, thereby increasing their contribution to the global image embedding. Experiments on multiple medical image datasets and frozen medical VLM backbones show that EasyLens improves subtle-lesion detection and outperforms existing encoder-enhancement baselines.

AI.tldr: EasyLens is a training-free plug-and-play method that boosts subtle-lesion representation in medical VLMs by building a pathology-anatomy prototype space (EasyBank), selecting lesion-relevant patches via counterfactual reasoning (EasyTag), and enhancing their representations through morphology-guided residual amplification (EasyAmplifier), leading to improved subtle-lesion detection without retraining.
AI.motivation: Medical vision-language models struggle to detect subtle, low-contrast lesions because weak cues can be diluted in global image embeddings, and existing improvements often require training or model-specific adaptation. A training-free approach is desired to generalize across frozen backbones and disease morphologies.
AI.method: Construct EasyBank containing pathology-anatomy prototypes and normal references; use EasyTag to select lesion-relevant patches with counterfactual prototype reasoning; apply EasyAmplifier with morphology-guided residual enhancement to strengthen lesion patches’ representations and boost their contribution to the global image embedding. Implemented as a plug-in for frozen medical VLMs.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on multiple medical image datasets with frozen VLM backbones shows improved detection of subtle lesions and superior performance compared with existing encoder-enhancement baselines.
AI.conclusion: EasyLens provides a training-free, plug-and-play pathway to amplify subtle-lesion representations, enabling better lesion sensitivity for medical VLMs without additional training or architectural changes.

title: Design a Reliable LLM-Integrated Interface for Mortality Forecasting
summary: Mortality forecasting plays an important role in actuarial and policy decision-making, but its implementation remains technically complex and inaccessible to non-expert users. This project proposes a reliable large language model (LLM)-integrated interface that improves usability while maintaining statistical power. The LLM is designed as a constrained orchestration layer that translates natural-language inputs into structured configurations for a deterministic forecasting pipeline. A three-phase methodology is employed to ensure accuracy, usability, and transparency. First, a baseline pipeline is implemented using the CoMoMo package, reproducing established mortality forecasting results. Second, the pipeline is extended to generate multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and mean squared error (MSE). Third, a prototype interface uses a local LLM to handle users' forecasting requests in plain language. The system demonstrates that LLMs can enhance accessibility without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes analytical workflows.

AI.tldr: An LLM serves as a constrained orchestration layer to translate natural-language requests into a deterministic CoMoMo mortality-forecasting pipeline, enabling accessible, reproducible multi-step forecasts evaluated via rolling-origin and MSE, with a local LLM prototype interface.
AI.motivation: Address the technical barrier in mortality forecasting for non-experts while preserving statistical power, reproducibility, and actuarial validity in high-stakes decisions.
AI.method: Three-phase: 1) implement baseline pipeline with CoMoMo to reproduce established results; 2) extend to multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and MSE as metrics; 3) prototype a local LLM interface to parse plain-language requests into structured configurations, acting as a constrained orchestration layer.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates that LLMs can improve usability without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in mortality-forecasting workflows.
AI.conclusion: A constrained LLM orchestration layer is a viable, practical path to making sophisticated statistical forecasting more accessible while maintaining methodological rigor.

title: Boosting Brain-to-Image Decoding with TRIBE v2 Data Augmentation
summary: Brain decoding is limited by the availability of labeled neural data, and remains challenging in low-data regimes. To address this issue, we investigate whether and when brain decoding can be boosted by augmenting small fMRI datasets with synthetic data generated by a pretrained model of fMRI responses to stimuli. We use TRIBE v2, a large encoding model pretrained on more than 1000 hours of fMRI responses to video, audio and language. For each dataset, we evaluate systematic grids that show how the performance of image decoders varies with the amount of synthetic data used for training. Our results, based on two datasets (the 7T fMRI Natural Scenes Dataset and 3T fMRI BOLD5000), show up to 68% improvement in Top-10 image-retrieval accuracy compared to decoders trained only on real data. Importantly, the proportion of augmented data required to reach a given image decoding performance needs to be adjusted depending on the data source. Surprisingly, image decoders trained exclusively on synthetic fMRI can perform above chance in some settings, suggesting that TRIBE v2 can support zero-shot brain-to-image decoding. Together, these results show how large-scale models of the fMRI responses to sight, sound and language may provide a foundation to improve the data efficiency for image decoding.

AI.tldr: TRIBE v2, a large pretrained fMRI-response model, is used to augment small fMRI datasets with synthetic data to boost image decoding, achieving up to 68% gain in Top-10 accuracy and enabling possible zero-shot decoding; effectiveness depends on dataset and synthetic data proportion.
AI.motivation: Overcome labeled neural data scarcity in brain decoding by leveraging large-scale pretrained models of fMRI responses to improve data efficiency in image decoding.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation with TRIBE v2-generated synthetic fMRI data. For each dataset (7T Natural Scenes, 3T BOLD5000), train image decoders with varying amounts of synthetic data and measure Top-10 image-retrieval performance; compare to decoders trained on real data only; also assess zero-shot potential with synthetic-only training.
AI.result: Up to 68% improvement in Top-10 image-retrieval accuracy over real-data-only decoders. The optimal synthetic-to-real data ratio depends on data source. In some settings, decoders trained solely on synthetic fMRI perform above chance, indicating potential for zero-shot brain-to-image decoding.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale fMRI response models like TRIBE v2 can substantially improve data efficiency for brain-to-image decoding and may support zero-shot decoding, though the optimal augmentation strategy is dataset-dependent.

title: HomeWorld: A Unified Floorplan-to-Furnished Framework for Generating Controllable, Densely Interactive Whole-Home Scenes
summary: Indoor scene generation is crucial for robot simulation and modern interior design. However, complex layouts together with scarce 3D scene data make learning-based generation challenging. Existing methods often rely on hand-crafted rules or focus on isolated sub-tasks (e.g., floorplan synthesis or single-room furnishing), producing whole-home scenes that lack global coherence, realism, and simulation readiness. To mitigate these limitations, we propose a unified hierarchical framework that decomposes indoor scene synthesis into controllable stages. First, we curate a large-scale dataset of 300K real residential floorplans to train a large language model for whole-home floorplan generation. With detailed descriptions and a K-D tree-based representation, our method enables fine-grained, controllable whole-home floorplan generation. Building upon the generated whole-home floorplan, we leverage image generation models to draft furniture layouts from multi-level roaming viewpoints, and then generate the layouts of small manipulable objects on different supporting surfaces (e.g., cabinets, desks, and dining tables) for embodied AI simulation. During furniture and object layout generation, a VLM-based refiner iteratively corrects furniture and object placement, and a 3D generative model enables flexible replacement of individual assets. We further attach basic physical attributes and simple surface texture and lighting setups to complete the pipeline for embodied AI use. Experiments and user studies demonstrate that our pipeline produces indoor spaces with greater layout diversity and stronger 3D design appeal, outperforming prior methods on both quantitative and qualitative metrics. Finally, alongside our generation pipeline, we will release the floorplan dataset and 5K fully furnished scenes to the community. Project Page: https://kairos-homeworld.github.io/

AI.tldr: Unified hierarchical pipeline for whole-home indoor scene synthesis, from floorplans to furniture and object layouts, using an LLM, K-D tree floorplan representation, image generation, VLM refinement, and 3D asset replacement; backed by a 300k-floorplan dataset and 5k fully furnished scenes.
AI.motivation: tackle lack of global coherence, realism, and simulation readiness in existing methods by creating an end-to-end, controllable generation pipeline for whole-home indoor scenes.
AI.method: Stage 1: Train a large language model on 300K real residential floorplans to generate whole-home floorplans, using a K-D tree based representation for fine-grained control. Stage 2: From the generated floorplan, use image generation models to draft furniture layouts from multi-level roaming viewpoints. Stage 3: Generate layouts of small manipulable objects on different supporting surfaces (e.g., on cabinets, desks, dining tables). Stage 4: A VLM-based refiner iteratively corrects furniture and object placement; a 3D generative model enables flexible replacement of individual assets. Stage 5: Attach basic physical attributes and simple surface texture and lighting to complete the pipeline for embodied AI. Dataset and 5K furnished scenes provided; project page linked.
AI.result: The pipeline reportedly yields greater layout diversity and stronger 3D design appeal, with improvements over prior methods on quantitative and qualitative metrics and positive user studies.
AI.conclusion: A cohesive end-to-end framework for generating realistic, diverse, and simulation-ready entire homes, with public release of datasets to foster further research.

title: Tracing the Oracle: Improving Diffusion Timestep Scheduling for 3D CT Reconstruction
summary: Pretrained diffusion models demonstrate impressive potential in solving highly ill-posed 3D computed tomography (CT) inverse problems, while the inference process suffers from significant computational overhead. Furthermore, existing uniform timestep schedules fail to capture the non-uniform evolution of the reverse conditional diffusion stochastic differential equation, thereby introducing substantial truncation errors. To overcome this limitation, we propose Tracing the Oracle (TrO), a plug-and-play framework for improved timestep scheduling. Specifically, we treat densely sampled numerical integration trajectories on a few samples as the reference oracle. The optimized schedule is extracted by leveraging dynamic programming to globally minimize the cumulative error between the few-step approximation and the oracle. This mechanism precisely allocates the limited sampling steps to critical evolution stages that are highly susceptible to truncation errors. Our extensive experiments on the AAPM dataset across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks demonstrate that, when combined with the state-of-the-art 3D CT reconstruction method DDS, our optimized timesteps significantly improve reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency compared to existing heuristic schedules, especially under a strict budget of no more than 10 sampling steps.

AI.tldr: Oracle-guided nonuniform timestep scheduling (TrO) improves efficiency and fidelity for diffusion-based 3D CT reconstruction by minimizing truncation error against a dense oracle via dynamic programming, under few-step budgets (≤10 steps).
AI.motivation: Reduce computational overhead and truncation errors in diffusion-model-based 3D CT inverse problems with uniform timesteps; provide a plug-and-play, nonuniform scheduler that allocates steps to critical evolution stages.
AI.method: Treat densely sampled numerical trajectories on a subset as a reference oracle; use dynamic programming to derive an optimized timestepping schedule that minimizes the cumulative error between the sparse-step approximation and the oracle; apply with DDS (state-of-the-art 3D CT reconstruction) on the AAPM dataset across multiple tasks.
AI.result: Significant improvements in reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency compared with heuristic schedules when using ≤10 sampling steps; effective across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks and when combined with DDS.
AI.conclusion: TrO offers a principled, plug-and-play nonuniform timestepping framework for diffusion-based ill-posed 3D CT inverse problems, outperforming heuristic schedules under tight step budgets and likely generalizable to related problems.

title: Where Should Knowledge Enter? A Layered Framework for Knowledge Infusion in Multimodal Iterative Generative Mo
summary: Multimodal generative models produce fluent outputs but remain unreliable when generation must respect structured, domain-specific, or safety-critical knowledge. Existing methods incorporate knowledge through mechanisms such as prompt augmentation, guidance, latent editing, or fine-tuning, yet they are typically categorized by technique rather than by the component of the generative process they modify. We argue that knowledge infusion in iterative generative models is fundamentally anintervention-layer problem. Since thegenerative process unfolds as a trajectory of internal states, knowledge can act on four structurally distinct components of this process: the input/output boundary, the transition function, the intermediate state, and the model parameters. This maps to four intervention layers: surface, trajectory, latent, and parametric infusion. We instantiate the framework in diffusion models, map representative methods to all four layers, and derive design principles for multi-layer composition. In a controlled safety-alignment experiment using a multimodal knowledge graph with two diffusion backbones, we implement three of the four layers cumulatively, surface (input-side and output-side) and trajectory--latent (mid-generation). We show empirically that each additional layer addresses failure classes that prior layers cannot reach, reducing knowledge-violating outputs by 70.97% compared to vanilla generation and empirically confirming the framework's complementarity prediction.

AI.tldr: A unified intervention-layer framework for knowledge infusion in diffusion-based multimodal generators, classifying interventions into surface, trajectory, latent, and parametric layers; multi-layer infusion reduces knowledge-violating outputs by ~71% vs vanilla generation.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches are organized by technique rather than by the components of the generative process they modify. The paper posits an intervention-layer taxonomy to improve reliability, safety, and domain-specific fidelity in iterative generative models.
AI.method: Proposes a framework mapping knowledge-infusing methods to four layers (surface input/output boundary, trajectory transition function, latent intermediate state, parametric model parameters). Instantiates the framework in diffusion models, maps representative methods to none or all layers, and derives principles for multi-layer composition. In a controlled safety-alignment experiment with a multimodal knowledge graph and two diffusion backbones, implements three of the four layers cumulatively (surface and trajectory–latent).
AI.result: Demonstrates that each additional layer addresses failure classes beyond the reach of prior layers; the multi-layer setup reduces knowledge-violating outputs by 70.97% compared with vanilla generation, empirically confirming the framework’s complementarity.
AI.conclusion: The intervention-layer framework provides design principles for composing multiple knowledge infusion layers; multi-layer infusion yields complementary strengths and improves safety/alignment in multimodal diffusion generation.

title: A Vision-language Framework for Comparative Reasoning in Radiology
summary: Medical imaging artificial intelligence has achieved strong performance in isolated image interpretation, but remains poorly aligned with radiological practice, where diagnosis and follow-up rely on comparison across prior studies and analogous reference cases. Here we formulate radiological comparison as an entity-aware cross-image reasoning problem and introduce a framework that supports both reference-case retrieval and temporal comparative interpretation. We construct MedReCo-DB, a large-scale comparative imaging resource derived from routine image-report pairs, comprising more than 690,000 images from over 160,000 patients across eight institutions, four countries and seven imaging modalities. Reports are decomposed into anatomical structures, abnormal findings and pathological conditions to provide supervision for entity-conditioned retrieval and comparative visual question answering. Using this resource, we develop MedReCo, an entity-aware visual encoder for controllable retrieval of clinically analogous cases, and MedReCo-VLM, a vision--language extension for generative interpretation of interval change. Across internal, external and cross-center evaluations, MedReCo achieved the highest Recall@1 in all 12 internal retrieval settings and improved external retrieval by a mean of 6.0 percentage points. In clinically confusable differential groups, it consistently outperformed the strongest baselines. MedReCo-VLM achieved the best performance across all comparative generation evaluations and improved longitudinal follow-up accuracy by 14.5-46.5 percentage points on chest radiographs and 13.0-27.9 percentage points on CT. These findings suggest that entity-aware comparative reasoning can be learned from routine clinical data at scale and may provide a more clinically aligned foundation for medical imaging AI.

AI.tldr: MedReCo delivers entity-aware cross-image reasoning for radiology, enabling reference-case retrieval and temporal comparison via a large-scale MedReCo-DB dataset; shows strong retrieval and generative interpretation performance and improved longitudinal follow-up.
AI.motivation: Radiology relies on comparing current studies with prior/reference cases; standard image interpretation AI lacks explicit cross-image reasoning and entity-level supervision. The work proposes an entity-aware framework to align AI with radiological practice through comparative imaging tasks.
AI.method: Build MedReCo-DB from routine image-report pairs (≈690k images, >160k patients, 8 institutions, 4 countries, 7 modalities). Decompose reports into anatomical structures, abnormal findings, and pathologies to supervise entity-conditioned retrieval and comparative visual QA. Develop MedReCo (entity-aware visual encoder) for controllable retrieval of clinically analogous cases and MedReCo-VLM (vision-language model) for generative interpretation of interval change. Evaluate across internal, external, and cross-center settings.
AI.result: MedReCo achieved the highest Recall@1 in 12 internal retrieval settings; external retrieval improved by ≈6.0 percentage points. In confusable differential groups, it outperformed baselines. MedReCo-VLM delivered best performance across comparative generation tasks and improved longitudinal follow-up accuracy by 14.5–46.5 percentage points on chest radiographs and 13.0–27.9 percentage points on CT.
AI.conclusion: Entity-aware comparative reasoning learned from routine clinical data scales and provides a clinically aligned foundation for medical imaging AI, with strong potential to improve retrieval and descriptive/generative interpretation of interval changes.

title: Generative Criticality in Large Language Model Temperature Scaling
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, contributed to PAI 2026 Conference
summary: We propose a statistical-field framework for text generated by large language models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a one-dimensional chain. Defining a susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field, we vary the \texttt{softmax} temperature $T$ and observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic $T_c$ with power-law-like scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and a collapse onto a single semantic direction below $T_c$. The intrinsic dimension estimated by the two nearest neighbor (TwoNN) method independently corroborates these findings, reaching a minimum near $T_c$. Results are robust across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B--32B) and prompt categories. While the phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation. Our framework provides quantitative tools for probing the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.

AI.tldr: Statistical-field framing of LLM outputs treated as a 1D continuous-spin system; reveals a susceptibility peak at Tc, order-parameter change, and dimension collapse; TwoNN corroborates Tc; robust across scales/prompts; notes non-equilibrium aspects.
AI.motivation: Quantify collective structure in generated text and connect decoding dynamics to critical phenomena; provide quantitative, model-scale-robust tools for probing LLM outputs.
AI.method: Model token embeddings as continuous spins on a 1D chain; define susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator; define an order parameter as the ensemble-averaged embedding field; vary the softmax temperature T; analyze a sharp susceptibility peak with scaling and a rapid order-parameter change; use TwoNN to estimate intrinsic dimension and detect a minimum near Tc; test across model scales (Qwen3 0.6B–32B) and prompt categories.
AI.result: Observed a sharp susceptibility peak near Tc with power-law-like scaling; concurrent rapid change in the order parameter; embedding vectors collapse onto a single semantic direction below Tc; intrinsic dimension from TwoNN reaches a minimum near Tc; results robust across model scales and prompt categories.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides quantitative tools to probe the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena; non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation.

title: An Infectious Disease Spread Simulation Based on Large Language Model Decision Making
summary: Modelling individual decision-making during infectious disease outbreaks is crucial for understanding behavioural dynamics and informing effective public health interventions. Prior work has shown that large language models can simulate realistic human behaviour by generating agent decisions based on demographic prompts and situational context. We build on this foundation with a spatially grounded, agent-based simulation framework that integrates LLM-generated decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness into a census-based synthetic population of agents. Location is treated as a central feature: agents are assigned to spatial units within cities, capturing the spatial distributions of different demographic groups using real-world census data and enabling geographically diverse behavioural modelling. We implement and compare three decision scenarios, independent reasoning, household influence, and message framing, and simulate self-reporting outcomes in San Francisco and Atlanta. Results reveal that income and education are the dominant drivers of reporting rate variation, with smaller but consistent effects from geography, LLM model choice, and message framing. Our framework generates synthetic data that captures both social and geographic heterogeneity, supporting spatial epidemiological modelling and bias-aware behavioural analysis.

AI.tldr: A spatially grounded agent-based framework using LLM-informed decisions to simulate self-reported influenza-like illness in a census-based population, revealing income and education as primary drivers of reporting rates and enabling bias-aware, geographically nuanced epidemiological analyses.
AI.motivation: Understand how behavioural responses influence disease surveillance and surveillance bias by integrating LLM-driven decision making within a spatially explicit population for nuanced, geography-aware simulations.
AI.method: Developed a synthetic population for San Francisco and Atlanta with agents assigned to spatial units. Implemented three decision scenarios (independent reasoning, household influence, message framing). LLMs generate decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness (ILI); outcomes are simulated and compared across LLM models and geographies.
AI.result: Income and education dominate reporting rate variation; geography, LLM model choice, and message framing have smaller but consistent effects. The framework captures social and geographic heterogeneity in reporting behavior.
