AI.conclusion: The framework supports spatial epidemiological modelling and bias-aware behavioural analysis, enabling synthetic data generation that reflects social and geographic heterogeneity for policy-relevant analyses.

title: Thinking with Imagination: Agentic Visual Spatial Reasoning with World Simulators
summary: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong visual reasoning capabilities, their spatial reasoning abilities remain largely constrained to the observed images and text-oriented chain-of-thought. They often struggle to infer unobserved layouts, maintain cross-view consistency, and reason from alternative viewpoints when only limited egocentric observations are available. In this work, we study this problem as thinking with imagination, where a VLM actively acquires imagined visual evidence by interacting with a world simulator during reasoning. We propose Astra, an agentic spatial reasoning framework that empowers VLMs with action-conditioned visual imagination. Specifically, Astra couples Astra-VL, an RL-trained VLM policy, with Astra-WM, a Bagel-based world simulator that generates novel-view observations from context images and natural-language camera motions. To provide reliable imagined evidence, Astra-WM is trained with view consistency tuning to improve pose and content consistency across views. In the RL stage, we propose a world-simulator-in-the-loop two-phase RL curriculum to stabilize tool-use exploration and advance the model's ability to invoke the simulator only when imagined observations improve over direct answering. Experiments demonstrate that both the world simulator and the agentic policy are necessary: Astra-WM improves simulator-augmented Gemini-3-Flash on MMSI-Bench from 45.1 to 49.5, while Astra-VL improves the Qwen3-VL backbone from 29.8 to 38.8 on MMSI-Bench and from 36.8 to 42.7 on MindCube. These results show that imagined observations can provide useful spatial evidence, but effective world-model-augmented reasoning requires learning when, where, and how to imagine.

AI.tldr: Proposes Astra, a framework for agentic spatial reasoning via action-conditioned imagined observations, combining Astra-VL (RL-trained VLM policy) and Astra-WM (Bagel-based world simulator) with view-consistency tuned training and a two-phase RL curriculum; yields improvements on MMSI-Bench and MindCube, showing imagined observations aid spatial reasoning when learned to imagine appropriately.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of Vision-Language Models in inferring unobserved layouts, maintaining cross-view consistency, and reasoning from alternative viewpoints under limited egocentric observations by enabling controlled visual imagination through a world simulator.
AI.method: Astra-VL (RL-trained policy) paired with Astra-WM (Bagel-based world simulator) that generates novel-view observations from context images and natural-language camera motions. Astra-WM trained with view consistency tuning for pose/content consistency across views. A world-simulator-in-the-loop two-phase RL curriculum stabilizes tool-use exploration and trains the model to invoke the simulator only when imagined observations improve over direct answering.
AI.result: Astra-WM improves Gemini-3-Flash on MMSI-Bench from 45.1 to 49.5. Astra-VL improves the Qwen3-VL backbone from 29.8 to 38.8 on MMSI-Bench and from 36.8 to 42.7 on MindCube.
AI.conclusion: Imagined observations provide useful spatial evidence, but effective world-model-augmented reasoning requires learning when, where, and how to imagine; both the world simulator and the agentic policy are necessary for the reported gains.

title: Your GFlowNet Secretly Learns an Optimal Transport Plan
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are a framework for sampling structured objects via stochastic trajectories in a directed graph. In this work, we establish a theoretical connection between non-acyclic GFlowNets and optimal transport (OT). We show that fixing the initial flow distribution in a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces its objective to a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest path costs. At the optimum, the learned GFlowNet policy therefore encodes an optimal transport plan from the source distribution to the target distribution: we show that sampling trajectories from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding optimal coupling. Our formulation enables applying the GFlowNet learning framework to OT problems on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization. Experiments confirm agreement with exact OT solvers and demonstrate that GFlowNets can learn high-quality transport plans.

AI.tldr: Non-acyclic GFlowNets with fixed initial flow reduce to Kantorovich OT on graphs; the GFlowNet learns an OT plan and sampling recovers the optimal coupling; scalable OT via edge flows with neural nets; empirical agreement with exact OT solvers.
AI.motivation: To connect stochastic trajectory-based sampling (GFlowNets) with optimal transport, enabling OT on large graphs via a learnable, flow-based framework.
AI.method: Theoretical reduction: fix the initial flow distribution in a minimum-flow GFlowNet so that the objective becomes a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest-path costs; show that at optimality the learned policy encodes an OT plan; use edge-flow parameterization and neural networks to implement GFlowNets for OT.
AI.result: Establishes an equivalence to Kantorovich OT; sampling from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding optimal coupling; experiments validate agreement with exact OT solvers and show high-quality transport plans.
AI.conclusion: GFlowNets provide a scalable learning framework for OT on graphs, enabling practical computation of OT plans via neural-parameterized edge flows; results suggest applicability to large-scale OT problems with competitive accuracy.

title: Rethinking Infrastructure Inspection as Image Difference Classification: A Traffic Sign Case Study
comment: CVPR 2026 Computer Vision for the Built World Workshop (CV4AEC @ CVPR)
summary: Digital twins (DTs) allow the digitalization of road infrastructure inspection, though this is hindered by limited annotated data. This work exploits the relational nature of continuous asset condition monitoring to reformulate image-based defect detection as image difference classification (IDC) to reduce data reliance. This was evaluated in a case study on low-resource traffic sign inspection with different IDC classifiers using a newly-curated, high quality dataset. Results indicate that the instruction-based classifier outperforms encoder-based ones and gains from comparison with reference images. This shows that IDC can be an effective task modeling for tackling data constraints in infrastructure inspection and DT asset condition updating.

AI.tldr: Reframe defect detection as image difference classification (IDC) leveraging relational cues in continuous asset monitoring; a traffic-sign case study shows instruction-based IDC surpasses encoder-based IDC and benefits from reference comparisons, reducing data needs for DT inspection.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity in image-based infrastructure inspection for digital twins. Exploit the relational, temporal nature of asset condition monitoring by recasting defect detection as a difference-classification problem to reduce annotation and data requirements.
AI.method: Reformulate image-based defect detection as IDC ; compare classifiers including instruction-based and encoder-based IDC; use a newly curated high-quality dataset for low-resource traffic sign inspection; evaluate with and without reference-image comparisons.
AI.result: Instruction-based IDC outperforms encoder-based IDC; incorporating reference images further improves performance; demonstrates IDC as an effective task modeling approach under data constraints for infrastructure inspection and DT asset condition updating.
AI.conclusion: IDC is a viable, data-efficient modeling paradigm for infrastructure inspection and digital-twin asset condition updating, enabling robust defect detection with limited labeled data and leveraging temporal/reference information.

title: Complexity-Balanced Diffusion Splitting
summary: Standard continuous-time generative models rely on monolithic architectures that must navigate vastly different signal regimes, from isotropic noise to intricate data distributions. While scaling model capacity improves performance, deploying a massive network uniformly across the entire generative timeline is inherently inefficient. In this work, we propose Complexity-Balanced Splitting (CBS), a principled framework for temporal capacity allocation that distributes the generative workload across multiple specialized sub-networks. Grounded in function approximation theory and de Boor's equidistribution principle, CBS partitions the diffusion timeline into segments of equal approximation burden, allocating more representational capacity to regions where the generative dynamics are more difficult to model. To estimate this local complexity, we introduce two complementary and tractable monitor functions: a spatial measure based on the flow's Dirichlet energy, and a geometric measure based on the acceleration of the sampling trajectories. Using a lightweight auxiliary model to estimate these complexity profiles, our approach eliminates the need for heuristic temporal splits or computationally expensive search procedures. Extensive evaluation across multiple architectures (SiT, JiT, and UNet) and datasets demonstrates that CBS consistently improves synthesis quality without increasing per-step inference cost. In particular, CBS improves FID by ~35% on SiT-XL with CFG relative to naive temporal partitioning. Project page is available at https://noamissachar.github.io/CBS/.

AI.tldr: CBS (Complexity-Balanced Splitting) allocates diffusion-time capacity to specialized sub-networks by partitioning the timeline into equal-approximation-burden segments, guided by complexity estimates (Dirichlet energy and trajectory acceleration) from a lightweight auxiliary model. This yields higher synthesis quality without increasing per-step cost.
AI.motivation: Monolithic continuous-time generative models struggle to efficiently model regions of vastly different difficulty along the diffusion timeline. Uniformly scaling capacity is wasteful; a principled, temporally adaptive allocation can improve efficiency and quality.
AI.method: Partition the diffusion timeline using the equidistribution principle so each segment has equal approximation burden. Estimate local complexity with two monitor functions: (1) a spatial measure via the flow's Dirichlet energy and (2) a geometric measure via acceleration of sampling trajectories. A lightweight auxiliary model predicts the complexity profile to guide the sub-network allocation (CBS). Applicable to multiple architectures (SiT, JiT, UNet) without extra per-step cost.
AI.result: CBS yields consistent synthesis-quality gains across architectures/datasets. Notably, FID improves by ~35% on SiT-XL with classifier-free guidance compared to naive temporal partitioning. The approach avoids heuristic splits and maintains the same per-step inference cost.
AI.conclusion: CBS provides a principled, scalable method to balance temporal capacity in diffusion models by equitably distributing complexity across segments, improving quality while preserving efficiency and generalizing across architectures.

title: PAC-Bayesian Adversarially Robust Generalization for Message Passing Graph Neural Networks: A Sensitivity Analysis
summary: Whilst the vulnerability of graph neural networks (GNNs) to adversarial attacks poses a critical threat to graph representation learning, the understanding of the robust generalization behavior remains a fundamental challenge in the adversarial setting. Recently, PAC-Bayesian margin-based generalization analysis substantially advances this line of research by providing a flexible and data-dependent analytical framework. However, existing robust analyses often rely on isotropic Gaussian posteriors and control weight perturbations in the full parameter space, which limits the ability to capture heterogeneous parameter sensitivity yet hinges on hidden-width-dependent complexity terms, resulting in not-tight-enough generalization bounds. In this paper, we extend a recently proposed sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework from deep neural networks to message passing GNNs (MPGNNs) and derive a tighter robust generalization bound in the adversarial setting. Specifically, we first quantify how sensitive the perturbations across different parameter blocks are to the network outputs by deriving the output Jacobians with respect to the weight parameters. Exploiting the fact that these Jacobian matrices have rank at most $K$ in $K$-class graph classification, we then construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices and use anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to upper bound the KL divergence in a tight way. Notably, by refining the spectral-norm dependence on the learned weights and reducing the leading dimension factor from hidden-width-dependent terms to the number of classes $K$, our analysis yields much tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs, thereby guiding their designs to enhance adversarial robustness.

AI.tldr: Extends sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian analysis to MPGNNs, yielding tighter robust generalization bounds via Jacobian-aligned anisotropic posteriors and rank-K Jacobians to reduce dependence on hidden width to class count K.
AI.motivation: Address robust generalization under adversarial perturbations for graph neural networks; overcome limitations of isotropic posteriors and full-parameter perturbations; provide tighter, data-dependent bounds.
AI.method: Compute output Jacobians with respect to weight parameters; leverage the fact that Jacobians have rank at most K for K-class graph classification; construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices; use anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to tightly bound the KL divergence; refine spectral-norm dependence on learned weights, reducing leading dimension factor from hidden width to number of classes K; apply a PAC-Bayesian margin framework to MPGNNs.
AI.result: Derivation yields tighter robust generalization bounds for MPGNNs under adversarial settings; provides improved theoretical guarantees and practical guidance for robust design and regularization.
AI.conclusion: Extends a sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework to MPGNNs, achieving tighter, data-dependent robust bounds by exploiting parameter sensitivity aligned with Jacobians and rank-K structure, and reducing dependence on hidden width to the number of classes K.

title: Humans' ALMANAC: A Human Collaboration Dataset of Action-Level Mental Model Annotations for Agent Collaboration
summary: Recent advances in LLM agents have enabled complex cognitive capabilities, such as multi-step reasoning, planning, and tool use, that increasingly position these agents as human collaborators. Effective collaboration, however, requires collaborators to continuously maintain and align mental models of their own reasoning,partners' intentions, and shared goals during the collaborative process. Today's agents rarely develop such capabilities since they are primarily optimized for task completion, and the community lacks authentic human collaboration data with action-level mental model annotations that could guide agents toward process-level collaborative competence. To bridge this gap, we present ALMANAC, a dataset of Action-Level Mental model ANnotations for Agent Collaboration built from the Map Task, a classic dyadic routing task from social science. ALMANAC contains 2,987 collaboration actions, each paired with theory-informed mental model annotations that record the participants' self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, and perceived team goal. We benchmark six LLMs on predicting humans' next-turn behavior and mental models. Our results demonstrate ALMANAC's utility in evaluating models' ability to simulate human collaborative behaviors and infer their underlying mental models.

AI.tldr: ALMANAC provides a dataset of action-level mental-model annotations for agent collaboration (2,987 actions from Map Task) to evaluate LLMs on predicting human next-turn behavior and mental models.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between task-oriented optimization of LLM agents and authentic human collaboration that relies on continuously updated, shared mental models; current data lacks action-level mental-model annotations.
AI.method: Constructed ALMANAC from the Map Task with 2,987 collaboration actions, each annotated with self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, and perceived team goal; benchmarked six LLMs on next-turn prediction and mental-model inference.
AI.result: ALMANAC demonstrates utility for evaluating models' ability to simulate human collaborative behaviors and infer underlying mental models.
AI.conclusion: The dataset enables process-level collaboration research and can guide the development of agents with improved collaborative competence; further expansion and validation across tasks are recommended.

title: PAR3D: A Unified 3D-MLLM with Part-Aware Representation for Scene Understanding
summary: Recent advances in 3D multimodal large language models (3D-MLLMs) have enabled unified solutions for 3D scene understanding tasks, including visual question answering, captioning, and referring segmentation. However, existing 3D-MLLMs remain largely object-centric, limiting their ability to model fine-grained part structures that are essential for embodied interaction with 3D environments. In this work, we present PAR3D, a unified part-aware 3D-MLLM framework that enables models to understand, reason about, and ground both objects and their parts in 3D scenes. To enable training and evaluation of part-aware 3D scene understanding, we introduce ScenePart, a synthetic 3D scene dataset with part-level annotations and language instructions. We further develop Part-Aware 3D Representation Learning to enrich 3D visual representations with fine-grained part-level semantics, and propose Hierarchical Segmentation Query Generation to ground part targets via hierarchical object-part queries. Extensive experiments show that our method substantially improves part-level question answering and referring segmentation, while also achieving strong performance across object-level vision-language tasks.

AI.tldr: PAR3D introduces a unified, part-aware 3D-MLLM framework that extends 3D multimodal models to understand and ground both objects and their parts; it comes with ScenePart dataset, Part-Aware 3D Representation Learning, and Hierarchical Segmentation Query Generation, yielding strong gains on part-level and object-level V+L tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D-MLLMs are largely object-centric and struggle with fine-grained part structures needed for embodied interaction in 3D environments; this work seeks to incorporate part-level semantics and grounding into 3D vision-language modeling.
AI.method: Propose PAR3D framework. Introduce ScenePart, a synthetic 3D scene dataset with part-level annotations and language instructions. Develop Part-Aware 3D Representation Learning to embed fine-grained part semantics into 3D representations. Propose Hierarchical Segmentation Query Generation to ground part targets via hierarchical object-part queries.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show substantial improvements in part-level question answering and referring segmentation, with maintained strong performance on object-level vision-language tasks.
AI.conclusion: PAR3D advances 3D-MLLMs toward unified part-aware understanding, enabling grounding of part-level semantics in 3D scenes with supporting dataset and learning techniques.

title: Reactive Flux Matching: Mechanism Discovery and Adaptive Sampling of Rare Events
summary: Path sampling methods generate ensembles of reactive trajectories connecting metastable states, but extracting mechanistic insight from these data remains nontrivial. We introduce Flux Matching, a framework that learns two complementary objects directly from reactive trajectory data: a current velocity $u(z)$, whose streamlines trace the dominant reaction pathways, and a scalar potential $h(z)$, obtained from a weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition of the reactive current, that serves as a data-driven reaction coordinate. Both minimize quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble, analogous to the flow matching loss in generative modeling, and require no knowledge of the underlying dynamics or stationary distribution. Unlike committor-based methods, $u$ and $h$ remain well-defined under projection onto non-Markovian collective variables, and their level sets in turn provide adaptive interfaces for improved sampling with enhanced sampling methods. Flux Matching is validated through the generation of current velocity trajectories and rate constant calculations on molecular systems.

AI.tldr: Flux Matching learns a current velocity u(z) and a scalar potential h(z) from reactive trajectories to reveal dominant pathways and provide a data-driven reaction coordinate, without requiring knowledge of dynamics or stationary distribution.
AI.motivation: To extract mechanistic insight from path sampling data by constructing a flow-based representation that remains applicable under non-Markovian CVs and facilitates enhanced sampling.
AI.method: Infer u(z) and h(z) by minimizing quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble; weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition of reactive current to obtain h; analogous to flow matching loss; no explicit dynamics model.
AI.result: Demonstrated on molecular systems: generate current velocity trajectories and compute rate constants; show level sets of h act as adaptive interfaces for enhanced sampling.
AI.conclusion: Flux Matching provides robust, data-driven mechanistic insight from reactive trajectories, offering a committor-free alternative and targeted sampling interfaces for complex molecular kinetics.

title: Risk Assessment of Autonomous Driving: Integrating Technical Failures, Ethical Dilemmas, and Policy Frameworks
summary: Autonomous driving technology has the potential to reduce the large number of road traffic accidents caused by human error each year, but it also brings new types of risks that need to be evaluated from the aspects of technology, ethics and regulations. Based on public crash data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), disengagement reports from the California Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV), the MIT Moral Machines dataset, and a comparative regulatory analysis of five jurisdictions, we have found that the main types of technical failure modes are perception and classification errors. These account for a relatively large proportion of the reported accidents, and it can be concluded that there are different ethical frameworks for autonomous vehicle decision-making, and inconsistent regulations in different areas increase the uncertainty of widespread application. Generally speaking, the problems of technology, ethics and regulation are closely related and need to be solved together. Therefore, this paper recommends a more adaptive and cooperative governance approach that combines engineering standards, ethical discussion, and institutional supervision.

AI.tldr: Perception/classification failures are central technical risks in autonomous driving; ethics and regulation vary across contexts, creating uncertainty; a cohesive governance approach blending engineering standards, ethical discourse, and institutional oversight is recommended.
AI.motivation: Evaluate technology, ethics, and regulatory risks of autonomous driving using public crash data, disengagement reports, the MIT Moral Machines dataset, and regulatory analysis across five jurisdictions.
AI.method: Analyze NHTSA crash data, DMV disengagement reports, the MIT Moral Machines dataset, and a comparative regulatory analysis across five jurisdictions to identify primary failure modes and variances in ethical frameworks and regulations.
AI.result: Identified perception and classification errors as major technical failure modes; observed multiple ethical frameworks guiding autonomous vehicle decision-making and regulatory heterogeneity across jurisdictions; concluded that technology, ethics, and regulation are interrelated and collectively influence deployment; proposed adaptive and cooperative governance integrating engineering standards, ethical discourse, and institutional supervision.
AI.conclusion: An integrated governance approach is needed to enable safe and widespread deployment of autonomous vehicles.

title: Ask-to-Clarify: Resolving Instruction Ambiguity through Multi-turn Dialogue
summary: The ultimate goal of embodied agents is to create collaborators that can interact with humans, not mere executors that passively follow instructions. This requires agents to communicate, coordinate, and adapt their actions based on human feedback. Recently, advances in VLAs have offered a path toward this goal. However, most current VLA-based embodied agents operate in a one-way mode: they receive an instruction and execute it without feedback. This approach fails in real-world scenarios where instructions are often ambiguous. In this paper, we address this problem with the Ask-to-Clarify framework. Our framework first resolves ambiguous instructions by asking questions in a multi-turn dialogue. Then it generates low-level actions end-to-end. Specifically, the Ask-to-Clarify framework consists of two components, one VLM for collaboration and one diffusion for action. We also introduce a connection module that generates conditions for the diffusion based on the output of the VLM. This module adjusts the observation by instructions to create reliable conditions. We train our framework with a two-stage knowledge-insulation strategy. First, we fine-tune the collaboration component using ambiguity-solving dialogue data to handle ambiguity. Then, we integrate the action component while freezing the collaboration one. This preserves the interaction abilities while fine-tuning the diffusion to generate actions. The training strategy guarantees our framework can first ask questions, then generate actions. During inference, a signal detector functions as a router that helps our framework switch between asking questions and taking actions. We evaluate the Ask-to-Clarify framework in 8 real-world tasks, where it outperforms existing state-of-the-art VLAs. The results suggest that our proposed framework, along with the training strategy, provides a path toward collaborative embodied agents.

AI.tldr: Ask-to-Clarify: a two-stage framework enabling embodied agents to ask clarifying questions before taking actions, using a VLM for dialogue and a diffusion model for end-to-end action, with a connection module to condition diffusion on dialogue outputs, and a signal-based router to switch between asking and acting; trained in two stages to preserve interaction while learning actions; tested on 8 real-world tasks with superior performance.
AI.motivation: Real-world instructions are often ambiguous; current VLA-based agents are one-way and fail to use human feedback; enabling back-and-forth dialogue improves reliability and collaboration.
AI.method: Two-component architecture: a Visual-Language Model (VLM) for collaborative dialogue and a diffusion-based action generator; a connection module that turns the VLM outputs into diffusion conditions; a two-stage training regime: first fine-tune the VLM on ambiguity-resolution dialogues, then integrate and fine-tune the diffusion component while keeping the VLM fixed; inference uses a signal detector to decide when to ask questions vs. act.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art VLAs across eight real-world tasks, demonstrating improved capability to resolve ambiguity and generate reliable actions in collaboration with humans.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable path toward truly collaborative embodied agents, combining clarifying dialogue with end-to-end action generation and a training strategy that preserves interaction ability while enabling robust action execution.

title: Tangram: Unlocking Non-Uniform KV Cache for Efficient Multi-turn LLM Serving
summary:
Multi-turn Large Language Model (LLM) serving is critical for consistent user experiences, yet the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache imposes significant pressure on GPU memory and bandwidth. Non-uniform KV compression effectively preserves more information by considering the individual importance of each KV cache. However, such KV cache heterogeneity introduces various systemic challenges - including memory fragmentation, scheduling complexities, and diminished kernel utilization - which collectively lead to significant inefficiencies in existing LLM serving systems.
  To overcome these challenges, we present Tangram, a novel serving system designed to make Non-uniform KV caches practical. Tangram addresses systemic inefficiencies through three core techniques: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation assigns a static memory footprint to each head based on its intrinsic pattern, entirely eliminating dynamic scheduling overhead and prefill stalls; (2) Head Group Page clusters attention heads with similar retention demands and manages them with independent, vectorized page tables, thereby maximizing physical memory reclamation; and (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing leverages static budget profiles to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead. Experimental results show that Tangram improves throughput by up to 2.6x compared to existing baselines, while fully preserving model accuracy. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/TANGRAM.

AI.tldr: Tangram enables practical non-uniform KV cache in multi-turn LLM serving with deterministic budgets, grouped head memory management, and AOT load balancing, achieving up to 2.6x throughput while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Non-uniform KV caches can preserve more information but introduce memory fragmentation, scheduling complexity, and poor kernel utilization, degrading serving efficiency.
AI.method: 1) Deterministic budget allocation per attention head to fix memory footprint; 2) Head Group Page clusters group heads by retention demands with independent vectorized page tables to improve memory reclamation; 3) Ahead-of-Time load balancing uses static budgets to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead.
AI.result: Throughput improvements up to 2.6x versus baselines, with full preservation of model accuracy; code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Tangram demonstrates a practical path to deploy non-uniform KV caches in LLM serving by addressing core systemic inefficiencies through fixed budgets, grouped memory management, and static load balancing.

title: Unsupervised Skill Discovery for Agentic Data Analysis
summary: Inference-time skill augmentation provides a lightweight way to improve data-analytic agents by injecting reusable procedural knowledge without updating model parameters. However, discovering effective skills for data analysis remains challenging, as reliable supervision is expensive and success criteria vary across analytical formats. This raises the key question of how to discover reusable data-analysis skills from unlabeled exploration alone. We propose DataCOPE, an unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery framework for data-analytic agents. DataCOPE derives verifier signals from the exploration trajectories and uses them to characterize relative quality or aggreement among trajectories. It iteratively coordinates a Data-Analytic Agent for trajectory generation, an Unsupervised Verifier for signal extraction, and a Skill Manager for contrastive skill distillation. For report-style analysis, we instantiate the verifier as an Adaptive Checklist Verifier that derives task-specific criteria, scores reports by verifiable coverage, and iteratively refines the checklist. For reasoning-style analysis, we instantiate it as an Answer Agreement Verifier that groups trajectories by answer agreement and uses self-consistency as an auxiliary signal. We evaluate DataCOPE on report-style analysis from Deep Data Research and reasoning-style analysis from DABStep. Across both settings, DataCOPE consistently improves held-out performance over baselines. Averaged across four model settings, DataCOPE improves the mean score by 9.71% and 32.30% on report-style and reasoning-style tasks respectively.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery (DataCOPE) learns reusable data-analysis skills from unlabeled exploration by coordinating a Data-Analytic Agent, an Unsupervised Verifier, and a Skill Manager. Verifiers derive trajectory-quality signals (Adaptive Checklist Verifier for reports; Answer Agreement Verifier for reasoning) to guide contrastive skill distillation, yielding improvements over baselines in both report-style and reasoning-style tasks.
AI.motivation: Discovering effective data-analysis skills without expensive labeled supervision is challenging due to varying success criteria across analytical formats. Unlabeled exploration requires a method to extract reliable supervisory signals.
AI.method: Iterative coordination of three components: (1) Data-Analytic Agent that generates trajectories, (2) Unsupervised Verifier that extracts signals from trajectories and assesses relative quality/agreement, and (3) Skill Manager that performs contrastive skill distillation. For reports, the Adaptive Checklist Verifier derives task-specific criteria and scores coverage; for reasoning, the Answer Agreement Verifier groups trajectories by answer agreement and uses self-consistency as an auxiliary signal.
AI.result: DataCOPE yields consistent improvements over baselines in held-out performance. On average across four model settings, it improves mean scores by 9.71% for report-style tasks and 32.30% for reasoning-style tasks.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised, verifier-guided skill discovery can produce transferable, high-quality data-analysis skills that improve model performance across different analytical formats without labeled supervision.

title: Plug-and-Play Guidance for Discrete Diffusion Models via Gradient-Informed Logit Correction
summary: Controllable generation with discrete diffusion models is often hindered by high computational overhead or the need for retraining. In this paper, we present \underline{\textbf{G}}radient-\underline{\textbf{I}}nformed \underline{\textbf{L}}ogit \underline{\textbf{C}}orrection (\textbf{GILC}), a plug-and-play framework that efficiently estimates guidance signals by repurposing the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy. To circumvent the gradient instability inherent in high-dimensional discrete spaces, we introduce a Jacobian-free mechanism that directly corrects the clean prediction logits, facilitating stable and effective guidance. Our method accommodates both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions. Extensive experiments across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks demonstrate that GILC achieves state-of-the-art performance without additional training, frequently outperforming fine-tuning approaches.

AI.tldr: GILC presents a plug-and-play gradient-informed logit correction for discrete diffusion models, using a Jacobian-free correction of logits to enable stable guidance without retraining, achieving state-of-the-art results on DNA, protein, and molecular generation with differentiable/non-differentiable rewards.
AI.motivation: Overcome high computational cost and retraining needs in controllable discrete diffusion, and address gradient instability in high-dimensional discrete spaces.
AI.method: Repurpose pretrained denoising network as variational proxy; devise Jacobian-free mechanism that directly corrects clean logits; support both differentiable and non-differentiable rewards; plug-and-play, no additional training.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance across DNA, protein sequences, and molecular generation; often outperforms fine-tuning baselines; no extra training required.
AI.conclusion: GILC offers efficient, stable guidance for discrete diffusion controllable generation, broad applicability across biological and chemical sequence/data types, enabling practical deployment without retraining.

title: Agent Memory: Characterization and System Implications of Stateful Long-Horizon Workloads
summary: LLM agents are increasingly deployed on long-horizon tasks requiring sustained reasoning over extended interaction histories. Realizing this at scale requires agents to persistently store, retrieve, and update their own memory across sessions. A rich ecosystem of agent memory systems has emerged spanning flat retrieval, LLM-mediated extraction, consolidating fact stores, and agentic control flows. Yet, their system-level behavior remains uncharacterized. We present the first systems characterization of agent memory. First, we introduce a system-oriented taxonomy classifying agent memory systems along four axes. Second, we build a phase-aware profiling harness attributing cost to construction, retrieval, and generation. Third, we characterize ten representative systems across two benchmark suites, uncovering how design choices shift cost across the write and read paths. Finally, we derive 10 system recommendations covering construction scheduling, capability floors, amortization via query volume, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management.

AI.tldr: First systems characterization of agent memory for long-horizon LLM-enabled agents, with taxonomy, a phase-aware profiling harness, empirical study of ten memory systems across two benchmarks, and 10 design recommendations for scalable memory management.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, persistent memory across sessions for long-horizon agent tasks; current literature lacks a holistic, systems-level view of memory architectures, costs, and tradeoffs.
AI.method: Develop a system-oriented taxonomy along four axes; build a phase-aware profiler that attributes cost to memory construction, retrieval, and generation; empirically evaluate ten representative systems across two benchmarks to observe cost shifts; derive actionable design recommendations.
AI.result: Characterization shows how design choices affect write/read costs; identifies cost bottlenecks; provides practical guidelines for memory construction scheduling, freshness-latency tradeoffs, amortization via query volume, and fleet-scale management.
AI.conclusion: Provides the first comprehensive systems characterization of agent memory, enabling informed design and deployment of scalable memory systems for LLM agents; lays groundwork for future research and tooling.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Adaptive Sensor Fusion for Autonomous Navigation
summary: This work introduces a hybrid deep learning approach integrated with an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) to enhance pose estimation accuracy in Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) for autonomous navigation. The proposed model employs a Vision Transformer (ViT) network to effectively capture temporal dependencies from inertial measurement unit (IMU) data and utilizes a Multiscale Convolutional Neural Network (MCNN) to learn optical flow-based motion cues from visual data. An adaptive sensor fusion module dynamically weights IMU and visual features by leveraging estimated uncertainty, thus improving robustness in diverse and challenging environmental conditions. Additionally, a novel uncertainty-aware loss function is proposed to explicitly incorporate prediction uncertainty into the learning process, enabling robust and accurate navigation under noisy, incomplete, or unreliable sensor inputs. Comprehensive evaluations of the KITTI dataset demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline approaches, achieving superior performance in terms of Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE). The lightweight and computationally efficient model processes data at 155 FPS on an NVIDIA A100 GPU, making it highly suitable for deployment in resource-constrained autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: Hybrid ViT-MCNN with UKF for improved VIO pose estimation; adaptive uncertainty-aware fusion and loss; KITTI evaluation with state-of-the-art results; real-time 155 FPS on A100.
AI.motivation: Enhance pose estimation robustness and accuracy in Visual-Inertial Odometry under noisy, incomplete, or unreliable sensor data by exploiting temporal modeling, optical-flow cues, and uncertainty-aware fusion.
AI.method: A Vision Transformer processes IMU-derived temporal features to capture temporal dependencies; a Multiscale CNN extracts optical-flow-based visual motion cues; an adaptive fusion module weights IMU and visual features using estimated uncertainty; an Unscented Kalman Filter integrates the fused cues for pose estimation; an uncertainty-aware loss function explicitly incorporates prediction uncertainty into training.
AI.result: On KITTI, the approach outperforms baselines in Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE); achieves 155 FPS on an NVIDIA A100, indicating suitability for real-time deployment on resource-constrained systems.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid architecture effectively combines transformer-based temporal modeling, multi-scale visual cues, and uncertainty-aware learning with UKF fusion to deliver robust, accurate VIO for autonomous navigation, with strong real-time performance on modern GPUs.

title: Learning What to Forget: Improving LLM Unlearning via Learned Token-Level Importance
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model while preserving its general capabilities. For autoregressive language models, not all tokens in a forget sample are equally relevant to forgetting. Existing approaches either ignore this heterogeneity or rely on auxiliary models, heuristics, or external annotations to estimate each token's relevance for forgetting. We instead characterize it through the interaction with the retain objective: a token is forget-specific to the extent that minimizing the forget loss on that token does not conflict with retain optimality. We formalize this perspective as a joint optimization problem over the model parameters and the token weights and show that, under a natural separation condition, the resulting objective recovers the oracle forget-specific token support. Motivated by this formulation, we introduce Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU), a lightweight framework that jointly learns token forget-specificity and model parameters during unlearning using a simple linear scorer over the hidden states, without external token level supervision. Across TOFU and RWKU, ATWU achieves state of the art forget-retain trade-offs, outperforming sample-level methods, probability-based token weighting heuristics, and auxiliary-model-based approaches. Moreover, the learned scores align substantially better with ground truth forget-specific spans, indicating that ATWU identifies semantically meaningful token level forgetting signals. Overall, our results suggest that retain conflict provides an effective criterion for identifying what language models should forget, enabling unsupervised learning of token level forget-specificity directly from model representations with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: Proposes Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU) to jointly learn token forget-specificity and model parameters, using a simple linear scorer over hidden states, achieving state-of-the-art forget-retain trade-offs without external supervision.
AI.motivation: In autoregressive LMs, tokens within a forget sample contribute unevenly to forgetting. Existing methods ignore this heterogeneity or rely on auxiliary models/annotations. The authors define forget-specificity by the interaction with the retain objective, formalizing it as a joint optimization over model parameters and token weights under a separation condition, which recovers the oracle forget-specific token set.
AI.method: ATWU jointly optimizes model parameters and token weights via alternating updates. It uses a lightweight linear scorer on hidden states to assign token forget-specificity, trained without external token-level supervision, and operates within a simple retain-forget objective framework. The approach is evaluated on TOFU and RWKU datasets and is designed to be computationally efficient.
AI.result: ATWU achieves state-of-the-art forget-retain trade-offs on TOFU and RWKU, outperforming sample-level methods, probability-based token weighting heuristics, and auxiliary-model-based approaches. The learned forget-specific scores correlate strongly with ground-truth forget spans, indicating semantically meaningful token-level forgetting signals.
AI.conclusion: The retain-conflict criterion is effective for identifying what to forget. ATWU enables unsupervised learning of token-level forget-specificity directly from model representations with minimal computational overhead.

title: Vortex: Efficient and Programmable Sparse Attention Serving for AI Agents
summary: Sparse attention is becoming increasingly important for serving large language models (LLMs) as generation lengths continue to grow. However, deploying and evaluating new sparse attention algorithms at scale remains highly engineering-intensive, slowing both human researchers and AI agents in exploring the sparse attention design. To address this challenge, we present Vortex, a system that combines a Python-embedded frontend language atop a page-centric tensor abstraction for expressing a broad range of sparse attention algorithms, with an efficient backend tightly integrated into modern LLM serving stacks. Vortex enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms, effectively translating their theoretical efficiency gains into real-world throughput improvements. As a result, Vortex substantially accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms. First, AI agents use Vortex to automatically generate and refine diverse algorithms, the best reaching up to $3.46\times$ higher throughput than full attention while preserving accuracy. Second, Vortex extends sparse attention to emerging architectures and very large models that are otherwise hard to experiment with, reaching up to $4.7\times$ higher throughput on the MLA-based GLM-4.7-Flash and $1.37\times$ on the 229B-parameter MiniMax-M2.7 on NVIDIA B200 GPUs.

AI.tldr: Vortex provides a Python-embedded frontend atop a page-centric tensor abstraction with an efficient backend integrated into LLM serving stacks, enabling rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms.
AI.motivation: Reduce engineering effort and time required to design, prototype, and benchmark sparse attention algorithms at scale for LLMs.
AI.method: A frontend language (Python-embedded) built on a page-centric tensor abstraction to express sparse attention algorithms, plus a tightly integrated backend in modern LLM serving stacks; AI agents autonomously generate and refine diverse sparse attention algorithms.
AI.result: Best-performing algorithms reach up to 3.46x throughput improvement over full attention with preserved accuracy; performance gains extend to emerging architectures and very large models (up to 4.7x on GLM-4.7-Flash MLA-based models and 1.37x on 229B MiniMax-M2.7 on NVIDIA B200 GPUs).
AI.conclusion: Vortex accelerates the design, prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention, enabling experimentation on architectures and model scales that were previously hard to test.

title: PAMF: Prior-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Incomplete Time Series Data
summary: In healthcare, multimodal time series tasks often operate on incomplete observations in practice, for example when ECG segments are lost because electrodes detach or an entire respiratory channel is unavailable during overnight monitoring. Such missingness typically appears in two structurally distinct patterns: within-modality missing, where values are absent within an otherwise observed modality, and modality-level missing, where an entire modality is unavailable. Existing methods typically represent unobserved data implicitly through masks or missing embeddings, without learning instance-specific missing information, and most are designed for only one missingness pattern. A natural approach is to explicitly estimate the missing data; however, existing imputation methods treat missingness uniformly despite their different structural priors, and the imputation process is often isolated from downstream tasks, preventing downstream tasks from guiding imputation toward more informative representations. To address these limitations, we present PAMF, a multimodal time-series framework that explicitly handles different missingness patterns while coupling imputation with downstream prediction through prior-aware flow matching and weight sharing. Specifically, the method initializes the flow-matching source state with type-specific priors to distinguish two missing types. It further connects imputation and classification through architecturally matched encoders with weight sharing, transferring task-relevant representations into the imputation process. Experiments on multiple multimodal healthcare time-series benchmarks show that the proposed method achieves the strongest overall downstream performance across diverse datasets and missing settings compared with existing baselines.

AI.tldr: PAMF is a multimodal time-series framework that explicitly handles two missingness patterns (within-modality and modality-level) using prior-aware flow matching and shared encoders to jointly impute data and perform downstream predictions, achieving strong performance across diverse healthcare benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal time-series methods either mask missing data or treat different missingness patterns uniformly, and often decouple imputation from prediction, limiting task-relevant information transfer from the target task to the imputation process.
AI.method: PAMF initializes flow-matching with type-specific priors to distinguish missingness types and uses architecturally matched encoders with weight sharing to transfer task-relevant representations into the imputation pathway, coupling imputation and classification in a task-guided framework.
AI.result: Empirical results show PAMF achieving the strongest overall downstream performance across multiple healthcare time-series benchmarks under diverse missing settings when compared with existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling missingness with type-aware priors and sharing representations between imputation and prediction yields more informative imputations and improved predictive performance in multimodal healthcare time-series with incomplete observations.

title: Benchmark Everything Everywhere All at Once
summary: Benchmarks are fundamental for evaluating and advancing LLMs and MLLMs by providing standardized and explicit measures of performance. However, their construction is labor-intensive and hard to reuse, raising concerns about sustainability and scalability. Moreover, existing benchmarks often quickly reach performance saturation after their release, resulting in insufficient discrimination among state-of-the-art models. To address these challenges, we introduce Benchmark Agent, a fully autonomous agentic system designed for benchmark building. Our framework orchestrates the complete benchmark construction pipeline, from user query analysis and subtask design to data annotation and quality control. To assess Benchmark Agent, we implement it to produce 15 representative benchmarks, spanning diverse evaluation scenarios, including text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning. Extensive experiments, including human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge assessment, and consistency checks, demonstrate Benchmark Agent can generate high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement. More importantly, through continual evaluation, we observe several insightful findings, including that current models struggle with certain domain-specific reasoning tasks. We believe that rapidly evolving benchmarks can contribute significantly to the research community. The preview and code will be publicly available at the demo page and code repository.

AI.tldr: Autonomous Benchmark Agent for end-to-end benchmark construction; demonstrates scalable, discriminative benchmarks with minimal human input across multi-domain tasks.
AI.motivation: Benchmarks are essential for evaluating LLMs/MLLMs but are costly to build, hard to reuse, and quickly saturate; there is a need for autonomous, scalable systems that continuously generate high-quality benchmarks.
AI.method: Introduce Benchmark Agent, an autonomous agentic system that orchestrates the full benchmark construction pipeline: user query analysis, subtask design, data annotation, and quality control. Implemented to produce 15 benchmarks spanning text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning. Evaluations include human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge, and consistency checks.
AI.result: Benchmark Agent generates high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement. 15 representative benchmarks produced across diverse evaluation scenarios. Continual evaluation reveals current models struggle with certain domain-specific reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Rapidly evolving benchmarks can significantly contribute to the research community; preview and code will be publicly available on the demo page and code repository.

title: Two-Way Is Better Than One: Bidirectional Alignment with Cycle Consistency for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. 23 pages, 8 figures. Code: https://github.com/HXuSz11/BiCyc_ICLR2026
summary: Continual learning (CL) seeks models that acquire new skills without erasing prior knowledge. In exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL), this challenge is amplified because past data cannot be stored, making representation drift for old classes particularly harmful. Prototype-based EFCIL is attractive for its efficiency, yet prototypes drift as the embedding space evolves; therefore, projection-based drift compensation has become a popular remedy. We show, however, that existing one-directional projections introduce systematic bias: they either retroactively distort the current feature geometry or align past classes only locally, leaving cycle inconsistencies that accumulate across tasks. We introduce BiCyc, a bidirectional projector alignment approach with a cycle-consistency objective. BiCyc jointly optimizes two maps, old-to-new and new-to-old, with stop-gradient gating so that transport and representation co-evolve. Analytically, we show that the cycle loss contracts the singular spectrum toward unity in whitened space, and that improved transport of class means and covariances yields smaller perturbations of classification log-odds, preserving old-class decisions and mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Empirically, across standard EFCIL benchmarks, BiCyc substantially reduces forgetting and improves accuracy in from-scratch settings, while remaining competitive in the pretrained fine-grained regime.

AI.tldr: BiCyc presents bidirectional projection alignment with a cycle-consistency objective to mitigate prototype drift in exemplar-free class-incremental learning, yielding reduced forgetting and improved accuracy across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL), past data cannot be stored, so prototype-based representations drift as the embedding space evolves, causing degradation of old-class performance. One-directional projections introduce bias and only local alignment, leading to cycle inconsistencies and accumulating errors across tasks.
AI.method: Introduce BiCyc: jointly learn two projection maps old→new and new→old with stop-gradient gating; enforce cycle-consistency loss so transport and representation co-evolve; provide theoretical analysis showing cycle loss contracts the singular spectrum toward unity in whitened space and improves transport of class means/covariances; validate empirically on standard EFCIL benchmarks.
AI.result: BiCyc substantially reduces forgetting and improves accuracy in from-scratch settings; remains competitive in pretrained fine-grained regimes.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional, cycle-consistent projection alignment effectively mitigates prototype drift in EFCIL, offering principled, scalable gains for prototype-based methods.

title: Efficient Mean Curvature Computation on High-Dimensional Data Manifolds
summary: Estimating local mean curvature at each point of a high-dimensional dataset is a key ingredient of geometry-aware machine learning algorithms, such as the Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP) method. The naive implementation of this computation, based on a local shape operator approximated from k-nearest neighbor patches, involves an explicit construction of a matrix $H$ whose trace form yields an $O(m^4)$ cost per point, rendering the approach intractable for datasets with more than a few dozen features. This paper introduces two complementary contributions that together reduce this cost by several orders of magnitude. The first contribution is an exact algebraic identity. This identity, derived from the orthogonality of the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix and the cyclicity of the trace operator, eliminates $H$ entirely and reduces the per-point cost to $O(m^2)$ after the eigendecomposition. The second contribution addresses the remaining $O(m^3)$ bottleneck of the full eigendecomposition. Since the local covariance matrix has rank at most $k-1 \ll m$, we replace it with a truncated SVD of the $k \times m$ centered data matrix, an $O(k^2 m)$ operation, and derive an analytical approximation for the contribution of the null-space eigenvectors based on the expected value of their outer product under the Haar measure. The resulting estimator has total cost $O(k^2 m + k m p^2)$, where $p = k-1$. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm speedups of 50 to 300 times relative to the original implementation, with negligible loss when the fast estimator is used to replace the original version. By providing a scalable and data-driven estimate of local curvature, the proposed method establishes curvature as a practical geometric feature for a broad range of machine learning tasks, from classical to modern deep learning pipelines.

AI.tldr: A scalable method for local mean curvature estimation in high-dimensional data that avoids the full shape operator construction and uses truncated SVD with an analytical approximation for null-space components, achieving large speedups with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Curvature estimation in high-dimensional, geometry-aware ML methods (e.g., Mean Curvature Boundary Points) is computationally expensive (O(m^4) per point) due to explicit H construction. A scalable, data-driven curvature estimator is needed to enable curvature-based features in modern pipelines.
AI.method: 1) Derive an exact algebraic identity leveraging eigenvector orthogonality and trace cyclicity to eliminate the full H, reducing cost to O(m^2) after eigendecomposition. 2) Replace the full local covariance with a truncated SVD of the k x m centered data matrix (rank at most k-1), costing O(k^2 m). Derive an analytical approximation for the null-space eigenvector contribution based on the Haar measure. The final estimator cost is O(k^2 m + k m p^2) with p = k-1.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world datasets show speedups of 50 to 300 times compared with the original implementation, with negligible loss when using the fast estimator.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method yields a scalable, data-driven local curvature estimate, making curvature a practical geometric feature for a broad range of ML tasks from classical to deep learning pipelines.

title: Goedel-Architect: Streamlining Formal Theorem Proving with Blueprint Generation and Refinement
summary: We introduce Goedel-Architect, an agentic framework for formal theorem proving in Lean 4 centered on blueprint generation and refinement. A blueprint is a dependency graph of definitions and lemmas that builds up to the main theorem. First, Goedel-Architect generates a blueprint of formally stated definitions and lemmas, along with declared dependencies. This blueprint is optionally guided by a natural language proof. Then, a tool-equipped Lean prover component closes each open lemma node in parallel using relevant dependencies. Failed lemmas in turn drive refinement of the global blueprint. This strategy contrasts with other mainstream approaches which use recursive lemma decomposition, and can inefficiently loop on dead-end strategies. Using the open-weight DeepSeek-V4-Flash (284B-A13B) as the backbone, Goedel-Architect attains 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test and 75.6% pass@1 on PutnamBench. With an optional natural-language proof seeding the initial blueprint on the harder problems, we additionally close the remaining two MiniF2F-test problems (reaching 100%), lift PutnamBench to 88.8% (597/672), and solve 4/6 on IMO 2025, 11/12 on Putnam 2025, and 3/6 on USAMO 2026. This represents state-of-the-art performance for an open-source pipeline at a price point up to 500x less than comparable open-source pipelines.

AI.tldr: Blueprint-driven agentic theorem prover for Lean 4. Generates and refines a dependency blueprint of definitions/lemmas; closes lemmas in parallel; failure-driven refinements; uses DeepSeek-V4-Flash backbone; achieves state-of-the-art open-source results on MiniF2F, PutnamBench, and some math olympiad benchmarks; cost-effective.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies of recursive lemma decomposition and dead-end search in formal theorem proving. Introduce a planning-based blueprint that guides parallel proving and is refined by failures, enabling scalable, high-performance proof synthesis at lower cost.
AI.method: Generate an initial blueprint of formally stated definitions and lemmas with dependencies; optionally seed with a natural-language proof. A Lean prover component closes open lemmas in parallel using dependencies. Failed lemmas trigger refinement of the global blueprint. Backbone is DeepSeek-V4-Flash (284B-A13B). Optional NL seeding improves harder problems. Evaluate on MiniF2F, PutnamBench, IMO 2025, Putnam 2025, USAMO 2026.
AI.result: MiniF2F pass@1: 99.2%; PutnamBench pass@1: 75.6%. With NL seeding: MiniF2F 100%; PutnamBench 88.8% (597/672); IMO 2025: 4/6; Putnam 2025: 11/12; USAMO 2026: 3/6.
AI.conclusion: Claims state-of-the-art performance for open-source theorem-proving pipelines at substantially lower cost (up to 500x cheaper) than comparable pipelines, via blueprint-based planning and parallel, failure-driven refinement.

title: Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders for Effective Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for mechanistic interpretability in large language models, yet their formulation assigns each latent feature a single decoder direction, implicitly assuming features to be one-dimensional. We show that this assumption mismatches with the multi-dimensional structure of model features, provably inducing feature splitting through two distinct mechanisms. Geometrically, reconstructing a feature of intrinsic dimension $d_i \ge 2$ to error $\varepsilon$ with single-direction decoders forces a number of atoms that is exponential in $d_i$. From an end-to-end optimization perspective, this splitting is not merely possible but actively preferred. We prove that there exists a continuous path from the true $d_i$-dimensional basis to a strictly lower risk of the $\ell_1$-regularized SAE objective, whose descent directions drive any trained dictionary into that exponential regime. A single coherent feature is therefore fragmented across many near-collinear latents, producing spurious multiplicity and obscuring the intrinsic geometry. Motivated by this, we introduce Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA), which replace single-vector decoders with learned decoder subspaces, enforce block sparsity via Top-$s$ group gating, and adapt each group's effective rank with a nuclear-norm regularizer. We then show that once the block size satisfies $r \ge d_i$, a single group not only can represent the entire feature slice but is the global minimizer of the SASA objective. This consolidation yields a sample complexity polynomial in $d_i$ rather than exponential -- a decisive advantage given that every training activation costs an LLM forward pass. Empirically, on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches or exceeds standard SAEs while training on roughly half the token budget.

AI.tldr: SAEs with single-direction decoders misrepresent multi-dimensional model features, causing exponential growth in latent atoms and intrinsic feature splitting. SASA replaces decoders with learned subspaces, enforces block sparsity and adaptively tunes rank, yielding polynomial sample complexity and consolidated, more interpretable features. Empirically, SASA improves monosemanticity on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B while matching or exceeding standard SAEs at roughly half the token budget.
AI.motivation: Standard Sparse Autoencoders impose 1D latent directions, which conflicts with the multi-dimensional intrinsic structure of LLM features. This mismatch induces feature splitting and obscures geometry, hindering interpretability and efficiency. A subspace-aware architecture aims to align the model's latent geometry with its true feature dimensions.
AI.method: Introduce Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA): replace single-vector decoders with learned decoder subspaces organized into groups, enforce block sparsity via Top-s group gating, and apply a nuclear-norm regularizer to adapt each group's effective rank. Theoretical results show that if a group's size r ≥ d_i, a single group can represent the entire feature slice and becomes the global minimizer of the SASA objective. A continuous path exists from the true d_i-dimensional basis to a lower-risk regime under l1-regularized SAE, with descent directions driving toward exponential regimes in standard SAEs. This yields polynomial sample complexity in d_i. Empirically, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches or exceeds standard SAEs on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B while using about half the token budget.
AI.result: Theoretically, feature reconstruction with single-direction decoders for a feature of intrinsic dimension d_i ≥ 2 demands exponentially many atoms; end-to-end optimization under L1-regularization biases toward an exponential regime, fragmenting features into near-collinear latents. SASA demonstrates that a properly designed subspace-based decoder with block sparsity and nuclear-norm regularization can consolidate a feature into a single group when r ≥ d_i, reducing sample complexity to polynomial in d_i. Empirically, SASA reduces splitting, enhances monosemanticity, and achieves competitive or superior performance relative to standard SAEs on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B with roughly half the token budget.
AI.conclusion: SASA aligns decoder capacity with the intrinsic multi-dimensional geometry of model features, mitigating feature splitting and enabling more sample-efficient, interpretable mechanistic representations in large language models. This approach is likely to improve interpretability without sacrificing performance and scales more favorably with feature dimensionality than traditional SAEs.

title: MLEvolve: A Self-Evolving Framework for Automated Machine Learning Algorithm Discovery
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly applied to long-horizon tasks such as scientific discovery and machine learning engineering (MLE), where sustained self-evolution becomes a key capability. However, existing MLE agents suffer from inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, which together hinder long-horizon optimization. We present MLEvolve, an LLM-based self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end machine learning algorithm discovery. By extending tree search to Progressive MCGS, MLEvolve enables cross-branch information flow through graph-based reference edges and gradually shifts the search from broad exploration to focused exploitation with an entropy-inspired progressive schedule. To allow the agent to evolve with accumulated experience, we introduce Retrospective Memory, which combines a cold-start domain knowledge base with a dynamic global memory for task-specific experience retrieval and reuse. For stable long-horizon iteration, we further decouple strategic planning from code generation with adaptive coding modes. Evaluation on MLE-Bench shows that MLEvolve achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple dimensions including average medal rate and valid submission rate under a 12-hour budget (half the standard runtime). Moreover, MLEvolve also outperforms specialized algorithm discovery methods including AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at https://github.com/InternScience/MLEvolve.

AI.tldr: MLEvolve is an LLM-driven self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end ML algorithm discovery that tackles inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchy by using Progressive MCGS with graph-based reference edges, entropy-inspired scheduling, Retrospective Memory, and decoupled planning from code generation; shows SOTA results on MLE-Bench with efficient 12h budgets and cross-domain generalization; code available.
AI.motivation: Enable sustained, long-horizon optimization for ML algorithm discovery by enabling cross-branch information sharing, memory-augmented search, and hierarchical control.
AI.method: Extend tree search to Progressive MCGS; introduce graph-based reference edges for cross-branch information flow; apply entropy-inspired progressive schedule from exploration to exploitation; Retrospective Memory combining a cold-start knowledge base with dynamic global memory for task-specific experience retrieval and reuse; decouple strategic planning from code generation using adaptive coding modes.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on MLE-Bench across several metrics under a 12-hour budget (half standard runtime); outperforms specialized algorithm discovery methods including AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks; demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: MLEvolve advances LLM-based search for ML algorithm discovery by integrating cross-branch memory, progressive search, and stable planning-execution separation; code is released at the provided URL.

title: Quantifying the Privacy of Counterfactuals by Leveraging Membership Inference Attacks Against Synthetic Data
summary: Counterfactuals are typically used in high-stakes decision areas to explain a machine learning model by showing how changes to the user profiles result in the desired outcome. However, explaining the model's decisions through counterfactuals can also be exploited by an adversary to conduct privacy attacks against the model or its training data. Drawing on the analogy that counterfactuals provide realistic substitutes for real training data, similar to synthetic data, we demonstrate in this paper how it is possible to successfully perform privacy attacks on counterfactuals by drawing on the attacks developed against synthetic data. More precisely, we investigate the effectiveness of the membership inference attacks designed for synthetic data on various types of counterfactuals. Additionally, while existing membership inference attacks against counterfactuals usually require to be able to query the model, we show how it is possible to perform successful membership inference attacks using only a set of counterfactuals, with no access to the model from which they are generated. Our results demonstrate that model developers should be more cautious when releasing counterfactuals to various users, as it can lead to a privacy breach.

AI.tldr: Counterfactual explanations pose privacy risks: membership inference attacks from synthetic-data literature can successively infer training data membership from counterfactuals, even with no model access.
AI.motivation: Explainability via counterfactuals is valuable in high-stakes ML, but these explanations can leak training data. The paper investigates privacy risks by transferring synthetic-data membership inference attacks to counterfactuals.
AI.method: Adapt and apply membership inference attacks (originally for synthetic data) to various counterfactual types. Demonstrate attacks that succeed using only a set of counterfactuals, with no access to the underlying model.
AI.result: Counterfactuals can leak training-data membership; attacks succeed across counterfactual types and can be performed without model access, revealing a privacy breach risk.
AI.conclusion: Caution is warranted when releasing counterfactuals. Implement privacy-preserving measures (e.g., differential privacy, data minimization, sanitization) to mitigate leakage through explanations.

title: Temporal Preference Concepts and their Functions in a Large Language Model
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being deployed to make decisions that require trading off near-term gains against long-term consequences, yet little is known about how they internally represent or resolve these tradeoffs. In this work, we causally localize an underlying subgraph for temporal preference in a distilled LLM (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507), identifying mid-to-upper-layer nodes through converging evidence from gradient-based attribution and activation patching. We find that the geometry of time horizon is encoded in the residual stream at the expected localized layers. A behavioral analysis reveals that unintervened LLMs discount the future several times less steeply than humans, yet this preference is unstable across contexts, motivating explicit control rather than implicit reliance on training. Finally, we find suggestive evidence that steering vectors can shift temporal preference. Our work demonstrates how mechanistic interpretability can bring us closer to reliable control over how LLMs plan and reason

AI.tldr: Mechanistic interpretability localizes a mid-to-upper-layer subgraph encoding temporal preference in a distilled LLM; future discounting is shallower than humans and contextually unstable, but steering vectors can adjust temporal preference, suggesting paths to reliable control of planning and reasoning.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLMs internally represent near-term vs. long-term tradeoffs, and to enable reliable control over their temporal planning, addressing concerns about misaligned or unstable long-horizon reasoning.
AI.method: Causal localization of temporal-preference subgraph in Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 using gradient-based attribution and activation patching; identify mid-to-upper-layer nodes; analyze residual-stream geometry; conduct behavioral comparisons to human discounting; test steering vectors.
AI.result: Located a subgraph in middle-to-upper layers; time-horizon geometry encoded in the residual stream at the localized layers; LLMs discount the future less steeply than humans; discounting is unstable across contexts; steering vectors can shift temporal preference.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic interpretability enables more reliable control over LLM planning and reasoning by identifying and manipulating the temporal-preference representations.

title: LadderMan: Learning Humanoid Perceptive Ladder Climbing
summary: Humanoid robots hold great promise for operating in human-centered environments, yet ladder climbing remains one of the most challenging tasks due to sparse footholds and handholds, complex whole-body coordination, and sensitivity to perception and control errors. We present \textbf{LadderMan}, a unified system that enables humanoid robots to robustly climb diverse ladders and perform manipulation under such constrained conditions. Our climbing policy is built on a scalable two-stage learning pipeline, where we use hybrid motion tracking to learn multiple climbing experts from a single reference motion, and distill these experts into a unified depth-based visuomotor climbing policy via hybrid imitation and reinforcement learning. To enable real-world deployment, we leverage vision foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap in depth perception. Building on the learned climbing policy, we further train a separate manipulation policy using a dual-agent formulation, allowing stable on-ladder manipulation via teleoperation. Experiments demonstrate that LadderMan achieves robust ladder climbing across a wide range of geometries, successfully transfers to real-world hardware in a zero-shot manner, and supports various manipulation tasks under challenging ladder constraints. Video results are available at https://ladderman-robot.github.io .

AI.tldr: A two-stage learning framework (hybrid motion tracking and policy distillation) enabling robust ladder climbing and on-ladder manipulation for humanoid robots, with sim-to-real depth perception bridging via vision foundation models and a dual-agent manipulation policy, achieving zero-shot real-world transfer across diverse ladders.
AI.motivation: Ladder climbing is exceptionally challenging in human-centered environments due to sparse footholds and handholds, complex whole-body coordination, and sensitivity to perception/control errors. A robust, adaptable system is needed that generalizes across ladder geometries and can operate under real-world sensing.
AI.method: Stage 1: Use hybrid motion tracking to learn multiple climbing experts from a single reference motion. Stage 2: Distill these experts into a unified depth-based visuomotor climbing policy via hybrid imitation and reinforcement learning. Employ vision foundation models to bridge the sim-to-real gap in depth perception. Build a separate manipulation policy on top using a dual-agent formulation for stable on-ladder manipulation via teleoperation.
AI.result: LadderMan demonstrates robust ladder climbing across diverse ladder geometries, transfers to real hardware in a zero-shot manner, and supports on-ladder manipulation tasks under challenging ladder constraints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage learning pipeline, depth-based visuomotor policy, and dual-agent manipulation enable robust, real-world ladder climbing and manipulation for humanoid robots, with effective sim-to-real transfer and broad generalization across ladder geometries.

title: Bridging Domain Expertise and Generalization for Performance Estimation
summary: Performance estimation under distribution shift aims to predict how a model behaves on an unlabeled test set whose distribution differs from the training data, a scenario that requires reliable indicators that can faithfully reflect model behavior without ground-truth labels.
