Existing approaches rely solely on the outputs of the given model whose biases are amplified once the distribution shifts, weakening the correlation with the true performance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose Fused Reference Alignment Prediction (FRAP), which leverages the complementary strengths of an external foundation model and the base model to construct a more reliable surrogate of the ground-truth labels. FRAP aligns the prediction distribution of the foundation model with that of the base model by applying temperature-scaled calibration that minimizes their divergence. The aligned predictions are fused through confidence-based weighting into a refined reference distribution that integrates robustness from the foundation model and domain-specific expertise from the base model, and performance estimation is obtained by measuring how closely the base model predictions agree with this reference. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and architectures show that FRAP provides consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: FRAP improves performance estimation under distribution shift by fusing an external foundation model with the base model. It calibrates the foundation-model predictions to align with the base model, then combines them via confidence-based weighting to create a refined reference distribution, against which base-model predictions are compared to estimate performance. Empirical results show consistent improvements over baseline estimation methods across datasets and architectures.
AI.motivation: Existing distribution-shift performance estimators rely solely on the base model outputs, which can be biased and misrepresent true performance when distributions shift. There is a need for reliable indicators that do not require ground-truth labels on the shifted test set.
AI.method: 1) Use a foundation model to generate predictions on the unlabeled test distribution. 2) Apply temperature-scaled calibration to align the foundation-model prediction distribution with the base model's distribution by minimizing divergence between the two. 3) Fuse aligned predictions with the base-model predictions using confidence-based weighting to form a refined reference distribution that combines robustness from the foundation model with domain-specific knowledge from the base model. 4) Estimate performance by measuring agreement between the base-model predictions and the refined reference distribution.
AI.result: FRAP yields consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods under distribution shift across diverse datasets and architectures.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging complementary signals from a foundation model and a base model, FRAP provides a robust, calibrated, and fused reference to more accurately estimate model performance under distribution shift, reducing reliance on ground-truth labels on the shifted test set.

title: Gradient Descent with Large Step Size Restores Symmetry in Deep Linear Networks with Multi-Pathway
summary: Recent analyses of multi-pathway Deep Linear Networks use Gradient Flow to predict a "winner-takes-all" specialization in which path symmetry breaks and each feature concentrates in a single pathway. In this work, we show that discrete Gradient Descent (GD) with a large step size tells a different story. We prove that single-path solutions are sharp minima, whereas distributing signals across pathways reduces sharpness by a factor that decreases with both the number of pathways and depth. Consequently, while early training reproduces the depth-driven symmetry breaking predicted by GF, oscillations at the Edge of Stability subsequently override this tendency and drive the network into a re-balancing phase, where signals redistribute across pathways. Together, these results clarify how depth shapes pathway competition and explain why large-step GD favors shared representations rather than persistent single-pathway dominance.

AI.tldr: Large-step discrete gradient descent on multi-pathway deep linear networks promotes shared representations; depth and pathway count reduce sharpness and overturn gradient-flow predictions through Edge-of-Stability dynamics, yielding a re-balancing phase where signals distribute across pathways.
AI.motivation: Reconcile gradient-flow predictions of winner-takes-all specialization with discrete GD dynamics, and understand how step size and depth influence pathway competition and solution sharpness.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of discrete gradient descent on multi-pathway deep linear networks; proofs that single-path minima are sharp while distributing across pathways reduces sharpness; characterization of dynamic phases including early depth-driven symmetry breaking, oscillations at the Edge of Stability, and subsequent re-balancing.
AI.result: Single-path solutions are sharp minima; spreading signals across multiple pathways lowers sharpness by a factor depending on the number of pathways and the network depth. Early training follows depth-driven symmetry breaking predicted by gradient flow, but Edge-of-Stability oscillations induce a re-balancing phase that redistributes signals across pathways. Overall, large-step GD favors shared representations over persistent single-pathway dominance.
AI.conclusion: Depth shapes pathway competition and, under large-step GD, the network tends toward shared representations rather than persistent single-pathway dominance; discrete dynamics (EOSt) critically alter long-run pathway allocation beyond gradient-flow predictions.

title: Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation
summary: Probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables requires beliefs that respect $SE(3)$ symmetry, yet existing equivariant networks produce only scalars and vectors -- not the rank-2 precision tensors needed for anisotropic uncertainty, and single-component messages collapse multi-modal energy landscapes to physically meaningless averages. We introduce Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP), a factor-graph framework whose messages are equivariant Gaussian mixture models with sufficient statistics that transform exactly under $SE(3)$. Rank-2 precision matrices are synthesised via equivariant outer products, ingested through differentiable spectral decomposition, and kept tractable by a greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with $SE(3)$. On GEOM-QM9 and GEOM-Drugs, ENBP achieves 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 $\mathring{A}$ error with sub-second latency -- over $100\times$ faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. On multi-body robotic inference, vanilla loopy BP diverges at 15+ agents while ENBP converges with near-zero collision rates and machine-precision equivariance error (${\sim}10^{-7}$ vs.\ $10^{-1}$ for augmented baselines).

AI.tldr: ENBP extends belief propagation to SE(3) equivariant messages using Gaussian mixture models with rank-2 precision constructed via equivariant outer products, enabling principled anisotropic uncertainty and multi-modal inference with strong empirical performance and stability.
AI.motivation: Probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables must respect SE(3) symmetry. Existing equivariant networks typically yield only scalars or vectors, failing to capture anisotropic uncertainty and multi-modal landscapes.
AI.method: Propose Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP): a factor-graph framework where messages are equivariant Gaussian mixture models whose statistics transform under SE(3). Rank-2 precision matrices are built via equivariant outer products and learned through a differentiable spectral decomposition, with a greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with SE(3).
AI.result: On GEOM-QM9 and GEOM-Drugs: 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 Å error with sub-second latency, over 100x faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. On multi-body robotics: ENBP converges with near-zero collision rates and machine-precision SE(3) equivariance error (~1e−7), while vanilla loopy BP diverges with 15+ agents.
AI.conclusion: ENBP provides SE(3)-equivariant probabilistic inference with expressive, physically meaningful uncertainty representations (rank-2 tensors) and robust convergence, delivering substantial speedups and accuracy gains over diffusion and augmented baselines in geometry-rich tasks.

title: Differentiable Efficient Operator Search
summary: Efficient multimodal foundation models often rely on manually designed token-reduction operators, such as pruning, merging, pooling, and adaptive reweighting. Although these operators appear different, we show that they can be interpreted as distinct regimes of a shared operator space. Based on this view, we introduce Efficient Operator Search, a differentiable framework that jointly searches where to reduce tokens, how many tokens to retain, and how reduced token information should be processed. The proposed search space parameterizes layer activation, retention budget, and operator behavior, while the search policy optimizes task performance under one-sided budget and cost constraints. This formulation recovers representative hand-designed baselines as special cases and further discovers hybrid operators beyond isolated manual designs. Experiments on multimodal benchmarks show that the searched operators achieve competitive accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, especially under aggressive visual-token reduction. These results suggest that efficient multimodal inference can be reframed from manual operator design to differentiable operator search.

AI.tldr: A differentiable framework to search and optimize token-reduction operators for multimodal models, unifying pruning, merging, pooling, and reweighting under a shared operator space.
AI.motivation: Manual design of token-reduction operators is ad-hoc and may limit efficiency; a unified search can discover better hybrids and automate efficient inference.
AI.method: Defines a differentiable search space over layer activation, token retention budgets, and operator behavior; uses a search policy that optimizes task performance under one-sided budget and cost constraints; recovers baseline manual operators as special cases; discovers new hybrid operators.
AI.result: On multimodal benchmarks, the learned operators achieve competitive accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, notably under aggressive visual-token reduction; demonstrates promise of differentiable operator search for efficient multimodal inference.
AI.conclusion: Shifts the design paradigm from hand-crafted operators to differentiable search in the operator space, enabling efficient and flexible inference.

title: AffordanceVLA: A Vision-Language-Action Model Empowering Action Generation through Affordance-Aware Understanding
comment: Preprint. Code and project page are available. Code: https://github.com/Skywalker-yqz/AffordanceVLA Project page: https://skywalker-yqz.github.io/AffordanceVLA/
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models leverage the rich world knowledge of pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to enable instruction-following robotic manipulation. However, the structural mismatch between VLM semantic spaces and embodied control policies often hinders the learning of precise perception--action mappings. To address this challenge, we propose \textbf{AffordanceVLA}, a unified framework that introduces structured affordance forecasting as a task-oriented intermediate representation to establish a more precise and robust perception--action mapping. Specifically, we progressively model manipulation priors through three complementary components: 1) \textbf{Which2Act} for object-centric grounding via visual latent prediction to suppress distractions; 2) \textbf{Where2Act} for 2D interaction localization via affordance map estimation; and 3) \textbf{How2Act} for 3D geometric reasoning to guide manipulation policies. These affordance cues provide spatially grounded, semantically conditioned, and action-coupled intermediate representations, thereby naturally bridging vision, language and action. We integrate these modules into a Mixture-of-Transformer (MoT) architecture with specialized experts and train the model using a three-stage training strategy with a progressive data curriculum. To overcome the scarcity of dense affordance labels in robotic datasets, we also develop a robust automated data augmentation pipeline. Extensive experiments on simulation and real-world demonstrate that AffordanceVLA achieves strong performance across diverse manipulation scenarios.

AI.tldr: AffordanceVLA introduces a structured affordance forecasting pipeline (Which2Act, Where2Act, How2Act) within a Mixture-of-Transformer to align vision-language models with embodied control, aided by curriculum training and automated data augmentation, achieving strong manipulation performance in simulation and real-world.
AI.motivation: There is a structural mismatch between pretrained vision-language model semantics and embodied control policies, which hampers learning precise perception–action mappings in instruction-following robotics. An intermediate, task-oriented representation of affordances can ground semantics into actionable cues and improve robustness and accuracy.
AI.method: Three complementary components: (1) Which2Act for object-centric grounding via visual latent prediction to suppress distractions; (2) Where2Act for 2D interaction localization via affordance map estimation; (3) How2Act for 3D geometric reasoning to guide manipulation policies. These are integrated in a Mixture-of-Transformer with specialized experts. Training uses a three-stage curriculum and an automated data augmentation pipeline to address scarce dense affordance labels.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world show strong performance across diverse manipulation scenarios, validating the efficacy of the affordance-based intermediate representations and the MoT architecture.
AI.conclusion: Affordance cues yield spatially grounded, semantically conditioned, and action-coupled representations that bridge vision, language, and action, improving perception–action mappings for VLA robots and enabling robust manipulation under varied conditions.

title: Performance Evaluation of GraphCast for Medium-Range Weather Forecasting over Brazil
summary: The paradigm of global weather forecasting is rapidly shifting with the emergence of Machine Learning Weather Prediction models (MLWP). While these data-driven architectures demonstrate remarkable global skill, regional benchmarks in the Global South remain scarce, leaving their efficacy in complex, highly convective environments largely unverified. This study evaluates the performance of GraphCast operational against the deterministic ECMWF IFS HRES as baseline across four distinct Brazilian climatic sub-regions. Utilizing a scalable, cloud-native pipeline and the WeatherBench-X framework for benchmarking weather models, we assess selected tropospheric variables ($T_{850}$, $Q_{850}$, $Z_{500}$) over four selected seasonal windows, employing the operational IFS analysis as the ground truth to calculate the statistical metrics for both models. Results reveal a regime-dependent skill profile. During the austral winter, GraphCast underperforms in the medium range (lead days 2-7) for $Z_{500}$ when resolving fast-propagating baroclinic systems over southern Brazil, but regains an advantage in the extended range, where its inherent smoothing of chaotic small-scale variability becomes beneficial under deterministic skill metrics. Conversely, during the austral summer wet season, GraphCast accurately captures large-scale moisture transport while intrinsically dampening the high-frequency convective variability that degrades deterministic NWP temperature forecasts. These findings establish a baseline for Brazil and define the specific physical boundaries that will guide future ``tropicalization'' efforts, aiming to optimize these foundational AI models for regional resilience.

AI.tldr: GraphCast shows regime-dependent skill relative to ECMWF IFS HRES across four Brazilian sub-regions: it underperforms in mid-range (days 2–7) for Z500 during austral winter due to fast-propagating baroclinic systems, but gains advantage at longer leads; in the austral summer, it preserves large-scale moisture transport while dampening high-frequency convective noise, improving forecast stability.
AI.motivation: Evaluate regional performance of ML weather prediction (MLWP) models in the Global South, specifically Brazil, to establish benchmarks and inform model adaptations for tropical/subtropical regions.
AI.method: Comparative evaluation of GraphCast operational forecasts against deterministic ECMWF IFS HRES baselines across four Brazilian climatic sub-regions. Variables: T850, Q850, Z500. Time frames: four seasonal windows. Ground truth: IFS analysis. Tools: scalable cloud-native pipeline and WeatherBench-X framework, with standard statistical metrics computed for both models.
AI.result: The study reveals regime-dependent skill: during austral winter, GraphCast underperforms in the 2–7 day window for Z500 over southern Brazil due to fast-moving baroclinic systems, but exhibits an advantage at extended ranges where smoothing of chaotic small-scale variability enhances deterministic skill. In the austral summer wet season, GraphCast captures large-scale moisture transport and intrinsically dampens high-frequency convective variability that degrades deterministic temperature forecasts.
AI.conclusion: Provides a Brazil-specific baseline and delineates the physical boundaries for tropicalization efforts, guiding future adaptation of foundational AI models to improve regional resilience.

title: ActiveMimic: Egocentric Video Pretraining with Active Perception
summary: Egocentric human video offers a scalable alternative to robot data for pretraining, yet models pretrained on such video consistently underperform those pretrained on robot data. We attribute this gap to a missing signal, the active perception behavior in egocentric videos, where humans continuously reposition their viewpoint during manipulation, inducing camera motion that standard pipelines treat as noise. To address this, we present ActiveMimic, a pretraining framework that recovers synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn RGB camera, models camera motion as a viewpoint action, and jointly learns active perception and manipulation from in-the-wild egocentric human video before adapting to a target robot. Empirically, real-world experiments across tasks with diverse active perception demands show that ActiveMimic consistently surpasses baselines pretrained on human video and matches state-of-the-art models pretrained on robot data. Further analysis provides evidence that active perception capability originates from egocentric human video pretraining rather than robot-specific fine-tuning, confirming active perception as the key to unlocking egocentric human video for robot pretraining.

AI.tldr: ActiveMimic enables effective pretraining on egocentric human video by recovering synchronized camera and wrist trajectories and treating camera motion as a viewpoint action, yielding active perception capabilities that close the gap with robot-data pretraining.
AI.motivation: Egocentric videos lack the active perception signal; models pretrained on such video underperform versus robot data; need to exploit camera motion as informative signal.
AI.method: Proposes ActiveMimic: pretrain on in-the-wild egocentric video; recover synchronized camera and wrist trajectories from a single body-worn camera; model camera motion as viewpoint action; jointly learn active perception and manipulation; then adapt to a target robot.
AI.result: Empirically, in real-world tasks with diverse active perception demands, ActiveMimic surpasses baselines trained on human video and matches state-of-the-art robot-data pretrained models.
AI.conclusion: Active perception signals originate from egocentric pretraining; active perception is the key to unlocking egocentric human video for robot pretraining, without robot-specific fine-tuning.

title: Maximising the Set-Piece Return: Optimising Football Corner Tactics with Graph Reinforcement Learning
summary: Machine learning is increasingly employed for the evaluation of football tactics. However, existing approaches focus on characterising historical actions or analyst-specified counterfactual scenarios. In this work, we seek to go beyond the imitation of historically observed patterns towards discovering new generalisable player configurations and strategies. To tackle this, we focus on optimising corner kick routines, and formulate a decision-making problem in which a central policy makes adjustments to attacking player positions and velocities to maximise first contact shot probability. Unlike classic optimisation that solves for isolated setups, we contribute a reinforcement learning architecture operating on graph-structured data that yields a general policy for adjusting arbitrary starting player positions. Evaluated on over 3,000 Premier League corners, our approach strongly outperforms baseline optimisation techniques under matched inference budgets. Our results suggest that graph reinforcement learning can shift set-piece analysis from historical evaluation and imitation towards reward-driven tactical discovery.

AI.tldr: Graph-based reinforcement learning is proposed to optimize corner-kick tactics for generalizable, reward-driven discovery, outperforming baseline optimization on Premier League data.
AI.motivation: Move beyond imitation of historical actions toward discovering novel, generalizable set-piece configurations that improve first-contact shot probability.
AI.method: A graph-structured reinforcement learning architecture with a central policy that adjusts attacking player positions and velocities; applicable to arbitrary starting configurations; trained to maximize first-contact shot probability; evaluated on 3,000+ Premier League corners.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms baseline optimization under matched inference budgets, indicating effective generalization and reward-driven tactical discovery.
AI.conclusion: Graph reinforcement learning can shift set-piece analysis from historical evaluation toward reward-driven tactical discovery, enabling generalizable strategies for attacking routines.

title: Policy-Conditioned Counterfactual Credit for Verifiable Reinforcement Learning of Long-Horizon Language Agents
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards improves reasoning and tool use, yet long-horizon language agents still learn unsupported evidence chains, belief drift, and shortcut actions that satisfy terminal checks. Existing process rewards are mostly correlational: they reward retrieval-, reflection-, or verification-like steps without estimating whether the step contributes to final verified success under a specified intervention. We propose CVT-RL, a constrained policy-gradient algorithm with dense verifiable rewards, intervention-validity gating, and a policy-conditioned counterfactual contribution (PCCC) estimator. Deletion, semantic substitution, evidence substitution, and tool-output perturbation define separate controlled interventions; continuations are sampled from a frozen reference policy, and a selection-adjusted doubly robust estimator augments the advantage. Belief control uses only prefix-observable labels, while an augmented Lagrangian constrains unsupported claims, skipped verification, tool tampering, and unsafe calls. On long-context QA, ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, and web/tool tasks, CVT-RL improves average task success from 71.8% for compute-matched non-causal RL and 75.4% for an information-matched counterfactual-process baseline to 78.9%, improves evidence F1 from 78.9 to 82.8 over the information-matched baseline, and reduces measured hacking from 7.2% to 3.9%. Independent human audit estimates 4.6% hacking for CVT-RL versus 8.1% for the information-matched baseline, and adaptive detector-evasion attacks raise hacking only to 7.1%. Stratified bootstrap and mixed-effects tests give p<0.01 after Holm correction for all primary metrics. Carefully scoped counterfactual credit, paired with validity gating, diagnostics, and verifiable constraints, provides a reproducible route toward more reliable long-horizon RL for language agents.

AI.tldr: CVT-RL introduces a constrained policy-gradient framework with dense verifiable rewards and counterfactual credit estimation to align long-horizon RL for language agents with verifiable success, reducing hacking and improving evidence quality.
AI.motivation: Standard process rewards in long-horizon language agents are mostly correlational and do not ensure that intermediate steps causally contribute to verified success under specified interventions. This leads to unsupported evidence chains, belief drift, and shortcut actions that pass terminal checks.
AI.method: CVT-RL uses dense verifiable rewards, intervention-validity gating, and a policy-conditioned counterfactual contribution (PCCC) estimator. It defines controlled interventions (deletion, semantic substitution, evidence substitution, tool-output perturbation), samples continuations from a frozen reference policy, and applies a selection-adjusted doubly robust estimator for advantages. Belief control uses only prefix-observable labels; an augmented Lagrangian constrains unsupported claims and unsafe actions.
AI.result: On long-context QA benchmarks (ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, web/tool tasks), CVT-RL raises average task success to 78.9% (from 71.8% and 75.4% baselines), improves evidence F1 to 82.8, and reduces hacking to 3.9% (vs 7.2% baseline). Independent human audits estimate hacking at 4.6% (CVT-RL) vs 8.1% (baseline). Adaptive detector-evasion attacks raise hacking to 7.1%. Stratified bootstrap and mixed-effects tests show p<0.01 for all primary metrics.
AI.conclusion: Carefully scoped counterfactual credit with validity gating and verifiable constraints yields a reproducible path toward more reliable long-horizon RL for language agents, reducing vulnerability to manipulation while improving success and evidence quality.

title: End-to-End Subgraph Detection with GraphDETR
summary: Subgraph detection seeks to identify whether and where instances of query patterns occur within a larger graph. This problem is fundamental across scientific domains and is closely related to subgraph isomorphism, which is NP-complete, limiting combinatorial approaches to small patterns or moderately sized graphs. We introduce GraphDETR, a deep learning framework that formulates subgraph detection as a set prediction problem, analogous to DETR in object detection. GraphDETR encodes the target graph with a graph neural network, and employs a fixed set of learnable query vectors, decoded via a transformer decoder, to predict all pattern occurrences jointly in a single forward pass. This is enabled by training the model end-to-end with bipartite matching. Unlike traditional combinatorial methods that only solve exact structural matching, GraphDETR naturally extends to approximate matching, enabling detection beyond exact pattern correspondence. Empirically, we show that GraphDETR can detect diverse patterns, such as molecular structures, cycles, cliques, and fuzzy patterns of up to 50 nodes, in target graphs with up to 1000 nodes. We further evaluate on molecular functional group detection over the ChEMBL dataset, where GraphDETR predicts the complete set of functional groups per molecule, achieving a strong performance of $\text{AP}_{100} = 91.2$.

AI.tldr: GraphDETR reframes subgraph detection as a set-prediction task using a fixed set of learnable queries decoded by a transformer, enabling end-to-end training with bipartite matching and approximate matching for detecting diverse patterns in large graphs; achieves strong molecular results (AP_100=91.2 on ChEMBL).
AI.motivation: Subgraph detection is NP-hard (related to subgraph isomorphism), making combinatorial methods impractical for larger patterns or graphs. A learnable, end-to-end approach that can handle approximate matches and scale to sizable graphs is desirable.
AI.method: Encode target graph with a graph neural network, attend to a fixed set of learnable query vectors via a transformer decoder to predict all pattern occurrences jointly in a single forward pass; trained end-to-end with bipartite matching losses to align predictions with ground truth, enabling both exact and approximate matching.
AI.result: Demonstrates detection of diverse patterns (molecular structures, cycles, cliques, fuzzy patterns) up to 50 nodes in graphs up to 1000 nodes; on ChEMBL molecular functional group detection, achieves AP_100 = 91.2, indicating strong performance in recovering complete sets of functional groups per molecule.
AI.conclusion: GraphDETR offers a scalable, end-to-end framework for subgraph detection that extends beyond exact matching, with strong empirical results on chemistry data and applicability to various pattern types.

title: Personal AI Agent for Camera Roll VQA
comment: Project page, code, and demo: https://thaoshibe.github.io/camroll
summary: We study the personal camera roll visual question answering setting. In this setting, a conversational AI assistant can access a user's personal camera roll and retrieve relevant photos to answer queries, ranging from simple factual questions (e.g., ``Name of the food I tried yesterday?'') to more open-ended ones (e.g., ``Recommend some dishes I have never eaten before''). Given the vast nature of the personal camera roll (i.e., multiple years, hundreds to thousands of photos), a successful AI assistant needs to understand a long-horizon, highly personalized visual content stream in order to navigate and locate the correct and/or relevant information. To support this, we collect and manually annotate questions that mimic real-world usage. The final dataset, camroll, contains 50 users, 31,476 images, and 2,500 QA pairs. We further design camroll-agent, a conversational AI agent equipped with hierarchical memory and a minimal set of tools for efficient navigation over large, personalized visual memory. Experimental results show that camroll-agent outperforms numerous baselines and methods for long-context understanding AI agents system. Together, the camroll dataset and camroll-agent highlight the gap in AI agents' long-context reasoning: personalized visual memory requires different approaches from standard long-context textual memory, especially when consistency, visual details, and user-specific context are present.

AI.tldr: Introduces the camroll dataset and camroll-agent for personal camera-roll QA via conversation; demonstrates the need for long-horizon, personalized visual memory and shows the agent surpasses baselines.
AI.motivation: To enable a conversational assistant to access and reason over a long, private photo collection to answer user queries, including open-ended ones, with attention to user-specific context and visual detail.
AI.method: Construct camroll dataset (50 users, 31,476 images, 2,500 QA pairs) and design camroll-agent with hierarchical memory and a minimal toolset to efficiently navigate large, personalized visual memory; perform experiments against baselines for long-context reasoning in AI agents.
AI.result: camroll-agent outperforms numerous baselines; demonstrates effectiveness of memory hierarchy and efficient navigation for personalized visual content; highlights that personalized visual memory requires approaches different from standard long-context textual memory.
AI.conclusion: Personalized visual memory demands specialized long-context reasoning; the dataset and agent reveal gaps in current AI; future work includes improving retrieval efficiency, privacy-preserving methods, and modeling user-specific context in memory.

title: Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator for HEC-RAS 2D Water-Surface Elevation Prediction
comment: Preprint manuscript prepared using IEEEtran journal format
summary: This article presents a cross-dataset evaluation of learned native-cell surrogate models for solver-consistent water-surface elevation (WSE) prediction in HEC-RAS 2D. To avoid raster remapping error and information-access confounding, surrogates are evaluated directly on the original nonuniform computational cells under an explicit policy that separates static project inputs, current hydraulic state, project-input forcing, calibration-derived quantities, and future solver-output targets. We introduce the Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator (LRFIO), a no-forcing, increment-based learned surrogate that calibrates an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories and deploys the retained operator through closed-form native-cell rollout. LRFIO evaluates a base-case-first response hierarchy consisting of persistence, global calibrated inertia, and segmented response-field inertia. Segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia are treated as learnable modeling choices, with added complexity retained only when validation evidence justifies its cost. Evaluated across four diverse HEC-RAS 2D benchmarks, LRFIO retains different response structures for different domains, demonstrating adaptive learned complexity. The selector audit shows controlled complexity with a maximum validation regret of 4.30%. During deployment, retained rollout times range from 0.003 s to 0.242 s, and the Beaver Bayou measured-solve comparison gives an estimated 2.75 x 10^4 horizon-normalized speedup over HEC-RAS. These results indicate that the current native-cell increment is a strong solver-conditioned predictive scaffold and that added response-field, neural, or spatial complexity should be retained only when empirically justified.

AI.tldr: Cross-dataset evaluation of native-cell surrogate models for solver-consistent water-surface elevation prediction in HEC-RAS 2D using an inertial, increment-based operator (LRFIO) with adaptive complexity and significant speedups.
AI.motivation: Enable fast, physics-aware WSE predictions directly on nonuniform cells across diverse domains without raster remapping errors, while controlling model complexity via empirical validation.
AI.method: Introduce LRFIO, a no-forcing, increment-based surrogate that learns an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories and applies it through closed-form native-cell rollout. Build a base response hierarchy (persistence, global inertia, segmented inertia); treat segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia as optional, learnable components. Evaluate across four diverse HEC-RAS 2D benchmarks and perform a selector audit to manage complexity.
AI.result: LRFIO adapts its response structure by domain, achieving a maximum validation regret of 4.30%. Deployment rollouts range from 0.003 s to 0.242 s. Estimated horizon-normalized speedup over HEC-RAS is ~2.75×10^4 using Beaver Bayou data. Current native-cell increment acts as a strong predictive scaffold; additional response-field, neural, or spatial complexity is retained only with empirical justification.
AI.conclusion: The native-cell increment is an effective, solver-conditioned predictive scaffold. Adaptive inclusion of extra response features should be data-justified, yielding solver-consistent, fast surrogates with domain-adaptive complexity.

title: In-Context Multiple Instance Learning
summary: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) addresses problems where supervision is available at the level of bags of instances and has been successfully applied in fields ranging from computational pathology to satellite imagery. Nevertheless, existing algorithms struggle in the low-label regime that characterizes many real-world applications. Flexible models overfit and rigid ones fail to adapt to the task at hand. We show that pretraining an in-context learner with a Perceiver-style architecture on synthetic data yields a model that can solve new tasks from a handful of labeled bags. At inference time, classification happens in a single forward pass and requires no gradient updates. We propose and investigate different synthetic data generators for bag-structured data and find that they capture complementary inductive biases. A model pretrained on a mixture of these generators inherits their per-task strengths and achieves the best average performance across twelve MIL benchmarks, outperforming supervised baselines that require task-specific training.

AI.tldr: A Perceiver-style in-context learner pretrained on synthetic bag data enables few-shot MIL with a single forward pass, beating task-specific supervised baselines across 12 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MIL often operates in low-label settings; flexible models overfit while rigid ones fail to adapt. Pretraining on synthetic bag-structured data can imbue the model with transferable inductive biases that generalize to new bag-level tasks.
AI.method: Pretrain an in-context Perceiver-style model on synthetic bag data generated by multiple generators that encode complementary inductive biases. Use a mixture of generators so the model inherits diverse per-task strengths. At inference, perform a single forward pass with no gradient updates to classify new bags.
AI.result: A model pretrained on the mixture of synthetic generators achieves the best average performance across twelve MIL benchmarks, outperforming supervised baselines that require task-specific training.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic-data pretraining with diverse bag generators yields a task-agnostic MIL model capable of solving new tasks from few labeled bags with efficient one-pass inference.

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Right-Censored Survival Data
summary: Proper scoring rules provide a rigorous theoretical basis for the training and evaluation of probabilistic forecasts. However, in the presence of right censoring, the event time is only partially observed, rendering conventional scoring rules inapplicable in their standard form. We propose a framework for proper scoring of right-censored survival outcomes based on a simple idea: first, map the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism, then apply the underlying proper score on the induced observed-data law. This yields localized scores for fixed censoring times and marginalized scores when the censoring time is random or only partially observed. The resulting construction recovers familiar right-censored likelihood and IPCW-type criteria within a coherent framework, while also yielding right-censored versions of the CRPS, pinball loss, Brier score, and energy score. We show that the marginalized score is proper under conditional independent censoring and strictly proper on the identifiable region. The same principle also leads to censored engression, a sample-based learning objective for multivariate right-censored survival modeling. In experiments, our scores correctly rank the oracle forecast across several censoring regimes, whereas forecast-dependent plug-in weighted scores can exhibit ranking reversals. Censored engression likewise substantially improves over naive training on censored outcomes.

AI.tldr: A general framework for proper scoring of right-censored survival forecasts, via mapping through the censoring mechanism to derive localized and marginalized scores, recovering standard criteria and enabling censored engression as a learning objective.
AI.motivation: Proper scoring is difficult under right censoring; need coherent, statistically valid scores that respect censoring to evaluate and train probabilistic survival forecasts.
AI.method: Map the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism to obtain the induced observed-data law. Define localized scores at fixed censoring times and marginalized scores when censoring time is random or partially observed. Show that familiar criteria (likelihood, IPCW) arise within this framework; derive right-censored versions of CRPS, pinball loss, Brier score, and energy score. Establish propriety of the marginalized score under conditional independent censoring and strict propriety on the identifiable region. Introduce censored engression as a sample-based objective for multivariate right-censored survival modeling.
AI.result: The framework yields right-censored analogues of standard scoring rules and a theoretical guarantee of propriety for the marginalized score under stated assumptions. Empirically, the marginalized score ranks oracle forecasts correctly across censoring regimes; naive plug-in weighted scores can fail by ranking reversals. Censored engression improves training over naive methods.
AI.conclusion: A coherent, unified approach to scoring and training under right-censoring, reconciling likelihood/IPCW with classic scores, and enabling multivariate survival modeling via censored engression. Provides practitioners with principled tools for evaluation and learning in censored survival settings.

title: Agentic Monte Carlo: Simulating Reinforcement Learning for Black-Box Agents
summary: LLM agents operate in two distinct regimes: open-weight agents amenable to reinforcement learning (RL) and black-box agents whose behaviour must be controlled purely at test time. Although black-box agents are often backed by state-of-the-art proprietary LLMs, API-only access precludes parameter-level optimization, rendering most RL methods inapplicable. To address this limitation, we turn to a known equivalence between RL and Bayesian inference. We propose Agentic Monte Carlo (AMC) to directly sample from the optimal policy of a black-box agent rather than training it through RL. The optimal policy is a posterior over trajectories whose prior we define as the fixed black-box LLM agent. We employ Sequential Monte Carlo to sample from this posterior by learning a value function to steer the agent while leaving the underlying black-box model unchanged. We validate AMC on three diverse environments from the AgentGym benchmark, demonstrating significant improvements over prompting baselines and even outperforming Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) as we scale the test-time compute of our method. AMC demonstrates the feasibility of performing principled RL-style optimization of black-box LLM agents. Code is available at https://github.com/layer6ai-labs/Agentic-Monte-Carlo

AI.tldr: Agentic Monte Carlo (AMC) enables RL-like optimization of black-box LLM agents by sampling from the posterior over trajectories (defined with a fixed LLM as prior) via Sequential Monte Carlo, guided by a learned value function, without modifying the underlying model; yields significant gains over prompting baselines and competitive results with GRPO on AgentGym.
AI.motivation: Black-box LLMs offered via API lack parameter access for traditional RL; there is a need for principled optimization of such agents at test time without retraining or fine-tuning.
AI.method: Define the prior over trajectories using the fixed black-box LLM; formulate the optimal policy as a posterior over trajectories; apply Sequential Monte Carlo to sample trajectories from this posterior; learn a value function to steer sampling, enabling policy improvement without changing the LLM; evaluate on three environments from the AgentGym benchmark and compare to prompting baselines and GRPO.
AI.result: AMC achieves significant improvements over prompting baselines and, with increased test-time compute, can outperform Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) on AgentGym tasks; demonstrates feasibility of RL-style optimization for black-box LLM agents.
AI.conclusion: It is feasible to perform principled, RL-style optimization of black-box LLM agents without parameter updates to the LLM itself, by casting the problem as Bayesian inference and using AMC with SMC and a learned value function; code is available.

title: The Post-GCN Decade Revisited: Curvature-Stratified Evaluation of Relational Learning
summary: Current evaluation practices in relational learning rely heavily on flat leaderboards that average performance across heterogeneous datasets, implicitly assuming a uniform underlying structure. We show that this assumption introduces systematic bias: it obscures geometry-dependent performance variations and can lead to misleading conclusions about model generalization. In this work, we identify intrinsic geometry as a key latent factor governing model effectiveness. We demonstrate that conventional aggregated metrics mask critical performance trade-offs that only become visible when datasets are stratified by their geometric properties. To address this issue, we introduce a curvature-stratified evaluation framework that partitions datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. Our benchmark evaluates 18 representative models including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), Graph Foundation Models (GFMs), and tabular learning methods across 14 datasets. We find that model rankings are highly stable within each curvature regime but shift significantly across regimes, indicating that performance is fundamentally geometry-dependent rather than universally transferable. Notably, we identify regimes where GFMs offer diminishing returns compared to geometry-aligned GNNs. Based on these findings, we propose a geometry-aware evaluation protocol that yields more reliable and interpretable comparisons than standard aggregated benchmarks. We release all code, curvature-stratified dataset splits, and evaluation tools to support reproducible and rigorous assessment of future relational learning methods. Code and datasets are provided in our project homepage: https://sirbabbage.github.io/CurvBench_HOME/.

AI.tldr: Aggregated performance on heterogeneous relational datasets masks geometry-dependent differences. A curvature-stratified evaluation framework reveals that model rankings vary by dataset geometry, not universally transferable; GFMs are not always superior to geometry-aligned GNNs. A geometry-aware protocol and releases are provided.
AI.motivation: Standard leaderboards average across datasets with diverse intrinsic geometries, biasing conclusions about generalization. Intrinsic geometry is a latent factor shaping model effectiveness.
AI.method: Introduce curvature-stratified evaluation by partitioning datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. Evaluate 18 models (GCNs, GFMs, and tabular methods) across 14 datasets. Analyze regime-specific rankings and geometry-aligned performance.
AI.result: Rankings are stable within curvature regimes but shift across regimes. GFMs show diminishing returns in certain geometry-aligned contexts. A geometry-aware evaluation protocol yields more reliable, interpretable comparisons. Curated curvature-stratified splits and code are released.
AI.conclusion: Geometry is a key latent factor in relational learning performance. Aggregated benchmarks obscure trade-offs; geometry-aware evaluation provides more valid comparisons and guidance for method selection.

title: Statistically Reliable LLM-Based Ranking Evaluation via Prediction-Powered Inference
summary: With PRECISE, we extended Prediction-Powered Inference to produce bias-corrected estimates of ranking evaluation metrics by combining a small human-labeled set with a large LLM-judged set. PPI is provably unbiased regardless of the LLM judge's error profile. We make it applicable to hierarchical metrics like Precision@K, where annotations are per-document but the metric is per-query, by reducing the output-space computation from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K). On the ESCI benchmark, augmenting 30 human annotations with Claude 3 Sonnet judgments reduces the standard error of Precision@4 estimates from 4.45 to 3.50 (a 21% relative reduction). In a production system, our framework correctly identified the best of three system variants from 100 human labels and 2 hours of domain-expert annotation; A/B testing confirmed this ranking with +407 bps in daily sales.

AI.tldr: PRECISE extends Prediction-Powered Inference to obtain bias-corrected, unbiased estimates of ranking metrics by combining a small human-labeled set with a large LLM-judged set; it scales to hierarchical metrics and yields practical improvements in accuracy and real-world performance.
AI.motivation: To obtain unbiased, variance-reduced estimates of ranking metrics (like Precision@K) in the presence of biased or imperfect LLM judgments, while keeping labeling costs low.
AI.method: Introduce PPI (Prediction-Powered Inference) which is provably unbiased regardless of the LLM's error profile. Apply to hierarchical metrics by reducing output-space from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K). Use a small human-labeled subset combined with large LLM judgments to estimate bias-corrected metrics.
AI.result: On ESCI: using 30 human annotations plus Claude 3 Sonnet judgments reduces the standard error of Precision@4 from 4.45 to 3.50 (21% improvement). In production: correctly identified the best among three system variants using 100 human labels and 2 hours of domain-expert annotation; A/B testing showed +407 bps daily sales for the chosen variant.
AI.conclusion: The PRECISE/PPI framework provides unbiased, scalable, and data-efficient estimates for ranking evaluation, enabling reliable decision-making with smaller human labeling and leveraging LLM judgments without bias propagation.

title: Double Preconditioning (DoPr): Optimization for Test-Time Performance, not Validation Loss
summary: Many modern applications of deep learning involve training a neural network via a one-step prediction loss (e.g., $L^2$ regression, cross-entropy), but deploy the network by rolling out along its own predictions. Key examples include autoregressive language modeling, flow-based generative modeling, and robot policy learning. It is well-documented that these settings induce a phenomenon we call test-time feedback (TTF): the mismatch between the training/validation loss and downstream metrics of interest, such as task success rate and generation quality, which grows with task length. While data curation, architecture, and objective design have been proposed to combat train-test shift in TTF settings, this paper proposes optimization as a new design axis to mitigate error accumulation. Specifically, we introduce a new optimization paradigm called double-preconditioning (DoPr) uniquely tailored to the challenges of TTF. DoPr combines gradient-wise preconditioning, as in Adam and Muon, with activation-wise preconditioning (AP), such as in KFAC. We show that the addition of AP yields a drop-in intervention for increasing downstream model performance across a range of TTF settings. Interestingly, these gains in test-time performance do not consistently accompany improvements in validation loss, opening new questions about how to properly evaluate models trained with one-step supervised objectives.

AI.tldr: Introduces double-preconditioning (DoPr) to mitigate test-time feedback (TTF) in one-step supervised training with rollout deployment; combines gradient-wise and activation-wise preconditioning to improve downstream performance, even when validation loss does not align with test-time metrics.
AI.motivation: TTF arises when models trained with a single-step objective are evaluated by rollout-based metrics that accumulate errors over time; performance degrades with task length, and existing remedies focus on data/architecture/objectives, leaving optimization as a design axis.
AI.method: Proposes DoPr, a two-tier preconditioning strategy that merges gradient-wise preconditioning (e.g., Adam-like) with activation-wise preconditioning (e.g., KFAC). It is described as a drop-in intervention to boost downstream performance under TTF.
AI.result: Empirical gains in test-time metrics across various TTF regimes, indicating improved downstream performance; however, gains do not consistently accompany improvements in validation loss.
AI.conclusion: DoPr offers a promising optimization framework for TTF, highlighting that evaluation should consider downstream rollout metrics and that one-step training objectives may not fully capture deployment performance; invites further study on when and how to optimize for test-time outcomes.

title: Causal Atlases from Entropic Inference: Bayesian Networks beyond Optimal DAGs
summary: Data-driven causal relationship identification is pertinent to advancing understanding of complex systems both within and beyond science. Bayesian networks offer a probabilistic method for modelling generic causal relationships via directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). However, typical techniques for constructing Bayesian networks rely on optimization, which can be ill-suited for learning causal relationships because the underlying data may admit multiple chains of causation. More data-faithful representations of causal relationships would provide frameworks for constructing multiple causal maps that are consistent with the variability that is inherent in underlying data. Here, we show that entropy-based inference generates atlases of plausible causal relationships that are consistent with underlying data. On simulated noisy data of 2- and 20-node linear structural equation models, we sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of graphs that allow us to quantify the inherent structural ambiguity in underlying causal relationships. Our method shows that "optimized" DAGs can contain causal artifacts are not consistent across equivalently accurate topologies.

AI.tldr: Entropy-based inference yields a maximum-entropy ensemble (atlas) of DAGs to represent plausible causal relationships consistent with data, highlighting ambiguity that single optimized DAGs may miss or misrepresent as artifacts.
AI.motivation: To address identifiability and uncertainty in causal discovery by moving beyond a single optimized DAG to a data-faithful representation that captures multiple plausible causal maps.
AI.method: Sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of DAGs that fit data from simulated noisy linear structural equation models (2- and 20-node). Compare the resulting ensemble with conventional optimized DAGs, quantifying structural ambiguity.
AI.result: The ensemble uncovers inherent structural ambiguity in the underlying causal relations; optimized DAGs can contain artifacts and may not be consistent across equivalently accurate topologies.
AI.conclusion: Maximum-entropy causal atlases provide a more faithful representation of causal uncertainty, enabling multiple plausible maps and reducing overreliance on single optimized structures; broad applicability to real data demands attention to model assumptions and computational challenges.

title: VASO: Formally Verifiable Self-Evolving Skills for Physical AI Agents
summary: Reusable robot skills are becoming the basic units through which embodied agents turn open-ended instructions into long-horizon physical behavior. We argue that, while foundation models have collapsed the cost of creating these skills, the cost of trusting them has not. Existing skill-evolution loops refine skills through execution feedback, unit tests, environment reward, or LLM self-critique, but these signals provide only trace-level evidence: they show that a skill worked on sampled executions, not that skill-induced plans satisfy temporal safety contracts under untested conditions. We introduce VASO, a framework for verification-guided self-evolution of LLM-generated robot skill contracts. In VASO, each skill is represented as a semantic contract with two coupled interfaces: a formal interface that aligns robot states, observations, and control commands with logical propositions for model checking, and a planner-facing interface that guides executable behavior generation. A model checker first filters logically inconsistent skill contracts, then verifies plans induced by the skill against global and local temporal specifications. When verification fails, VASO translates the counterexample trace into a textual gradient that updates the reusable skill contract while keeping foundation-model weights frozen. On Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter tasks, VASO reaches 97.2% formal-specification compliance using fewer than 100 optimization samples, outperforming execution-feedback, prompt-optimization, and fine-tuning baselines. To our knowledge, VASO is the first framework that closes the loop between formal verification and self-evolving LLM-generated skills for physical AI agents: formal counterexamples become optimization feedback for reusable robot skill contracts, rather than merely verifying one-off plans, tuning planner prompts, or fine-tuning model weights.

AI.tldr: VASO links formal verification with self-evolution of LLM-generated robot skills, using semantic contracts and model checking to guarantee temporal safety; achieves high formal compliance with limited optimization samples.
AI.motivation: Trustworthiness of foundation-model–generated robotic skills; trace-level feedback is insufficient for temporal safety under untested conditions.
AI.method: Define skill as a dual-interface semantic contract: a formal interface for model checking and a planner-facing interface for behavior generation. A model checker filters contracts, verifies plans against temporal specs; counterexample traces are converted into textual gradients to update the contract while freezing LLM weights.
AI.result: On Clearpath Jackal and PX4 quadcopter tasks, 97.2% formal-spec compliance with <100 optimization samples; outperforms execution-feedback, prompt-optimization, and fine-tuning baselines.
AI.conclusion: VASO demonstrates a closed loop where formal verification guides self-evolution of LLM-generated robot skills; formal counterexamples serve as optimization feedback rather than weight updates.

title: Event Detection for Parameter-to-KPI Dependency Learning for AI-RAN
summary: Next-generation wireless networks are expected to rely on multiple concurrent AI-driven control functions that optimize different network objectives simultaneously, particularly in AI-integrated and open radio access network architectures such as AI Radio Access Network (AI-RAN) and Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN). When these functions interact, they can interfere with one another in ways that are difficult to detect from raw network data alone. A key missing piece for managing such interactions is a reliable, interpretable dependency structure that captures which control parameters are actively influencing which network performance outcomes at any given time. This paper focuses on the event-detection step needed to support such dependency learning by converting noisy continuous telemetry into binary indicators of parameter activity and KPI response. The central difficulty is that not every fluctuation in the data reflects a genuine control interaction, so the method must distinguish real parameter-outcome relationships from background variation. Because real AI-RAN traffic traces with known parameter-KPI ground truth are difficult to obtain, we introduce a synthetic closed-loop traffic generator with planted latent dependencies. We use this controlled telemetry to evaluate a machine-learning-based dependency recovery pipeline that formulates the conversion of continuous traces into binary event indicators as a significance-detection problem. Experimental evaluation shows that the proposed pipeline reliably recovers the latent dependency structure from noisy continuous traces when the signal is sufficiently separated from background variation, while highlighting threshold calibration as the key factor controlling event-detection quality. These results constitute a foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems.

AI.tldr: A synthetic closed-loop telemetry framework converts noisy continuous network traces into binary event indicators to recover latent parameter–KPI dependency structures in AI-RAN; the method uses significance-detection with a ML-based dependency recovery pipeline, with performance contingent on signal-to-background separation and threshold calibration.
AI.motivation: To enable interpretable dependency learning for AI-driven control in AI-RAN/O-RAN by identifying which control parameters influence which KPIs, despite noisy data and absence of ground-truth traces in real networks.
AI.method: Generate synthetic, closed-loop traffic with planted latent dependencies; transform continuous telemetry into binary activity indicators via a significance-detection pipeline; apply a machine-learning-based dependency recovery process to infer parameter–KPI relationships; evaluate on synthetic data focusing on calibration of detection thresholds.
AI.result: The pipeline reliably recovers the latent dependency structure when the signal is sufficiently separated from background variation; performance is highly sensitive to threshold calibration; results validate the feasibility of interpretable dependency learning in AI-RAN control.
AI.conclusion: A foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems, highlighting threshold calibration as a key factor and the need for controlled synthetic data to validate dependency discovery.

title: PC Layer: Polynomial Weight Preconditioning for Improving LLM Pre-Training
summary: We propose a preconditioning (PC) layer, a weight parameterization via polynomial preconditioner that ensures stable weight conditioning throughout LLM training. The PC module reshapes the singular-value spectrum of weight matrices via low-degree polynomial preconditioning. After training, the preconditioned weights can be merged back into the original architecture, incurring no inference overhead. We demonstrate the advantage of the proposed PC layer over standard transformers in Llama-1B pre-training, for both the AdamW and Muon optimizers. Theoretically, we justify this spectrum-control principle by proving that uniformly bounding each layer's singular values ensures geometric convergence of gradient descent to global minima, for certain deep linear networks. Our code is available at https://github.com/Empath-aln/PC-layer.

AI.tldr: A preconditioning (PC) layer using a low-degree polynomial to reshape weight-SVD spectra during LLM training, stabilizing conditioning without inference overhead; demonstrated on Llama-1B with AdamW and Muon; supported by a convergence-theory claim for deep linear nets; code released.
AI.motivation: To address ill-conditioned weight matrices and unstable conditioning during large-scale transformer/LLM training, which can slow convergence and harm stability, by enforcing a controlled spectrum and enabling seamless merge-back after training.
AI.method: Introduce a PC layer that parameterizes weights via a low-degree polynomial preconditioner, reshaping the singular-value spectrum to bound condition numbers during training. After training, preconditioned weights are merged back into the original architecture with no inference overhead. Empirical evaluation on Llama-1B pre-training comparing AdamW and Muon optimizers. Theoretical justification shows that uniformly bounding layer singular values yields geometric convergence of gradient descent for certain deep linear networks.
AI.result: The PC layer provides improved stability and conditioning during training compared to standard transformers, with no inference overhead after merging. It demonstrates advantages in Llama-1B pre-training for both AdamW and Muon optimizers. The spectrum-control approach is theoretically grounded in a result that bounded singular values imply geometric convergence for specific deep linear networks.
AI.conclusion: A spectrum-control PC layer is a viable augmentation for stable LLM training, offering conditioning benefits during training and zero overhead at inference once weights are merged; theoretical rationale underpins the approach, and code is publicly available.

title: RREDCoT: Segment-Level Reward Redistribution for Reasoning Models
summary: Recent advancements in reasoning language models have been driven by Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning. Most often, these rely on the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm or modifications thereof to steer the models to produce Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces. The final answer can only be verified, and the reward assigned, after the CoT trace is complete, making it a delayed reward problem. GRPO and its modifications correspond to Monte Carlo methods in standard RL, which are known to suffer from high variance. A possible solution to this problem is the redistribution of rewards through credit assignment, where segments of the CoT trace that are important for arriving at the desirable solution are emphasized by assigning a higher reward. While Monte Carlo sampling can be used to provide an unbiased estimate of intermediate state values, its computational overhead makes it unsuitable for train-time credit assignment in long contexts at high granularity. We introduce RREDCoT (Reward REDistribution for Chain of Thoughts), which utilizes the model itself to approximate the optimal reward redistribution without additional generation. We investigate the advantages of our method compared to MC sampling and several attribution methods. We further analyze several aspects relevant to the construction of the redistribution such as segmentation of CoT traces and state value estimation.

AI.tldr: RREDCoT proposes reward redistribution for chain-of-thought prompts to address high variance in delayed-reward RL fine-tuning, using the model itself to approximate optimal credit assignment without extra generation.
AI.motivation: Delays in reward due to requiring complete CoT traces lead to high-variance Monte Carlo-based credit assignment; existing GRPO-based approaches are MC-like and expensive for fine-grained intermediate-state rewards.
AI.method: Introduce RREDCoT that estimates reward redistribution internally (model-based) without generating additional traces; study segmentation of CoT traces and state-value estimation; compare against MC sampling and attribution baselines.
AI.result: Claimed advantages over MC sampling and attribution methods; analysis of how segmentation and state-value estimation affect redistribution; specific empirical results not provided in abstract.
AI.conclusion: RREDCoT offers a model-sourced, low-overhead approach to train-time credit assignment for long CoT traces, reducing variance and potentially improving CoT-guided reasoning under RL fine-tuning.

title: Selective-Advantage Entropy-Adaptive Horizon GRPO: Asymmetric Token-Level Discounting for Efficient Reinforcement Learning of Language Models
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) has emerged as an effective reinforcement-learning algorithm for aligning language models on reasoning tasks, but it treats every token position and every sampled rollout symmetrically. We introduce two complementary extensions: (i) Adaptive-Horizon GRPO (AH-GRPO), which weights each token's policy gradient using a cumulative entropy-based discount that reduces the effective horizon when the model is uncertain, and (ii) Selective-Advantage AH-GRPO (SA-AH-GRPO), which applies this discounting only to negative-advantage rollouts, leaving positive-advantage, successful trajectories unattenuated. We evaluate standard GRPO with alpha = 0, AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5, and SA-AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5 on the GSM8K mathematical reasoning benchmark using both Qwen 2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen 2.5-3B-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA. On the 3B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves Pass@1 = 0.858 at its peak at step 30 and maintains 0.846 at 180 steps, with training variance reduced to 0.0246, a 3.6 times reduction relative to GRPO while matching its peak accuracy. On the 1.5B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves a peak Pass@1 of 0.686, improving over the zero-shot baseline of 0.637. Our analysis shows that asymmetric discounting preserves the full gradient signal on correct solutions, prevents entropy collapse, and substantially stabilises training, suggesting a principled inductive bias for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks.

AI.tldr: Adaptive-Horizon and Selective-Advantage GRPO improve stability and performance on GSM8K by applying entropy-based, asymmetric discounting to token-level policy gradients, especially to negative-advantage rollouts; achieves state-like improvements over standard GRPO, particularly on larger models.
AI.motivation: Standard GRPO treats all tokens and rollouts uniformly; this symmetry can hinder learning under uncertain states and negative outcomes. Introducing horizon-adaptive, selective attenuation aims to preserve learning signals for correct trajectories while dampening uncertain, error-prone ones, stabilizing training on structured reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Propose AH-GRPO with entropy-based discount factor alpha controlling cumulative entropy discount. Propose SA-AH-GRPO that applies discount only to negative-advantage rollouts. Evaluate three variants: GRPO (alpha=0), AH-GRPO (alpha=0.5), SA-AH-GRPO (alpha=0.5). Datasets: GSM8K; models: Qwen 2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen 2.5-3B-Instruct LoRA fine-tuned. Training metrics: Pass@1 over steps (e.g., step 30, 180).
AI.result: SA-AH-GRPO with alpha=0.5 achieves peak Pass@1 0.858 at step 30 on 3B model and 0.846 at step 180, with training variance 0.0246 (3.6x lower than GRPO); matches GRPO peak accuracy. For 1.5B model, peak Pass@1 0.686, outperforming zero-shot 0.637. Training becomes more stable; entropy is preserved; gradient signals preserved for correct solutions.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetric, entropy-based horizon discounting yields a principled inductive bias for RL with verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks, improving stability and performance without sacrificing peak accuracy; selective attenuation of negative-advantage trajectories yields greater gains on larger models and can generalize to similar tasks.

title: Pretraining Recurrent Networks without Recurrence
summary: Training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) requires assigning credit across long sequences of computations. Standard backpropagation through time (BPTT) addresses this problem poorly: it is sequential in time, limiting parallelism, and suffers from vanishing or exploding gradients, making long-range associations difficult to learn. We propose Supervised Memory Training (SMT), a method for training nonlinear RNNs that sidesteps recurrent credit propagation entirely by reducing RNN training to supervised learning on one-step memory transition labels $(m_t, x_{t+1}) \rightarrow m_{t+1}$. SMT acquires these memory labels by training a Transformer-based encoder on a predictive state objective--retaining only information from the past necessary to predict the future. By decoupling what to remember from how to update memory, SMT enables time-parallel RNN training with a stable $O(1)$ length gradient path between any two tokens--without ever unrolling the RNN. We find that SMT outperforms BPTT when pretraining various RNN architectures on tasks like language modeling and pixel sequence modeling. SMT enables nonlinear RNNs to better capture long-range dependencies and train in parallel, potentially unlocking the scaling of models that build temporal abstractions of past experience.

AI.tldr: SMT reframes RNN training as supervised learning on one-step memory transitions, enabling parallel training and improved long-range dependency learning versus BPTT.
AI.motivation: Address the core limitations of BPTT: sequential unrolling, vanishing/exploding gradients, and limited parallelism, hindering scaling of RNNs that model long-range temporal structure.
AI.method: Train a Transformer-based encoder to produce memory labels (m_t, x_{t+1}) → m_{t+1} via a predictive state objective, decouple memory content/updates, and train nonlinear RNNs on one-step memory transitions without unrolling the RNN, yielding a constant (O(1)) gradient path.
AI.result: SMT outperforms BPTT in pretraining various RNN architectures on language modeling and pixel sequence modeling, indicating better capture of long-range dependencies and enabling time-parallel training for scalable temporal models.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling what to remember from how to update memory allows stable, parallel RNN training with improved performance, offering a viable path to scaling models that learn temporal abstractions.

title: GOTabPFN: From Feature Ordering to Compact Tokenization for Tabular Foundation Models on High-Dimensional Data
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). Code and resources are available at: GitHub: https://github.com/zadid6pretam/GOTabPFN; PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/gotabpfn/; Project webpage: https://www.zadidhabib.com/gotabpfn.html; Hugging Face Space: https://huggingface.co/spaces/zadid6pretam/GOTabPFN and https://zadid6pretam-GOTabPFN.hf.space
summary: We investigate how to make small tabular foundation models effective for High-Dimensional, Low-Sample Size (HDLSS) tabular prediction without retraining large backbones. We introduce Graph-guided Ordering with Local Refinement (GO-LR), show its equivalence to weighted Minimum Linear Arrangement, and interpret the practical solver as a TSP-path-style surrogate. We propose GOTabPFN,which builds on GO-LR, and a Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression (NSC) unit to pool locally adjacent ordered features into meta-features, yielding a compact representation that makes TabPFN-style prediction practical in HDLSS regimes. Across tabular benchmarks, GOTabPFN improves stability and accuracy under tight token budgets.

AI.tldr: Proposes GO-LR for ordering features in HDLSS tabular prediction, linking to weighted minimum linear arrangement and a TSP-path surrogate; builds GOTabPFN with NSC to produce compact, stable TabPFN-like predictions under tight token budgets.
AI.motivation: HDLSS tabular data face overfitting and scalability challenges. There is a need for non-retraining strategies that leverage small backbones and yield stable, accurate predictions with compact representations.
AI.method: Graph-guided Ordering with Local Refinement (GO-LR); shows equivalence to weighted Minimum Linear Arrangement; interprets the optimization as a TSP-path surrogate; constructs GOTabPFN built on GO-LR; introduces Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression (NSC) to pool locally adjacent ordered features into meta-features for a compact representation compatible with TabPFN-style prediction.
AI.result: Across tabular benchmarks, GOTabPFN improves stability and accuracy under tight token budgets.
AI.conclusion: GO-LR provides a principled, tractable feature ordering for HDLSS tabular data; NSC enables effective compression; together enabling practical TabPFN-style predictions without retraining large backbones and improving stability/accuracy in HDLSS settings.

title: Regret Minimization with Adaptive Opponents in Repeated Games
summary: In this paper, we study regret minimization in repeated games with \emph{adaptive} opponents who can respond based on histories of play. The standard metric of \emph{external regret} in online learning is known to fail to capture such adaptivity. To account for players' counterfactual reasoning, we introduce {\tt Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret)}, a game-theoretic metric that measures the difference between the \emph{realized} and the \emph{best-in-hindsight} accumulated utility when all players can \emph{respond} to the history of play. Compared to existing regret notions in this setting, ours is native to repeated game playing, enabling stronger comparators and opponents with fewer constraints, while maintaining the possibility of finding better equilibria when all players minimize it. We first identify necessary conditions for obtaining {\tt RP-Regret} sublinear in time, on the variation of the player's comparator strategies in the regret definition and on the memories of both the comparator and opponents' strategies. We then study additional conditions and provable algorithms to minimize {\tt RP-Regret}, which is by definition \emph{non-convex} in the strategy space. To address this challenge, we propose three algorithms: (i) one based on an optimization oracle, as assumed in some prior work in online non-convex learning; (ii) one that minimizes a convex and \emph{linearized} surrogate of {\tt RP-Regret} at each iteration; (iii) one that directly minimizes {\tt RP-Regret} when opponents change strategies slowly. Furthermore, when all players can run algorithms to minimize the {\tt RP-Regret} (or its linearized variant), certain subgame perfect equilibria of the repeated game can be learned. We also provide experiments showing that minimizing our regret notions can lead to more cooperative solutions with higher utility in games such as Stag-Hunt.

AI.tldr: Introduces Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret) for adaptive opponents in repeated games; analyzes conditions for sublinear RP-Regret; proposes three algorithms (oracle-based, linearized surrogate, slow-opponent minimization); shows learning subgame-perfect equilibria under joint minimization; empirical results suggest more cooperative outcomes (e.g., Stag-Hunt).
AI.motivation: Standard external regret does not capture adaptivity and counterfactual reasoning in repeated games with responsive opponents. A native metric is needed to compare realized utilities against best-in-hindsight when opponents can adapt to history, enabling better equilibrium behavior.
AI.method: Define RP-Regret as the gap between realized cumulative utility and the best-in-hindsight cumulative utility under optimal response to history by all players. Derive necessary conditions for sublinear RP-Regret given variation in comparator strategies and memory constraints. Develop three algorithms to minimize RP-Regret: (i) oracle-based optimization, (ii) convex surrogate via linearization at each step, (iii) direct minimization when opponents change slowly. Analyze when such minimization yields subgame perfect equilibria; conduct experiments in games like Stag-Hunt to compare outcomes.
AI.result: RP-Regret is inherently non-convex in strategy space. With various assumptions, sublinear RP-Regret can be achieved. The proposed algorithms provide practical avenues to minimize RP-Regret, with joint minimization enabling learning of certain subgame-perfect equilibria. Experiments indicate that minimizing RP-Regret can lead to more cooperative solutions and higher utility in relevant games.
AI.conclusion: RP-Regret offers a natural and robust framework for regret in repeated games with adaptive opponents. Under appropriate memory and variation assumptions, it is possible to design algorithms that achieve sublinear RP-Regret and learn favorable equilibria. This work bridges online non-convex learning and repeated-game theory and opens avenues for cooperative strategy development in adaptive environments.

title: TailLoR: Protecting Principal Components in Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning
summary: Parameter-efficient finetuning methods based on spectral decomposition have enabled progress in Continual Learning. In this paper we introduce TailLoR, which utilizes the singular bases U and V of the pre-trained weights as a fixed reference frame to learn a low-rank update applied to the singular value matrix.
