A soft spectral penalty discourages updates aligned with dominant singular directions, reducing interference while routing fine-grained adaptation into the highly flexible, long-tail spectral coordinates.

AI.tldr: TailLoR learns a low-rank update to the singular value matrix in the SVD of pretrained weights, using fixed left/right singular bases U and V as a reference frame, with a soft spectral penalty to discourage updates along dominant singular directions and route adaptation into long-tail spectral coordinates for parameter-efficient continual learning.
AI.motivation: In continual learning, preserving prior knowledge while adapting to new tasks is challenging and prone to interference. Spectral decomposition offers a natural basis to separate stable (dominant) directions from flexible (tail) directions. The approach aims to minimize task interference by constraining updates to the singular-value matrix and cooling updates along dominant modes, thereby enabling stable, low-parameter adaptation.
AI.method: Compute the SVD of pretrained weight matrices to obtain U, Σ, V^T. Freeze U and V as reference frames. Learn a low-rank update ΔΣ to the singular-value matrix and apply it to the pretrained weights (as W_new = U (Σ + ΔΣ) V^T). Introduce a soft spectral penalty that discourages alignment with directions corresponding to large singular values, encouraging updates to concentrate in tail spectral coordinates. Train across tasks to achieve continual learning with reduced parameter overhead.
AI.result: Abstract claims that TailLoR reduces interference and routes fine-grained adaptation into long-tail spectral coordinates, suggesting improved parameter efficiency and task separation, though explicit empirical metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Fixing the spectral bases of pretrained weights and applying a soft penalty to singular-value updates yields a principled, parameter-efficient finetuning approach for continual learning, mitigating forgetting by directing adaptation into less dominant spectral directions.

title: Almieyar-Oryx-BloomBench: A Bilingual Multimodal Benchmark for Cognitively Informed Evaluation of Vision-Language Models
summary: Despite the rapid progress of Vision-Language Models (VLMs), the field lacks benchmarks that rigorously diagnose their true reasoning abilities and chart meaningful progress toward human-like multimodal intelligence. Most existing evaluations focus on piecemeal or disconnected tasks, obscuring critical cognitive weaknesses and providing little insight for targeted improvement. To address this gap, we introduce BloomBench, part of the Almieyar benchmarking series, the first cognitively human-grounded, bilingual (English-Arabic) multimodal benchmark for VLMs. Grounded in Bloom's Taxonomy, BloomBench systematically evaluates six levels of cognition (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create) through carefully designed image-question-answer tasks. Built with a semi-automated pipeline and validated through a stratified hybrid quality assurance protocol, it ensures scalability, cultural inclusivity, and linguistic fidelity. Leveraging this framework, we conduct a comprehensive study of state-of-the-art VLMs to diagnose their cognitive profiles. Our analysis reveals a sharp cognitive asymmetry: while state-of-the-art models achieve strong performance ceilings in semantic understanding, they struggle substantially with factual recall and creative synthesis. This demonstrates that current general multimodal proficiency masks deeper limitations in specific cognitive layers. Furthermore, our study highlights a critical performance gap between Arabic and English, exposing limitations in current cross-lingual multimodal reasoning. These findings establish a foundation for developing more cognitively aligned and inclusive VLMs. The benchmark framework and dataset is available at: https://github.com/qcri/Almieyar-Oryx-BloomBench.

AI.tldr: BloomBench is a bilingual English-Arabic multimodal benchmark for VLMs grounded in Bloom's Taxonomy with six cognitive levels, using a semi-automated pipeline and QA validation to diagnose cognitive profiles; it reveals that current models excel at semantic understanding but struggle with recall and creative synthesis, and shows a notable English-Arabic cross-lingual gap.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of cognitively structured, human-grounded benchmarks for VLM reasoning, aiming for inclusive, multilingual evaluation that informs targeted improvements.
AI.method: Six task types aligned to Bloom's taxonomy (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create) implemented as image-question-answer datasets. Semi-automated construction with stratified hybrid quality assurance; bilingual dataset (English-Arabic); evaluation of state-of-the-art VLMs.
AI.result: Reveals cognitive asymmetry: strong semantic understanding but weak factual recall and creative synthesis; identifies a cross-lingual gap between Arabic and English in multimodal reasoning; provides diagnostic insights for improving VLM cognition.
AI.conclusion: BloomBench offers a foundation for cognitively aligned and inclusive VLM development; the dataset and framework enable targeted improvements and cross-linguistic evaluation; available at the provided GitHub link.

title: OLIVE: Online Low-Rank Incremental Learning for Efficient Adaptive Exoskeletons
summary: Wearable exoskeleton systems hold promise for restoring mobility in individuals with physical impairments, yet most existing controllers rely on static gait policies that lack the ability to adapt to dynamic real-world environments or individual user characteristics. We present \olive (\underline{O}nline \underline{L}ow-rank \underline{I}ncremental Learning for Efficient Adapti\underline{ve} Exoskeletons), a parameter-efficient online adaptation framework that continuously personalizes exoskeleton control during deployment. \olive decomposes the adaptive component of the control policy into a low-rank residual form~$\dW = \At\Bt^\top$ with rank~$r!\ll!\min(d,k)$, reducing online update cost from $\mathcal{O}(dk)$ to $\mathcal{O}(r(d{+}k))$ while preserving the stability of a pretrained base controller~$\Wz$. Parameters are updated via a reward-shaped policy gradient driven purely by on-body sensor feedback (EMG, IMU, vibration), eliminating dependence on offline reference trajectories. A gating mechanism modulates the strength of personalization based on contextual state, and a dynamic rank scheduler adapts the update dimensionality to terrain complexity -- allocating minimal capacity on simple flat terrain and expanding to higher-rank updates on demanding uneven surfaces -- enabling robust performance across diverse activities: flat walking, stair navigation, slopes, and uneven terrain. Experiments on the wearable platform demonstrate that \olive achieves +13, +22, and +15 percentage-point improvements in gait smoothness, effort reduction, and motion stability over the strongest baseline, converging within $\sim$1{,}800 walking steps at 7.4,ms end-to-end latency. Our code implementation is available at https://github.com/FastLM/OLIVE.

AI.tldr: OLIVE is an online, low-rank adaptive framework for exoskeleton control that personalizes behavior during deployment with low online computation, stability guarantees from a pretrained base controller, and sensor-driven, reward-shaped updates. It uses a low-rank residual to the base policy, with a dynamic rank scheduler and gating to adapt to terrain complexity, achieving faster convergence and robust performance across activities.
AI.motivation: Static gait controllers struggle to adapt to real-world variability in terrain and individual user characteristics. There is a need for parameter-efficient, online adaptation that preserves stability and works with on-body sensors rather than offline references.
AI.method: Represent the adaptive component as a low-rank residual W~ = A_t B_t^T with rank r much smaller than min(d,k), reducing online update cost from O(dk) to O(r(d+k)). Update parameters via reward-shaped policy gradient using on-body sensor feedback (EMG, IMU, vibration). Include a gating mechanism to modulate personalization strength and a dynamic rank scheduler to adjust update dimensionality to terrain complexity. Maintain stability by anchoring to a pretrained base controller W0. End-to-end latency ~7.4 ms. Code available.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows +13, +22, and +15 percentage-point gains in gait smoothness, effort reduction, and motion stability over the strongest baseline. Converges in ~1,800 walking steps.
AI.conclusion: OLIVE enables robust, data-driven online adaptation for exoskeleton control with low computational cost and stability guarantees, yielding substantial improvements across activities and terrains; the approach is deployable in real-world settings.

title: A New Quaternion-Joint Cable-Driven Redundant Manipulator Configuration and its Control Through FABRIK and Residual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Robotic arms capable of traversing arbitrary spatial paths, especially in highly obstructed workspaces, are highly desired across several industries. Quaternion-joints have recently empowered a specific class of robotic arms -- cable-driven redundant manipulators -- beyond its prior capabilities. Specifically, quaternion-joints reduce the number of required motors per degree of freedom, paving the way for more compact solutions.An ongoing challenge is that the complexity of the kinematic model of quaternion joints challenges a priori decisions on manipulator configurations and imposes higher computational demands on the control system and its non-linearities amplify all discrepancies between design and physical artifact arising from fabrication imprecision. Here we show a that a 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator can achieve a broader workspace than extant configurations, at lower hardware cost, and that Residual Reinforcement Learning outperforms extant state-of-the-art methods -- specifically, the FABRIK algorithm -- on the control of such manipulator. Our results show that this configuration is more workspace-effective than prior designs, and that Residual Reinforcement Learning outperforms FABRIK by three orders of magnitude on positional and orientational accuracy, effecting precise control of the novel 4-segment, 8-joint manipulator. Additionally, the control implementation is simpler: we describe the complete FABRIK process for control and corresponding learning implementation. Our methodology is applicable to the design of new systems, providing designers with further tools for the development of this class of manipulators and corresponding control systems for novel configurations.

AI.tldr: A 4-segment, 8-joint cable-driven manipulator with quaternion joints is proposed to achieve a broader workspace at lower hardware cost. Residual Reinforcement Learning (RR-Learning) is shown to outperform FABRIK by orders of magnitude in pose accuracy, with a simpler control pipeline.
AI.motivation: Address the need for compact, obstacle-robust manipulators with large workspaces and efficient, accurate control. Quaternion joints reduce motor count per DoF but complicate kinematics and control; improving control performance and reducing hardware could enable practical deployment in obstructed workspaces.
AI.method: Design of a 4-segment, 8-joint cable-driven manipulator employing quaternion joints. Compare control schemes: Residual Reinforcement Learning vs FABRIK-based control. Quantify workspace breadth and tracking accuracy. Provide complete FABRIK control description and integrate it with the learning framework.
AI.result: The 4-segment, 8-joint configuration achieves a broader workspace at lower hardware cost. Residual Reinforcement Learning outperforms FABRIK by three orders of magnitude in positional and orientational accuracy for controlling the manipulator. The control implementation is simpler, with a complete FABRIK process described and applied.
AI.conclusion: The methodology supports designing new, efficient quaternion-joint manipulators and provides designers with tools for developing novel configurations and their controllers. RR-Learning is a promising approach for achieving high-precision control in such systems.

title: Noise-Aware Visual Representation Learning for Medical Visual Question Answering
summary: Medical visual question answering (Med-VQA) has strong potential for clinical decision support by enabling AI models to interpret medical images and answer clinically relevant queries. Recent approaches typically connect off-the-shelf vision encoders with large language models (LLMs) through lightweight mapping networks to reduce computational cost. However, these methods often overlook the importance of handling noise and small irrelevant changes in visual representations. To address these challenges, we propose a noise-aware Med-VQA framework that incorporates a denoising autoencoder before visual embeddings are mapped into the input space of an LLM. The denoising autoencoder is pretrained to reconstruct clean visual embeddings from corrupted inputs, encouraging the model to learn robust visual representations that are less sensitive to noise. The resulting embeddings are then projected into the language model embedding space using a multi-layer perceptron (MLP), forming visual prefix tokens that provide image information to the LLM. To enable efficient adaptation without full retraining, we employ parameter-efficient fine-tuning using low-rank adaptation (LoRA). The proposed method is evaluated on the SLAKE and PathVQA benchmarks. Experimental results show improved robustness to noisy input embeddings while maintaining competitive clean performance across multiple evaluation criteria. These findings suggest that learning more robust visual representations can enhance Med-VQA performance and robustness.

AI.tldr: Proposes a noise-aware Med-VQA framework using a denoising autoencoder to produce robust visual embeddings for LLMs; uses MLP projection and LoRA for efficient fine-tuning; shows increased robustness to noisy embeddings on SLAKE and PathVQA with competitive clean performance.
AI.motivation: Med-VQA systems suffer from noise and small changes in visual representations when interfacing vision encoders with LLMs; need robustness and efficient adaptation.
AI.method: Denoising autoencoder pretrained to reconstruct clean visual embeddings from corrupted inputs; map to LLM embedding space via MLP to form visual prefix tokens; use LoRA for parameter-efficient fine-tuning; evaluate on SLAKE and PathVQA.
AI.result: Improved robustness to noisy input embeddings while maintaining competitive performance on clean data across evaluation criteria.
AI.conclusion: Learning robust visual representations can enhance Med-VQA performance and robustness; the approach offers a viable, efficient pathway for Med-VQA systems.

title: LeanMarathon: Toward Reliable AI Co-Mathematicians through Long-Horizon Lean Autoformalization
summary: Long-horizon autoformalization of research mathematics fails not only at hard lemmas, but at scale: statements drift, dependencies tangle, context decays, and local repairs corrupt distant work. We present LeanMarathon, a multi-agent harness for reliable research-level Lean autoformalization. Its core abstraction is an evolving blueprint: a Lean file that serves simultaneously as formal proof skeleton, natural-language proof graph, and shared system of record. Four contract-scoped agents construct, audit, prove, and repair this blueprint. These agents are coordinated by a two-stage orchestrator that first stabilizes target fidelity through adversarial review and then discharges the proof directed acyclic graph (DAG) from its dynamic leaves upward in parallel CI-gated rounds. LeanMarathon turns one brittle multi-hour run into many local, recoverable, parallel transactions. We evaluate LeanMarathon on two recent research papers spanning four Erdős problems (#1051, #1196, #164, #1217). Across three autonomous runs, it formalizes all seven target theorems with no sorry, proving 258 lemmas and theorems. These results show that reliable AI co-mathematics requires not only stronger provers, but durable harnesses that preserve target fidelity across long mathematical developments. The code can be found at https://github.com/YuanheZ/LeanMarathon.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent framework (LeanMarathon) for reliable long-horizon autoformalization in Lean, using an evolving blueprint and a two-stage orchestrator to stabilize fidelity and discharge a proof DAG in parallel CI rounds. Evaluated on two Erdős-related papers across three autonomous runs, formalizing seven theorems and 258 lemmas/theorems with no sorries; code available.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon autoformalization of research mathematics fails due to drift, tangled dependencies, context decay, and local repairs corrupting distant work. A reliable, durable harness is needed to preserve fidelity across extended formal developments.
AI.method: LeanMarathon introduces an evolving Lean file blueprint that serves as formal skeleton, natural-language proof graph, and shared record. Four contract-scoped agents (construct, audit, prove, repair) operate under a two-stage orchestrator: (1) adversarial review to stabilize target fidelity, (2) DAG discharge from dynamic leaves upward in parallel CI-gated rounds. This turns a brittle multi-hour run into many recoverable, parallel transactions.
AI.result: Three autonomous runs on two Erdős-related papers (#1051, #1196, #164, #1217) formalized all seven target theorems with no sorries, proving 258 lemmas and theorems.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that reliable AI co-mathematics requires durable harnesses in addition to stronger provers; contributes a practical, scalable workflow and codebase for long-horizon formalization (LeanMarathon).

title: Harnessing Generalist Agents for Contextualized Time Series
summary: Time series are often embedded in rich contexts that are essential for holistic modeling. Moreover, real-world practitioners often require end-to-end workflows for analyzing temporal dynamics, where widely studied tasks such as forecasting are only one step in a broader solution loop. While generalist AI agents offer a promising interface for such workflows under complex contexts, they still operate primarily in textual spaces that are not fully aligned with structured temporal signals. In this work, we introduce TimeClaw, an agentic harness framework for time series that equips generalist LLM agents with the time series-native runtime support needed for contextualized temporal reasoning. TimeClaw integrates executable temporal tools for grounded and auditable analysis, experience-driven capability evolution for creating reusable analytical routines, and episodic multimodal memory for retrieving relevant reasoning traces. Together, these components unlock harnessed open-ended temporal reasoning with contextual information. Extensive evaluation on multiple benchmarks covering diverse tasks across energy, finance, weather, traffic, and other real-world domains demonstrates improved performance of TimeClaw. Code is available at https://github.com/iDEA-iSAIL-Lab-UIUC/TimeClaw.

AI.tldr: TimeClaw provides an LLM-powered, time-series–aware harness that enables grounded, auditable temporal reasoning with reusable analytical routines and episodic multimodal memory, improving end-to-end performance on real-world temporal tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series analysis requires end-to-end workflows and contextualized temporal reasoning, but generalist LLMs operate primarily in textual spaces and lack structured temporal signal integration.
AI.method: Proposes TimeClaw, a framework that gives time-series-native runtime support to LLMs, including executable temporal tools for grounded analysis, experience-driven capability evolution to create reusable analytical routines, and episodic multimodal memory to retrieve relevant reasoning traces.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation across benchmarks in energy, finance, weather, traffic, and other domains shows improved performance of TimeClaw compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: TimeClaw enables harnessed open-ended temporal reasoning with contextual information, demonstrating strong potential for end-to-end temporal workflows in diverse real-world domains; code is available.

title: Agents' Last Exam
summary: Recent AI systems have achieved strong results on a wide range of benchmarks, yet these gains have not translated into economically meaningful deployment across many professional domains. We argue that this gap is largely an evaluation problem: widely used benchmarks lack sustained performance measurement on real and economically valuable workflows. This paper introduces Agents' Last Exam (ALE), a benchmark designed to evaluate AI agents on long-horizon, economically valuable, real-world tasks with verifiable outcomes. Developed in collaboration with 250+ industry experts, ALE covers non-physical industries defined with reference to O*NET / SOC 2018 (the U.S. federal occupational taxonomy). It is organized around a task taxonomy with 55 subfields grouped into 13 industry clusters covering 1K+ tasks. Current results show that the hardest tier remains far from saturated: across mainstream harness and backbone configurations, the average full pass rate is 2.6%. ALE is designed as a living benchmark: its task pool grows continuously as new workflows and industries are onboarded. More broadly, ALE is intended not merely as another leaderboard, but as an instrument for closing the gap between benchmark success and GDP-relevant impact.

AI.tldr: ALE benchmarks AI agents on long-horizon, economically valuable real-world tasks across 55 subfields in 13 industry clusters, with 1K+ tasks, aiming to close the benchmark-to-deployment gap and GDP impact.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where high benchmark performance does not translate into economically meaningful deployment, by creating a benchmark that measures sustained performance on real workflows with verifiable outcomes.
AI.method: Developed with 250+ industry experts; non-physical industries defined via O*NET/SOC 2018; organized as a task taxonomy with 55 subfields in 13 clusters and 1K+ tasks; designed as a living benchmark that expands with new workflows and industries; evaluates AI agents on long-horizon tasks with verifiable outcomes.
AI.result: Current results show the hardest tier remains under-saturated, with an average full pass rate of 2.6% across mainstream harness and backbone configurations.
AI.conclusion: ALE is intended as a living instrument to close the gap between benchmark success and GDP-relevant impact, beyond being a traditional leaderboard.

title: Cross-Epoch Adaptive Rollout Optimization for RL Post-Training
summary: LLM post-training often relies on reinforcement learning methods that sample multiple rollouts per prompt, yet most existing approaches use a fixed rollout budget for every prompt, despite large differences in the training signal different prompts provide. In this paper, we study adaptive rollout allocation under a fixed global budget and formulate the problem as online resource allocation with prompt-level diminishing returns. Our method, CERO, maintains a Beta posterior over each prompt's success probability and uses the posterior expected Bernoulli variance as a Bayesian estimate of the value of additional rollouts. We use this estimate to construct a concave, saturating utility over cumulative allocations, yielding an objective in which decisions across prompts and epochs are coupled by the global budget. Since the resulting objective is temporally nonseparable, we derive a Fenchel-dual reformulation and update both prompt-level and budget-level dual variables via projected online gradient descent. Under fixed prompt utilities, we prove an $O(\sqrt{K})$ regret bound against the offline allocation benchmark. Experiments on mathematical-reasoning problems show that CERO consistently outperforms GRPO across multiple open-weight LLMs and benchmarks, demonstrating that adaptive rollout budgeting can improve sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: Adaptive rollout budgeting for LLM reinforcement learning using per-prompt Beta posteriors to value additional rollouts, with a concave saturating utility and Fenchel-dual online optimization under a fixed global budget; achieves O(√K) regret and empirical gains over GRPO on math-reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce sample inefficiency in LLM post-training RL by allocating a fixed budget of rollouts adaptively across prompts, accounting for differing learning signals per prompt.
AI.method: Maintain a Beta posterior for each prompt's success probability; use posterior expected Bernoulli variance as a Bayesian estimate of the value of extra rollouts; define a concave, saturating utility over cumulative allocations; derive a nonseparable objective across prompts and epochs; apply Fenchel-dual reformulation; update prompt-level and budget-level dual variables via projected online gradient descent.
AI.result: Theoretical: O(√K) regret bound against the offline allocation benchmark under fixed prompt utilities. Empirical: CERO outperforms GRPO on multiple open-weight LLMs and math-reasoning benchmarks, showing improved sample efficiency through adaptive budgeting.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive rollout budgeting with Bayesian value estimates and dual online optimization is effective for LLM post-training RL, delivering both theoretical guarantees and empirical gains over a baseline.

title: Can We Predict The Human Preference For Text-to-Image Content Prior To Generation And Is It Even Useful To Do So?
summary: Diffusion Models (DM) have revolutionized text-driven generation by enabling the synthesis of high-quality, photorealistic visual content from user prompts. Whereas prior advances in visual generation such as VAEs and GANs were primarily evaluated on perceptual or visual similarity metrics such as FID PSNR, DM advances have fostered the development of more advanced Human Preference Metrics (HPM) that model and quantify human judgment as scalar values. However, DMs synthesize content using an inherently stochastic process where random noise seeds generation. The initial random noise directly affects the quality of generated outputs, both qualitatively and quantitatively. This influence is pronounced in smaller models for local deployment scenarios. Given this phenomenon, we first investigate to what extent we can predict scalar HPM scores prior to committing compute resources for generation. Further, we then investigate to what extent we can leverage such prediction to improve the quality of generated images, and also study which HPMs are best suited for this task. Our investigation reveals that not only is this possible, but that it is feasible to achieve negligible hardware overhead.

AI.tldr: Predict and use scalar human preference metrics (HPMs) for diffusion models before generation, enabling seed/parameter selection that improves image quality with negligible hardware overhead.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are stochastic; random seeds heavily influence output quality, especially in smaller models. Efficiently predicting HPM scores can save compute and guide optimization by focusing on seeds/conditions with higher predicted human preference.
AI.method: Empirical study estimating the predictability of various scalar HPMs from initial seeds/inputs prior to generation, followed by experiments that leverage these predictions to steer generation and improve image quality, while assessing which HPMs best correlate with perceived quality.
AI.result: Demonstrates that scalar HPM scores can be predicted prior to generation with negligible hardware overhead. Using these predictions to guide seed/conditioning improves image quality, and certain HPMs align better with human judgment for this steering task.
AI.conclusion: Predicting and leveraging HPMs before diffusion runs is feasible and beneficial, enabling more efficient quality control and optimization of text-driven image generation with minimal additional cost.

title: Safe Embodied AI for Long-horizon Tasks: A Cross-layer Analysis of Robotic Manipulation
summary: Embodied AI systems are increasingly expected to reason and act over extended horizons in physical environments. This growing capability brings safety to the foreground, because failures in the physical world can harm people, damage objects, and disrupt workplaces. Although safe embodied AI has attracted substantial attention, the literature remains fragmented across planning, policy design, and runtime execution. Long-horizon robotic manipulation is a particularly revealing anchor domain for this problem because semantic misgrounding, subtask-level error propagation, execution drift, and contact-rich physical risk can accumulate within the same closed-loop system. This survey therefore provides a structured review of safety in long-horizon robotic manipulation from an embodied AI perspective. We organize the literature by intervention locus, covering planning-time, policy-time, and execution-time safety, and we analyze the strength of the evidence that each line of work provides, distinguishing formal guarantees, statistical support, and empirical safety heuristics. This framework clarifies the distinct roles of backbone capability papers, direct safety mechanisms, and benchmark or evaluation studies, while exposing where current safety claims are well supported and where they remain indirect. We identify persistent gaps, including limited evidence for policy-time safety, weak formal support for contact-rich long-horizon manipulation, immature uncertainty-triggered intervention, and a shortage of manipulation-specific safety benchmarks. We conclude by outlining research directions for cross-layer assurance, evaluation design, and safer deployment of long-horizon robotic agents in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: Survey of safety in long-horizon embodied AI for robotic manipulation; analyzes planning-, policy-, and execution-time safety, and identifies gaps and cross-layer assurance needs.
AI.motivation: Safety is critical for embodied AI operating over extended horizons in physical environments due to potential harm and disruptions from misgrounding, error propagation, drift, and contact-rich risk; current literature is fragmented and lacks integrated safety evaluation.
AI.method: Structured literature review organized by intervention locus (planning-time, policy-time, execution-time), with assessment of evidence types (formal guarantees, statistical support, empirical heuristics). Distinguishes backbone capability papers, direct safety mechanisms, and benchmarks; analyzes strength of claims and gaps.
AI.result: Finds limited policy-time safety evidence, weak formal guarantees for contact-rich long-horizon manipulation, immature uncertainty-triggered intervention, and a shortage of manipulation-specific safety benchmarks; proposes a cross-layer assurance framework.
AI.conclusion: Outlines research directions for cross-layer assurance, evaluation design, and safer deployment of long-horizon robotic agents in real-world settings.

title: LongSpace: Exploring Long-Horizon Spatial Memory from Perception to Recall in Video
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have advanced image and video understanding and can increasingly handle longer visual inputs. Long-horizon tasks such as autonomous driving and robotic navigation require more than recognizing the current view, as models must remember and retrieve previously observed spatial layouts, routes, viewpoint changes, and object states. To evaluate this capability, we introduce LongSpace-Bench, a room-tour video benchmark for long-horizon spatial memory, covering scene perception, spatial relations, and spatial memory. In this work, we further propose LongSpace, a memory framework for long-video spatial reasoning. LongSpace models long videos as sequential chunks, incorporates 3D structural cues into early decoder layers, and constructs layer-aware memory for question-guided retrieval. Experiments on multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks show that LongSpace improves long-video spatial understanding, further demonstrating explicit spatial memory as a key capability for long-horizon video MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces LongSpace-Bench for long-horizon spatial memory in room-tour videos and LongSpace, a memory-based framework for long-video spatial reasoning; demonstrates memory-enhanced understanding over extended visual sequences.
AI.motivation: To enable and evaluate persistent spatial memory in multimodal large language models for long-horizon tasks (e.g., autonomous driving, robotics) where current models struggle to retain and reason over past observations.
AI.method: Treats long videos as sequential chunks, injects 3D structural cues into early decoder layers, and builds layer-aware memory to support question-guided retrieval.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks show improved long-video spatial understanding when using LongSpace; highlights explicit spatial memory as a key capability for long-horizon video MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Explicit spatial memory is essential for long-horizon visual tasks; the proposed LongSpace framework and LongSpace-Bench provide a path to more capable long-video MLLMs.

title: ViCuR: Visual Cues as Recoverable Privilege for Multimodal On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves reasoning by training a student on trajectories sampled from its own policy under supervision from a teacher. In multimodal reasoning, a common extension is to use a privileged teacher that observes training-time-only signals such as reference answers or rationales. However, such answer-side privilege creates a train-test mismatch: the teacher's supervision may depend on signals unavailable to the student, encouraging shortcut imitation rather than visually grounded reasoning. We propose ViCuR, a visually grounded privileged-teacher distillation framework that replaces answer-side privilege with visual cues (query-related evidence in the input). Because these cues are derived from the same visual input available at inference, their evidence is recoverable by the student. To support this, ViCuR introduces a lightweight cue recovery module that uses dedicated sink-token cross-attention during prefill to aggregate task-relevant visual evidence into an internal representation, without changing the inference interface or requiring auxiliary cue-generation losses. Across seven benchmarks with Qwen3-VL-2B and 8B students, ViCuR consistently improves over answer-based on-policy self-distillation by +1.19 and +1.24 on overall average performance. It also extends naturally to stronger-teacher OPD, surpassing OPD baselines by +0.64 and +1.08, with consistent out-of-domain gains at the 8B scale. These results show that, in multimodal on-policy distillation, the design of teacher privilege is as important as teacher strength.

AI.tldr: ViCuR introduces visually grounded privileged-teacher distillation for multimodal on-policy learning, replacing answer-side privilege with input-derived visual cues that are recoverable by the student, leading to systematic gains over standard OPD.
AI.motivation: Answer-side privileged supervision in multimodal on-policy distillation creates train–test mismatch and encourages shortcut imitation; cues derived from the same visual input available at test time can ground reasoning without changing inference interfaces.
AI.method: A cue-recovery module with sink-token cross-attention during prefill aggregates task-relevant visual evidence into an internal representation. No auxiliary cue-generation losses or changes to inference are required; the system leverages visually grounded cues available at test time.
AI.result: ViCuR yields consistent gains over answer-based OPD across seven benchmarks with Qwen3-VL-2B and 8B students (+1.19 and +1.24 on average). It also improves stronger-teacher OPD by +0.64 and +1.08, with robust out-of-domain gains at 8B scale.
AI.conclusion: The design of teacher privilege is as important as teacher strength in multimodal on-policy distillation; visually grounding supervision via recoverable cues enhances reasoning without altering inference burden.

title: Next-Generation Parallel Decoder for LPDR: Architectural Optimization and Class-Balanced GAN-Augmentation
summary: Real-Time License Plate Detection and Recognition (LPDR) forms the backbone of modern smart cities. Although the YOLOV5-PDLPR model substantially improved system efficiency through a parallel decoder approach, its performance is still affected by spatial character mismatches and data imbalance within the training set. This paper addresses these limitations by introducing Cross-Spatial Hybrid Attention (CSHA) and Class-Balanced Synthetic Augmentation (CBSA). An extensive study involving 75,000 synthetic samples is conducted and evaluated on four benchmarks: CCPD, CLPD, PKU, and an application-specific dataset. Experimental results demonstrate a substantial improvement in the recognition rate of minority provincial license plates from 78.2% to 91.5% while maintaining real-time processing performance of 152 FPS. The results indicate that spatially-aware parallel decoding combined with class-balanced augmentation provides an effective solution for high-speed license plate recognition systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes Cross-Spatial Hybrid Attention (CSHA) and Class-Balanced Synthetic Augmentation (CBSA) to address spatial mismatches and data imbalance in YOLOV5-PDLPR, achieving 152 FPS and improving minority license plate recognition from 78.2% to 91.5% across four benchmarks using 75k synthetic samples.
AI.motivation: Improve real-time license plate detection/recognition by mitigating spatial character misalignment and class imbalance that degrade minority plate recognition, while maintaining real-time throughput.
AI.method: Enhance a YOLOV5-PDLPR-based pipeline with two components: Cross-Spatial Hybrid Attention (CSHA) to align spatial features within a parallel decoder framework, and Class-Balanced Synthetic Augmentation (CBSA) to balance training data using 75k synthetic samples across CCPD, CLPD, PKU, and a custom dataset.
AI.result: Significant improvement in minority provincial plate recognition from 78.2% to 91.5%; real-time performance at 152 FPS maintained; validated on four benchmarks with extensive synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: Spatially-aware parallel decoding coupled with class-balanced augmentation is effective for high-speed license plate recognition, offering robust performance gains on diverse datasets without sacrificing throughput.

title: Learning Geometric Representations from Videos for Spatial Intelligent Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at 2D semantic understanding but lack intrinsic 3D awareness, resulting in representations that fail to maintain geometric and spatial consistency across video frames. Given the scarcity of large-scale 3D data, we present GeoVR, a novel framework that learns geometric representations using purely 2D video sequences. This approach effectively restructures the semantic latent space within MLLMs to unlock spatial intelligence. Rather than employing superficial feature mixing, GeoVR reshapes the internal representations of the MLLM by distilling geometry knowledge from pre-trained 3D foundation models. This is accomplished through a multi-objective learning strategy driven by four complementary geometric targets: (1) estimating inter-frame camera poses to embed varying viewpoint dynamics, (2) regressing dense depth maps to anchor physical distances, (3) predicting a metric scale factor for real-world calibration, and (4) distilling multi-scale 3D features to align the intermediate feature space. Guided by these explicit physical and geometric constraints, the model's internal representations naturally develop strong 3D awareness. Extensive experiments on spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GeoVR achieves state-of-the-art performance, establishing a new paradigm for endowing foundation models with spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: GeoVR introduces 3D spatial awareness into multimodal LLMs using purely 2D video data, via multi-task geometric supervision and distillation from pre-trained 3D models, achieving state-of-the-art spatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Bridges the gap in intrinsic 3D awareness in MLLMs and mitigates the scarcity of large-scale 3D data by grounding representations in geometry learned from 2D sequences.
AI.method: Multi-objective learning on 2D videos with four geometric targets: (1) inter-frame pose estimation, (2) dense depth regression, (3) metric scale regression, (4) distillation of multi-scale 3D features to align intermediate representations; avoids superficial feature mixing by reshaping the model's internal latent space and distilling 3D priors from pre-trained 3D foundations.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on spatial reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating a new paradigm for instilling spatial intelligence in foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Explicit geometric supervision and cross-domain distillation enable 3D awareness in MLLMs without explicit 3D data, opening pathways for robust spatial understanding and consistency across video frames.

title: Can LLMs Write Correct TLA+ Specifications? Evaluating Natural-Language-to-TLA+ Generation
comment: 12 pages, 11 tables. Accepted at the 21st International Conference on Software Technologies (ICSOFT 2026); Recommended as Best Paper Award Candidate
summary: TLA+ has supported industrial verification at companies such as Amazon and Microsoft, yet writing correct TLA+ specifications from natural language still requires time and expertise, which limits adoption. LLMs show promise, but no prior study measures whether they produce semantically correct TLA+ specifications from natural language. This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of LLM-based TLA+ specification synthesis from natural language. Our study evaluates 30 LLMs across eight families on a curated dataset of 205 TLA+ specifications: 25 open-weight models across four prompting strategies (2,600 runs) and 5 proprietary models under few-shot prompting (130 runs), all validated by the SANY parser and TLC model checker. LLMs achieve up to 26.6% syntactic correctness but only 8.6% semantic correctness, with successes exclusive to progressive prompting. Results show that model size does not predict quality, e.g., DeepSeek r1:8b outperforms its 70B variant across all strategies, which suggests the importance of reasoning alignment for formal languages. Code-specialized models consistently underperform due to negative transfer from mainstream language training. We identify five recurring hallucination categories, all traceable to specific training data biases. These results suggest that current LLMs do not generate reliable TLA+ specifications without expert oversight. We release the evaluation framework, code, and dataset to support reproducibility and future research.

AI.tldr: LLMs currently struggle to produce correct TLA+ specifications from natural language; syntactic correctness up to 26.6%, semantic correctness up to 8.6%, with improvements only under progressive prompting; model size alone does not predict quality; code-specialized models underperform likely due to negative transfer; five hallucination categories traced to training data biases; expert oversight remains essential; evaluation framework, code, and dataset released for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Evaluate the feasibility of using large language models to synthesize semantically correct TLA+ specifications from natural language and understand the limits, biases, and prompting effects.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of 30 LLMs across eight families on 205 TLA+ specifications (25 open-weight models across four prompting strategies: ~2,600 runs; 5 proprietary models under few-shot prompting: ~130 runs). Validation via the SANY parser (syntax) and TLC model checker (semantics). Analysis of prompting strategies, model size, domain specialization, and hallucination categories.
AI.result: Performance is limited: up to 26.6% syntactic correctness and 8.6% semantic correctness; progress-promoting prompting (progressive prompting) yields successes; model size is not predictive (e.g., a smaller DeepSeek model outperforms larger variants); code-specialized models underperform due to negative transfer; five recurring hallucination categories tied to training data biases.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs do not reliably generate correct, verifiable TLA+ specifications from natural language without expert oversight. Alignment with formal reasoning and robust verification remains essential. The authors provide an evaluation framework, code, and dataset to facilitate reproducibility and future research.

title: Learning of Robot Safety Policies via Adversarial Synthetic Scenarios
summary: In this work, we propose an agentic gamification framework for hazard-informed learning of robot safety policies through synthetic scenarios. We model scenario generation as an adversarial game between two agents: a Red Team that explores the space of potential failures by constructing hazardous situations, and a Blue Team that incrementally refines safety policies to prevent them. This iterative process enables efficient discovery of high-risk edge cases that are unlikely to be captured through random simulation or manual enumeration. By combining classical risk modeling with adversarial scenario generation and modern learning paradigms, this work provides a scalable pathway for embedding safety into Physical AI systems operating in complex real-world environments. The paper describes ongoing work. The contribution is a problem formulation and a proposed solution architecture.

AI.tldr: Adversarial Red Team/Blue Team framework for hazard-informed learning of robot safety policies through synthetic scenarios, enabling efficient discovery of high-risk edge cases; current work provides problem formulation and architecture.
AI.motivation: Addresses the challenge of capturing rare but critical failure modes in robot safety policies. Random simulations and manual enumeration often miss edge cases; a scalable, adversarial approach aims to systematically uncover high-risk scenarios in complex real-world contexts.
AI.method: Formulates scenario generation as an adversarial game: Red Team probes potential failures by constructing hazardous situations, while Blue Team iteratively refines safety policies to mitigate them. Combines classical risk modeling with adversarial scenario generation and modern learning paradigms in an iterative loop.
AI.result: Describes ongoing work; no empirical results yet. The stated contributions are a problem formulation and a proposed solution architecture, with a claim of efficient discovery of edge cases compared to random or manual enumeration.
AI.conclusion: Provides a scalable framework to embed safety into Physical AI in complex environments; work is at the formulation/architectural stage and subject to future empirical evaluation.

title: World-Language-Action Model for Unified World Modeling, Language Reasoning, and Action Synthesis
summary: We propose world-language-action (WLA) models as a new class of embodied foundation models. WLA takes textual instructions, images, and robot states as inputs to jointly predict textual subtasks, subgoal images, and robot actions, conjoining the \emph{world modeling interface} to learn from extensive egocentric videos as in the world-action model (WAM) and the \emph{language reasoning} capacities to solve complex long-horizon tasks as in vision-language-action (VLA) models. At the core of WLA lies an \emph{autoregressive (AR)} Transformer backbone, instead of a bidirectional diffusion Transformer as in WAMs, to predict the \emph{next state}, comprising the \emph{semantic-level} textual intention and complementary \emph{fine-grained} physical dynamics. The physical dynamics are supervised by the world modeling objective based on a dedicated World Expert, and are leveraged to ease the characterization of the state-action correlation for the Action Expert. WLA leverages meta-queries to make the world prediction \emph{implicitly} impact the action generation so that the former can be disabled during inference. The world prediction can also be activated to enable test-time scaling for improved robot control. Our WLA-0 prototype, with 2B active parameters, achieves 40 ms per inference on an NVIDIA RTX 5090. Evaluations across simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that WLA-0 achieves state-of-the-art multi-task and long-horizon learning abilities, e.g., 92.94\% success rate on RoboTwin2.0 Clean and 56.5\% success rate on RMBench. WLA-0 also holds the promise to learn novel tasks directly from \emph{cross-embodiment robot videos} without action annotations.

AI.tldr: Proposes world-language-action (WLA) models combining world modeling and language reasoning for embodied AI. Uses AR Transformer to predict next state (textual intention and fine-grained dynamics) from text/images/states; world modeling via World Expert; meta-queries allow world predictions to influence action generation; efficient inference; state-of-the-art results on RoboTwin2.0 and RMBench; cross-embodiment learning from robot videos without actions.
AI.motivation: Bridge world modeling (WAM) and language-enabled action (VLA) to handle long-horizon tasks with multimodal inputs, enabling scalable, interpretable planning and control in embodied agents.
AI.method: Autoregressive Transformer backboned model that takes instructions, images, and robot states, outputs textual subtasks, subgoal images, and actions. World Expert supervises dynamics; Action Expert uses world-model hints. Meta-queries allow optional influence; test-time enablement of world prediction for scaling. WLA-0 with 2B parameters; 40 ms inference on RTX 5090.
AI.result: Achieves 92.94% success on RoboTwin2.0 Clean and 56.5% on RMBench; demonstrates state-of-the-art multi-task and long-horizon learning; enables learning from cross-embodiment robot videos without action annotations.
AI.conclusion: WLA offers a unified, scalable framework integrating world modeling and language reasoning for embodied AI, with efficient inference and potential for continued gains via test-time world-prediction scaling and cross-embodiment learning.

title: ATT-CR: Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal
summary: Cloud removal aims to accurately reconstruct the ground objects obscured by clouds in remote sensing images. Existing Transformer-based methods utilizing self-attention have shown impressive results by effectively modeling long-range dependencies in cloudy images. However, they suffer from the following issues: 1) the high computational complexity of self-attention limits scalability; 2) treating both cloudy and clean pixels as valid within the attention computation brings disturbances in subsequent layers, leading to suboptimal performance. To address these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal (ATT-CR), a model that effectively reduces computational costs and mitigates interference from cloudy pixels. Specifically, it consists of two core components: Triangular Attention (TAN) and Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM). TAN employs lower and upper triangular matrices to approximate Softmax attention with O(N) computational complexity, significantly reducing the computational costs. The FSGM, on the other hand, integrates with TAN to adaptively distinguish between cloudy and clean features, which minimizes the introduction of invalid information into subsequent layers. Extensive experiments on cloud removal benchmarks demonstrate that ATT-CR delivers superior performance compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes ATT-CR, an efficient Transformer variant for cloud removal that uses Triangular Attention (TAN) for linear-complexity attention and a Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM) to filter cloudy vs clean features, yielding superior accuracy with lower cost.
AI.motivation: Self-attention is computationally expensive for high-resolution cloudy remote sensing images and treating all pixels as valid introduces disturbances from clouds in downstream layers.
AI.method: Introduce Triangular Attention with lower and upper triangular matrices to approximate Softmax attention with O(N) complexity; integrate with a Feature Selected Gating Module to adaptively distinguish cloudy from clean features and suppress invalid information.
AI.result: On cloud removal benchmarks, ATT-CR achieves superior performance compared to existing methods while reducing computational costs.
AI.conclusion: ATT-CR provides an efficient and robust cloud removal framework by combining TAN and FSGM, enabling scalable long-range modeling with reduced interference from cloudy regions.

title: Video-Rate Streaming Stylization on a Vision-Aware MLLM-Conditioned Edit Diffusion: Asymmetric Batched Inference on a Distilled UNet + MLLM Text Encoder
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures, 12 tables. Under review at IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology. Code, evaluation harness, and the released v3 Temporal LLLite adapter weights are at https://github.com/otanl/dreamlite-stream (also mirrored to Hugging Face and Zenodo)
summary: Aggressive distillation of the diffusion U-Net inverts the per-frame bottleneck of real-time text-to-image pipelines: once the denoiser is a 4-step or 1-step distilled student, the text encoder becomes the critical path. This inversion is most acute in vision-aware edit diffusion, where the encoder is a multimodal large language model (MLLM). We study the case of a 0.39B distilled edit U-Net paired with a 2.13B MLLM text encoder (Qwen3-VL) and present a streaming pipeline targeted at this regime built around three engineering mechanisms: asymmetric side-stream / main-stream CUDA pipelining with batched text-encoder amortisation (and optional static-prompt caching), a compile-friendly ControlNet-LLLite reformulation that folds the entire U-Net + adapter stack into a single fused graph, and a periodic conditioning-refresh schedule with a hook subset that amortises the per-frame conditioning cost. On a single consumer RTX 3090 Ti at 512x512 the pipeline sustains 27.4 fps over a 480-frame run at batch size B=8 and 29.6 fps at B=16, with end-to-end p50 latency of approximately 0.5 and 1.0 seconds respectively; the same operating point measures 54.9 fps on RTX 4090 and 74.1 fps on RTX 5090. We report video-rate streaming throughput rather than interactive low latency, and locate our numbers against same-stack StreamDiffusion re-runs as systems context, not as a benchmark superiority claim. For the trained oil-painting style, the released temporal adapter generalises within in-clip noise to 19 unused DAVIS-2017 sequences and 15 non-DAVIS clips from seven sources; prompt-level generalisation to unseen style families is bounded and reported separately.

AI.tldr: Video-rate streaming for diffusion-edit pipelines by aggressively distilling the U-Net and optimizing the text-encoder bottleneck, enabling near-real-time editing with a multimodal LLM encoder on consumer GPUs and three engineering mechanisms to fuse and amortize costs.
AI.motivation: When diffusion U-Nets are aggressively distilled (4-step/1-step), the text encoder becomes the critical throughput bottleneck, especially in vision-aware edit diffusion using a multimodal LLM (MLLM). The work targets streaming, video-like throughput rather than interactive ultra-low latency.
AI.method: Use a 0.39B distilled edit U-Net paired with a 2.13B MLLM text encoder (Qwen3-VL). Propose a streaming pipeline built around: (1) asymmetric side-stream/main-stream CUDA pipelining with batched text-encoder amortisation and optional static-prompt caching; (2) a ControlNet-LLLite reformulation folding the U-Net + adapter stack into a single fused graph; (3) a periodic conditioning-refresh schedule with a hook subset to amortise per-frame conditioning costs.
AI.result: On 512x512 images, RTX 3090 Ti: 27.4 fps (B=8) over 480 frames; 29.6 fps (B=16); end-to-end p50 latency ~0.5 s (B=8) and ~1.0 s (B=16). RTX 4090: 54.9 fps; RTX 5090: 74.1 fps. The work reports video-rate streaming throughput (not interactive latency) and situates results relative to StreamDiffusion re-runs. Oil-painting style generalisation: 19 unused DAVIS-2017 sequences and 15 non-DAVIS clips; prompt-level generalisation to unseen style families is bounded and reported separately.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that engineering the encoder bottleneck and fusing the model components enables video-rate streaming diffusion editing on consumer GPUs. Results are context-specific and framed as streaming throughput rather than a universal benchmark, with generalization to new styles bounded and discussed separately.

title: Merging model-based control with multi-agent reinforcement learning for multi-agent cooperative teaming strategies
summary: In this work, we propose a framework that combines multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with model-based control to achieve safe, dynamically feasible actions in cooperative multi-agent tasks. Multi-agent reinforcement learning provides the advantage of learning cooperative policies for multi-agent teams from discrete non-differentiable rewards in a long planning horizon. Model-predictive control is robust and offers safe, dynamically feasible actions in a fast replanning framework for short horizons. We propose an algorithm that extends actor-critic model predictive control for MARL which we refer to as multi-agent actor-critic model predictive control (MA-AC-MPC). We demonstrate the capabilities of this algorithm by applying it to a multi-agent pursuit-evasion scenario. Specifically, we compare the evader team's strategy using the MA-AC-MPC model and a multi-layer perceptron model (MA-AC-MLP). The pursuer team uses augmented proportional navigation as it is accepted as an advanced adversarial control law. We also provide an example with a heterogeneous environment where a drone and omni-wheeled rover cooperate to achieve repeatable and successful landing with 100% success rate in hardware for MA-AC-MPC compared to 60% for MA-AC-MLP. We demonstrate the robustness of the proposed MA-AC-MPC algorithm in hardware for both environments.

AI.tldr: A framework MA-AC-MPC that merges multi-agent actor-critic learning with model-predictive control for safe, dynamically feasible cooperative tasks; evaluated in pursuit-evasion and heterogeneous drone-rover landing with robust hardware performance.
AI.motivation: To reconcile learning-based MARL with the safety and feasibility guarantees of model-based control in multi-agent settings, addressing non-differentiable rewards and long horizons.
AI.method: Introduce MA-AC-MPC, an extension of actor-critic MPC to multi-agent RL; compare against MA-AC-MLP; use augmented proportional navigation for pursuers; test in simulated pursuit-evasion and a hardware landing task with drone and rover in a heterogeneous environment; assess robustness.
AI.result: MA-AC-MPC yields superior performance, including 100% hardware landing success versus 60% for MA-AC-MLP in a heterogeneous setup; demonstrates robustness in hardware across environments; improved safety and feasibility.
AI.conclusion: MA-AC-MPC effectively combines MARL and MPC to achieve safe, dynamically feasible cooperative behavior in multi-agent tasks; validated in both simulation and hardware; suggests broader applicability to complex, real-time multi-agent control.

title: Sample-efficient Low-level Motion Planning for Robotic Manipulation Tasks via Zero-shot Transfer Learning
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, International Conference on Artificial Neural Networks (ICANN) 2026 conference accepted
summary: As robotic systems become more sophisticated, the growing complexity of their motion planning models and the longer training times pose substantial challenges. Evolutionary algorithms such as the Sample-efficient Cross-Entropy Method (iCEM) have recently demonstrated promising potential for low-level real-time planning by leveraging efficient knowledge reuse strategies to improve performance. Although effective in many control tasks, iCEM's performance can be constrained in more complex scenarios, particularly those requiring stacking, sliding, and shelf placement. In this work, we propose a novel iCEM+TL framework that explicitly leverages Transfer Learning (TL), where key iCEM parameters are transferred from simpler upstream tasks to guide more complex downstream tasks. Additionally, we applied Reward Redesign (RR) through task decomposition for stacking objects and shelf placement to optimize task-specific performance. Results from the simulation show that our framework achieves success rate improvements of up to 23%. The framework is further validated on a real Franka Emika robot in a stacking task, demonstrating its practical feasibility for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: iCEM+TL with Reward Redesign (RR) enhances low-level planning for complex robotic tasks by transferring iCEM parameters from simpler upstream tasks and decompose-based task rewards; achieves up to 23% improvement in simulation and validates on a real Franka Emika robot for stacking tasks.
AI.motivation: To tackle rising planning model complexity and long training times in robotic motion planning, especially for stacking, sliding, and shelf placement, by leveraging knowledge reuse through transfer learning and task-specific reward shaping.
AI.method: Apply Transfer Learning to initialize and guide iCEM parameters using upstream simple tasks; implement Reward Redesign via task decomposition for stacking and shelf tasks; evaluate in simulation and validate on a real robot.
AI.result: Simulation shows improvements in success rate by up to 23%; real-world stacking experiment with a Franka Emika robot confirms practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: TL and RR enable more efficient, effective, and deployable iCEM-based planning for complex manipulation tasks, suggesting potential for broader applicability in real-time robotics.

title: MDP-GRPO: Stabilized Group Relative Policy Optimization for Multi-Constraint Instruction Following
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference. 14 pages, 9 figures
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is ideal for multi-constraint instruction following, yet standard group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) becomes unstable under discrete, low-dispersion rewards, where within-group reward distributions are frequently homogeneous. We identify and formalize three pathologies of z-score group normalization in this regime: low-variance amplification, mean-centering blindness, and zero-variance collapse. To address them, we propose MDP-GRPO, which stabilizes learning through (1) multi-temperature sampling to increase reward dispersion, (2) dual-anchor advantages to restore gradients in homogeneous groups and stop mean-centering blindness, (3) prospect-theoretic shaping to bound updates and penalize violations based on Kahneman and Tversky's theory, and (4) asymmetric KL regularization. Evaluated on FollowBench, IFEval, and a curated multi-constraint dataset, MDP-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO, improving strict constraint satisfaction by up to 5.0% on Llama-3.2-3B. Our method also enables stable convergence with small group sizes while preserving general capabilities on MMLU and ARC.

AI.tldr: MDP-GRPO stabilizes GRPO under discrete, low-dispersion rewards using multi-temperature sampling, dual-anchor advantages, prospect-theoretic shaping, and asymmetric KL regularization, yielding up to 5% improvement in strict constraint satisfaction and stable convergence on small groups.
AI.motivation: To address instability and three pathologies of z-score group normalization in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards when within-group reward distributions are homogeneous, enabling reliable multi-constraint instruction following.
AI.method: MDP-GRPO introduces four components: (1) multi-temperature sampling to increase reward dispersion; (2) dual-anchor advantages to restore gradients in homogeneous groups and avoid mean-centering blindness; (3) prospect-theoretic shaping to bound updates and penalize violations; (4) asymmetric KL regularization, all within the GRPO framework.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on FollowBench, IFEval, and a curated multi-constraint dataset shows MDP-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO, improving strict constraint satisfaction by up to 5.0% on Llama-3.2-3B, with stable convergence even for small group sizes and preserved performance on MMLU and ARC.
AI.conclusion: MDP-GRPO effectively mitigates z-score normalization pathologies under low-dispersion rewards, stabilizing learning and enhancing multi-constraint instruction-following performance while maintaining general capabilities.

title: Integrating Mechanistic and Data-Driven Models for Neurological Disorders through Differentiable Programming
summary: Advances in computational modeling, neuroimaging, and artificial intelligence are revolutionizing the modeling of neurological disorders for improved diagnostics, prognosis, and treatment planning. Mechanistic models provide valuable scientific insight into the disorders, but in practice they are often simplified with assumptions or computationally expensive and slow to solve. However, while purely data driven approaches provide speed and scalability, they require large, high quality data to train and generally suffer from interpretability and generalization issues. This perspective paper presents a structured overview of hybrid modeling strategies, which combine deep learning models with physics based solvers, and are categorized into parallel, series, and parallel-series architectures. Three main approaches that have been emphasized are residual modeling for missing or incomplete physics, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs) for continuous time dynamics approximation, and solver in the loop that accelerates traditional solvers with neural approximations. These hybrid models integrate the governing differential equation based formulations and deep learning to characterize the evolution of neurological disorders, and promise advanced personalized neurological modeling. In addition, the study explores and proposes different hybrid configurations to improve diagnosis accuracy, predict disease progression, and inform treatment strategies across a range of neurological disorders. These capabilities outperform standalone mechanistic or purely data driven approaches, making hybrid modeling a powerful tool, especially in applications involving modeling the progression and treatment responses in neurological conditions such as brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke.

AI.tldr: Hybrid physics-informed deep learning architectures (parallel, series, and parallel-series) that combine DL with physics-based solvers to model neurological disorders, leveraging residual physics modeling, Neural ODEs, and solver-in-the-loop techniques to enhance diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of purely mechanistic models (simplifications and slow solvers) and purely data-driven approaches (data requirements, limited interpretability and generalization) by providing a structured framework for integrated models in neurology.
AI.method: A perspective that reviews and organizes hybrid modeling strategies, categorizing architectures into parallel, series, and parallel-series. Emphasizes three core approaches: residual modeling for missing/incomplete physics, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for continuous-time dynamics, and solver-in-the-loop to accelerate traditional solvers with neural approximations. Discusses integrating differential-equation-based formulations with deep learning to model progression and treatment response in neurological disorders, and proposes configurations to improve diagnosis accuracy and disease progression prediction across brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke.
AI.result: Synthesis and classification of existing hybrid models; conceptual framework with qualitative insights on benefits, trade-offs, and potential applications. No reported empirical results within the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid models hold promise for personalized neurological modeling, potentially outperforming purely mechanistic or purely data-driven approaches in diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning. They are particularly relevant for modeling disease progression and treatment response in conditions such as brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke; further work is needed to evaluate and operationalize these configurations.

title: On Advantage Estimates for Max@K Policy Gradients
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is widely used for post-training reasoning models, but sparse outcome rewards make exploration difficult. A complementary approach is to optimize inference-time objectives such as pass@K and max@K directly, yet existing policy-gradient estimators for these objectives use different signals, baselines, and normalizations, making their relationships unclear. We study this issue through baseline design and advantage centering. Starting from the advantage estimator of a leading method in the field, we show that it is policy-gradient unbiased but yields a non-centered advantage. We then introduce a Leave-Two-Out baseline that preserves policy-gradient unbiasedness while making realized batch advantages exactly centered. The resulting method, MaxPO, has an efficient quadratic-time implementation and integrates naturally into group-based RL for LLM post-training. We further derive the canonical finite-batch advantage for max@K, providing a unified view of existing advantage estimators. Empirically, we verify that the L2O baseline reduces gradient variance and outperforms non-centered alternatives.

AI.tldr: Unifies and improves policy-gradient estimators for max@K objectives in RL for LLM post-training by centering advantages via a Leave-Two-Out baseline (MaxPO), deriving a canonical finite-batch advantage, and showing variance reduction and practical efficiency.
AI.motivation: Sparse outcome rewards hinder exploration; there is ambiguity in how different max@K estimators relate due to differing baselines/normalizations; need a centered, unbiased, efficient approach for post-training LLMs.
AI.method: Analyze existing advantage estimator; prove unbiased but non-centered; introduce Leave-Two-Out (L2O) baseline; show it preserves unbiasedness and centers batch advantages; derive canonical finite-batch advantage for max@K; provide quadratic-time MaxPO algorithm; integrate with group-based RL for LLM post-training.
AI.result: L2O baseline reduces gradient variance; MaxPO outperforms non-centered baselines; provides efficient quadratic-time implementation; yields a unified view of existing estimators; empirical validation on LLM post-training tasks.
AI.conclusion: Baseline design clarifies relationships among max@K estimators; canonical finite-batch advantage established; MaxPO offers variance reduction and practical efficiency for post-training RL of LLMs; supports better exploration and learning with sparse rewards.

title: GRAMformer: Any-Order Modality Interactions via Volumetric Multimodal Cross-Attention
summary: Transformer-based multimodal models rely on attention mechanisms to integrate information across heterogeneous modalities. Despite their success, existing multimodal attention formulations compute their scores through collections of pairwise dot-product interactions or by concatenating all the modalities into the keys, even when multiple modalities should be jointly involved. As a consequence, current approaches either incur quadratic complexity in the number of modalities or fail to explicitly model interactions that depend on the joint configuration of multiple representations. In this work, we introduce the Volumetric Multimodal cross-Attention (VMA), a novel cross-attention mechanism in which attention scores are defined as a function of the joint geometry of a query and multiple modality-specific keys. VMA computes the volume spanned by query and key vectors across multiple modalities, capturing joint multimodal dependencies beyond pairwise similarity, enabling native modeling of any-order modality interactions. We integrate VMA into our novel multimodal transformer architecture, named GRAMformer, explicitly designed to integrate any number of modalities. We evaluate the proposed model on multimodal learning tasks, demonstrating improved effectiveness and efficiency.

AI.tldr: Introduces Volumetric Multimodal cross-Attention (VMA) that defines attention scores via the joint geometry of a query and multiple modality-specific keys, enabling any-order multimodal interactions; integrated into GRAMformer to handle any number of modalities with improved effectiveness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal attention either relies on pairwise dot-product interactions (quadratic in the number of modalities) or concatenates modalities into the keys, which overlooks joint interactions and scales poorly. There is a need for a mechanism that captures higher-order, joint interactions across multiple modalities efficiently.
AI.method: Proposes VMA, where attention scores are computed from the volume spanned by the query and key vectors across multiple modalities, capturing joint multimodal dependencies beyond pairwise similarity. This enables explicit modeling of any-order modality interactions. VMA is integrated into a novel multimodal transformer architecture called GRAMformer that can ingest and fuse any number of modalities.
AI.result: Evaluated on multimodal learning tasks, the model demonstrates improved effectiveness and efficiency compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: VMA enables native modeling of higher-order (any-order) modality interactions and can improve performance and efficiency in multimodal transformers; GRAMformer provides a flexible architecture for integrating arbitrary numbers of modalities.

title: TAM: Torque Adaptation Module for Robust Motion Transfer in Manipulation
summary: A policy tuned for one robot often behaves differently on another, whether due to the sim-to-real gap, unknown payloads, or the differing dynamics of two instances of the same robot. In contact-rich, dynamic manipulation, even small motion discrepancies can result in failure to track reference motion, since they disrupt the timing and modes of contact. Common remedies, such as domain randomization or system identification, either produce overly conservative task policies or require data that must be recollected for each robot or payload. We introduce the Torque Adaptation Module (TAM), a learned module that adapts the torque commands sent to the robot to match the behavior of an ideal robot. TAM operates between the low-level controller that tracks the policy's actions and the robot's torque interface. It includes a history encoder that embeds proprioceptive history into a latent state and a torque adaptor that computes residual torque corrections. Because TAM depends only on proprioceptive history and not on policy observations, or the action space, the same TAM weights can be reused to adapt policies with different action spaces (joint targets, end-effector targets, or direct torques). The policies themselves do not need to be trained with domain randomization of robot parameters. Instead, we offload the need for domain randomization to TAM by training it entirely in randomized simulation, using multi-robot pretraining followed by a robot-specific fine-tuning step that still requires no real-robot data. We evaluate TAM zero-shot on a real Franka Panda robot across dynamic manipulation tasks that include a vision-based box pushing policy (from RL), a flip policy (from BC), and an MPC ball-on-plate balancing. Our experiments show that TAM improves zero-shot real-robot execution compared to online system identification and RMA baselines and enables robust dynamic manipulation performance.

AI.tldr: A torque adaptation module (TAM) sits between the policy controller and the robot to adapt torque commands using proprioceptive history, enabling zero-shot transfer across robots and payloads in dynamic manipulation.
AI.motivation: Policy transfer across different robots is fragile due to sim-to-real gaps and unmodeled payload dynamics. Domain randomization and system identification either over-constrain policies or require data per robot. A learnable, robot-agnostic torque adaptor can bridge this gap.
AI.method: TAM comprises a history encoder that embeds proprioceptive history into a latent state and a torque adaptor that outputs residual torque corrections. It acts between the policy’s low-level controller and the robot’s torque interface, making it agnostic to policy observations and action spaces. TAM is trained entirely in randomized simulation with multi-robot pretraining and a robot-specific fine-tuning step, requiring no real-robot data. It generalizes across different action spaces (joint targets, end-effector targets, direct torques). Zero-shot real-robot tests on a Franka Panda with three tasks (vision-based box pushing, BC flip policy, MPC ball-on-plate) show improved performance over online system identification and RMA baselines.
AI.result: TAM improves zero-shot real-robot execution across dynamic manipulation tasks compared to online system identification and RMA baselines, enabling more robust and accurate tracking of reference motions in the presence of parameter variations and contact dynamics.
AI.conclusion: A robot-agnostic torque adaptation module can offload domain randomization to the adapter, enabling cross-robot policy transfer without real-data collection, while maintaining performance in dynamic manipulation tasks.

title: CLEAR: Cognition and Latent Evaluation for Adaptive Routing in End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving models often struggle to balance multi-modal maneuver generation with real-time inference constraints. While diffusion models successfully capture diverse driving behaviors, their iterative denoising process incurs unacceptable latency for safety-critical deployment. To address this, we propose CLEAR (Cognition and Latent Evaluation for Adaptive Routing), a framework that combines ultra-fast generative planning with deep semantic reasoning. CLEAR employs Drive-JEPA as the visual encoder and replaces the multi-step denoising chain with a single-step conditional drift in a VAE latent space, introducing a conditioning coefficient to balance diversity and expert precision. Meanwhile, we fully fine-tune Qwen~3.5~0.8B on driving QA pairs to extract scene-aware hidden states. These states guide both an Adaptive Scheduler, which selects the conditioning coefficient $α$ and sample count $N$ from a discrete set of predefined schemes, and a cross-attention scorer that selects the optimal trajectory from candidates. On the NAVSIM v1 benchmark, CLEAR achieves a state-of-the-art PDMS of 93.7.
