Our results demonstrate that high-fidelity, multi-modal planning can be executed efficiently without dense geometric annotations or iterative sampling.

AI.tldr: CLEAR is a fast, adaptive planning framework for end-to-end autonomous driving that replaces diffusion with a single-step latent drift and uses QA-driven scene reasoning to select diverse, high-precision trajectories, achieving state-of-the-art PDMS on NAVSIM v1.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based planning yields diverse maneuvers but incurs unacceptable latency for real-time safety-critical driving; need fast, multi-modal planning with minimal geometric supervision.
AI.method: Use Drive-JEPA as visual encoder; single-step conditional drift in VAE latent space with a conditioning coefficient α; fine-tune Qwen-3.5-0.8B on driving QA pairs to obtain scene-aware hidden states; Adaptive Scheduler selects α and N from predefined schemes; cross-attention scorer selects the best trajectory from candidates.
AI.result: On NAVSIM v1, PDMS 93.7; demonstrates high-fidelity, multi-modal planning with efficient inference and without dense geometric annotations or iterative sampling.
AI.conclusion: CLEAR enables efficient, high-quality multi-modal autonomous driving planning by combining fast conditional generative planning with semantic reasoning, outperforming diffusion-based approaches in real-time settings.

title: MPCoT: Reward-Guided Multi-Path Latent Reasoning for Test-Time Scalable Vision-Language-Action
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies remain brittle in long-horizon and high-uncertainty control, where one-pass action decoding provides limited inference-time deliberation. Explicit chain-of-thought can increase reasoning depth, but introduces token latency and an indirect text-to-action interface. We propose MPCoT, a reward-guided multi-path latent reasoning framework that initializes $M$ hypotheses, refines them for K weight-tied steps, and softly aggregates them before action decoding. A training-only path-preference objective evaluates candidate action branches with expert-action consistency, world-model/VLM-based progress, and success feedback to align the latent path scorer with downstream execution quality. MPCoT preserves the original 8-step action interface, generates zero reasoning tokens, and exposes configurable inference controls (K,M). Under matched protocols on LIBERO and CALVIN, MPCoT improves long-horizon performance, with ablations confirming depth-width effects, confidence-weighted aggregation, and reward-guided path supervision.

AI.tldr: MPCoT introduces a reward-guided multi-path latent reasoning framework for vision-language-action policies that preserves a fixed 8-step action interface, generates zero reasoning tokens, and uses M initial hypotheses refined over K steps with soft aggregation to improve long-horizon control without adding interface latency.
AI.motivation: One-pass action decoding often fails in long-horizon, high-uncertainty control; explicit chain-of-thought improves reasoning but adds token latency and a text-to-action roundtrip. A latent, reward-guided multi-path approach aims to enhance reasoning depth while keeping a non-tokenized action interface and configurable inference controls.
AI.method: Initialize M hypotheses; refine them for K weight-tied steps; softly aggregate them before action decoding; train with a path-preference objective that assesses candidate branches via expert-action consistency, world-model/VLM-based progress, and success feedback to align the latent path scorer with downstream execution quality; retain 8-step action interface and zero-token reasoning; expose inference controls (K, M).
AI.result: Under matched protocols on LIBERO and CALVIN, MPCoT improves long-horizon performance. Ablations indicate effects of depth (K) and width (M), benefit of confidence-weighted aggregation, and effectiveness of reward-guided path supervision in aligning latent paths with execution quality.
AI.conclusion: MPCoT provides a scalable, token-free latent multi-path reasoning framework that boosts long-horizon robustness in vision-language-action policies while preserving the original action interface and offering controllable inference depth and width.

title: HANDOFF: Humanoid Agentic Task-Space Whole-Body Control via Distilled Complementary Teachers
summary: For a humanoid robot to be deployed in the real world, the choice of command space (i.e., the interface between task planning and whole-body control) is crucial. Existing whole-body controllers typically demand dense kinematic or spatial references that planners struggle to synthesize from task semantics. We instead propose a compact, explicit interface that is intuitive, general, modular, and expressive enough for diverse manipulation skills. To this end, we introduce HANDOFF, a single humanoid whole-body controller that follows this interface and is distilled via multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme into a mixture-of-experts student from three complementary specialists: whole-body motion tracking with safety-filtered data, locomotion, and fall-recovery. On the Unitree G1, HANDOFF matches state-of-the-art velocity tracking and offers one of the largest robust manipulation workspaces. We further demonstrate hardware feasibility through multiple natural-language-driven task roll-outs, powered by a VLM-driven agentic planner with no task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: HANDOFF is a single humanoid whole-body controller with an explicit, modular interface for planning-control, distilled via multi-teacher KL into a mixture-of-experts, enabling robust manipulation on a Unitree G1 and NL-driven task rollouts with a VLM planner.
AI.motivation: Current whole-body controllers require dense kinematic or spatial references that planners struggle to synthesize from task semantics; there is a need for a compact, explicit, intuitive interface between task planning and control that is general and expressive enough for diverse manipulation skills.
AI.method: Introduce HANDOFF, a single humanoid WBC that follows an explicit interface; distilled via multi-teacher KL distillation under a context-conditioned gating scheme into a mixture-of-experts student from three specialists: whole-body motion tracking with safety-filtered data, locomotion, and fall-recovery. Demonstrated on the Unitree G1; hardware-feasible with NL-driven task roll-outs powered by a VLM-driven agentic planner with no task-specific data or controller fine-tuning.
AI.result: Handoff matches state-of-the-art velocity tracking and offers one of the largest robust manipulation workspaces on the Unitree G1; experiments include multiple NL-driven task roll-outs confirming hardware feasibility without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The HANDOFF interface is intuitive, general, modular, and expressive, enabling robust, diverse manipulation through a single controller; the approach demonstrates real-world feasibility and scalable integration with language-driven planning.
